title: Design, Modelling and Experimental Evaluation of a Tendon-driven Wrist Abduction-Adduction Mechanism for an upper limb exoskeleton
comment: 8 pages and 8 figures. Submitted to IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. Includes experimental validation on human participants
summary: Wrist exoskeletons play a vital role in rehabilitation and assistive applications, yet conventional actuation mechanisms such as electric motors or pneumatics often introduce undesirable weight, friction, and complexity. This paper presents a novel single-cable (tendon), torsional-spring-assisted actuation mechanism for wrist abduction-adduction, and a simulation-based method for selecting its stiffness parameters. The mechanism employs a single Bowden cable passively tensioned by a spiral torsional spring (clock spring) to maintain continuous cable tension without antagonistic actuation. Kinematic and dynamic modeling of the mechanism was performed to estimate the required torque and identify optimal spring parameters. These simulation-derived parameters guided the design of a functional prototype, which was experimentally evaluated with five participants with no motor disabilities (NMD) under varying arm positions and loading conditions using three spring configurations to account for user variability and modeling uncertainties. Experimental results show consistent agreement with simulation-derived trends, with the nominal spring configuration achieving balanced motion range, torque demand, and repeatability. The results demonstrate that simulation-informed stiffness selection can effectively guide the design of compact, cable-driven wrist exoskeletons while reducing reliance on empirical tuning.

AI.tldr: A single-cable, torsional-spring-assisted wrist exoskeleton uses a Bowden cable tensioned by a spiral torsional spring to actuate wrist abduction-adduction with passive tensioning. Simulation-guided stiffness selection optimizes parameters, reducing empirical tuning. A prototype tested on five healthy participants validates the approach, showing agreement between simulations and experiments and balanced motion, torque, and repeatability.
AI.motivation: To address drawbacks of conventional actuation in wrist exoskeletons (weight, friction, complexity) by enabling compact, low-profile actuation with minimal active control, using a passive spring-tensioned single-cable mechanism to reduce weight and improve reliability.
AI.method: Developed kinematic and dynamic models of the single-cable Bowden setup with a spiral torsional spring. Employed simulation to pick optimal spring stiffness for torque needs. Built a functional prototype and evaluated it experimentally on five healthy participants under varying arm positions and loads using three spring configurations to account for variability and modeling uncertainty.
AI.result: Simulation-derived stiffness parameters produced trends aligned with experimental data. The nominal spring configuration offered a balanced trade-off among range of motion, torque demand, and repeatability, indicating effective design guidance from simulation.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-informed stiffness selection can effectively guide the design of compact, cable-driven wrist exoskeletons, reducing the need for extensive empirical tuning and improving integration of passive actuation with cable-driven mechanisms.

title: A Tendon-Driven Wrist Abduction-Adduction Joint Improves Performance of a 5 DoF Upper Limb Exoskeleton -- Implementation and Experimental Evaluation
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures and 1 table. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering as invited IEEE EMBC special issue paper. Under review after first revision
summary: Wrist function is essential in performing activities of daily living (ADLs). However, there is limited experimental evidence on the functional impact of wrist Abduction-Adduction (Ab-Ad) joint assistance in upper limb exoskeletons (ULEs) for rehabilitation. This study evaluates the effect of implementing an active wrist Ab-Ad joint in a five degree of freedom (DoF) ULE, EXOTIC2 exoskeleton, to support individuals with severe motor impairments. Methods: A compact, lightweight wrist module with tendon-driven abduction and spring-driven adduction was integrated into the EXOTIC exoskeleton. Eight adults with no motor disabilities completed drinking and scratching tasks under randomized wrist-enabled and wrist-locked conditions along with a preliminary feasibility test in one individual with Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Kinematic and task performance metrics including wrist range of motion, task completion time, spillage and leveling metrics were assessed. Results: Implementing the wrist Ab-Ad DoF improved task success metrics. Spill incidence during the drinking task decreased from 56% to 3%, and leveling success for scratching task improved from 28% to 75%. Conclusion: Integrating wrist Ab-Ad assistance improved key functional task outcomes without increasing execution time. Significance: The study provides the experimental evidence that active wrist Ab-Ad control enhances task-level performance in exoskeleton-assisted ADLs.

AI.tldr: Wrist Ab-Ad joint actuation in a 5-DoF upper limb exoskeleton improves ADL task performance (drinking, scratching) in healthy adults and a ALS case, reducing spills and improving leveling without increasing task time.
AI.motivation: There is limited experimental evidence on the functional impact of wrist abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad) joint assistance in upper limb exoskeletons for rehabilitation; evaluating its effect on functional tasks can validate its practical benefit.
AI.method: Integrate a compact tendon-driven abduction and spring-driven adduction wrist module into the EXOTIC2 exoskeleton (5 DoF). Eight healthy adults performed drinking and scratching tasks under randomized wrist-enabled vs wrist-locked conditions; one ALS participant underwent a preliminary feasibility test. Measured wrist ROM, task completion time, spillings, and leveling metrics.
AI.result: Wrist Ab-Ad DoF improved task success metrics. Spill incidence for drinking decreased from 56% to 3%; scratching leveling success improved from 28% to 75%. No increase in task execution time.
AI.conclusion: Active wrist Ab-Ad assistance enhances functional task performance in exoskeleton-assisted ADLs without adding execution time.

title: Clinical Evaluation of a Tongue-Controlled Wrist Abduction-Adduction Assistance in a 6-DoF Upper-Limb Exoskeleton for Individuals with ALS and SCI
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures and 2 tables. This work has been submitted to the IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
summary: Upper-limb exoskeletons (ULEs) have the potential to restore functional independence in individuals with severe motor impairments; however, the clinical relevance of wrist degrees of freedom (DoF), particularly abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad), remains insufficiently evaluated. This study investigates the functional and user-perceived impact of wrist Ab-Ad assistance during two activities of daily living (ADLs). Wrist Ab-Ad assistance in a tongue-controlled 6-DoF ULE, EXOTIC2, was evaluated in a within-subject study involving one individual with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and five individuals with spinal cord injury. Participants performed drinking and scratch stick leveling tasks with EXOTIC2 under two conditions: with and without wrist Ab-Ad assistance. Outcome measure included task success, task completion time, kinematic measures, and a usability questionnaire capturing comfort, functional perception, and acceptance. Enabling wrist Ab-Ad improved task success rates across both ADLs, with consistent reductions in spillage (from 77.8% spillages to 22.2%) and failed placements (from 66.7% to 16.7%). Participants utilized task-specific subsets of the available wrist range of motion, indicating that effective control within functional ranges was more critical than maximal joint excursion. Questionnaire responses indicated no increase in discomfort with the additional DoF and reflected perceived improvements in task performance. In conclusion, wrist Ab-Ad assistance enhances functional task performance in assistive exoskeleton use without compromising user comfort. However, its effectiveness depends on task context, control usability, and individual user strategies. This study provides clinically relevant, user-centered evidence supporting the inclusion of wrist Ab-Ad in ULEs, emphasizing the importance of balancing functional capability with usability in assistive device design.

AI.tldr: Wrist abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad) in a tongue-controlled 6-DoF upper-limb exoskeleton improves task success and reduces errors in two ADLs without increasing discomfort; effectiveness depends on task context and user strategy.
AI.motivation: To assess the clinical relevance of wrist Ab-Ad DoF in upper-limb exoskeletons, addressing a gap where Ab-Ad evaluation is limited and determining functional/user-perceived impact.
AI.method: Within-subject study with EXOTIC2 (tongue-controlled, 6-DoF). Participants: 1 ALS, 5 SCI. Tasks: drinking and scratch stick leveling. Conditions: with vs without wrist Ab-Ad. Measures: task success, completion time, kinematics, and usability questionnaire (comfort, function, acceptance).
AI.result: Wrist Ab-Ad enabled improved task success across both ADLs; spillages reduced from 77.8% to 22.2% and failed placements from 66.7% to 16.7%. Participants used task-specific wrist ROM subsets; effectiveness linked to functional ranges rather than maximal excursion. No increase in discomfort; reported performance improvements. Overall, Ab-Ad enhances performance without compromising comfort, but effectiveness depends on task context, control usability, and user strategies.
AI.conclusion: Inclusion of wrist Ab-Ad in ULEs can enhance functional task performance and user-perceived usefulness without added discomfort; success requires alignment of Ab-Ad control with task demands and user strategies, underpinning a user-centered design approach.

title: A Survey of Legged Robotics in Non-Inertial Environments: Past, Present, and Future
summary: Legged robots have demonstrated remarkable agility on rigid, stationary ground, but their locomotion reliability remains limited in non-inertial environments, where the supporting ground moves, tilts, or accelerates. Such conditions arise in ground transportation, maritime platforms, and aerospace settings, and they introduce persistent time-varying disturbances that break the stationary-ground assumptions underlying conventional legged locomotion. This survey reviews the state of the art in modeling, state estimation, and control for legged robots in non-inertial environments. We summarize representative application domains and motion characteristics, analyze the root causes of locomotion performance degradation, and review existing methods together with their key assumptions and limitations. We further identify open problems in robot-environment coupling, observability, robustness, and experimental validation, and discuss future directions in autonomy, system-level design, bio-inspired strategies, safety, and testing. The survey aims to clarify the technical foundations of this emerging area and support the development of reliable legged robots for real-world dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: This survey analyzes legged robotics in non-inertial environments, focusing on modeling, state estimation, and control; it catalogs domains, diagnoses performance degradation, summarizes existing methods and assumptions, and outlines open problems and future directions.
AI.motivation: Non-inertial ground conditions (moving, tilting, accelerating) violate stationary-ground assumptions and undermine legged locomotion reliability. Applications span ground transport, maritime platforms, and aerospace, necessitating robust, reliable locomotion in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and synthesis: categorize application domains and motion characteristics; analyze root causes of performance degradation; compare existing methods, assumptions, and limitations; identify gaps in robot–environment coupling, observability, robustness, and experimental validation; outline open problems and future directions across autonomy, system design, bio-inspired strategies, safety, and testing.
AI.result: Comprehensive mapping of the state of the art in legged locomotion under non-inertial ground conditions, with analysis of key limitations (robot–environment coupling, observability, robustness, experimental validation) and a catalog of open problems and recommended directions for future research.
AI.conclusion: The survey clarifies the technical foundations of legged robots operating in dynamically evolving environments and aims to guide development toward reliable, safe, and autonomous systems for real-world applications, highlighting the need for cross-domain validation and advanced control/estimation strategies.

title: Frequency-Forcing: From Scaling-as-Time to Soft Frequency Guidance
summary: While standard flow-matching models transport noise to data uniformly, incorporating an explicit generation order - specifically, establishing coarse, low-frequency structure before fine detail - has proven highly effective for synthesizing natural images. Two recent works offer distinct paradigms for this. K-Flow imposes a hard frequency constraint by reinterpreting a frequency scaling variable as flow time, running the trajectory inside a transformed amplitude space. Latent Forcing provides a soft ordering mechanism by coupling the pixel flow with an auxiliary semantic latent flow via asynchronous time schedules, leaving the pixel interpolation path itself untouched. Viewed from the angle of improving pixel generation, we observe that forcing - guiding generation with an earlier-maturing auxiliary stream - offers a highly compatible route to scale-ordered generation without rewriting the core flow coordinate. Building on this, we propose Frequency-Forcing, which realizes K-Flow's frequency ordering through Latent Forcing's soft mechanism: a standard pixel flow is guided by an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier in time. Unlike Latent Forcing, whose scratchpad relies on a heavy pretrained encoder (e.g., DINO), our frequency scratchpad is derived from the data itself via a lightweight learnable wavelet packet transform. We term this a self-forcing signal, which avoids external dependencies while learning a basis better adapted to data statistics than the fixed bases used in hard frequency flows. On ImageNet-256, Frequency-Forcing consistently improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines, and naturally composes with a semantic stream to yield further gains. This illustrates that forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile, path-preserving alternative to hard frequency flows.

AI.tldr: Introduce Frequency-Forcing, a soft, data-adaptive forcing mechanism that guides pixel flow with an auxiliary low-frequency stream derived from a learnable wavelet packet transform, achieving scale-ordered generation and improved FID on ImageNet-256, compatible with semantic streams.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of hard frequency constraints (K-Flow) and soft but encoder-dependent approaches (Latent Forcing) by offering a self-contained, data-adaptive, path-preserving method to impose coarse-to-fine structure in generative flows.
AI.method: Integrate a standard pixel flow with an auxiliary low-frequency stream; the auxiliary stream matures earlier in time; derive the scratchpad from learnable wavelet packet transform of data, creating a self-forcing signal; can compose with semantic stream.
AI.result: Empirically improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines on ImageNet-256; enjoys improved performance when combined with semantic stream; demonstrates versatility and path-preserving nature relative to hard frequency flows.
AI.conclusion: Forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile alternative to hard frequency flows; can be used to guide generation without rewriting core flow coordinates, and reduces external dependencies by learning data-adaptive bases.

title: Thinking Like a Botanist: Challenging Multimodal Language Models with Intent-Driven Chain-of-Inquiry
summary: Vision evaluations are typically done through multi-step processes. In most contemporary fields, experts analyze images using structured, evidence-based adaptive questioning. In plant pathology, botanists inspect leaf images, identify visual cues, infer diagnostic intent, and probe further with targeted questions that adapt to species, symptoms, and severity. This structured probing is crucial for accurate disease diagnosis and treatment formulation. Yet current vision-language models are evaluated on single-turn question answering. To address this gap, we introduce PlantInquiryVQA, a benchmark for studying multi-step, intent-driven visual reasoning in botanical diagnosis. We formalize a Chain of Inquiry framework modeling diagnostic trajectories as ordered question-answer sequences conditioned on grounded visual cues and explicit epistemic intent. We release a dataset of 24,950 expert-curated plant images and 138,068 question-answer pairs annotated with visual grounding, severity labels, and domain-specific reasoning templates. Evaluations on top-tier Multimodal Large Language Models reveal that while they describe visual symptoms adequately, they struggle with safe clinical reasoning and accurate diagnosis. Importantly, structured question-guided inquiry significantly improves diagnostic correctness, reduces hallucination, and increases reasoning efficiency. We hope PlantInquiryVQA serves as a foundational benchmark in advancing research to train diagnostic agents to reason like expert botanists rather than static classifiers.

AI.tldr: Introduces PlantInquiryVQA, a benchmark for multi-step, intent-driven visual reasoning in plant disease diagnosis, with a large annotated dataset and a Chain of Inquiry framework; findings show LLMs struggle with safe clinical reasoning but structured questioning enhances accuracy and reduces hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Address the gap that contemporary vision-language models are evaluated on single-turn QA and do not emulate expert diagnostic workflows. There is a need for datasets and frameworks that support ordered, intent-driven inquiry aligned with botanical diagnosis and grounding.
AI.method: Proposes a Chain of Inquiry framework that models diagnostic trajectories as ordered QA sequences conditioned on grounded visual cues and explicit epistemic intent. Builds PlantInquiryVQA with 24,950 expert-curated plant images and 138,068 QA pairs annotated with visual grounding, severity labels, and reasoning templates. Evaluates top-tier Multimodal LLMs on this benchmark, emphasizing safety and reasoning quality.
AI.result: LLMs can adequately describe visual symptoms but struggle with safe clinical reasoning and accurate diagnosis. Structured, question-guided inquiry significantly improves diagnostic correctness, reduces hallucination, and increases reasoning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PlantInquiryVQA provides a foundational benchmark for training diagnostic agents that reason like expert botanists, moving beyond static classifiers toward structured, trajectory-based diagnosis.

title: Open-H-Embodiment: A Large-Scale Dataset for Enabling Foundation Models in Medical Robotics
summary: Autonomous medical robots hold promise to improve patient outcomes, reduce provider workload, democratize access to care, and enable superhuman precision. However, autonomous medical robotics has been limited by a fundamental data problem: existing medical robotic datasets are small, single-embodiment, and rarely shared openly, restricting the development of foundation models that the field needs to advance. We introduce Open-H-Embodiment, the largest open dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics to date, spanning more than 49 institutions and multiple robotic platforms including the CMR Versius, Intuitive Surgical's da Vinci, da Vinci Research Kit (dVRK), Rob Surgical BiTrack, Virtual Incision's MIRA, Moon Surgical Maestro, and a variety of custom systems, spanning surgical manipulation, robotic ultrasound, and endoscopy procedures. We demonstrate the research enabled by this dataset through two foundation models. GR00T-H is the first open foundation vision-language-action model for medical robotics, which is the only evaluated model to achieve full end-to-end task completion on a structured suturing benchmark (25% of trials vs. 0% for all others) and achieves 64% average success across a 29-step ex vivo suturing sequence. We also train Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator, the first action-conditioned world model to enable multi-embodiment surgical simulation from a single checkpoint, spanning nine robotic platforms and supporting in silico policy evaluation and synthetic data generation for the medical domain. These results suggest that open, large-scale medical robot data collection can serve as critical infrastructure for the research community, enabling advances in robot learning, world modeling, and beyond.

AI.tldr: Open-H-Embodiment offers the largest open medical robotics dataset with synchronized video and kinematics across 49 institutions and multiple platforms; demonstrates two open foundation models, GR00T-H (vision-language-action) achieving end-to-end suturing and 64% success on 29-step ex vivo suturing, and Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator (action-conditioned world model) enabling cross-platform surgical simulation from a single checkpoint.
AI.motivation: Current medical robotic datasets are small, single-embodiment, and rarely shared, impeding the development of scalable foundation models and robust learning across platforms; there is a need for open, diverse, multi-platform data to accelerate research.
AI.method: Assemble Open-H-Embodiment: a large-scale dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics, spanning 49 institutions and multiple platforms (e.g., CMR Versius, da Vinci, dVRK, Rob Surgical BiTrack, Virtual Incision MIRA, Moon Surgical Maestro, and custom systems), covering surgical manipulation, robotic ultrasound, and endoscopy. Train two foundation models: GR00T-H, an open foundation vision-language-action model for medical robotics; Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator, an action-conditioned world model enabling multi-embodiment surgical simulation from a single checkpoint. Evaluate on automated suturing tasks and ex vivo benchmarks.
AI.result: GR00T-H is the first open foundation model to achieve end-to-end task completion on a structured suturing benchmark (25% of trials vs. 0% for all others) and attains 64% average success across a 29-step ex vivo suturing sequence. Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator supports nine robotic platforms and enables in silico policy evaluation and synthetic data generation for the medical domain.
AI.conclusion: Open, large-scale medical robot data collection can serve as critical infrastructure for the research community, enabling advances in robot learning, world modeling, and beyond.

title: Reinforcing privacy reasoning in LLMs via normative simulacra from fiction
summary: Information handling practices of LLM agents are broadly misaligned with the contextual privacy expectations of their users. Contextual Integrity (CI) provides a principled framework, defining privacy as the appropriate flow of information within context-relative norms. However, existing approaches either double inference cost via supervisor-assistant architectures, or fine-tune on narrow task-specific data. We propose extracting normative simulacra (structured representations of norms and information flows) from fiction novels and using them to fine-tune LLMs via supervised learning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning. Our composite reward function combines programmatic signals, including task clarity (subsuming schema validity, construct discrimination, and extraction confidence), structural completeness, internal consistency, and context identification, with an LLM judge that evaluates whether the model's privacy reasoning is grounded in the held-out normative universe of the source text. To mitigate overfitting, we introduce per-completion contrastive scoring: each completion is evaluated against both the correct normative universe and a randomly selected wrong one, teaching the model to condition on context rather than memorize source-specific norms. We evaluate on five CI-aligned benchmarks spanning distinct societal contexts and ablate the contributions of RL and normative grounding. Across seven models, SFT introduces a conservative prior toward restricting information flow, improving recognition of privacy-relevant situations but not the correctness of privacy judgments. GRPO with normative grounding achieves the highest score on a law compliance benchmark and strongest correlation with crowdsourced human privacy expectations, demonstrating that fiction-derived normative simulacra can teach contextual privacy reasoning that transfers to real-world domains.

AI.tldr: Fiction-derived normative simulacra guide LLM privacy reasoning via supervised fine-tuning and GRPO RL; improves contextual privacy alignment under Contextual Integrity across benchmarks; outperform baseline SFT and demonstrate transfer to real-world domains.
AI.motivation: Context-relative privacy norms (Contextual Integrity) are not captured by existing supervision or narrow fine-tuning; need scalable methods that align LLM information flows with user privacy expectations.
AI.method: Extract normative simulacra from fiction; supervised fine-tuning; GRPO reinforcement learning with a composite reward including task clarity, structural completeness, internal consistency, context identification; an LLM judge ensures grounding in normative universe; per-completion contrastive scoring; evaluate on five CI-aligned benchmarks across seven models; ablation studies on RL and normative grounding.
AI.result: SFT yields conservative information flow restriction and better recognition of privacy-relevant situations but weaker privacy-judgment accuracy; GRPO with normative grounding yields top score on law compliance and strongest correlation with human privacy expectations; demonstrates transfer from fiction-derived norms to real-world privacy reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Fiction-derived normative simulacra are a viable approach to train LLMs for contextual privacy reasoning; normative grounding via GRPO improves alignment with law and crowdsourced expectations, with robust transfer across societal contexts.

title: Linear Image Generation by Synthesizing Exposure Brackets
summary: The life of a photo begins with photons striking the sensor, whose signals are passed through a sophisticated image signal processing (ISP) pipeline to produce a display-referred image. However, such images are no longer faithful to the incident light, being compressed in dynamic range and stylized by subjective preferences. In contrast, RAW images record direct sensor signals before non-linear tone mapping. After camera response curve correction and demosaicing, they can be converted into linear images, which are scene-referred representations that directly reflect true irradiance and are invariant to sensor-specific factors. Since image sensors have better dynamic range and bit depth, linear images contain richer information than display-referred ones, leaving users more room for editing during post-processing. Despite this advantage, current generative models mainly synthesize display-referred images, which inherently limits downstream editing. In this paper, we address the task of text-to-linear-image generation: synthesizing a high-quality, scene-referred linear image that preserves full dynamic range, conditioned on a text prompt, for professional post-processing. Generating linear images is challenging, as pre-trained VAEs in latent diffusion models struggle to simultaneously preserve extreme highlights and shadows due to the higher dynamic range and bit depth. To this end, we represent a linear image as a sequence of exposure brackets, each capturing a specific portion of the dynamic range, and propose a DiT-based flow-matching architecture for text-conditioned exposure bracket generation. We further demonstrate downstream applications including text-guided linear image editing and structure-conditioned generation via ControlNet.

AI.tldr: Proposes text-to-linear (scene-referred) image synthesis by representing a linear image as a sequence of exposure brackets and using a DiT-based flow-matching architecture to generate bracket sequences, enabling text-guided linear-image editing and ControlNet-conditioned generation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between display-referred generative outputs and professional post-processed RAW/linear imagery. Preserve full dynamic range and scene-referred information to enable flexible editing, which current VAEs struggle with due to HDR and bit-depth limitations.
AI.method: Represent linear images as a sequence of exposure brackets spanning the dynamic range. Introduce a diffusion-tempered DiT (DiT-based) flow-matching architecture to generate text-conditioned bracket sequences. Demonstrate downstream applications via text-guided linear image editing and structure-conditioned generation using ControlNet.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of text-to-linear-image synthesis and downstream capabilities, including text-guided edits and ControlNet-based structure conditioning, enabling professional post-processing on scene-referred images.
AI.conclusion: A viable path to extend text-to-image synthesis to high dynamic range, scene-referred imagery, preserving linear radiance information for post-processing, with practical editing and structure-conditioning workflows.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Manipulation Understanding with Enriched Semantic Event Chains
summary: Robotic systems operating in human environments must reason about how object interactions evolve over time, which actions are currently being performed, and what manipulation step is likely to follow. Classical enriched Semantic Event Chains (eSECs) provide an interpretable relational description of manipulation, but remain primarily descriptive and do not directly support uncertainty-aware decision making. In this paper, we propose eSEC-LAM, a neuro-symbolic framework that transforms eSECs into an explicit event-level symbolic state for manipulation understanding. The proposed formulation augments classical eSECs with confidence-aware predicates, functional object roles, affordance priors, primitive-level abstraction, and saliency-guided explanation cues. These enriched symbolic states are derived from a foundation-model-based perception front-end through deterministic predicate extraction, while current-action inference and next-primitive prediction are performed using lightweight symbolic reasoning over primitive pre- and post-conditions. We evaluate the proposed framework on EPIC-KITCHENS-100, EPIC-KITCHENS VISOR, and Assembly101 across action recognition, next-primitive prediction, robustness to perception noise, and explanation consistency. Experimental results show that eSEC-LAM achieves competitive action recognition, substantially improves next-primitive prediction, remains more robust under degraded perceptual conditions than both classical symbolic and end-to-end video baselines, and provides temporally consistent explanation traces grounded in explicit relational evidence. These findings demonstrate that enriched Semantic Event Chains can serve not only as interpretable descriptors of manipulation, but also as effective internal states for neuro-symbolic action reasoning.

AI.tldr: eSEC-LAM extends enriched Semantic Event Chains (eSECs) with uncertainty-aware, symbolic reasoning to enable robust, explainable manipulation comprehension in robots; it yields better next-primitive prediction, competitive action recognition, and consistent explanations under noisy perception.
AI.motivation: Classical eSECs describe manipulation relations but lack explicit uncertainty handling and decision-support capabilities required for robust robotics in human environments.
AI.method: Transform eSECs into event-level symbolic states augmented with confidence-aware predicates, functional object roles, affordance priors, primitive-level abstraction, and saliency-guided explanations. Perception is foundation-model-based with deterministic predicate extraction; current-action inference and next-primitive prediction are performed via lightweight symbolic reasoning over primitive pre- and post-conditions.
AI.result: Evaluated on EPIC-KITCHENS-100, EPIC-KITCHENS VISOR, and Assembly101 for action recognition, next-primitive prediction, robustness to perception noise, and explanation consistency. eSEC-LAM achieves competitive action recognition, substantially improves next-primitive prediction, is more robust under degraded perception than classical symbolic and end-to-end baselines, and provides temporally consistent explanation traces grounded in relational evidence.
AI.conclusion: Enriched Semantic Event Chains can function as interpretable descriptors and effective internal states for neuro-symbolic action reasoning, enabling uncertainty-aware decision making in manipulation tasks.

title: Do Masked Autoencoders Improve Downhole Prediction? An Empirical Study on Real Well Drilling Data
summary: Downhole drilling telemetry presents a fundamental labeling asymmetry: surface sensor data are generated continuously at 1~Hz, while labeled downhole measurements are costly, intermittent, and scarce. Current machine learning approaches for downhole metric prediction universally adopt fully supervised training from scratch, which is poorly suited to this data regime. We present the first empirical evaluation of masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining for downhole drilling metric prediction. Using two publicly available Utah FORGE geothermal wells comprising approximately 3.5 million timesteps of multivariate drilling telemetry, we conduct a systematic full-factorial design space search across 72 MAE configurations and compare them against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines on the task of predicting Total Mud Volume. Results show that the best MAE configuration reduces test mean absolute error by 19.8\% relative to the supervised GRU baseline, while trailing the supervised LSTM baseline by 6.4\%. Analysis of design dimensions reveals that latent space width is the dominant architectural choice (Pearson $r = -0.59$ with test MAE), while masking ratio has negligible effect, an unexpected finding attributed to high temporal redundancy in 1~Hz drilling data. These results establish MAE pretraining as a viable paradigm for drilling analytics and identify the conditions under which it is most beneficial.

AI.tldr: MAE pretraining with masked autoencoders on downhole drilling data yields up to ~20% MAE improvement vs GRU; LSTM remains the strongest baseline; latent space width is the dominant design knob, while masking ratio is largely inconsequential due to high 1 Hz temporal redundancy.
AI.motivation: Address labeling asymmetry in downhole telemetry by leveraging self-supervised pretraining to better utilize abundant surface data and limited downhole labels for metric prediction.
AI.method: Full-factorial evaluation of 72 MAE configurations; compare against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines on Total Mud Volume prediction using ~3.5 million timesteps from two Utah FORGE wells; analyze influence of architectural choices.
AI.result: Best MAE configuration reduces test MAE by 19.8% relative to the GRU baseline; however, it trails the LSTM baseline by 6.4%. Latent space width shows the strongest correlation with performance (Pearson r = -0.59); masking ratio has negligible effect, attributed to high temporal redundancy in 1 Hz drilling data.
AI.conclusion: Masked Autoencoder pretraining is a viable paradigm for drilling analytics. The study characterizes when MAE pretraining helps (notably with sufficient latent width) and explains why masking is less impactful in highly redundant, low-rate telemetry.

title: Micro-DualNet: Dual-Path Spatio-Temporal Network for Micro-Action Recognition
comment: Accepted to International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG)
summary: Micro-actions are subtle, localized movements lasting 1-3 seconds such as scratching one's head or tapping fingers. Such subtle actions are essential for social communication, ubiquitously used in natural interactions, and thus critical for fine-grained video understanding, yet remain poorly understood by current computer vision systems. We identify a fundamental challenge: micro-actions exhibit diverse spatio-temporal characteristics where some are defined by spatial configurations while others manifest through temporal dynamics. Existing methods that commit to a single spatio-temporal decomposition cannot accommodate this diversity. We propose a dual-path network that processes anatomically-grounded spatial entities through parallel Spatial-Temporal (ST) and Temporal-Spatial (TS) pathways. The ST path captures spatial configurations before modeling temporal dynamics, while the TS path inverts this order to prioritize temporal dynamics. Rather than fixed fusion, we introduce entity-level adaptive routing where each body part learns its optimal processing preference, complemented by Mutual Action Consistency (MAC) loss that enforces cross-path coherence. Extensive experiments demonstrate competitive performance on MA-52 dataset and state-of-the-art results on iMiGUE dataset. Our work reveals that architectural adaptation to the inherent complexity of micro-actions is essential for advancing fine-grained video understanding.

AI.tldr: A dual-path architecture for micro-actions using Spatial-Temporal (ST) and Temporal-Spatial (TS) pathways, with per-body-part adaptive routing and a Mutual Action Consistency (MAC) loss, achieving strong results on MA-52 and iMiGUE and highlighting the need for architectural adaptation to micro-action complexity.
AI.motivation: Micro-actions are brief (1-3s) and exhibit diverse spatio-temporal characteristics. A single fixed spatio-temporal decomposition struggles to capture both spatial configurations and temporal dynamics, hindering fine-grained video understanding.
AI.method: A dual-path network with two parallel pathways: ST path (spatial configurations first, then temporal dynamics) and TS path (temporal dynamics first, then spatial configurations). Entity-level adaptive routing allows each body part to learn its preferred processing order. Mutual Action Consistency (MAC) loss enforces coherence across the two paths.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows competitive performance on the MA-52 dataset and state-of-the-art results on the iMiGUE dataset, with extensive experiments supporting the method’s effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Architectural adaptation to the intrinsic complexity of micro-actions is essential for advancing fine-grained video understanding; the proposed dual-path, adaptive routing, and MAC loss collectively address the diverse spatio-temporal nature of micro-actions.

title: Impact-Aware Model Predictive Control for UAV Landing on a Heaving Platform
comment: To be published in the proceedings of International Federation of Automatic Control (IFAC) World Congress 2026
summary: Landing UAVs on heaving marine platforms is challenging because relative vertical motion can generate large impact forces and cause rebound on touchdown. To address this, we develop an impact-aware Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework that models landing as a velocity-level rigid-body impact governed by Newton's restitution law. We embed this as a linear complementarity problem (LCP) within the MPC dynamics to predict the discontinuous post-impact velocity and suppress rebound. In simulation, restitution-aware prediction reduces pre-impact relative velocity and improves landing robustness. Experiments on a heaving-deck testbed show an 86.2% reduction in post-impact deflection compared to a tracking MPC.

AI.tldr: Impact-aware MPC for UAV landing on heaving decks uses a velocity-level rigid-body impact model with Newton restitution, embedded as an LCP within MPC to predict post-impact velocity and suppress rebound. Simulation shows reduced pre-impact velocity and improved robustness; experiments show 86.2% reduction in post-impact deflection vs a tracking MPC.
AI.motivation: Landing on moving marine platforms causes large relative vertical motion and rebounds; reducing impact forces and rebound is essential for robust, safe landings.
AI.method: Model landing as a velocity-level rigid-body impact governed by Newton's restitution law; embed this as a linear complementarity problem within the MPC dynamics to predict discontinuous post-impact velocity and suppress rebound.
AI.result: Simulation indicates reduced pre-impact relative velocity and improved landing robustness; experimental results on a heaving-deck testbed show an 86.2% reduction in post-impact deflection compared to a tracking MPC.
AI.conclusion: Restitution-aware MPC with an embedded LCP is effective for mitigating rebound and enhancing robustness of UAV landings on moving platforms; demonstrates real-time feasibility on a heaving deck.

title: FairyFuse: Multiplication-Free LLM Inference on CPUs via Fused Ternary Kernels
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed on CPU-only platforms where memory bandwidth is the primary bottleneck for autoregressive generation. Weight quantization to four bits or below reduces memory pressure, yet existing systems still dequantize weights and perform floating-point multiplications, limiting the achievable gains. Ternary weights in {-1, 0, +1} provide a more efficient alternative, replacing multiplications with conditional additions, subtractions, or no-ops. While Fairy2i shows that ternary LLMs can match FP16 quality, its runtime does not exploit this structure. We present FairyFuse, an inference system that enables multiplication-free execution on commodity CPUs by fusing the eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of each widely-linear layer into a single AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions, with zero floating-point multiplications. Roofline analysis shows that 16x weight compression shifts memory-bound GEMV toward the compute regime on bandwidth-limited CPUs, yielding a 29.6x kernel speedup while offering little benefit on GPUs. End-to-end, FairyFuse achieves 32.4 tokens per second on a single Intel Xeon 8558P, outperforming llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x with near-lossless quality (WikiText-2 perplexity 5.52 vs. 5.47 FP16; downstream accuracy 66.0%).

AI.tldr: Ternary-weight, multiplication-free CPU inference (FairyFuse) enables large speedups by fusing sub-GEMVs into a single AVX-512 loop, achieving near-lossless quality with substantial memory-bandwidth acceleration.
AI.motivation: CPU-only autoregressive generation is memory-bandwidth bound. 4-bit or lower quantization reduces memory but usually requires dequantization and FP multiplications; ternary weights offer a multiplication-free alternative.
AI.method: Represent eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of widely-linear layers as a single fused AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions with zero FP multiplications; achieve 16x weight compression; validate with roofline analysis and end-to-end benchmarks on CPU and GPUs.
AI.result: Kernel speedup of 29.6x; end-to-end throughput of 32.4 tokens/s on a single Intel Xeon 8558P; outperforms llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x; WikiText-2 perplexity = 5.52 (vs 5.47 FP16); downstream accuracy = 66.0%.
AI.conclusion: On bandwidth-bound CPUs, 16x quantization shifts GEMV toward the compute regime, yielding large CPU-side speedups with minimal quality loss; GPUs see limited benefit.

title: Unlocking Multi-Spectral Data for Multi-Modal Models with Guided Inputs and Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Multi-spectral imagery is a valuable input signal for Remote Sensing applications, such as land-use and land-cover classification and environmental monitoring. However, generalist Large Multi-modal Models (LMMs) are typically trained on RGB images, limiting their applicability to the RGB domain. At the same time, training multi-spectral multi-modal models is expensive and produces uniquely specialized models. To address this, we propose a novel training-free approach that introduces multi-spectral data within the inference pipeline of standard RGB-only LMMs, allowing large gains in performance. Our approach leverages the LMMs' understanding of the visual space by adapting non-RGB inputs to that space and injecting domain-specific information and Chain-of-Thought reasoning as instructions. We demonstrate this with the Gemini 2.5 model and observe strong Zero-Shot performance gains on popular Remote Sensing benchmarks. These results highlight the potential for geospatial professionals to leverage powerful generalist models for specialized sensor inputs, benefiting from rich reasoning capabilities grounded in specialized data.

AI.tldr: A training-free inference-time method to augment RGB-only LMMs with multi-spectral data by mapping inputs into the model's visual space and guiding with domain-specific cues and reasoning, achieving notable zero-shot gains on RS benchmarks (Gemini 2.5).
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between RGB-trained generalist LMMs and multi-spectral remote sensing data; avoid expensive multi-modal training while enabling specialized sensor inputs.
AI.method: Inference-time data adaptation: convert non-RGB inputs into an RGB-compatible representation, inject domain-specific information and Chain-of-Thought style reasoning as prompts, applied to Gemini 2.5.
AI.result: Observes strong zero-shot performance gains on popular RS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of leveraging generalist LMMs for specialized sensor inputs, enabling geospatial professionals to benefit from common reasoning and data richness without retraining.

title: Self-Predictive Representation for Autonomous UAV Object-Goal Navigation
summary: Autonomous Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have revolutionized industries through their versatility with applications including aerial surveillance, search and rescue, agriculture, and delivery. Their autonomous capabilities offer unique advantages, such as operating in large open space environments. Reinforcement Learning (RL) empowers UAVs to learn intricate navigation policies, enabling them to optimize flight behavior autonomously. However, one of its main challenge is the inefficiency in using data sample to achieve a good policy. In object-goal navigation (OGN) settings, target recognition arises as an extra challenge. Most UAV-related approaches use relative or absolute coordinates to move from an initial position to a predefined location, rather than to find the target directly. This study addresses the data sample efficiency issue in solving a 3D OGN problem, in addition to, the formalization of the unknown target location setting as a Markov decision process. Experiments are conducted to analyze the interplay of different state representation learning (SRL) methods for perception with a model-free RL algorithm for planning in an autonomous navigation system. The main contribution of this study is the development of the perception module, featuring a novel self-predictive model named AmelPred. Empirical results demonstrate that its stochastic version, AmelPredSto, is the best-performing SRL model when combined with actor-critic RL algorithms. The obtained results show substantial improvement in RL algorithms' efficiency by using AmelPredSto in solving the OGN problem.

AI.tldr: Proposes AmelPred, a self-predictive state representation model, and its stochastic variant AmelPredSto to improve data efficiency in 3D object-goal navigation (OGN) for autonomous UAVs; AmelPredSto best complements actor-critic RL, yielding substantial efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: RL for UAV navigation is data-intensive, and object-goal navigation adds the challenge of target recognition. Formalizes unknown target location as a Markov decision process (MDP) and seeks more sample-efficient learning through better perception representations.
AI.method: Systematically evaluate state representation learning (SRL) methods for perception within a model-free RL framework; develop AmelPred (self-predictive model) and its stochastic variant AmelPredSto; integrate with actor-critic RL algorithms; conduct experiments on 3D UAV navigation in OGN settings to assess SRL performance.
AI.result: AmelPredSto emerges as the best-performing SRL model when paired with actor-critic RL, with substantial improvements in RL data efficiency and navigation performance in solving the OGN problem.
AI.conclusion: A perception module based on AmelPredSto markedly enhances data-efficient learning for 3D UAV OGN, validating the value of self-predictive SRL for incorporating target recognition into RL-based autonomous navigation.

title: Projected Gradient Unlearning for Text-to-Image Diffusion Models: Defending Against Concept Revival Attacks
summary: Machine unlearning for text-to-image diffusion models aims to selectively remove undesirable concepts from pre-trained models without costly retraining. Current unlearning methods share a common weakness: erased concepts return when the model is fine-tuned on downstream data, even when that data is entirely unrelated. We adapt Projected Gradient Unlearning (PGU) from classification to the diffusion domain as a post-hoc hardening step. By constructing a Core Gradient Space (CGS) from the retain concept activations and projecting gradient updates into its orthogonal complement, PGU ensures that subsequent fine-tuning cannot undo the achieved erasure. Applied on top of existing methods (ESD, UCE, Receler), the approach eliminates revival for style concepts and substantially delays it for object concepts, running in roughly 6 minutes versus the ~2 hours required by Meta-Unlearning. PGU and Meta-Unlearning turn out to be complementary: which performs better depends on how the concept is encoded, and retain concept selection should follow visual feature similarity rather than semantic grouping.

AI.tldr: Adapts Projected Gradient Unlearning (PGU) to diffusion models as a post-hoc hardening step, constructing a Core Gradient Space from retain activations and projecting updates into its orthogonal complement to prevent erasure revival; complements existing methods (ESD, UCE, Receler) and is faster than Meta-Unlearning; effectiveness depends on concept encoding and visual feature similarity.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-model unlearning methods suffer from revival: erased concepts reappear during downstream fine-tuning, even on unrelated data. A fast, layer-agnostic post-hoc mechanism is needed to harden erasures and reduce retraining cost.
AI.method: Create a Core Gradient Space (CGS) from retain-concept activations. During fine-tuning, project gradient updates into the orthogonal complement of CGS to prevent erasure from reviving. Apply as a post-hoc step on top of existing methods (ESD, UCE, Receler) to harden erasures. Compared to Meta-Unlearning, the approach is roughly 6 minutes vs ~2 hours.
AI.result: Eliminates revival for style concepts and substantially delays revival for object concepts. PGU is complementary to Meta-Unlearning: performance depends on how the concept is encoded, and retain-concept selection should rely on visual feature similarity rather than semantic grouping. Significant speedup (6 minutes vs ~2 hours).
AI.conclusion: PGU and Meta-Unlearning are complementary tools for diffusion-model unlearning. The choice between them should consider concept encoding and feature-based retain selection; combining PGU with existing unlearning methods yields robust, efficient erasure that resists revival.

title: Navigating the Clutter: Waypoint-Based Bi-Level Planning for Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Multi-robot control in cluttered environments is a challenging problem that involves complex physical constraints, including robot-robot collisions, robot-obstacle collisions, and unreachable motions. Successful planning in such settings requires joint optimization over high-level task planning and low-level motion planning, as violations of physical constraints may arise from failures at either level. However, jointly optimizing task and motion planning is difficult due to the complex parameterization of low-level motion trajectories and the ambiguity of credit assignment across the two planning levels. In this paper, we propose a hybrid multi-robot control framework that jointly optimizes task and motion planning. To enable effective parameterization of low-level planning, we introduce waypoints, a simple yet expressive representation for motion trajectories. To address the credit assignment challenge, we adopt a curriculum-based training strategy with a modified RLVR algorithm that propagates motion feasibility feedback from the motion planner to the task planner. Experiments on BoxNet3D-OBS, a challenging multi-robot benchmark with dense obstacles and up to nine robots, show that our approach consistently improves task success over motion-agnostic and VLA-based baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/UCSB-NLP-Chang/navigate-cluster

AI.tldr: A hybrid framework jointly optimizes task and motion planning for multi-robot control in cluttered environments, using waypoints for low-level trajectory parameterization and a curriculum-based RLVR-style training to propagate feasibility feedback; demonstrated on a dense obstacle benchmark with up to nine robots, yielding higher task success than baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge of coupling and credit-assignment between high-level task planning and low-level motion planning in cluttered, multi-robot settings, which often causes constraint violations when planning is performed separately.
AI.method: Propose a hybrid framework that (1) uses waypoints as a simple, expressive representation for low-level trajectories; (2) employs a curriculum-based training strategy with a modified RLVR algorithm to propagate motion-feasibility feedback from the motion planner to the task planner, enabling joint optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on BoxNet3D-OBS with dense obstacles and up to nine robots shows consistent improvement in task success over motion-agnostic and VLA-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization of task and motion planning with lightweight trajectory parameterization and feasibility-aware curriculum learning can significantly improve multi-robot performance in cluttered environments; code is publicly available.

title: The Path Not Taken: Duality in Reasoning about Program Execution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities across diverse coding tasks. However, their adoption requires a true understanding of program execution rather than relying on surface-level patterns. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on predicting program properties tied to specific inputs (e.g., code coverage, program outputs). As a result, they provide a narrow view of dynamic code reasoning and are prone to data contamination. We argue that understanding program execution requires evaluating its inherent duality through two complementary reasoning tasks: (i) predicting a program's observed behavior for a given input, and (ii) inferring how the input must be mutated toward a specific behavioral objective. Both tasks jointly probe a model's causal understanding of execution flow. We instantiate this duality in DexBench, a benchmark comprising 445 paired instances, and evaluate 13 LLMs. Our results demonstrate that dual-path reasoning provides a robust and discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding.

AI.tldr: DexBench introduces dual-path reasoning for dynamic code understanding, using 445 paired instances across 13 LLMs to evaluate two complementary tasks: predict a program's observed behavior for a given input, and infer input mutations to achieve a behavioral objective; findings suggest that dual-path reasoning is a robust, discriminative proxy for dynamic code execution understanding.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks focus on surface-level program properties tied to specific inputs, risking misalignment with true dynamic execution understanding and data contamination. A dual-task approach probes causal understanding and execution flow.
AI.method: DexBench provides 445 paired instances; evaluate 13 LLMs on two tasks per instance: (i) behavior prediction given input, and (ii) mutation inference to steer behavior toward a target; analysis of results and discriminative power across models.
AI.result: Dual-path reasoning yields robust, discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding; performance differences reveal strengths/limits of LLMs; dual tasks complement each other in diagnosing causality in code execution.
AI.conclusion: Dual-path reasoning is effective for probing dynamic code understanding; DexBench constitutes a valuable benchmark for evaluating LLMs' causal comprehension of code execution and can guide future model development.

title: StyleVAR: Controllable Image Style Transfer via Visual Autoregressive Modeling
summary: We build on the Visual Autoregressive Modeling (VAR) framework and formulate style transfer as conditional discrete sequence modeling in a learned latent space. Images are decomposed into multi-scale representations and tokenized into discrete codes by a VQ-VAE; a transformer then autoregressively models the distribution of target tokens conditioned on style and content tokens. To inject style and content information, we introduce a blended cross-attention mechanism in which the evolving target representation attends to its own history, while style and content features act as queries that decide which aspects of this history to emphasize. A scale-dependent blending coefficient controls the relative influence of style and content at each stage, encouraging the synthesized representation to align with both the content structure and the style texture without breaking the autoregressive continuity of VAR. We train StyleVAR in two stages from a pretrained VAR checkpoint: supervised fine-tuning on a large triplet dataset of content--style--target images, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) against a DreamSim-based perceptual reward, with per-action normalization weighting to rebalance credit across VAR's multi-scale hierarchy. Across three benchmarks spanning in-, near-, and out-of-distribution regimes, StyleVAR consistently outperforms an AdaIN baseline on Style Loss, Content Loss, LPIPS, SSIM, DreamSim, and CLIP similarity, and the GRPO stage yields further gains over the SFT checkpoint, most notably on the reward-aligned perceptual metrics. Qualitatively, the method transfers texture while maintaining semantic structure, especially for landscapes and architectural scenes, while a generalization gap on internet images and difficulty with human faces highlight the need for better content diversity and stronger structural priors.

AI.tldr: StyleVAR extends Visual Autoregressive Modeling to conditional image style transfer in a learned latent space, using tokenized VQ-VAE representations and a blended cross-attention transformer, trained in two stages (supervised fine-tuning on content-style-target triplets, then GRPO-based reinforcement fine-tuning).
AI.motivation: To achieve robust, texture-aware style transfer that preserves semantic content while enabling high-fidelity texture/style manipulation, leveraging VAR's multi-scale autoregressive modeling and a learned latent representation.
AI.method: Decompose images with VQ-VAE into multi-scale tokens; train a transformer to autoregressively model target tokens conditioned on content and style tokens; implement blended cross-attention where queries are from style/content to modulate the target history; use a scale-dependent blending coefficient to vary influence across stages; two-stage training: supervised fine-tuning on content–style–target triplets, followed by reinforcement fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using a DreamSim-based perceptual reward, with per-action normalization across VAR's scales.
AI.result: Outperforms AdaIN on Style Loss, Content Loss, LPIPS, SSIM, DreamSim, and CLIP similarity across three benchmarks (in-, near-, out-of-distribution). GRPO stage yields further gains over the SFT checkpoint, especially on reward-aligned perceptual metrics. Qualitatively, Texture transfer with preserved semantic structure, particularly effective for landscapes and architectural scenes; generalization gap on internet images and difficulty with human faces indicate need for broader content diversity and stronger structural priors.
AI.conclusion: StyleVAR demonstrates a viable, scalable approach to content–style conditioned image synthesis within the VAR framework, achieving improved perceptual and structural alignment over AdaIN and benefiting from staged training with reinforcement learning; however, domain gaps (internet images, faces) reveal limitations in content diversity and the necessity for stronger priors.

title: Full-Body Dynamic Safety for Robot Manipulators: 3D Poisson Safety Functions for CBF-Based Safety Filters
summary: Collision avoidance for robotic manipulators requires enforcing full-body safety constraints in high-dimensional configuration spaces. Control Barrier Function (CBF) based safety filters have proven effective in enabling safe behaviors, but enforcing the high number of constraints needed for safe manipulation leads to theoretic and computational challenges. This work presents a framework for full-body collision avoidance for manipulators in dynamic environments by leveraging 3D Poisson Safety Functions (PSFs). In particular, given environmental occupancy data, we sample the manipulator surface at a prescribed resolution and shrink free space via a Pontryagin difference according to this resolution. On this buffered domain, we synthesize a globally smooth CBF by solving Poisson's equation, yielding a single safety function for the entire environment. This safety function, evaluated at each sampled point, yields task-space CBF constraints enforced by a real-time safety filter via a multi-constraint quadratic program. We prove that keeping the sample points safe in the buffered region guarantees collision avoidance for the entire continuous robot surface. The framework is validated on a 7-degree-of-freedom manipulator in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: A Poisson-based safety function framework for full-body collision avoidance in manipulators, yielding a single, globally smooth CBF by solving Poisson's equation on a buffered environment, enabling real-time multi-constraint QP safety filtering.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional configuration spaces and many constraints impede traditional CBF-based safety filters; need scalable, guaranteed collision avoidance for full-body surfaces in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Sample the manipulator surface over a buffered free space via Pontryagin difference, solve Poisson's equation to produce a global safety function (PSF) over the domain, evaluate at sample points to generate task-space CBF constraints, and enforce via a multi-constraint QP in real-time; prove that pointwise safety implies full-surface safety; validate on a 7-DoF manipulator.
AI.result: A single Poisson-based safety function per environment, with a real-time multi-constraint QP safety filter; theoretical guarantee that sampling in the buffered region ensures full-surface collision avoidance; validated experimentally in dynamic environments on a 7-DoF arm.
AI.conclusion: PSF-based approach provides a scalable, globally smooth CBF for full-body collision avoidance, reducing constraint complexity and enabling safe manipulation in dynamic environments; potential for real-time applicability and generalization to varying environments.

title: Forget, Then Recall: Learnable Compression and Selective Unfolding via Gist Sparse Attention
summary: Scaling large language models to long contexts is challenging due to the quadratic computational cost of full attention. Mitigation approaches include KV-cache selection or compression techniques. We instead provide an effective and end-to-end learnable bridge between the two without requiring architecture modification. In particular, our key insight is that interleaved gist compression tokens -- which provide a learnable summary of sets of raw tokens -- can serve as routing signals for sparse attention. Building on this, we introduce selective unfolding via GSA, which first compresses the context into gist tokens, then selects the most relevant gists, and subsequently restores the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. This yields a simple coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines compact global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. We further incorporate this process directly into training in an end-to-end fashion, avoiding the need for external retrieval modules. In addition, we extend the framework hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction, enabling multi-resolution context access with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity. Empirical results on LongBench and RAG benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms other compression baselines as well as inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $32\times$. The code is available at: https://github.com/yuzhenmao/gist-sparse-attention/

AI.tldr: A learnable gist-based sparse attention method (GSA) for long-context LLMs that compresses tokens into gist tokens to route sparse attention, with selective unfolding and recursive gist-of-gist for multi-resolution context; end-to-end trainable without external retrievers; achieves 8–32x compression with superior performance on LongBench and RAG benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Mitigate quadratic complexity of full attention for long contexts by offering an end-to-end trainable bridge between KV-cache selection and compression, avoiding architecture changes while enabling efficient long-range reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce interleaved gist compression tokens that summarize sets of raw tokens and serve as routing signals for sparse attention. GSA: compress context into gist tokens, select the most relevant gists, then restore the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. A coarse-to-fine mechanism combining global compact representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. Trainable end-to-end without external retrieval. Extend hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction for multi-resolution context with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity.
AI.result: Empirical results on LongBench and RAG show consistent improvement over compression baselines and inference-time sparse attention across compression ratios from 8x to 32x.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GSA framework provides an effective end-to-end, scalable long-context attention mechanism that combines compact global summaries with selective high-resolution access, and can be extended hierarchically for multi-resolution decoding; code is publicly available.

title: Clinically-Informed Modeling for Pediatric Brain Tumor Classification from Whole-Slide Histopathology Images
comment: Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI), 2026
summary: Accurate diagnosis of pediatric brain tumors, starting with histopathology, presents unique challenges for deep learning, including severe data scarcity, class imbalance, and fine-grained morphologic overlap across diagnostically distinct subtypes. While pathology foundation models have advanced patch-level representation learning, their effective adaptation to weakly supervised pediatric brain tumor classification under limited data remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce an expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework for pediatric brain tumor diagnosis from whole-slide images (WSI). Our approach integrates contrastive learning into slide-level multiple instance learning (MIL) to explicitly regularize the geometry of slide-level representations during downstream fine-tuning. We propose both a general supervised contrastive setting and an expert-guided variant that incorporates clinically informed hard negatives targeting diagnostically confusable subtypes. Through comprehensive experiments on pediatric brain tumor WSI classification under realistic low-sample and class-imbalanced conditions, we demonstrate that contrastive fine-tuning yields measurable improvements in fine-grained diagnostic distinctions. Our experimental analyses reveal complementary strengths across different contrastive strategies, with expert-guided hard negatives promoting more compact intra-class representations and improved inter-class separation. This work highlights the importance of explicitly shaping slide-level representations for robust fine-grained classification in data-scarce pediatric pathology settings.

AI.tldr: An expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework integrates slide-level MIL with contrastive learning for pediatric brain tumor classification from whole-slide images, with supervised and expert-guided hard-negative variants; it improves fine-grained distinctions under low-sample, imbalanced data, by shaping slide-level representations to be compact intra-class and well-separated inter-class.
AI.motivation: pediatric brain tumor diagnosis via histopathology faces data scarcity, severe class imbalance, and fine-grained morphologic overlap across subtypes. Patch-level representations from foundation models do not readily adapt to weakly supervised, data-scarce, slide-level tasks. There is a need for methods that regularize slide-level feature geometry to improve robust, fine-grained classification.
AI.method: The authors propose an expert-guided contrastive fine-tuning framework that embeds contrastive learning into slide-level multiple instance learning (MIL). They develop two variants: (1) a general supervised contrastive setting for fine-tuning, and (2) an expert-guided variant that incorporates clinically informed hard negatives targeting diagnostically confusable subtypes. The approach explicitly regularizes the geometry of slide-level representations during downstream fine-tuning to improve separation and compactness.
AI.result: Across pediatric brain tumor WSI classification tasks under realistic low-sample and class-imbalanced conditions, contrastive fine-tuning yields improvements in fine-grained diagnostic distinctions. Expert-guided hard negatives provide more compact intra-class representations and better inter-class separation. The results indicate complementary strengths from different contrastive strategies and underscore the benefit of explicitly shaping slide-level representations for data-scarce pediatric pathology.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly shaping slide-level representations via contrastive fine-tuning—including expert-guided hard negatives—benefits robust, fine-grained pediatric brain tumor classification in data-scarce settings, and enhances the effectiveness of slide-level MIL approaches.

title: How VLAs (Really) Work In Open-World Environments
summary: Vision-language-action models (VLAs) have been extensively used in robotics applications, achieving great success in various manipulation problems. More recently, VLAs have been used in long-horizon tasks and evaluated on benchmarks, such as BEHAVIOR1K (B1K), for solving complex household chores. The common metric for measuring progress in such benchmarks is success rate or partial score based on satisfaction of progress-agnostic criteria, meaning only the final states of the objects are considered, regardless of the events that lead to such states. In this paper, we argue that using such evaluation protocols say little about safety aspects of operation and can potentially exaggerate reported performance, undermining core challenges for future real-world deployment. To this end, we conduct a thorough analysis of state-of-the-art models on the B1K Challenge and evaluate policies in terms of robustness via reproducibility and consistency of performance, safety aspects of policies operations, task awareness, and key elements leading to the incompletion of tasks. We then propose evaluation protocols to capture safety violations to better measure the true performance of the policies in more complex and interactive scenarios. At the end, we discuss the limitations of the existing VLAs and motivate future research.

AI.tldr: VLAs for robotic manipulation are evaluated with final-state success metrics (B1K); authors argue this overlooks safety and process-level issues and propose protocols to measure safety violations and robustness, encouraging more comprehensive benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Final-state, progress-agnostic metrics can overstate performance and miss safety, robustness, and task-awareness issues in long-horizon manipulation; need evaluation protocols that capture safety violations.
AI.method: Critical analysis of state-of-the-art models on BEHAVIOR1K; evaluate robustness via reproducibility and consistency, safety of policy operations, task awareness, and factors behind incomplete tasks; propose evaluation protocols for safety violations; discuss limitations and future directions.
AI.result: Identification of gaps in current evaluation—final-state metrics hide safety issues; initial protocols to quantify safety violations and robustness; improved framework for safer, more informative benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: VLAs show limitations in current benchmarks; future work should integrate safety, reproducibility, and task awareness into evaluation to drive safer real-world deployment.

title: Validating a Deep Learning Algorithm to Identify Patients with Glaucoma using Systemic Electronic Health Records
summary: We evaluated whether a glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model trained on All of Us national data can identify patients at high probability of glaucoma using only systemic electronic health records (EHR) at an independent institution. In this cross-sectional study, 20,636 Stanford patients seen from November 2013 to January 2024 were included (15% with glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and tested on a held-out set using demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements as inputs. The best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration was consistent with clinical risk: the highest prediction decile showed the greatest glaucoma diagnosis rate (65.7%) and treatment rate (57.0%). Performance improved with more trainable layers up to 15 and with additional data. An EHR-only GRA model may enable scalable and accessible pre-screening without specialized imaging.

AI.tldr: An EHR-only glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model trained on All of Us data, fine-tuned on Stanford data, identifies glaucoma risk using systemic EHR features with AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657 in an external cohort, with good calibration; suggests scalable pre-screening without imaging.
AI.motivation: Assess whether a national-data-trained GRA model can detect high glaucoma risk using only systemic EHR data at an independent institution, enabling scalable pre-screening without ophthalmic imaging.
AI.method: Cross-sectional study of 20,636 Stanford patients (2013–2024; 15% glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and evaluated on a held-out set using inputs from demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements.
AI.result: Best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration aligned with clinical risk: top decile glaucoma diagnosis rate 65.7% and treatment rate 57.0%. Model performance improved with more trainable layers (up to 15) and with more input data.
AI.conclusion: An EHR-only GRA model could enable scalable, accessible glaucoma pre-screening without the need for specialized imaging.

title: Optimizing Diffusion Priors with a Single Observation
summary: While diffusion priors generate high-quality posterior samples across many inverse problems, they are often trained on limited training sets or purely simulated data, thus inheriting the errors and biases of these underlying sources. Current approaches to finetuning diffusion models rely on a large number of observations with varying forward operators, which can be difficult to collect for many applications, and thus lead to overfitting when the measurement set is small. We propose a method for tuning a prior from only a single observation by combining existing diffusion priors into a single product-of-experts prior and identifying the exponents that maximize the Bayesian evidence. We validate our method on real-world inverse problems, including black hole imaging, where the true prior is unknown a priori, and image deblurring with text-conditioned priors. We find that the evidence is often maximized by priors that extend beyond those trained on a single dataset. By generalizing the prior through exponent weighting, our approach enables posterior sampling from both tempered and combined diffusion models, yielding more flexible priors that improve the trustworthiness of the resulting posterior image distribution.

AI.tldr: Proposes a product-of-experts prior for diffusion models to tune from a single observation by optimizing exponents to maximize Bayesian evidence; demonstrates improved posterior sampling in black hole imaging and deblurring.
AI.motivation: Diffusion priors trained on limited data inherit biases; tuning from a single observation is challenging and existing finetuning requires many measurements with varying forward operators, risking overfitting on small datasets.
AI.method: Construct a product-of-experts prior by combining existing diffusion priors and learn exponents that maximize Bayesian evidence; enable tempered and combined diffusion models; validate on real inverse problems including black hole imaging and text-conditioned deblurring.
AI.result: Evidence favors priors that extend beyond a single training dataset; exponent-weighted priors yield more trustworthy posterior image distributions and improved posterior sampling.
AI.conclusion: Exponent-weighted product-of-experts priors provide a flexible, data-efficient approach to tune diffusion priors from minimal data, improving robustness and applicability to inverse problems.

title: CorridorVLA: Explicit Spatial Constraints for Generative Action Heads via Sparse Anchors
summary: Vision--Language--Action (VLA) models often use intermediate representations to connect multimodal inputs with continuous control, yet spatial guidance is often injected implicitly through latent features. We propose $CorridorVLA$, which predicts sparse spatial anchors as incremental physical changes (e.g., $Δ$-positions) and uses them to impose an explicit tolerance region in the training objective for action generation.
