HypEHR is pretrained with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology. On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HypEHR.

AI.tldr: HypEHR: a compact Lorentzian (hyperbolic) model for EHR QA that embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic space, using geometry-aware cross-attention and type-specific pointer heads, pretrained on next-visit diagnosis and hierarchy-aware regularization to align with ICD ontology; achieves near-LLM performance with far fewer parameters on MIMIC-IV EHR-QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: EHR QA pipelines often rely on large LLMs which are costly and may not exploit the hierarchical structure of clinical data; clinical ontologies and patient trajectories naturally exhibit hyperbolic geometry.
AI.method: Embed codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic (Lorentzian) space. Use geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads to answer queries. Pretrain with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology.
AI.result: On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: HypEHR demonstrates that hyperbolic geometry and hierarchy-aware training can yield competitive EHR QA performance with significantly fewer parameters; code is publicly available.

title: Hi-WM: Human-in-the-World-Model for Scalable Robot Post-Training
summary: Post-training is essential for turning pretrained generalist robot policies into reliable task-specific controllers, but existing human-in-the-loop pipelines remain tied to physical execution: each correction requires robot time, scene setup, resets, and operator supervision in the real world. Meanwhile, action-conditioned world models have been studied mainly for imagination, synthetic data generation, and policy evaluation. We propose \textbf{Human-in-the-World-Model (Hi-WM)}, a post-training framework that uses a learned world model as a reusable corrective substrate for failure-targeted policy improvement. A policy is first rolled out in closed loop inside the world model; when the rollout becomes incorrect or failure-prone, a human intervenes directly in the model to provide short corrective actions. Hi-WM caches intermediate states and supports rollback and branching, allowing a single failure state to be reused for multiple corrective continuations and yielding dense supervision around behaviors that the base policy handles poorly. The resulting corrective trajectories are then added back to the training set for post-training. We evaluate Hi-WM on three real-world manipulation tasks spanning both rigid and deformable object interaction, and on two policy backbones. Hi-WM improves real-world success by 37.9 points on average over the base policy and by 19.0 points over a world-model closed-loop baseline, while world-model evaluation correlates strongly with real-world performance (r = 0.953). These results suggest that world models can serve not only as generators or evaluators, but also as effective corrective substrates for scalable robot post-training.

AI.tldr: Hi-WM uses a learned world model as a corrective substrate for post-training: roll out the policy in the model, have humans intervene inside the model to provide short corrections, cache states with rollback/branching, and add corrective trajectories to training; yields large gains in real-world success and strong model–real-world correlation.
AI.motivation: Reduce real-world data collection cost and time in post-training by leveraging a reusable, corrective world-model substrate to generate dense supervision around failure-prone behaviors.
AI.method: Run policy in a closed-loop world model; when rollout becomes incorrect or failure-prone, a human provides corrective actions directly within the model; cache intermediate states and enable rollback/branching to reuse a single failure state for multiple corrections; augment training data with the corrective trajectories.
AI.result: Evaluated on three real-world manipulation tasks (rigid and deformable objects) across two policy backbones. Hi-WM improves real-world success by 37.9 points on average over the base policy and by 19.0 points over a world-model closed-loop baseline. World-model evaluation correlates with real-world performance (r = 0.953).
AI.conclusion: World models can serve as corrective substrates to enable scalable post-training, beyond their traditional roles as generators or evaluators.

title: Exploring the Role of Synthetic Data Augmentation in Controllable Human-Centric Video Generation
summary: Controllable human video generation aims to produce realistic videos of humans with explicitly guided motions and appearances,serving as a foundation for digital humans, animation, and embodied AI.However, the scarcity of largescale, diverse, and privacy safe human video datasets poses a major bottleneck, especially for rare identities and complex actions.Synthetic data provides a scalable and controllable alternative,yet its actual contribution to generative modeling remains underexplored due to the persistent Sim2Real gap.In this work,we systematically investigate the impact of synthetic data on controllable human video generation. We propose a diffusion-based framework that enables fine-grained control over appearance and motion while providing a unfied testbed to analyze how synthetic data interacts with real world data during training. Through extensive experiments, we reveal the complementary roles of synthetic and real data and demonstrate possible methods for efficiently selecting synthetic samples to enhance motion realism,temporal consistency,and identity preservation.Our study offers the first comprehensive exploration of synthetic data's role in human-centric video synthesis and provides practical insights for building data-efficient and generalizable generative models.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based controllable human video generation studied with synthetic data; synthetic data complements real data, with strategies to select synthetic samples to boost motion realism, temporal consistency, and identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Addresses scarcity of large-scale, diverse, privacy-safe human video data. Investigates whether synthetic data can bridge the Sim2Real gap and how it interacts with real data in training controllable video generators.
AI.method: Proposes a diffusion-based framework that offers fine-grained control over appearance and motion. Establishes a unified testbed to systematically analyze interactions between synthetic and real data and to test sample-selection strategies for synthetic data.
AI.result: Experiments show synthetic and real data play complementary roles. Demonstrates methods to efficiently select synthetic samples that improve motion realism, temporal coherence, and identity preservation in generated videos.
AI.conclusion: First comprehensive exploration of synthetic data's role in human-centric video synthesis, providing practical guidance for data-efficient and generalizable generative models.

title: Who Defines Fairness? Target-Based Prompting for Demographic Representation in Generative Models
summary: Text-to-image(T2I) models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E have made generative AI widely accessible, yet recent studies reveal that these systems often replicate societal biases, particularly in how they depict demographic groups across professions. Prompts such as 'doctor' or 'CEO' frequently yield lighter-skinned outputs, while lower-status roles like 'janitor' show more diversity, reinforcing stereotypes. Existing mitigation methods typically require retraining or curated datasets, making them inaccessible to most users. We propose a lightweight, inference-time framework that mitigates representational bias through prompt-level intervention without modifying the underlying model. Instead of assuming a single definition of fairness, our approach allows users to select among multiple fairness specifications-ranging from simple choices such as a uniform distribution to more complex definitions informed by a large language model(LLM) that cites sources and provides confidence estimates. These distributions guide the construction of demographic specific prompt variants in the corresponding proportions, and we evaluate alignment by auditing adherence to the declared target and measuring the resulting skin tone distribution rather than assuming uniformity as 'fairness'. Across 36 prompts spanning 30 occupations and 6 non-occupational contexts, our method shifts observed skin-tone outcomes in directions consistent with the declared target, and reduces deviation from targets when the target is defined directly in skin-tone space(fallback). This work demonstrates how fairness interventions can be made transparent, controllable, and usable at inference time, directly empowering users of generative AI.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, inference-time bias-mitigation framework for text-to-image models that avoids retraining, using prompt-level distributions to enforce targeted demographic fairness; supports uniform or LLM-informed fairness specs; evaluates via skin-tone distributions across 36 prompts in 30 occupations and 6 contexts; shows alignment with declared targets and reduced deviation when targets are skin-tone-centric; emphasizes transparency and user control.
AI.motivation: T2I models reproduce societal biases in depictions of demographics across professions, and retraining or curated datasets for mitigation are impractical for most users. The work seeks an accessible, inference-time solution that gives users control over fairness definitions.
AI.method: Construct demographic-specific prompt variants guided by specified target distributions (uniform or LLM-informed with sources and confidence estimates). At inference, generate prompts with these variants in corresponding proportions. Evaluate alignment by auditing adherence to the declared target and by measuring the resulting skin-tone distribution rather than assuming uniform fairness. Supports multiple fairness specifications, including skin-tone-centric targets (fallback) and other distributions.
AI.result: Across 36 prompts spanning 30 occupations and 6 non-occupational contexts, the method shifts observed skin-tone outcomes toward the declared targets and reduces deviation from targets when the target is defined in skin-tone space. Demonstrates that fairness interventions can be transparent, controllable, and usable at inference time.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of user-controlled, inference-time fairness interventions for generative AI without modifying underlying models, highlighting transparency and practicality for end users.

title: Task-Driven Co-Design of Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Designing multi-agent robotic systems requires reasoning across tightly coupled decisions spanning heterogeneous domains, including robot design, fleet composition, and planning. Much effort has been devoted to isolated improvements in these domains, whereas system-level co-design considering trade-offs and task requirements remains underexplored. In this work, we present a formal and compositional framework for the task-driven co-design of heterogeneous multi-robot systems. Building on a monotone co-design theory, we introduce general abstractions of robots, fleets, planners, executors, and evaluators as interconnected design problems with well-defined interfaces that are agnostic to both implementations and tasks. This structure enables efficient joint optimization of robot design, fleet composition, and planning under task-specific performance constraints. A series of case studies demonstrates the capabilities of the framework. Various component models can be seamlessly incorporated, including new robot types, task profiles, and probabilistic sensing objectives, while non-obvious design alternatives are systematically uncovered with optimality guarantees. The results highlight the flexibility, scalability, and interpretability of the proposed approach, and illustrate how formal co-design enables principled reasoning about complex heterogeneous multi-robot systems.

AI.tldr: A formal, compositional framework for task-driven co-design of heterogeneous multi-robot systems, unifying robot design, fleet composition, and planning under task constraints, with interfaces and monotone co-design guarantees; demonstrated via case studies showing flexibility and optimality.
AI.motivation: Address system-level co-design across tightly coupled decisions (robot design, fleet, planning) in heterogeneous multi-robot systems, beyond isolated improvements, to understand trade-offs and satisfy task requirements with principled guarantees.
AI.method: Develop a monotone co-design-based framework with abstractions for robots, fleets, planners, executors, and evaluators as interconnected design problems with well-defined interfaces. Enable joint optimization under task performance constraints, and demonstrate extensibility to new components and probabilistic sensing objectives via case studies.
AI.result: Demonstrates capabilities to integrate diverse component models, uncover non-obvious design alternatives with optimality guarantees, and preserve flexibility, scalability, and interpretability in co-design of heterogeneous multi-robot systems.
AI.conclusion: Formal, compositional co-design enables principled reasoning about complex heterogeneous multi-robot systems and supports principled exploration of design trade-offs with optimality guarantees.

title: Interpretable Quantile Regression by Optimal Decision Trees
summary: The field of machine learning is subject to an increasing interest in models that are not only accurate but also interpretable and robust, thus allowing their end users to understand and trust AI systems. This paper presents a novel method for learning a set of optimal quantile regression trees. The advantages of this method are that (1) it provides predictions about the complete conditional distribution of a target variable without prior assumptions on this distribution; (2) it provides predictions that are interpretable; (3) it learns a set of optimal quantile regression trees without compromising algorithmic efficiency compared to learning a single tree.

AI.tldr: A framework to learn multiple optimal quantile regression trees to estimate the full conditional distribution with interpretability and no loss of efficiency.
AI.motivation: There is a need for models that are both accurate and interpretable, and that can provide distributional predictions without assuming a specific distribution.
AI.method: A novel approach to learn a set of optimal quantile regression trees that together predict multiple quantiles, enabling the recovery of the full conditional distribution while preserving interpretability and computational efficiency.
AI.result: The method delivers complete conditional distribution predictions via quantiles, maintains interpretability through tree structures, and achieves efficiency comparable to learning a single tree.
AI.conclusion: Learning a set of optimal quantile regression trees provides accurate, interpretable, and robust distributional predictions without sacrificing efficiency.

title: an interpretable vision transformer framework for automated brain tumor classification
summary: Brain tumors represent one of the most critical neurological conditions, where early and accurate diagnosis is directly correlated with patient survival rates. Manual interpretation of Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans is time-intensive, subject to inter-observer variability, and demands significant specialist expertise. This paper proposes a deep learning framework for automated four-class brain tumor classification distinguishing glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and healthy brain tissue from a dataset of 7,023 MRI scans. The proposed system employs a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) pretrained on ImageNet-21k as the backbone, augmented with a clinically motivated preprocessing and training pipeline. Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) is applied to enhance local contrast and accentuate tumor boundaries invisible to standard normalization. A two-stage fine-tuning strategy is adopted: the classification head is warmed up with the backbone frozen, followed by full fine-tuning with discriminative learning rates. MixUp and CutMix augmentation is applied per batch to improve generalization. Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of weights and Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) further stabilize and boost performance. Attention Rollout visualization provides clinically interpretable heatmaps of the brain regions driving each prediction. The proposed model achieves a test accuracy of 99.29%, macro F1-score of 99.25%, and perfect recall on both healthy and meningioma classes, outperforming all CNN-based baselines

AI.tldr: ViT-based four-class brain tumor classifier achieving near-perfect metrics on 7,023 MRI scans using CLAHE, MixUp/CutMix, EMA, TTA, and Attention Rollout heatmaps.
AI.motivation: Automate brain tumor classification to reduce time and inter-observer variability in MRI interpretation and improve diagnostic accuracy.
AI.method: Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) pretrained on ImageNet-21k; CLAHE preprocessing; two-stage fine-tuning with frozen backbone then full fine-tuning with discriminative learning rates; MixUp and CutMix for augmentation; Exponential Moving Average of weights; Test-Time Augmentation; Attention Rollout for interpretable heatmaps.
AI.result: Test accuracy 99.29%, macro F1-score 99.25%, and recall of 1.0 for healthy and meningioma classes; outperforms CNN-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates strong performance and interpretability; the integration of CLAHE, advanced fine-tuning, and augmentation strategies contributes to robustness; external validation would further establish generalizability.

title: Active Data
summary: In some complex domains, certain problem-specific decompositions can provide advantages over monolithic designs by enabling comprehension and specification of the design. In this paper we present an intuitive and tractable approach to reasoning over large and complex data sets. Our approach is based on Active Data, i.e., data as atomic objects that actively interact with environments. We describe our intuition about how this bottom-up approach improves designs confronting computational and conceptual complexity. We describe an implementation of the base Active Data concepts within the air traffic flow management domain and discuss performance for this implementation.

AI.tldr: Active Data-based bottom-up decomposition to manage large, complex data sets; implemented in air traffic flow management; performance discussed.
AI.motivation: Address complexity in domains by problem-specific decompositions that enhance comprehension and specification; reduce reliance on monolithic designs.
AI.method: Define Active Data as atomic objects that actively interact with environments; present intuition for bottom-up design; implement base Active Data concepts in ATFM; evaluate performance.
AI.result: Describes the implementation and discusses its performance; indicates tractability and potential design improvements.
AI.conclusion: Bottom-up Active Data approach can improve designs confronting computational and conceptual complexity; decomposition facilitates understanding and specification; demonstrated in ATFM.

title: VistaBot: View-Robust Robot Manipulation via Spatiotemporal-Aware View Synthesis
comment: This paper has been accepted to ICRA 2026
summary: Recently, end-to-end robotic manipulation models have gained significant attention for their generalizability and scalability. However, they often suffer from limited robustness to camera viewpoint changes when training with a fixed camera. In this paper, we propose VistaBot, a novel framework that integrates feed-forward geometric models with video diffusion models to achieve view-robust closed-loop manipulation without requiring camera calibration at test time. Our approach consists of three key components: 4D geometry estimation, view synthesis latent extraction, and latent action learning. VistaBot is integrated into both action-chunking (ACT) and diffusion-based ($π_0$) policies and evaluated across simulation and real-world tasks. We further introduce the View Generalization Score (VGS) as a new metric for comprehensive evaluation of cross-view generalization. Results show that VistaBot improves VGS by 2.79$\times$ and 2.63$\times$ over ACT and $π_0$, respectively, while also achieving high-quality novel view synthesis. Our contributions include a geometry-aware synthesis model, a latent action planner, a new benchmark metric, and extensive validation across diverse environments. The code and models will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: VistaBot fuses 4D geometry with diffusion-based view synthesis to achieve view-robust, calibration-free closed-loop manipulation, boosting cross-view generalization (VGS) and enabling public release; validated on sim and real tasks, with integration into ACT and π0.
AI.motivation: End-to-end robotic manipulation models struggle with robustness to camera viewpoint changes when trained with a fixed camera. A calibration-free, geometry-aware approach is needed to improve cross-view generalization in closed-loop manipulation.
AI.method: Three components: (1) 4D geometry estimation, (2) view synthesis latent extraction, and (3) latent action learning. VistaBot is integrated into action-chunking (ACT) and diffusion-based (π0) policies, uses feed-forward geometric models with video diffusion for view synthesis, and introduces View Generalization Score (VGS) for evaluation across views. Evaluated in both simulation and real-world tasks; results include high-quality novel view synthesis and cross-view robustness.
AI.result: VistaBot achieves 2.79× and 2.63× improvements in VGS over ACT and π0, respectively. It demonstrates high-quality novel view synthesis and strong cross-view generalization across diverse environments, with code and models to be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware synthesis model coupled with a latent action planner enables robust, calibration-free cross-view generalization in closed-loop manipulation. The work introduces the View Generalization Score, provides extensive validation, and supplies code/models for broader adoption.

title: JEPAMatch: Geometric Representation Shaping for Semi-Supervised Learning
summary:
Semi-supervised learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for leveraging large amounts of unlabeled data to improve the performance of machine learning models when labeled data are scarce. Among existing approaches, methods derived from FixMatch have achieved state-of-the-art results in image classification by combining weak and strong data augmentations with confidence-based pseudo-labeling. Despite their strong empirical performance, these methods typically struggle with two critical bottlenecks: majority classes tend to dominate the learning process, which is amplified by incorrect pseudo-labels, leading to biased models. Furthermore, noisy early pseudo-labels prevent the model from forming clear decision boundaries, requiring prolonged training to learn informative representation. In this paper, we introduce a paradigm shift from conventional logical output threshold base, toward an explicit shaping of geometric representations. Our approach is inspired by the recently proposed Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (LeJEPA), a theoretically grounded framework asserting that meaningful representations should exhibit an isotropic Gaussian structure in latent space. Building on this principle, we propose a new training objective that combines the classical semi-supervised loss used in FlexMatch, an adaptive extension of FixMatch, with a latent-space regularization term derived from LeJEPA.
  Our proposed approach, encourages well-structured representations while preserving the advantages of pseudo-labeling strategies. Through extensive experiments on CIFAR-100, STL-10 and Tiny-ImageNet, we demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing baselines. In addition, our method significantly accelerates the convergence, drastically reducing the overall computational cost compared to standard FixMatch-based pipelines.

AI.tldr: A latent-space regularization approach that enforces isotropic Gaussian representations in latent space and complements FlexMatch-style pseudo-labeling, achieving better accuracy and faster convergence on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: FixMatch/FlexMatch-based semi-supervised methods suffer from class-imbalance bias and noisy early pseudo-labels, hindering generalization and slowing convergence; enforcing geometric latent-space structure can mitigate these issues.
AI.method: Combine FlexMatch-style semi-supervised loss with a latent-space regularization term inspired by LeJEPA to enforce isotropic Gaussian latent representations; integrate into training while preserving pseudo-labeling.
AI.result: On CIFAR-100, STL-10, Tiny-ImageNet, the method outperforms baselines and converges faster, reducing computation relative to standard FixMatch pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit latent-space geometric regularization can enhance semi-supervised learning by aligning representations with a theoretically grounded latent geometry, yielding better generalization and efficiency.

title: The First Challenge on Remote Sensing Infrared Image Super-Resolution at NTIRE 2026: Benchmark Results and Method Overview
summary: This paper presents the NTIRE 2026 Remote Sensing Infrared Image Super-Resolution (x4) Challenge, one of the associated challenges of NTIRE 2026. The challenge aims to recover high-resolution (HR) infrared images from low-resolution (LR) inputs generated through bicubic downsampling with a x4 scaling factor. The objective is to develop effective models or solutions that achieve state-of-the-art performance for infrared image SR in remote sensing scenarios. To reflect the characteristics of infrared data and practical application needs, the challenge adopts a single-track setting. A total of 115 participants registered for the competition, with 13 teams submitting valid entries. This report summarizes the challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, main results, and the representative methods of each team. The challenge serves as a benchmark to advance research in infrared image super-resolution and promote the development of effective solutions for real-world remote sensing applications.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 x4 IR remote-sensing SR challenge; bicubic x4 downsampling; single-track; 115 registered, 13 valid entries; report details challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, results, and representative methods; establishes a benchmark for infrared SR in remote sensing.
AI.motivation: To advance infrared image super-resolution for remote sensing by developing effective models that exploit infrared data characteristics and meet practical application needs, using a community benchmark to push state-of-the-art.
AI.method: Challenge setup with a single-track IR-SR task. LR images are generated from HR via bicubic downsampling with a factor of 4. Participants (115 registered; 13 valid entries) developed SR models. The paper summarizes challenge design, dataset, evaluation protocol, main results, and representative methods.
AI.result: The report reports participation statistics (115 registered, 13 valid entries) and highlights state-of-the-art performance trends and representative methods. It serves as a benchmark and baseline for NTIRE 2026 infrared SR in remote sensing.
AI.conclusion: The challenge advances infrared SR research for real-world remote sensing applications and promotes development of effective solutions for infrared image super-resolution.

title: InVitroVision: a Multi-Modal AI Model for Automated Description of Embryo Development using Natural Language
summary: The application of artificial intelligence (AI) in IVF has shown promise in improving consistency and standardization of decisions, but often relies on annotated data and does not make use of the multimodal nature of IVF data. We investigated whether foundational vision-language models can be fine-tuned to predict natural language descriptions of embryo morphology and development. Using a publicly available embryo time-lapse dataset, we fine-tuned PaliGemma-2, a multi-modal vision-language model, with only 1,000 images and corresponding captions, describing embryo morphology, embryonic cell cycle and developmental stage. Our results show that the fine-tuned model, InVitroVision, outperformed a commercial model, ChatGPT 5.2, and base models in overall metrics, with performance improving with larger training datasets. This study demonstrates the potential of foundational vision-language models to generalize to IVF tasks with limited data, enabling the prediction of natural language descriptions of embryo morphology and development. This approach may facilitate the use of large language models to retrieve information and scientific evidence from relevant publications and guidelines, and has implications for few-shot adaptation to multiple downstream tasks in IVF.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning a multimodal vision–language model (InVitroVision) from PaliGemma-2 on 1,000 embryo images with captions enables natural language descriptions of embryo morphology and development, outperforming ChatGPT 5.2 and base models; performance scales with more data, showing data-efficient generalization for IVF tasks and potential for LLM-assisted information retrieval.
AI.motivation: Address the reliance on annotated data and underutilization of IVF's multimodal information; explore whether foundational vision–language models can be repurposed to generate natural-language descriptions for embryo morphology/development with limited data, improving consistency and accessibility.
AI.method: Used a publicly available embryo time-lapse dataset; fine-tuned PaliGemma-2 (a multimodal vision–language model) on 1,000 image-caption pairs describing morphology, cell cycle, and developmental stage; produced InVitroVision; evaluated against a commercial model (ChatGPT 5.2) and base models using overall metrics; analyzed how performance scales with training data size.
AI.result: InVitroVision outperformed ChatGPT 5.2 and base models on overall metrics; performance improved as the training dataset size increased, indicating data-efficient generalization to the IVF task.
AI.conclusion: Foundational vision–language models can generalize to IVF tasks with limited data, enabling natural-language predictions of embryo morphology and development; the approach could support retrieval of evidence from publications/guidelines and enable few-shot adaptation to multiple IVF downstream tasks.

title: TRAVELFRAUDBENCH: A Configurable Evaluation Framework for GNN Fraud Ring Detection in Travel Networks
summary: We introduce TravelFraudBench (TFG), a configurable benchmark for evaluating graph neural networks (GNNs) on fraud ring detection in travel platform graphs. Existing benchmarks--YelpChi, Amazon-Fraud, Elliptic, PaySim--cover single node types or domain-generic patterns with no mechanism to evaluate across structurally distinct fraud ring topologies. TFG simulates three travel-specific ring types--ticketing fraud (star topology with shared device/IP clusters), ghost hotel schemes (reviewer x hotel bipartite cliques), and account takeover rings (loyalty transfer chains)--in a heterogeneous graph with 9 node types and 12 edge types. Ring size, count, fraud rate, scale (500 to 200,000 nodes), and composition are fully configurable. We evaluate six methods--MLP, GraphSAGE, RGCN-proj, HAN, RGCN, and PC-GNN--under a ring-based split where each ring appears entirely in one partition, eliminating transductive label leakage. GraphSAGE achieves AUC=0.992 and RGCN-proj AUC=0.987, outperforming the MLP baseline (AUC=0.938) by 5.5 and 5.0 pp, confirming graph structure adds substantial discriminative power. HAN (AUC=0.935) is a negative result, matching the MLP baseline. On the ring recovery task (>=80% of ring members flagged simultaneously), GraphSAGE achieves 100% recovery across all ring types; MLP recovers only 17-88%. The edge-type ablation shows device and IP co-occurrence are the primary signals: removing uses_device drops AUC by 5.2 pp. TFG is released as an open-source Python package (MIT license) with PyG, DGL, and NetworkX exporters and pre-generated datasets at https://huggingface.co/datasets/bsajja7/travel-fraud-graphs, with Croissant metadata including Responsible AI fields.

AI.tldr: TravelFraudBench (TFG) benchmarks GNNs for travel-fraud ring detection on a heterogeneous travel-graph with configurable ring topologies, showing graph models outperform MLP and identifying device/IP signals; released as open-source with datasets and exporters.
AI.motivation: Existing fraud benchmarks cover limited node types or domain-generic patterns and do not evaluate across topology-distinct fraud rings in a travel setting; a configurable, topology-aware benchmark is needed to assess GNNs' ability to generalize across fraud-ring structures.
AI.method: TFG simulates three travel-specific ring types (ticketing fraud with star topology and shared device/IP clusters; ghost hotel schemes as reviewer x hotel bipartite cliques; account takeover rings via loyalty transfer chains) in a heterogeneous graph with 9 node types and 12 edge types. Ring size, count, fraud rate, scale (500–200,000 nodes), and composition are configurable. Six methods (MLP, GraphSAGE, RGCN-proj, HAN, RGCN, PC-GNN) are evaluated under a ring-based split ensuring no transductive leakage. Evaluation includes ring-recovery tasks and edge-type ablations.
AI.result: GraphSAGE achieves AUC 0.992 and RGCN-proj 0.987 (vs. MLP 0.938; HAN 0.935). In ring-recovery, GraphSAGE reaches 100% recovery for all ring types; MLP recovers 17–88%. Edge-type ablation shows device and IP co-occurrence as primary signals; removing uses_device drops AUC by 5.2 points.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware GNNs leverage graph structure to substantially improve fraud-ring detection over baselines. TFG, released under MIT, provides PyG/DGL/NetworkX exporters and pre-generated datasets, enabling broader evaluation and Responsible AI metadata.

title: PLAS-Net: Pixel-Level Area Segmentation for UAV-Based Beach Litter Monitoring
summary: Accurate quantification of the physical exposure area of beach litter, rather than simple item counts, is essential for credible ecological risk assessment of marine debris. However, automated UAV-based monitoring predominantly relies on bounding-box detection, which systematically overestimates the planar area of irregular litter objects. To address this geometric limitation, we develop PLAS-Net (Pixel-level Litter Area Segmentor), an instance segmentation framework that extracts pixel-accurate physical footprints of coastal debris. Evaluated on UAV imagery from a monsoon-driven pocket beach in Koh Tao, Thailand, PLAS-Net achieves a mAP_50 of 58.7% with higher precision than eleven baseline models, demonstrating improved mask fidelity under complex coastal conditions. To illustrate how the accuracy of the masking affects the conclusions of environmental analysis, we conducted three downstream demonstrations: (i) power-law fitting of normalized plastic density (NPD) to characterize fragmentation dynamics; (ii) area-weighted ecological risk index (ERI) to map spatial pollution hotspots; and (iii) source composition analysis revealing the abundance-area paradox: fishing gear constitutes a small proportion of the total number of items, but has the largest physical area per unit item. Pixel-level area extraction can provide more valuable information for coastal monitoring compared to methods based solely on counting.

AI.tldr: Pixel-level segmentation of beach litter (PLAS-Net) yields accurate physical footprints for ecological risk assessment, outperforming bbox methods; on Koh Tao UAV data it achieves mAP50 of 58.7% and enables three downstream analyses (NPD, ERI, abundance-area paradox).
AI.motivation: Current automated monitoring relies on bounding-box detections that overestimate the planar area of irregular litter, compromising ecological risk assessment. Pixel-accurate footprints are needed for credible area-based analyses.
AI.method: Introduce PLAS-Net, an instance segmentation framework that outputs pixel-level masks of coastal debris. Evaluated on monsoon-driven UAV imagery from Koh Tao, Thailand, against 11 baselines, reporting mAP50 and mask fidelity.
AI.result: PLAS-Net achieves a mAP50 of 58.7%, with higher precision than the baselines, demonstrating improved mask fidelity in complex coastal environments. Three downstream demonstrations illustrate impact: (i) power-law fitting of normalized plastic density (NPD), (ii) area-weighted ecological risk index (ERI) mapping, and (iii) source composition analysis revealing the abundance-area paradox (fishing gear dominates physical area despite fewer items). Pixel-level area extraction yields more informative metrics than item counts for coastal monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-level area extraction provides more valuable information for coastal monitoring than counting alone, enabling more accurate fragmentation analysis, hotspot mapping, and source characterization.

title: Mind the Prompt: Self-adaptive Generation of Task Plan Explanations via LLMs
summary: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into complex software systems enables the generation of human-understandable explanations of opaque AI processes, such as automated task planning. However, the quality and reliability of these explanations heavily depend on effective prompt engineering. The lack of a systematic understanding of how diverse stakeholder groups formulate and refine prompts hinders the development of tools that can automate this process. We introduce COMPASS (COgnitive Modelling for Prompt Automated SynthesiS), a proof-of-concept self-adaptive approach that formalises prompt engineering as a cognitive and probabilistic decision-making process. COMPASS models unobservable users' latent cognitive states, such as attention and comprehension, uncertainty, and observable interaction cues as a POMDP, whose synthesised policy enables adaptive generation of explanations and prompt refinements. We evaluate COMPASS using two diverse cyber-physical system case studies to assess the adaptive explanation generation and their qualities, both quantitatively and qualitatively. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of COMPASS integrating human cognition and user profile's feedback into automated prompt synthesis in complex task planning systems.

AI.tldr: A cognitive-POMDP based framework (COMPASS) automates adaptive prompt generation for explanations in LLM-driven task planning; validated in two cyber-physical system case studies, showing feasibility and adaptive explainability.
AI.motivation: There is no systematic understanding of how diverse stakeholders formulate and refine prompts for explaining opaque AI processes; existing approaches are ad-hoc and may yield unreliable explanations in complex systems. COMPASS aims to formalize prompt engineering as a cognitive, probabilistic decision process to improve explanation quality and adaptability.
AI.method: Model latent user cognitive states (attention, comprehension, uncertainty) and observable cues as a POMDP. Synthesize a policy that adaptively generates explanations and refines prompts. Evaluate on two diverse cyber-physical system case studies, using quantitative and qualitative assessments.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated: COMPASS can integrate human cognition and user feedback into automated prompt synthesis for complex task planning, enabling adaptive explanations and prompt refinements.
AI.conclusion: COMPASS shows promise as a framework to automate prompt engineering by embedding cognitive states into the prompt generation process, potentially improving reliability and adaptability of explanations in LLM-assisted task planning.

title: Spectral Embeddings Leak Graph Topology: Theory, Benchmark, and Adaptive Reconstruction
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) excel on relational data, but standard benchmarks unrealistically assume the graph is centrally available. In practice, settings such as Federated Graph Learning, distributed systems, and privacy-sensitive applications involve graph data that are localized, fragmented, noisy, and privacy-leaking. We present a unified framework for this setting. We introduce LoGraB (Local Graph Benchmark), which decomposes standard datasets into fragmented benchmarks using three strategies and four controls: neighborhood radius $d$, spectral quality $k$, noise level $σ$, and coverage ratio $p$. LoGraB supports graph reconstruction, localized node classification, and inter-fragment link prediction, with Island Cohesion. We propose AFR (Adaptive Fidelity-driven Reconstruction), a method for noisy spectral fragments. AFR scores patch quality via a fidelity measure combining a gap-to-truncation stability ratio and structural entropy, then assembles fragments using RANSAC-Procrustes alignment, adaptive stitching, and Bundle Adjustment. Rather than forcing a single global graph, AFR recovers large faithful islands. We prove heat-kernel edge recovery under a separation condition, Davis--Kahan perturbation stability, and bounded alignment error. We establish a Spectral Leakage Proposition: under a spectral-gap assumption, polynomial-time Bayesian recovery is feasible once enough eigenvectors are shared, complementing AFR's deterministic guarantees. Experiments on nine benchmarks show that LoGraB reveals model strengths and weaknesses under fragmentation, AFR achieves the best F1 on 7/9 datasets, and under per-embedding $(ε,δ)$-Gaussian differential privacy, AFR retains 75% of its undefended F1 at $ε=2$. Our anonymous code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/JMLR_submission

AI.tldr: LoGraB provides a unified framework for fragmented graphs and AFR reconstructs noisy spectral fragments to assemble large faithful islands; it yields strong empirical results (best F1 on 7/9 datasets) with theoretical guarantees (heat-kernel edge recovery, perturbation stability, spectral leakage under a gap) and good privacy resilience (75% undefended F1 at ε=2).
AI.motivation: Current GNN benchmarks assume a centrally available graph, but real-world settings (federated graph learning, distributed/privacy-sensitive contexts) produce localized, fragmented, noisy, privacy-leaking graphs. A unified benchmarking and reconstruction approach is needed.
AI.method: LoGraB decomposes standard datasets into fragmented benchmarks using three strategies and four controls: neighborhood radius d, spectral quality k, noise level σ, and coverage ratio p. It supports graph reconstruction, localized node classification, and inter-fragment link prediction, with Island Cohesion. AFR (Adaptive Fidelity-driven Reconstruction) scores patch quality via a fidelity measure combining a gap-to-truncation stability ratio and structural entropy, then assembles fragments using RANSAC-Procrustes alignment, adaptive stitching, and Bundle Adjustment to recover large faithful islands. Theoretical results include heat-kernel edge recovery under a separation condition, Davis–Kahan perturbation stability, and bounded alignment error. A Spectral Leakage Proposition shows that under a spectral-gap assumption, polynomial-time Bayesian recovery is feasible once enough eigenvectors are shared.
AI.result: Experiments on nine benchmarks show that LoGraB reveals model strengths and weaknesses under fragmentation. AFR achieves the best F1 on 7/9 datasets. Under per-embedding (ε,δ)-Gaussian differential privacy, AFR retains 75% of its undefended F1 at ε=2.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables systematic analysis of fragmented graphs and provides strong reconstruction guarantees (AFR) alongside useful benchmarking via LoGraB. AFR combines fidelity-driven reconstruction with robust assembly and has competitive empirical performance and privacy resilience; code is released.

title: FryNet: Dual-Stream Adversarial Fusion for Non-Destructive Frying Oil Oxidation Assessment
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, this paper has been submitted and accepted for publication at CVPRW 2026
summary: Monitoring frying oil degradation is critical for food safety, yet current practice relies on destructive wet-chemistry assays that provide no spatial information and are unsuitable for real-time use. We identify a fundamental obstacle in thermal-image-based inspection, the camera-fingerprint shortcut, whereby models memorize sensor-specific noise and thermal bias instead of learning oxidation chemistry, collapsing under video-disjoint evaluation. We propose FryNet, a dual-stream RGB-thermal framework that jointly performs oil-region segmentation, serviceability classification, and regression of four chemical oxidation indices (PV, p-AV, Totox, temperature) in a single forward pass. A ThermalMiT-B2 backbone with channel and spatial attention extracts thermal features, while an RGB-MAE Encoder learns chemically grounded representations via masked autoencoding and chemical alignment. Dual-Encoder DANN adversarially regularizes both streams against video identity via Gradient Reversal Layers, and FiLM fusion bridges thermal structure with RGB chemical context. On 7,226 paired frames across 28 frying videos, FryNet achieves 98.97% mIoU, 100% classification accuracy, and 2.32 mean regression MAE, outperforming all seven baselines.

AI.tldr: FryNet is a dual-stream RGB-thermal model for real-time oil-degradation assessment, solving camera-fingerprint biases to jointly segment oil regions, classify serviceability, and regress four oxidation indices in one pass.
AI.motivation: Current oil-degradation assessment relies on destructive wet chemistry and provides no spatial, real-time information; video-era models suffer from learning sensor/thermal biases (camera fingerprint) rather than oxidation chemistry, leading to poor generalization.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture: ThermalMiT-B2 backbone for thermal features; RGB-MAE Encoder for chemically grounded RGB representations via masked autoencoding and chemical alignment; Dual-Encoder DANN adversarial regularization against video identity; FiLM fusion to combine thermal structure with RGB chemical context; tasks include oil-region segmentation, serviceability classification, and regression of PV, p-AV, Totox, and temperature.
AI.result: Evaluated on 7,226 frames from 28 frying videos, FryNet achieved 98.97% mean Intersection-over-Union, 100% classification accuracy, and 2.32 MAE for regression, outperforming seven baselines.
AI.conclusion: Joint RGB-thermal representation learning with attention, adversarial regularization, and fusion can overcome camera-fingerprint shortcuts, delivering accurate, real-time oil-oxidation assessment and comparable or superior performance to specialized baselines.

title: Propensity Inference: Environmental Contributors to LLM Behaviour
summary: Motivated by loss of control risks from misaligned AI systems, we develop and apply methods for measuring language models' propensity for unsanctioned behaviour. We contribute three methodological improvements: analysing effects of changes to environmental factors on behaviour, quantifying effect sizes via Bayesian generalised linear models, and taking explicit measures against circular analysis. We apply the methodology to measure the effects of 12 environmental factors (6 strategic in nature, 6 non-strategic) and thus the extent to which behaviour is explained by strategic aspects of the environment, a question relevant to risks from misalignment. Across 23 language models and 11 evaluation environments, we find approximately equal contributions from strategic and non-strategic factors for explaining behaviour, do not find strategic factors becoming more or less influential as capabilities improve, and find some evidence for a trend for increased sensitivity to goal conflicts. Finally, we highlight a key direction for future propensity research: the development of theoretical frameworks and cognitive models of AI decision-making into empirically testable forms.

AI.tldr: Measurement framework for LMs' propensity for unsanctioned behavior using Bayesian GLMs and controls against circular analysis; finds equal influence from strategic and non-strategic environmental factors across 23 models and 11 tasks; advocates theoretical cognitive models for future work.
AI.motivation: Address loss-of-control risks from misaligned AI by quantifying drivers of undesirable behavior and clarifying factors that influence it.
AI.method: Three enhancements: (1) analyze effects of environmental changes on behavior; (2) quantify effect sizes via Bayesian generalized linear models; (3) implement explicit prevention of circular analysis; empirical study across 12 environmental factors (6 strategic, 6 non-strategic), 23 LMs, and 11 evaluation environments.
AI.result: Approximately equal contributions from strategic and non-strategic factors in explaining behavior; no systematic change in strategic factor influence with increasing capabilities; some evidence of increased sensitivity to goal conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Calls for theoretical frameworks and cognitive models of AI decision-making that are empirically testable; future propensity research should formalize these frameworks and model-based predictions.

title: Task-specific Subnetwork Discovery in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Underwater Navigation
comment: To be published in IEEE OCEANS 2026 (Sanya) conference proceedings
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles are required to perform multiple tasks adaptively and in an explainable manner under dynamic, uncertain conditions and limited sensing, challenges that classical controllers struggle to address. This demands robust, generalizable, and inherently interpretable control policies for reliable long-term monitoring. Reinforcement learning, particularly multi-task RL, overcomes these limitations by leveraging shared representations to enable efficient adaptation across tasks and environments. However, while such policies show promising results in simulation and controlled experiments, they yet remain opaque and offer limited insight into the agent's internal decision-making, creating gaps in transparency, trust, and safety that hinder real-world deployment. The internal policy structure and task-specific specialization remain poorly understood. To address these gaps, we analyze the internal structure of a pretrained multi-task reinforcement learning network in the HoloOcean simulator for underwater navigation by identifying and comparing task-specific subnetworks responsible for navigating toward different species. We find that in a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning setting with related tasks, the network uses only about 1.5% of its weights to differentiate between tasks. Of these, approximately 85% connect the context-variable nodes in the input layer to the next hidden layer, highlighting the importance of context variables in such settings. Our approach provides insights into shared and specialized network components, useful for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring through a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning method.

AI.tldr: A pretrained contextual multi-task RL network for underwater navigation uses a small fraction of its weights to differentiate tasks, with context variables driving most task-specific distinctions, enabling interpretable insights for efficient editing and transfer learning.
AI.motivation: To overcome opacity and safety concerns in multi-task RL for autonomous underwater vehicles, by dissecting the network to understand how tasks are represented internally and how context inputs influence decisions.
AI.method: Analyze the internal structure of a pretrained multi-task RL network in the HoloOcean simulator: identify task-specific subnetworks, quantify the weight usage that differentiates tasks, and assess the role of context-variable inputs in shaping task specialization.
AI.result: Only about 1.5% of the network weights differentiate between tasks in a contextual multi-task setting; approximately 85% of these connect context-variable input nodes to the following hidden layer, highlighting the pivotal role of context variables in task differentiation; findings illuminate shared vs. specialized components and support ideas for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Context variables play a central role in contextual multi-task RL for underwater navigation, revealing sparse task-specific encodings; these insights enable more effective model editing, transfer, and continual learning strategies in dynamic underwater environments.

title: Preconditioned DeltaNet: Curvature-aware Sequence Modeling for Linear Recurrences
summary: To address the increasing long-context compute limitations of softmax attention, several subquadratic recurrent operators have been developed. This work includes models such as Mamba-2, DeltaNet, Gated DeltaNet (GDN), and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA). As the space of recurrences grows, a parallel line of work has arisen to taxonomize them. One compelling view is the test-time regression (TTR) framework, which interprets recurrences as performing online least squares updates that learn a linear map from the keys to values. Existing delta-rule recurrences can be seen as first-order approximations to this objective, but notably ignore the curvature of the least-squares loss during optimization. In this work, we address this by introducing preconditioning to these recurrences. Starting from the theory of online least squares, we derive equivalences between linear attention and the delta rule in the exactly preconditioned case. Next, we realize this theory in practice by proposing a diagonal approximation: this enables us to introduce preconditioned variants of DeltaNet, GDN, and KDA alongside efficient chunkwise parallel algorithms for computing them. Empirically, we find that our preconditioned delta-rule recurrences yield consistent performance improvements across synthetic recall benchmarks and language modeling at the 340M and 1B scale.

AI.tldr: Preconditioned delta-rule recurrences for subquadratic attention: deriving exact preconditioning equivalences with linear attention, implementing a diagonal preconditioner to build practical DeltaNet, GDN, and KDA variants, and achieving consistent gains on synthetic recall and LM tasks at 340M and 1B scales.
AI.motivation: Address the long-context compute bottleneck of softmax attention by improving online least-squares recurrences that map keys to values. By incorporating curvature via preconditioning, the approach aims to enhance the fidelity of delta-rule recurrences and close the gap to exact optimization.
AI.method: Derive equivalences between linear attention and delta-rule recurrences in the exactly preconditioned setting using online least squares theory. Propose a diagonal preconditioner as a tractable approximation. Implement preconditioned variants of DeltaNet, Gated DeltaNet (GDN), and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) and develop chunkwise parallel algorithms for efficient computation.
AI.result: Empirically observe consistent performance improvements for the preconditioned delta-rule recurrences across synthetic recall benchmarks and language modeling tasks at 340M and 1B scale.
AI.conclusion: Preconditioning delta-rule recurrences is a practical and beneficial extension for subquadratic attention models. A diagonal approximation enables scalable implementation and gains across tasks, suggesting a fruitful direction for more curvature-aware online learning in attention-free recurrences.

title: Temporal Prototyping and Hierarchical Alignment for Unsupervised Video-based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
summary: Visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) enables cross-modality identity matching for all-day surveillance, yet existing methods predominantly focus on the image level or rely heavily on costly identity annotations. While video-based VI-ReID has recently emerged to exploit temporal dynamics for improved robustness, existing studies remain limited to supervised settings. Crucially, the unsupervised video VI-ReID problem, where models must learn from RGB and infrared tracklets without identity labels, remains largely unexplored despite its practical importance in real-world deployment. To bridge this gap, we propose HiTPro (Hierarchical Temporal Prototyping), a prototype-driven framework without explicit hard pseudo-label assignment for unsupervised video-based VI-ReID. HiTPro begins with an efficient Temporal-aware Feature Encoder that first extracts discriminative frame-level features and then aggregates them into a robust tracklet-level representation. Building upon these features, HiTPro first constructs reliable intra-camera prototypes via Intra-Camera Tracklet Prototyping by aggregating features from temporally partitioned sub-tracklets. Through Hierarchical Cross-Prototype Alignment, we perform a two-stage positive mining process: progressing from within-modality associations to cross-modality matching, enhanced by Dynamic Threshold Strategy and Soft Weight Assignment. Finally, {Hierarchical Contrastive Learning} progressively optimizes feature-prototype alignment across three levels: intra-camera discrimination, cross-camera same-modality consistency, and cross-modality invariance. Extensive experiments on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus demonstrate that HiTPro achieves state-of-the-art performance under fully unsupervised settings, significantly outperforming adapted baselines and establishes a strong baseline for future research.

AI.tldr: HiTPro is a prototype-driven, unsupervised video VI-ReID framework that uses hierarchical temporal prototyping and cross-prototype alignment to learn discriminative features without identity labels, achieving state-of-the-art results on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised video visible-infrared re-identification is underexplored; existing approaches rely on supervised cues or image-level processing. There is a need to exploit temporal dynamics and learn robust cross-modality representations without costly identity annotations.
AI.method: An efficient Temporal-aware Feature Encoder extracts frame-level features and aggregates them into tracklet-level representations. Intra-Camera Tracklet Prototyping builds intra-camera prototypes from temporally partitioned sub-tracklets. Hierarchical Cross-Prototype Alignment performs two-stage positive mining from within-modality to cross-modality with Dynamic Threshold Strategy and Soft Weight Assignment. Hierarchical Contrastive Learning optimizes feature-prototype alignment across intra-camera discrimination, cross-camera same-modality consistency, and cross-modality invariance.
AI.result: HiTPro achieves state-of-the-art performance in fully unsupervised video VI-ReID settings on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus, significantly outperforming adapted baselines.
AI.conclusion: A novel prototype-driven, fully unsupervised framework that leverages hierarchical temporal prototyping and cross-prototype alignment to learn robust VI-ReID representations, setting strong baselines for future unsupervised video re-identification research.

title: AI Governance under Political Turnover: The Alignment Surface of Compliance Design
summary: Governments are increasingly interested in using AI to make administrative decisions cheaper, more scalable, and more consistent. But for probabilistic AI to be incorporated into public administration it must be embedded in a compliance layer that makes decisions reviewable, repeatable, and legally defensible. That layer can improve oversight by making departures from law easier to detect. But it can also create a stable approval boundary that political successors learn to navigate while preserving the appearance of lawful administration. We develop a formal model in which institutions choose the scale of automation, the degree of codification, and safeguards on iterative use. The model shows when these systems become vulnerable to strategic use from within government, why reforms that initially improve oversight can later increase that vulnerability, and why expansions in AI use may be difficult to unwind. Making AI usable can thus make procedures easier for future governments to learn and exploit.

AI.tldr: A formal model shows embedding probabilistic AI in a compliance layer can improve oversight and legal defensibility, but can also create stable, learnable approval boundaries that future governments exploit; expansion of AI use can be hard to unwind.
AI.motivation: Public administrations seek cheaper, scalable, and more consistent decision-making through AI. A compliance layer is required to make probabilistic AI decisions reviewable, repeatable, and legally defensible, enabling oversight while preventing unwarranted discretion.
AI.method: Develop a formal institutional model where agencies choose (i) the scale of automation, (ii) the degree of codification, and (iii) safeguards on iterative use of AI. Analyze how these choices interact with incentives for strategic behavior and path dependence.
AI.result: The model delineates conditions under which AI-enabled systems become vulnerable to internal strategic use. It explains why reforms that initially improve oversight can later increase vulnerability, and why expansions in AI use may be difficult to unwind. It also shows that making AI usable can inadvertently facilitate learning and exploitation by future governments within the approved boundary.
AI.conclusion: Effective governance of AI in public administration requires considering dynamic incentives and reversibility. A well-designed compliance layer must balance oversight with resilience against strategic manipulation, recognizing that ease of use can lower barriers for future actors to exploit existing structures.

title: A Hybridizable Neural Time Integrator for Stable Autoregressive Forecasting
summary: For autoregressive modeling of chaotic dynamical systems over long time horizons, the stability of both training and inference is a major challenge in building scientific foundation models. We present a hybrid technique in which an autoregressive transformer is embedded within a novel shooting-based mixed finite element scheme, exposing topological structure that enables provable stability. For forward problems, we prove preservation of discrete energies, while for training we prove uniform bounds on gradients, provably avoiding the exploding gradient problem. Combined with a vision transformer, this yields latent tokens admitting structure-preserving dynamics. We outperform modern foundation models with a $65\times$ reduction in model parameters and long-horizon forecasting of chaotic systems. A "mini-foundation" model of a fusion component shows that 12 simulations suffice to train a real-time surrogate, achieving a $9{,}000\times$ speedup over particle-in-cell simulation.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed autoregressive transformer embedded in a shooting-based mixed finite element scheme achieves provable stability and energy preservation for long-horizon forecasting of chaotic systems, enabling compact models and dramatic speedups.
AI.motivation: Stability and trainability of autoregressive models for chaotic dynamical systems over long horizons; need structure-preserving dynamics and parameter efficiency for scientific foundation models.
AI.method: Embed an autoregressive transformer within a shooting-based mixed finite element framework; prove discrete energy preservation and uniform gradient bounds; pair with a vision transformer to yield latent tokens with structure-preserving dynamics.
AI.result: 65x parameter reduction compared with modern foundation models; capable of long-horizon forecasting of chaotic systems; in a fusion component mini-model, 12 simulations suffice to train a real-time surrogate with ~9,000x speedup over particle-in-cell simulations.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers provable stability and substantial efficiency gains, highlighting a viable path toward robust, compact scientific foundation models and real-time surrogates for complex simulations.

title: MiMIC: Mitigating Visual Modality Collapse in Universal Multimodal Retrieval While Avoiding Semantic Misalignment
summary: Universal Multimodal Retrieval (UMR) aims to map different modalities (e.g., visual and textual) into a shared embedding space for multi-modal retrieval. Existing UMR methods can be broadly divided into two categories: early-fusion approaches, such as Marvel, which projects visual features into the language model (LM) space for integrating with text modality, and late-fusion approaches, such as UniVL-DR, which encode visual and textual inputs using separate encoders and obtain fused embeddings through addition. Our pilot study reveals that Marvel exhibits visual modality collapse, which is characterized by the model's tendency to disregard visual features while depending excessively on textual cues. In contrast, although UniVL-DR is less affected by this issue, it is more susceptible to semantic misalignment, where semantically related content is positioned far apart in the embedding space. To address these challenges, we propose MiMIC, which introduces two key innovations: (1) a fusion-in-decoder architecture for effective multimodal integration, and (2) robust training through single modality mixin and random caption dropout. Experiments on the WebQA+ and EVQA+ datasets, where image in documents or queries might lack captions, indicate that MiMIC consistently outperforms both early- and late-fusion baselines.

AI.tldr: MiMIC proposes fusion-in-decoder and robust training (single modality mixin and random caption dropout) to tackle visual modality collapse and semantic misalignment in Universal Multimodal Retrieval, outperforming Marvel and UniVL-DR on WebQA+ and EVQA+ with missing captions.
AI.motivation: Universal Multimodal Retrieval methods face two key failures: early fusion (e.g., Marvel) tends to collapse on visual cues, overrelying on text; late fusion (e.g., UniVL-DR) reduces collapse but suffers from semantic misalignment where related concepts are far apart in embedding space. A method that robustly fuses modalities and handles missing captions is needed.
AI.method: MiMIC introduces a fusion-in-decoder architecture to integrate modalities within the decoder, complemented by robust training techniques: single modality mixin (training with single-modality inputs) and random caption dropout (to simulate caption absence and improve robustness).
AI.result: Experiments on WebQA+ and EVQA+ show MiMIC consistently outperforms both early- and late-fusion baselines, including scenarios where images in documents or queries lack captions.
AI.conclusion: MiMIC effectively mitigates visual-collapse and misalignment issues in UMR, delivering robust multimodal retrieval in caption-absent settings and suggesting fusion-centric architectures with targeted robustness training as a promising direction.

title: Agentic AI for Personalized Physiotherapy: A Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Video Training and Real-Time Pose Correction
comment: 3 pages, 2 figures, submitted to ICDH IEEE conference
summary: At-home physiotherapy compliance remains critically low due to a lack of personalized supervision and dynamic feedback. Existing digital health solutions rely on static, pre-recorded video libraries or generic 3D avatars that fail to account for a patient's specific injury limitations or home environment. In this paper, we propose a novel Multi-Agent System (MAS) architecture that leverages Generative AI and computer vision to close the tele-rehabilitation loop. Our framework consists of four specialized micro-agents: a Clinical Extraction Agent that parses unstructured medical notes into kinematic constraints; a Video Synthesis Agent that utilizes foundational video generation models to create personalized, patient-specific exercise videos; a Vision Processing Agent for real-time pose estimation; and a Diagnostic Feedback Agent that issues corrective instructions. We present the system architecture, detail the prototype pipeline using Large Language Models and MediaPipe, and outline our clinical evaluation plan. This work demonstrates the feasibility of combining generative media with agentic autonomous decision-making to scale personalized patient care safely and effectively.

AI.tldr: A four-agent multi-agent system for tele-rehabilitation that uses generative AI and computer vision to create personalized exercise videos, perform real-time pose estimation, parse clinical notes into constraints, and provide corrective feedback; architecture and prototype pipeline described with an outlined clinical evaluation plan.
AI.motivation: At-home physiotherapy adherence is critically low due to lack of personalized supervision and dynamic feedback; existing digital health tools rely on static libraries or generic avatars that do not account for individual injury constraints or home environments.
AI.method: Four micro-agents: Clinical Extraction Agent parses unstructured medical notes into kinematic constraints; Video Synthesis Agent creates patient-specific exercise videos using foundational video generation models; Vision Processing Agent performs real-time pose estimation; Diagnostic Feedback Agent issues corrective instructions. System architecture and prototype pipeline described, leveraging LLMs and MediaPipe.
AI.result: No empirical results reported; the paper demonstrates feasibility of integrating generative media with agentic autonomous decision-making, and outlines the system architecture and prototype pipeline as well as a clinical evaluation plan.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MAS framework shows promise for scaling personalized tele-rehabilitation by combining generative media with autonomous agents to provide tailored supervision in home settings, while emphasizing safety and effectiveness through planned clinical evaluation.

title: How Much Is One Recurrence Worth? Iso-Depth Scaling Laws for Looped Language Models
summary: We measure how much one extra recurrence is worth to a looped (depth-recurrent) language model, in equivalent unique parameters. From an iso-depth sweep of 116 pretraining runs across recurrence counts $r \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\}$ spanning ${\sim}50\times$ in training compute, we fit a joint scaling law $L = E + A\,(N_\text{once} + r^{\varphi} N_\text{rec})^{-α} + B\,D^{-β}$ and recover a new recurrence-equivalence exponent $\varphi = 0.46$ at $R^2 = 0.997$. Intuitively, $\varphi$ tells us whether looping a block $r$ times is equivalent in validation loss to $r$ unique blocks of a non-looped model (full equivalence, $\varphi{=}1$) or to a single block run repeatedly with no capacity gain ($\varphi{=}0$). Our $\varphi = 0.46$ sits in between, so each additional recurrence predictably increases validation loss at matched training compute. For example, at $r{=}4$ a 410M looped model performs on par with a 580M non-looped model, but pays the training cost of a 1B non-looped one. On a five-axis downstream evaluation, the gap persists on parametric-knowledge tasks and closes on simple open-book tasks, while reasoning tasks are not resolvable at our compute budgets. For any looped LM, our $\varphi$ converts the design choice of $r$ into a predictable validation-loss cost, and future training recipes and architectures can be compared by how much they raise $\varphi$ above $0.46$.

AI.tldr: Introduces a scaling law for looped (depth-recurrent) LMs and a recurrence–equivalence exponent φ=0.46, enabling prediction of validation loss given recurrence and compute; offers design guidance for trade-offs between looping and new blocks.
AI.motivation: Quantify the value and trade-offs of recurrence in depth-recurrent LMs to inform architecture and training choices under fixed compute.
AI.method: Iso-depth sweep across recurrence counts r ∈ {1,2,4,8} with 116 pretrained runs (~50x compute); fit a joint scaling law L = E + A (N_once + r^φ N_rec)^{-α} + B D^{-β}; estimate φ and assess downstream task performance across five axes.
AI.result: Found φ = 0.46 with R^2 = 0.997. A 410M looped model with r=4 matches a 580M non-looped model, but incurs training cost of a 1B non-looped model. Across downstream tasks, the gap persists on parametric-knowledge tasks but closes on simple open-book tasks; reasoning tasks not resolvable at current budgets.
AI.conclusion: φ provides a concrete measure to translate recurrence design choices into expected validation loss. Future training recipes and architectures can be compared by how much they raise φ above 0.46; looped recurrence yields partial but not complete equivalence to increasing non-looped model size under fixed compute.

title: Teacher-Guided Routing for Sparse Vision Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Recent progress in deep learning has been driven by increasingly large-scale models, but the resulting computational cost has become a critical bottleneck. Sparse Mixture of Experts (MoE) offers an effective solution by activating only a small subset of experts for each input, achieving high scalability without sacrificing inference speed. Although effective, sparse MoE training exhibits characteristic optimization difficulties. Because the router receives informative gradients only through the experts selected in the forward pass, it suffers from gradient blocking and obtains little information from unselected routes. This limited, highly localized feedback makes it difficult for the router to learn appropriate expert-selection scores and often leads to unstable routing dynamics, such as fluctuating expert assignments during training. To address this issue, we propose TGR-MoE: Teacher-Guided Routing for Sparse Vision Mixture-of-Experts, a simple yet effective method that stabilizes router learning using supervision derived from a pretrained dense teacher model. TGR-MoE constructs a teacher router from the teacher's intermediate representations and uses its routing outputs as pseudo-supervision for the student router, suppressing frequent routing fluctuations during training and enabling knowledge-guided expert selection from the early stages of training. Extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100 demonstrate that TGR consistently improves both accuracy and routing consistency, while maintaining stable training even under highly sparse configurations.

AI.tldr: A teacher-guided routing framework for sparse Vision MoEs (TGR-MoE) that stabilizes router learning via pseudo-supervision from a pretrained dense teacher, improving accuracy and routing consistency in sparse MoE vision models.
AI.motivation: Sparse MoEs suffer from gradient blocking: routers receive informative gradients only from selected experts, causing unstable routing and fluctuating assignments, especially under high sparsity; need stable, informative guidance for routing.
AI.method: Construct a teacher router from teacher's intermediate representations; derive routing outputs as pseudo-labels for student router; train student MoE with this supervision to suppress routing fluctuations from early training; maintain sparse gating while benefiting from guidance.
AI.result: Empirical validation on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100 shows consistent accuracy gains and improved routing consistency; training remains stable under highly sparse configurations.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-guided routing is an effective strategy to stabilize and guide routing decisions in sparse vision MoEs, enabling better performance without sacrificing efficiency.

title: Multi-Agent Empowerment and Emergence of Complex Behavior in Groups
summary: Intrinsic motivations are receiving increasing attention, i.e. behavioral incentives that are not engineered, but emerge from the interaction of an agent with its surroundings. In this work we study the emergence of behaviors driven by one such incentive, empowerment, specifically in the context of more than one agent. We formulate a principled extension of empowerment to the multi-agent setting, and demonstrate its efficient calculation. We observe that this intrinsic motivation gives rise to characteristic modes of group-organization in two qualitatively distinct environments: a pair of agents coupled by a tendon, and a controllable Vicsek flock. This demonstrates the potential of intrinsic motivations such as empowerment to not just drive behavior for only individual agents but also higher levels of behavioral organization at scale.

AI.tldr: Extends empowerment as an intrinsic motivation to multi-agent settings; demonstrates emergent group organization in two setups.
AI.motivation: Investigate how intrinsic incentives like empowerment scale to multiple agents and whether they can induce coordinated group behavior without external rewards.
AI.method: Proposes a principled extension of empowerment to the multi-agent setting and provides an efficient computation approach; analyzes two environments—a tendon-coupled pair of agents and a controllable Vicsek flock—to study emergent group organization.
AI.result: Empowerment-driven intrinsic motivation yields characteristic modes of group organization; the two environments exhibit qualitatively distinct collective behaviors, illustrating the potential of empowerment to drive scalable social-like coordination.
AI.conclusion: Empowerment can drive not only individual agent behavior but higher-level organizational structure in multi-agent systems, suggesting a path toward scalable intrinsic-motivation-driven coordination.

title: Latent Denoising Improves Visual Alignment in Large Multimodal Models
summary: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) such as LLaVA are typically trained with an autoregressive language modeling objective, providing only indirect supervision to visual tokens. This often yields weak internal visual representations and brittle behavior under distribution shift. Inspired by recent progress on latent denoising for learning high-quality visual tokenizers, we show that the same principle provides an effective form of visual supervision for improving internal visual feature alignment and multimodal understanding in LMMs. We propose a latent denoising framework that corrupts projected visual tokens using a saliency-aware mixture of masking and Gaussian noising. The LMM is trained to denoise these corrupted tokens by recovering clean teacher patch features from hidden states at a selected intermediate LLM layer using a decoder. To prevent representation collapse, our framework also preserves the teacher's intra-image similarity structure and applies intra-image contrastive patch distillation. During inference, corruption and auxiliary heads are disabled, introducing no additional inference-time overhead.
