Across a broad suite of standard multimodal benchmarks, our method consistently improves visual understanding and reasoning over strong baselines, and yields clear gains on compositional robustness benchmarks (e.g., NaturalBench). Moreover, under ImageNet-C-style non-adversarial common corruptions applied to benchmark images, our method maintains higher accuracy and exhibits reduced degradation at both moderate and severe corruption levels. Our code is available at https://github.com/dhruvashp/latent-denoising-for-lmms.

AI.tldr: Proposes latent denoising for LMMs: corrupts visual tokens with saliency-guided masking and Gaussian noise; trains a denoiser using intermediate LLM layer features; preserves intra-image structure with contrastive patch distillation; disables corruption at inference for no overhead; yields improved multimodal understanding, compositional robustness, and corruption robustness.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive LM objectives provide indirect supervision for visual tokens, leading to weak internal visual representations and brittleness under distribution shifts. Stronger, more direct visual supervision is desired to improve alignment and multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Apply a saliency-aware mixture of masking and Gaussian noise to projected visual tokens. Train a decoder to denoise by recovering clean teacher patch features from hidden states at a chosen intermediate LLM layer. To prevent collapse, preserve the teacher's intra-image similarity structure and apply intra-image contrastive patch distillation. During inference, disable corruption and auxiliary heads to avoid extra cost.
AI.result: Empirically, the approach yields consistent improvements over strong baselines across standard multimodal benchmarks, with notable gains on compositional robustness tests (e.g., NaturalBench) and improved robustness to non-adversarial image corruptions (ImageNet-C style) at moderate to severe levels.
AI.conclusion: Latent denoising offers an effective, inference-cost-free supervision to enhance visual representation and multimodal understanding in LMMs, improving robustness and reasoning capabilities.

title: Trust but Verify: Introducing DAVinCI -- A Framework for Dual Attribution and Verification in Claim Inference for Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable fluency and versatility across a wide range of NLP tasks, yet they remain prone to factual inaccuracies and hallucinations. This limitation poses significant risks in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, law, and scientific communication, where trust and verifiability are paramount. In this paper, we introduce DAVinCI - a Dual Attribution and Verification framework designed to enhance the factual reliability and interpretability of LLM outputs. DAVinCI operates in two stages: (i) it attributes generated claims to internal model components and external sources; (ii) it verifies each claim using entailment-based reasoning and confidence calibration. We evaluate DAVinCI across multiple datasets, including FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER, and compare its performance against standard verification-only baselines. Our results show that DAVinCI significantly improves classification accuracy, attribution precision, recall, and F1-score by 5-20%. Through an extensive ablation study, we isolate the contributions of evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. We also release a modular DAVinCI implementation that can be integrated into existing LLM pipelines. By bridging attribution and verification, DAVinCI offers a scalable path to auditable, trustworthy AI systems. This work contributes to the growing effort to make LLMs not only powerful but also accountable.

AI.tldr: DAVinCI introduces a two-stage attribution and verification framework to improve factual reliability and interpretability of LLM outputs, achieving 5-20% gains on FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER over verification baselines and enabling auditable, modular integration.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit factual inaccuracies and hallucinations, which undermines trust in high-stakes domains (healthcare, law, science). There is a need for auditable, interpretable pipelines that can attribute claims to sources and internal components and verify them.
AI.method: Two-stage process: (i) attribution of generated claims to internal model components and external sources; (ii) verification of each claim using entailment-based reasoning and confidence calibration. Evaluation on FEVER and CLIMATE-FEVER, comparison against standard verification-only baselines, plus extensive ablations on evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. A modular DAVinCI implementation is released for integration into LLM pipelines.
AI.result: DAVinCI yields improvements in classification accuracy, attribution precision, recall, and F1-score by 5-20% relative to baselines across FEVER/CLIMATE-FEVER. Ablation shows the contributions of evidence span selection, recalibration thresholds, and retrieval quality. A modular implementation is released to facilitate integration into existing LLM systems.
AI.conclusion: By bridging attribution and verification, DAVinCI provides a scalable path to auditable, trustworthy AI systems and contributes to the broader effort to make LLMs not only powerful but also accountable.

title: Graph Neural Network-Informed Predictive Flows for Faster Ford-Fulkerson and PAC-Learnability
summary:
We propose a learning-augmented framework for accelerating max-flow computation and image segmentation by integrating Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm. Rather than predicting initial flows, our method learns edge importance probabilities to guide augmenting path selection. We introduce a Message Passing GNN (MPGNN) that jointly learns node and edge embeddings through coupled updates, capturing both global structure and local flow dynamics such as residual capacity and bottlenecks.
  Given an input image, we propose a method to construct a grid-based flow network with source and sink nodes, extract features, and perform a single GNN inference to assign edge probabilities reflecting their likelihood of belonging to high-capacity cuts. These probabilities are stored in a priority queue and used to guide a modified Ford-Fulkerson procedure, prioritizing augmenting paths via an Edmonds-Karp-style search with bottleneck-aware tie-breaking. This avoids repeated inference over residual graphs while leveraging learned structure throughout optimization.
  We further introduce a bidirectional path construction strategy centered on high-probability edges and provide a theoretical framework relating prediction quality to efficiency via a weighted permutation distance metric. Our method preserves max-flow/min-cut optimality while reducing the number of augmentations in practice. We also outline a hybrid extension combining flow warm-starting with edge-priority prediction, establishing a foundation for learning-guided combinatorial optimization in image segmentation.

AI.tldr: A learning-augmented framework that uses a MPGNN to predict edge importance for guiding Ford-Fulkerson in grid-based image segmentation, preserving optimality while reducing augmentations; includes bidirectional paths, a theoretical efficiency metric, and a hybrid warm-start extension.
AI.motivation: To accelerate max-flow/min-cut computations in image segmentation by leveraging learned structure to prioritize augmenting paths, reducing iterations without sacrificing optimality.
AI.method: Construct a grid-based flow network from the image, perform a single MPGNN inference to assign edge probabilities, store them in a priority queue, and perform an Edmonds-Karp-style augmenting-path search guided by edge priors with bottleneck-aware tie-breaking. Introduce bidirectional path construction on high-probability edges and a theoretical framework relating prediction quality to efficiency via a weighted permutation distance. Propose a hybrid extension combining flow warm-starting with edge-priority prediction.
AI.result: Claims preserved max-flow/min-cut optimality with fewer augmentations in practice; provides a theoretical framework and a hybrid extension, but does not report empirical results in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Learning-guided combinatorial optimization for image segmentation is feasible via a GNN-guided priority regime for augmentations, offering efficiency gains while maintaining exactness; future work includes warm-start and broader applicability.

title: Trust-SSL: Additive-Residual Selective Invariance for Robust Aerial Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a standard approach for representation learning in aerial imagery. Existing methods enforce invariance between augmented views, which works well when augmentations preserve semantic content. However, aerial images are frequently degraded by haze, motion blur, rain, and occlusion that remove critical evidence. Enforcing alignment between a clean and a severely degraded view can introduce spurious structure into the latent space. This study proposes a training strategy and architectural modification to enhance SSL robustness to such corruptions. It introduces a per-sample, per-factor trust weight into the alignment objective, combined with the base contrastive loss as an additive residual. A stop-gradient is applied to the trust weight instead of a multiplicative gate. While a multiplicative gate is a natural choice, experiments show it impairs the backbone, whereas our additive-residual approach improves it. Using a 200-epoch protocol on a 210,000-image corpus, the method achieves the highest mean linear-probe accuracy among six backbones on EuroSAT, AID, and NWPU-RESISC45 (90.20% compared to 88.46% for SimCLR and 89.82% for VICReg). It yields the largest improvements under severe information-erasing corruptions on EuroSAT (+19.9 points on haze at s=5 over SimCLR). The method also demonstrates consistent gains of +1 to +3 points in Mahalanobis AUROC on a zero-shot cross-domain stress test using BDD100K weather splits. Two ablations (scalar uncertainty and cosine gate) indicate the additive-residual formulation is the primary source of these improvements. An evidential variant using Dempster-Shafer fusion introduces interpretable signals of conflict and ignorance. These findings offer a concrete design principle for uncertainty-aware SSL. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/WadiiBoulila/trust-ssl.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware self-supervised learning for aerial imagery using per-sample per-factor trust weights added as an additive residual to a base SSL loss, with stop-gradient on weights; improves robustness to severe degradations and cross-domain shifts, outperforming SimCLR/VICReg on EuroSAT/AID/NWPU-RESISC45; validated via ablations and an evidential DS variant; code released.
AI.motivation: SSL methods assume augmentations preserve semantics, but aerial images are often degraded (haze, blur, rain, occlusion), risking spurious structure in latent space. A robust training strategy is needed to maintain semantic information under corruption and enable cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: Introduce per-sample per-factor trust weights into the alignment objective, applied as an additive residual to the base contrastive loss. A stop-gradient is applied to the trust weights. Compare additive-residual approach to a multiplicative gate (which performed worse). Evaluate on a 210k-image EuroSAT/AID/NWPU-RESISC45-scale dataset with 200 epochs. Investigate under severe corruptions (e.g., haze) and cross-domain weather splits (BDD100K). Conduct ablations (scalar uncertainty, cosine gate) and an evidential variant using Dempster-Shafer fusion.
AI.result: Achieves the highest mean linear-probe accuracy among six backbones on EuroSAT, AID, NWPU-RESISC45 (90.20% vs 88.46% for SimCLR and 89.82% for VICReg). Shows the largest improvements under severe information-erasing corruptions on EuroSAT (+19.9 points for haze at s=5 over SimCLR). Gains of +1 to +3 points in Mahalanobis AUROC on zero-shot cross-domain weather splits (BDD100K). Additive-residual approach outperforms multiplicative gate; two ablations indicate additive-residual as primary source of improvement. An evidential DS fusion variant provides interpretable signals of conflict/ignorance. Code released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: An additive-residual, uncertainty-aware SSL design with stop-gradient weighting yields robust representations for degraded aerial imagery and cross-domain transfer, suggesting a practical design principle for uncertainty-aware SSL in cluttered or corrupted visual domains.

title: Align Generative Artificial Intelligence with Human Preferences: A Novel Large Language Model Fine-Tuning Method for Online Review Management
comment: Accepted to Information Systems Research (ISR). This is a preliminary version
summary: Online reviews have played a pivotal role in consumers' decision-making processes. Existing research has highlighted the significant impact of managerial review responses on customer relationship management and firm performance. However, a large portion of online reviews remains unaddressed due to the considerable human labor required to respond to the rapid growth of online reviews. While generative AI has achieved remarkable success in a range of tasks, they are general-purpose models and may not align well with domain-specific human preferences. To tailor these general generative AI models to domain-specific applications, finetuning is commonly employed. Nevertheless, several challenges persist in finetuning with domain-specific data, including hallucinations, difficulty in representing domain-specific human preferences, and over conservatism in offline policy optimization. To address these challenges, we propose a novel preference finetuning method to align an LLM with domain-specific human preferences for generating online review responses. Specifically, we first identify the source of hallucination and propose an effective context augmentation approach to mitigate the LLM hallucination. To represent human preferences, we propose a novel theory-driven preference finetuning approach that automatically constructs human preference pairs in the online review domain. Additionally, we propose a curriculum learning approach to further enhance preference finetuning. To overcome the challenge of over conservatism in existing offline preference finetuning method, we propose a novel density estimation-based support constraint method to relax the conservatism, and we mathematically prove its superior theoretical guarantees. Extensive evaluations substantiate the superiority of our proposed preference finetuning method.

AI.tldr: Domain-specific preference finetuning for LLMs to generate online review responses, addressing hallucinations, human-preference representation, and offline conservatism via context augmentation, theory-driven preference construction, curriculum learning, and a density-estimation-based constraint with theoretical guarantees; validated with extensive evaluations.
AI.motivation: Online review platforms rely on manager responses to shape relationships and performance, but generating timely, high-quality replies is labor-intensive. General-purpose LLMs often hallucinate and misalign with domain-specific human preferences; offline methods can be overly conservative. The work aims to tailor LLMs to the online review domain.
AI.method: 1) Context augmentation to mitigate LLM hallucination. 2) Theory-driven automatic construction of domain-specific human preference pairs for finetuning. 3) Curriculum learning to enhance preference fine-tuning. 4) Density-estimation-based support constraint to relax offline conservatism, with theoretical guarantees. 5) Extensive evaluations comparing to baselines.
AI.result: The proposed preference finetuning method demonstrates superior performance over baselines in aligning LLM outputs with domain-specific preferences, reducing hallucinations, and mitigating conservatism, as shown by extensive empirical evaluations.
AI.conclusion: A novel, theory-informed, curriculum-guided, and density-constraint-based pretraining pipeline effectively aligns LLMs with domain-specific human preferences for online review response generation, with improved performance and theoretical guarantees.

title: Toward Efficient Membership Inference Attacks against Federated Large Language Models: A Projection Residual Approach
comment: This is the full version (including complete appendices and supplementary materials) of the paper accepted for publication at the 2026 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
summary: Federated Large Language Models (FedLLMs) enable multiple parties to collaboratively fine-tune LLMs without sharing raw data, addressing challenges of limited resources and privacy concerns. Despite data localization, shared gradients can still expose sensitive information through membership inference attacks (MIAs). However, FedLLMs' unique properties, i.e. massive parameter scales, rapid convergence, and sparse, non-orthogonal gradients, render existing MIAs ineffective. To address this gap, we propose ProjRes, the first projection residuals-based passive MIA tailored for FedLLMs. ProjRes leverages hidden embedding vectors as sample representations and analyzes their projection residuals on the gradient subspace to uncover the intrinsic link between gradients and inputs. It requires no shadow models, auxiliary classifiers, or historical updates, ensuring efficiency and robustness. Experiments on four benchmarks and four LLMs show that ProjRes achieves near 100% accuracy, outperforming prior methods by up to 75.75%, and remains effective even under strong differential privacy defenses. Our findings reveal a previously overlooked privacy vulnerability in FedLLMs and call for a re-examination of their security assumptions. Our code and data are available at $\href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Passive-MIA-5268}{link}$.

AI.tldr: ProjRes is a projection residuals-based passive membership inference attack for Federated LLMs (FedLLMs) that uses hidden embeddings and gradient-subspace residuals to reveal input information, achieving near-perfect accuracy and robust to differential privacy, without requiring shadow models or historical updates.
AI.motivation: FedLLMs reduce data sharing but still leak sensitive information via gradients; existing MIAs fail due to FedLLMs' scale, fast convergence, and sparse/non-orthogonal gradients, creating a gap for privacy leakage that this work aims to address.
AI.method: ProjRes computes projection residuals of hidden embedding vectors onto the gradient subspace and uses these residuals to uncover the link between inputs and gradients. It is a passive attack requiring no shadow models, auxiliary classifiers, or historical updates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four benchmarks and four LLMs shows near 100% attack accuracy, outperforming prior methods by up to 75.75%, and remains effective under strong differential privacy defenses.
AI.conclusion: Raises awareness of a previously overlooked privacy vulnerability in FedLLMs, suggesting security assumptions around FedLLMs require re-evaluation; code and data are publicly available at the provided link.

title: SparseGF: A Height-Aware Sparse Segmentation Framework with Context Compression for Robust Ground Filtering Across Urban to Natural Scenes
summary: High-quality digital terrain models derived from airborne laser scanning (ALS) data are essential for a wide range of geospatial analyses, and their generation typically relies on robust ground filtering (GF) to separate point clouds across diverse landscapes into ground and non-ground parts. Although current deep-learning-based GF methods have demonstrated impressive performance, especially in specific challenging terrains, their cross-scene generalization remains limited by two persistent issues: the context-detail dilemma in large-scale processing due to limited computational resources, and the random misclassification of tall objects arising from classification-only optimization. To overcome these limitations, we propose SparseGF, a height-aware sparse segmentation framework enhanced with context compression. It is built upon three key innovations: (1) a convex-mirror-inspired context compression module that condenses expansive contexts into compact representations while preserving central details; (2) a hybrid sparse voxel-point network architecture that effectively interprets compressed representations while mitigating compression-induced geometric distortion; and (3) a height-aware loss function that explicitly enforces topographic elevation priors during training to suppress random misclassification of tall objects. Extensive evaluations on two large-scale ALS benchmark datasets demonstrate that SparseGF delivers robust GF across urban to natural terrains, achieving leading performance in complex urban scenes, competitive results on mixed terrains, and moderate yet non-catastrophic accuracy in densely forested steep areas. This work offers new insights into deep-learning-based GF research and encourages further exploration toward truly cross-scene generalization for large-scale environmental monitoring.

AI.tldr: SparseGF introduces a height-aware, context-compressed sparse segmentation framework for ground filtering of ALS data, combining convex-mirror-inspired context compression, hybrid sparse voxel-point network, and height-aware loss to improve cross-scene generalization and robustness across terrains.
AI.motivation: Ground filtering for ALS point clouds generalizes poorly across scenes due to the context-detail trade-off and misclassification of tall objects; there is a need for scalable, generalizable GF that performs well from urban to forested terrains in large-scale processing.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) a convex-mirror-inspired context compression module to condense expansive contexts without losing central details; (2) a hybrid sparse voxel-point network to interpret compressed representations while mitigating compression-induced geometric distortion; (3) a height-aware loss that enforces topographic elevation priors during training to reduce random misclassification of tall objects.
AI.result: Evaluations on two large-scale ALS benchmarks show SparseGF achieves strong GF across urban to natural terrains, with leading performance in complex urban scenes, competitive results on mixed terrains, and moderate but non-catastrophic accuracy in densely forested steep areas.
AI.conclusion: SparseGF advances deep-learning-based ground filtering and suggests a viable path toward true cross-scene generalization for large-scale environmental monitoring.

title: ReCAPA: Hierarchical Predictive Correction to Mitigate Cascading Failures
summary: Vision-Language-Action systems follow instructions to execute multi-step tasks in multimodal environments. Recent VLA approaches typically rely on post-hoc correction mechanisms or operate under fixed task decompositions and alignment schemes. However, once an intermediate step is mis-specified, local errors propagate through subsequent steps and eventually accumulate into cascading failures. To mitigate this compounding effect, we propose Predictive Alignment and Planning Architecture, a framework that uses prediction and contrast to adjust deviations across three levels: actions, subgoals, and trajectories. Semantic alignment is enforced at all levels using a Sinkhorn-based module and a Score-field module. The predictive correction and alignment jointly update the action generator during training, enabling it to adjust fine-grained steps to remain aligned with the overall intent. We further introduce two new metrics to quantify error propagation and recovery processes in tasks, capturing how mistakes spread and fade over long-horizon execution. Experiments show that ReCAPA achieves competitive results on embodied agent benchmarks such as VisualAgentBench, MineDojo, and AI2-THOR, outperforming strong proprietary and open-source Large Language Model baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReCAPA, a predictive alignment and planning framework for Vision-Language-Action systems that mitigates cascading errors by predictive correction and semantic alignment across actions, subgoals, and trajectories; introduces two metrics for error propagation and recovery; achieves competitive performance on embodied benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cascading failures in multi-step VLA tasks arise when mis-specified intermediate steps propagate; fixed task decompositions and alignment schemes limit robustness. The work seeks proactive, predictive correction and cross-level alignment to maintain alignment with overall intent.
AI.method: Introduce Predictive Alignment and Planning Architecture (ReCAPA) with three-level correction: actions, subgoals, trajectories. Uses prediction and contrast to adjust deviations. Enforces semantic alignment with Sinkhorn-based module and a Score-field module across levels. Jointly updates the action generator during training to adjust fine-grained steps toward overall intent. Proposes two novel metrics to quantify error propagation and recovery across long-horizon tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on embodied agent benchmarks (VisualAgentBench, MineDojo, AI2-THOR) shows competitive results, with ReCAPA outperforming strong proprietary and open-source LLM baselines.
AI.conclusion: Predictive alignment and planning reduce error propagation in VLA systems, enabling robust long-horizon execution. The architecture’s multi-level corrective mechanism and the introduced metrics offer a viable path to improved alignment and recovery during planning and execution.

title: ARFBench: Benchmarking Time Series Question Answering Ability for Software Incident Response
summary: Time series question-answering (TSQA), in which we ask natural language questions to infer and reason about properties of time series, is a promising yet underexplored capability of foundation models. In this work, we present ARFBench, a TSQA benchmark that evaluates the understanding of multimodal foundation models (FMs) on time series anomalies prevalent in software incident data. ARFBench consists of 750 questions across 142 time series and 5.38M data points from 63 production incidents sourced exclusively from internal telemetry at Datadog. We evaluate leading proprietary and open-source LLMs, VLMs, and time series FMs and observe that frontier VLMs perform markedly better than existing baselines; the leading model (GPT-5) achieves a 62.7% accuracy and 51.9% F1. We next demonstrate the promise of specialized multimodal approaches. We develop a novel TSFM + VLM hybrid prototype which we post-train on a small set of synthetic and real data that yields comparable overall F1 and accuracy with frontier models. Lastly, we find models and human domain experts exhibit complementary strengths. We define a model-expert oracle, a best-of-2 oracle selector over model and expert answers, yielding 82.8% F1 and 87.2% accuracy and establishing a new superhuman frontier for future TSQA models. The benchmark is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Datadog/ARFBench.

AI.tldr: ARFBench provides a TSQA benchmark with 750 questions over 142 time series from Datadog incidents; frontier VLMs outperform baselines, with GPT-5 at 62.7% accuracy and 51.9% F1. A TSFM+VLM hybrid post-trained on synthetic/real data attains comparable performance to frontier models. A model-expert oracle yields 82.8% F1 and 87.2% accuracy, setting a new superhuman frontier when combining models with domain experts.
AI.motivation: To evaluate multimodal foundation models on time-series anomaly understanding and reasoning within production incident data, addressing the TSQA capability gap in TS-related tasks.
AI.method: Construct ARFBench with 750 questions across 142 time series (63 production incidents from internal telemetry). Evaluate leading proprietary and open-source LLMs, VLMs, and time-series FMs. Develop a TSFM + VLM hybrid prototype post-trained on synthetic and real data. Define a model-expert oracle (best-of-2 selector) to combine model and expert answers. Benchmark is released on HuggingFace.
AI.result: Frontier VLMs substantially outperform existing baselines; GPT-5 reaches 62.7% accuracy and 51.9% F1. The TSFM+VLM hybrid achieves comparable overall F1 and accuracy to frontier models after post-training. An oracle combining model and expert outputs yields 82.8% F1 and 87.2% accuracy, surpassing previous performance and establishing a new superhuman frontier for TSQA models.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal foundation models show strong promise for TSQA; targeted hybrid models and model-expert orchestration can surpass individual model capabilities, enabling more reliable anomaly reasoning in time-series data. The ARFBench dataset is publicly available for replication and further research.

title: Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a promising paradigm for vision-language models (VLMs) to bridge the distribution gap between pre-training and test data. Recent works have focused on backpropagation-free TTA methods that rely on cache-based designs, but these introduce two key limitations. First, inference latency increases as the cache grows with the number of classes, leading to inefficiencies in large-scale settings. Second, suboptimal performance occurs when the cache contains insufficient or incorrect samples. In this paper, we present Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation (PTA), an efficient and effective TTA paradigm that uses a set of class-specific knowledge prototypes to accumulate knowledge from test samples. Particularly, knowledge prototypes are adaptively weighted based on the zero-shot class confidence of each test sample, incorporating the sample's visual features into the corresponding class-specific prototype. It is worth highlighting that the knowledge from past test samples is integrated and utilized solely in the prototypes, eliminating the overhead of cache population and retrieval that hinders the efficiency of existing TTA methods. This endows PTA with extremely high efficiency while achieving state-of-the-art performance on 15 image recognition benchmarks and 4 robust point cloud analysis benchmarks. For example, PTA improves CLIP's accuracy from 65.64% to 69.38% on 10 cross-domain benchmarks, while retaining 92% of CLIP's inference speed on large-scale ImageNet-1K. In contrast, the cache-based TDA achieves a lower accuracy of 67.97% and operates at only 50% of CLIP's inference speed.

AI.tldr: Prototype-Based Test-Time Adaptation (PTA) replaces cache-based TTA with class-specific knowledge prototypes that accumulate knowledge from test samples, eliminating cache population/retrieval overhead and preserving high inference speed while achieving state-of-the-art accuracy.
AI.motivation: Cache-based TTA methods suffer from increasing latency with the number of classes and performance issues when the cache contains insufficient or incorrect samples, motivating a more scalable, efficient approach.
AI.method: PTA maintains a set of adaptive, class-specific prototypes. Each test sample is assigned a zero-shot class confidence score that weights its contribution to updating the corresponding prototype; the sample's visual features are incorporated into the prototype. Past test-time knowledge is stored exclusively in these prototypes, removing explicit cache population/retrieval.
AI.result: PTA achieves state-of-the-art performance on 15 image-recognition benchmarks and 4 robust point-cloud benchmarks. For example, CLIP accuracy improves from 65.64% to 69.38% on 10 cross-domain benchmarks, while retaining 92% of CLIP’s inference speed on large-scale ImageNet-1K. In contrast, cache-based TDA attains 67.97% accuracy with only 50% of CLIP’s speed.
AI.conclusion: PTA delivers an efficient and effective TTA paradigm that surpasses cache-based methods by achieving higher accuracy with minimal latency, across diverse visual tasks.

title: Robustness Analysis of POMDP Policies to Observation Perturbations
summary: Policies for Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) are often designed using a nominal system model. In practice, this model can deviate from the true system during deployment due to factors such as calibration drift or sensor degradation, leading to unexpected performance degradation. This work studies policy robustness against deviations in the POMDP observation model. We introduce the Policy Observation Robustness Problem: to determine the maximum tolerable deviation in a POMDP's observation model that guarantees the policy's value remains above a specified threshold. We analyze two variants: the sticky variant, where deviations are dependent on state and actions, and the non-sticky variant, where they can be history-dependent. We show that the Policy Observation Robustness Problem can be formulated as a bi-level optimization problem in which the inner optimization is monotonic in the size of the observation deviation. This enables efficient solutions using root-finding algorithms in the outer optimization. For the non-sticky variant, we show that when policies are represented with finite-state controllers (FSCs) it is sufficient to consider observations which depend on nodes in the FSC rather than full histories. We present Robust Interval Search, an algorithm with soundness and convergence guarantees, for both the sticky and non-sticky variants. We show this algorithm has polynomial time complexity in the non-sticky variant and at most exponential time complexity in the sticky variant. We provide experimental results validating and demonstrating the scalability of implementations of Robust Interval Search to POMDP problems with tens of thousands of states. We also provide case studies from robotics and operations research which demonstrate the practical utility of the problem and algorithms.

AI.tldr: Proposes Policy Observation Robustness for POMDPs under observation-model deviations; bi-level optimization with a monotone inner problem; introduces Robust Interval Search; shows polynomial time for non-sticky and exponential time for sticky variants; scalable to large POMDPs and validated on robotics/OR case studies.
AI.motivation: In real deployments, POMDP models drift due to calibration errors or sensor degradation, causing performance loss. The work seeks guarantees on how much observation-model deviation a policy can tolerate while preserving a target value.
AI.method: Formulate as a bi-level optimization problem. The inner problem is monotone in deviation; an outer root-finding approach solves for the maximum tolerable deviation. For non-sticky deviations, sufficiency holds that observations depend on FSC nodes rather than full histories. Introduces Robust Interval Search with soundness/convergence guarantees, applicable to both sticky and non-sticky variants. Complexity: polynomial time in the non-sticky case, at most exponential in the sticky case.
AI.result: Algorithmic framework and a concrete algorithm (Robust Interval Search) with proven guarantees. Demonstrated scalability to POMDPs with tens of thousands of states and validated via robotics and operations research case studies.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides scalable, theoretically-grounded methods to certify and compute maximal observation-model deviations that preserve policy performance in POMDPs, with practical relevance to robotics and operations research.

title: The Recurrent Transformer: Greater Effective Depth and Efficient Decoding
summary: Transformers process tokens in parallel but are temporally shallow: at position $t$, each layer attends to key-value pairs computed based on the previous layer, yielding a depth capped by the number of layers. Recurrent models offer unbounded temporal depth but suffer from optimization instability and historically underutilize modern accelerators. We introduce the Recurrent Transformer, a simple architectural change where each layer attends to key-value pairs computed off its own activations, yielding layerwise recurrent memory while preserving standard autoregressive decoding cost. We show that the architecture can emulate both (i) a conventional Transformer and (ii) token-to-token recurrent updates under mild assumptions, while avoiding optimization instability. Naively, prefill/training appears bandwidth-bound with effective arithmetic intensity near $1$ because keys and values are revealed sequentially; we give an exact tiling-based algorithm that preserves the mathematical computation while reducing HBM traffic from $Θ(N^2)$ to $Θ(N\log N)$, increasing effective arithmetic intensity to $Θ(N/\log N)$ for sequence length $N$. On 150M and 300M parameter C4 pretraining, Recurrent Transformers improve cross-entropy over a parameter-matched Transformer baseline and achieve the improvement with fewer layers (fixed parameters), suggesting that recurrence can trade depth for width, thus reducing KV cache memory footprint and inference latency.

AI.tldr: Recurrent Transformers integrate layerwise recurrence to gain unbounded temporal depth while preserving autoregressive decoding, with a tiling-based memory-traffic reduction that increases arithmetic intensity; pretrained on large C4-like data, they outperform parameter-matched Transformers with fewer layers and lower memory footprint.
AI.motivation: To combine the depth advantages of recurrence with the parallel efficiency of Transformers, addressing optimization instability and high KV-cache memory in standard autoregressive attention.
AI.method: Modify the Transformer by making each layer attend to keys/values derived from its own activations, yielding layerwise recurrent memory. Prove it can emulate a standard Transformer and token-to-token recurrence under mild assumptions. Introduce a tiling-based algorithm that preserves computation while reducing memory traffic from Θ(N^2) to Θ(N log N).
AI.result: Empirical gains on 150M and 300M parameter pretraining (C4): lower cross-entropy than a parameter-matched Transformer baseline, achieved with fewer layers (fixed parameters). The approach increases effective arithmetic intensity and reduces KV-cache memory footprint and inference latency, suggesting recurrence can trade depth for width.
AI.conclusion: Recurrence can complement Transformers to achieve unbounded temporal depth with better efficiency, enabling fewer layers for the same capacity and potentially lowering memory and latency costs in autoregressive decoding.

title: KD-CVG: A Knowledge-Driven Approach for Creative Video Generation
summary: Creative Generation (CG) leverages generative models to automatically produce advertising content that highlights product features, and it has been a significant focus of recent research. However, while CG has advanced considerably, most efforts have concentrated on generating advertising text and images, leaving Creative Video Generation (CVG) relatively underexplored. This gap is largely due to two major challenges faced by Text-to-Video (T2V) models: (a) \textbf{ambiguous semantic alignment}, where models struggle to accurately correlate product selling points with creative video content, and (b) \textbf{inadequate motion adaptability}, resulting in unrealistic movements and distortions. To address these challenges, we develop a comprehensive Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB) as a foundational resource and propose a knowledge-driven approach (KD-CVG) to overcome the knowledge limitations of existing models. KD-CVG consists of two primary modules: Semantic-Aware Retrieval (SAR) and Multimodal Knowledge Reference (MKR). SAR utilizes the semantic awareness of graph attention networks and reinforcement learning feedback to enhance the model's comprehension of the connections between selling points and creative videos. Building on this, MKR incorporates semantic and motion priors into the T2V model to address existing knowledge gaps. Extensive experiments have demonstrated KD-CVG's superior performance in achieving semantic alignment and motion adaptability, validating its effectiveness over other state-of-the-art methods. The code and dataset will be open source at https://kdcvg.github.io/KDCVG/.

AI.tldr: KD-CVG introduces the Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB) and a two-module, knowledge-driven framework (Semantic-Aware Retrieval and Multimodal Knowledge Reference) to tackle semantic alignment and motion adaptability in Text-to-Video advertising generation. It demonstrates state-of-the-art results and opens the code/dataset.
AI.motivation: Creative Video Generation for advertising lags behind text/image generation due to two core issues: ambiguous alignment between selling points and video content, and poor motion realism. A knowledge base tailored to advertising can bridge these gaps.
AI.method: Construct Advertising Creative Knowledge Base (ACKB). Propose KD-CVG with two modules: Semantic-Aware Retrieval (SAR) using graph attention networks and reinforcement learning feedback to map selling points to video content; Multimodal Knowledge Reference (MKR) to inject semantic and motion priors into the T2V model, improving knowledge grounding and motion quality.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show KD-CVG achieves superior semantic alignment and motion adaptability compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: A knowledge-driven approach with ACKB and SAR/MKR modules effectively addresses core CVG challenges, improving both semantic coherence and motion realism; code and dataset are to be released.

title: Trustworthy Clinical Decision Support Using Meta-Predicates and Domain-Specific Languages
summary:
\textbf{Background:} Regulatory frameworks for AI in healthcare, including the EU AI Act and FDA guidance on AI/ML-based medical devices, require clinical decision support to demonstrate not only accuracy but auditability. Existing formal languages for clinical logic validate syntactic and structural correctness but not whether decision rules use epistemologically appropriate evidence.
  \textbf{Methods:} Drawing on design-by-contract principles, we introduce meta-predicates -- predicates about predicates -- for asserting epistemological constraints on clinical decision rules expressed in a DSL. An epistemological type system classifies annotations along four dimensions: purpose, knowledge domain, scale, and method of acquisition. Meta-predicates assert which evidence types are permissible in any given rule. The framework is instantiated in AnFiSA, an open-source platform for genetic variant curation, and demonstrated using the Brigham Genomics Medicine protocol on 5.6 million variants from the Genome in a Bottle benchmark.
  \textbf{Results:} Decision trees used in variant interpretation can be reformulated as unate cascades, enabling per-variant audit trails that identify which rule classified each variant and why. Meta-predicate validation catches epistemological errors before deployment, whether rules are human-written or AI-generated. The approach complements post-hoc methods such as LIME and SHAP: where explanation reveals what evidence was used after the fact, meta-predicates constrain what evidence may be used before deployment, while preserving human readability.
  \textbf{Conclusions:} Meta-predicate validation is a step toward demonstrating not only that decisions are accurate but that they rest on appropriate evidence in ways that can be independently audited. While demonstrated in genomics, the approach generalises to any domain requiring auditable decision logic.

AI.tldr: Introduces meta-predicates to constrain epistemology of clinical decision rules in a DSL, enabling auditable, pre-deployment evidence constraints; demonstrated in genomics.
AI.motivation: Regulatory frameworks require not only accuracy but auditability and epistemic appropriateness of evidence behind decisions; current formal languages validate syntax but not evidence epistemology.
AI.method: Design-by-contract inspired meta-predicates (predicates about predicates) with an epistemological type system (purpose, knowledge domain, scale, acquisition method). Instantiate in AnFiSA for genetic variant curation; apply to Brigham Genomics Medicine protocol on 5.6M Genome in a Bottle variants.
AI.result: Reformulates decision trees as unate cascades allowing per-variant audit trails indicating which rule and why; meta-predicate validation detects epistemological errors before deployment; complements post-hoc explanations (LIME/SHAP) by constraining evidence before deployment while preserving readability.
AI.conclusion: Meta-predicate validation advances auditable, evidence-based decision making and is generalizable beyond genomics to any domain requiring auditable decision logic.

title: Learning Dynamic Representations and Policies from Multimodal Clinical Time-Series with Informative Missingness
summary: Multimodal clinical records contain structured measurements and clinical notes recorded over time, offering rich temporal information about the evolution of patient health. Yet these observations are sparse, and whether they are recorded depends on the patient's latent condition. Observation patterns also differ across modalities, as structured measurements and clinical notes arise under distinct recording processes. While prior work has developed methods that accommodate missingness in clinical time series, how to extract and use the information carried by the observation process itself remains underexplored. We therefore propose a patient representation learning framework for multimodal clinical time series that explicitly leverages informative missingness. The framework combines (1) a multimodal encoder that captures signals from structured and textual data together with their observation patterns, (2) a Bayesian filtering module that updates a latent patient state over time from observed multimodal signals, and (3) downstream modules for offline treatment policy learning and patient outcome prediction based on the learned patient state. We evaluate the framework on ICU sepsis cohorts from MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, and eICU. It improves both offline treatment policy learning and adverse outcome prediction, achieving FQE 0.679 versus 0.528 for clinician behavior and AUROC 0.886 for post-72-hour mortality prediction on MIMIC-III.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multimodal representation learning framework that leverages informative missingness in structured measurements and clinical notes, with Bayesian filtering for latent state, improving offline treatment policy evaluation and mortality prediction on ICU sepsis data.
AI.motivation: Observation patterns and missingness differ across modalities and carry information about latent patient state. Existing methods handle missing data but often ignore informative missingness in the observation process.
AI.method: Three components: (1) a multimodal encoder that jointly captures structured data, text, and their observation patterns, (2) a Bayesian filtering module to update a latent patient state over time from observed multimodal signals, and (3) downstream modules for offline treatment policy learning and outcome prediction based on the learned state.
AI.result: On ICU sepsis cohorts from MIMIC-III, MIMIC-IV, and eICU, the framework improves offline treatment policy evaluation and adverse outcome prediction, achieving FQE 0.679 vs 0.528 for clinician policy and AUROC 0.886 for post-72-hour mortality on MIMIC-III.
AI.conclusion: Modeling informative missingness in multimodal clinical time series improves representation quality and enhances downstream decision-making tasks like policy evaluation and outcome prediction.

title: EdgeFormer: local patch-based edge detection transformer on point clouds
comment: 22 pages, 9 figures. Published in Pattern Analysis and Applications
summary: Edge points on 3D point clouds can clearly convey 3D geometry and surface characteristics, therefore, edge detection is widely used in many vision applications with high industrial and commercial demands. However, the fine-grained edge features are difficult to detect effectively as they are generally densely distributed or exhibit small-scale surface gradients. To address this issue, we present a learning-based edge detection network, named EdgeFormer, which mainly consists of two stages. Based on the observation that spatially neighboring points tend to exhibit high correlation, forming the local underlying surface, we convert the edge detection of the entire point cloud into a point classification based on local patches. Therefore, in the first stage, we construct local patch feature descriptors that describe the local neighborhood around each point. In the second stage, we classify each point by analyzing the local patch feature descriptors generated in the first stage. Due to the conversion of the point cloud into local patches, the proposed method can effectively extract the finer details. The experimental results show that our model demonstrates competitive performance compared to six baselines.

AI.tldr: EdgeFormer: a two-stage, patch-based edge detector for 3D point clouds that converts edge detection into local patch-based point classification, achieving competitive results with six baselines.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained edge features in 3D point clouds are hard to detect due to dense distributions and small surface gradients; leveraging local neighborhood correlations can improve edge extraction.
AI.method: Stage 1 constructs local patch feature descriptors describing each point’s neighborhood. Stage 2 classifies each point by analyzing these local patch descriptors, effectively turning edge detection into a point-one classification on local patches.
AI.result: Experimental results show EdgeFormer achieves competitive performance compared with six baseline methods, indicating effective extraction of fine-grained edge details.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage, patch-based framework that exploits local surface correlations can convert edge detection into local patch-based point classification, enabling finer and more accurate edge detection on 3D point clouds.

title: Enhancing Online Recruitment with Category-Aware MoE and LLM-based Data Augmentation
summary: Person-Job Fit (PJF) is a critical component for online recruitment. Existing approaches face several challenges, particularly in handling low-quality job descriptions and similar candidate-job pairs, which impair model performance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a large language model (LLM) based method with two novel techniques: (1) LLM-based data augmentation, which polishes and rewrites low-quality job descriptions by leveraging chain-of-thought (COT) prompts, and (2) category-aware Mixture of Experts (MoE) that assists in identifying similar candidate-job pairs. This MoE module incorporates category embeddings to dynamically assign weights to the experts and learns more distinguishable patterns for similar candidate-job pairs. We perform offline evaluations and online A/B tests on our recruitment platform. Our method relatively surpasses existing methods by 2.40% in AUC and 7.46% in GAUC, and boosts click-through conversion rate (CTCVR) by 19.4% in online tests, saving millions of CNY in external headhunting expenses.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based framework for Person-Job Fit using (1) chain-of-thought-driven data augmentation to rewrite low-quality job descriptions and (2) a category-aware MoE to identify similar candidate-job pairs, yielding substantial offline/online gains in AUC, GAUC, and CTCVR with cost savings.
AI.motivation: Low-quality job descriptions and similar candidate-job pairs hamper PJF performance; leveraging LLMs for data polishing and MoE with category signals improves discrimination and data quality.
AI.method: 1) LLM-based data augmentation: polish and rewrite dysfunctional job descriptions using chain-of-thought prompts. 2) Category-aware MoE: incorporate category embeddings to dynamically weight experts, learning more discriminative patterns for similar candidate-job pairs. Evaluation on offline data and online A/B tests on a recruitment platform.
AI.result: Offline metrics show increases in AUC/GAUC by 2.40% and 7.46% respectively; online CTCVR increases by 19.4%, with substantial savings in external headhunting (millions of CNY).
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven data quality enhancement plus category-aware MoE yields meaningful gains in PJF performance and cost reductions, validating the efficacy of combining data polishing with adaptive ensemble modeling in recruitment.

title: CAP: Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on unfiltered corpora inherently risk retaining sensitive information, necessitating selective knowledge unlearning for regulatory compliance and ethical safety. However, existing parameter-modifying methods face fundamental limitations: high computational costs, uncontrollable forgetting boundaries, and strict dependency on model weight access. These constraints render them impractical for closed-source models, yet current non-invasive alternatives remain unsystematic and reliant on empirical experience. To address these challenges, we propose the Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning (CAP) framework, an end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning paradigm. CAP decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning, where a prompt generator collaborates with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities selectively. This approach enables reversible knowledge restoration through prompt revocation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAP achieves precise, controllable unlearning without updating model parameters, establishing a dynamic alignment mechanism that overcomes the transferability limitations of prior methods.

AI.tldr: CAP introduces a prompt-based unlearning framework that uses reinforcement learning to optimize a prompt, enabling reversible, controllable unlearning of target knowledge in LLMs without modifying model parameters, suitable for closed-source models.
AI.motivation: Address the risk of sensitive information retention in LLMs trained on unfiltered corpora and the limitations of parameter-modifying unlearning (cost, forgetting boundaries, weight-access constraints) and non-invasive methods (unsystematic).
AI.method: CAP decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning. A prompt generator collaborates with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities, enabling reversible unlearning through prompt revocation without updating model weights.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show precise, controllable unlearning without parameter updates, achieving a dynamic alignment mechanism that overcomes transferability limitations of prior methods.
AI.conclusion: CAP provides a practical, end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning framework that enables reversible, controllable suppression of targeted knowledge without touching model parameters, beneficial for compliance and safety in both open and closed models.

title: VG-CoT: Towards Trustworthy Visual Reasoning via Grounded Chain-of-Thought
summary: The advancement of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) requires precise local region-based reasoning that faithfully grounds the model's logic in actual visual evidence. However, existing datasets face limitations in scalability due to extensive manual annotation and lack of explicit alignment between multi-step reasoning and corresponding image regions, which constrains the evaluation of model trustworthiness. To address these challenges, we propose the Visual Grounding Chain-of-Thought (VG-CoT) dataset, which explicitly links each reasoning step to real visual evidence within the image through a fully automated three-stage pipeline. The pipeline first extracts object- and text-level visual evidence using state-of-the-art detection and OCR models, then generates step-by-step grounded reasoning with GPT-4o, and finally refines the grounding through a rationale-driven open-set detection process. In addition, we introduce a new benchmark that comprehensively evaluates LVLMs reasoning across three complementary dimensions: Rationale Quality, Answer Accuracy, and Reasoning-Answer Alignment. Experiments with representative LVLMs, including LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2-VL, demonstrate consistent improvements on most evaluation metrics, confirming that VG-CoT effectively enhances trustworthy, evidence-based reasoning while maintaining scalable and cost-efficient dataset construction. The dataset and code will be released publicly upon acceptance to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: VG-CoT offers a fully automated VG-grounded chain-of-thought dataset and benchmark for LVLMs, linking each reasoning step to real image evidence to enable scalable, trustworthy evaluation; experiments show improvements on LVLMs with cost-efficient data construction.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, explicit alignment between multi-step reasoning and visual evidence to evaluate and improve trustworthiness.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) extract visual evidence via object detection and OCR; (2) generate step-by-step grounded reasoning with GPT-4o; (3) refine grounding via rationale-driven open-set detection. Create VG-CoT dataset and a tri-dimensional benchmark: Rationale Quality, Answer Accuracy, Reasoning-Answer Alignment.
AI.result: LVLMs such as LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2-VL show consistent improvements on most metrics; demonstrates scalable, cost-efficient dataset construction and trustworthy reasoning grounding.
AI.conclusion: VG-CoT enhances trustworthy, evidence-based reasoning in LVLMs and enables scalable evaluation; code and dataset to be released.

title: Can MLLMs "Read" What is Missing?
summary: We introduce MMTR-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate the intrinsic ability of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to reconstruct masked text directly from visual context. Unlike conventional question-answering tasks, MMTR-Bench eliminates explicit prompts, requiring models to recover masked text from single- or multi-page inputs across real-world domains such as documents and webpages. This design isolates the reconstruction task from instruction-following abilities, enabling a direct assessment of a model's layout understanding, visual grounding, and knowledge integration. MMTR-Bench comprises 2,771 test samples spanning multiple languages and varying target lengths. To account for this diversity, we propose a level-aware evaluation protocol. Experiments on representative MLLMs show that the benchmark poses a significant challenge, especially for sentence- and paragraph-level reconstruction. The homepage is available at https://mmtr-bench-dataset.github.io/MMTR-Bench/.

AI.tldr: MMTR-Bench is a prompt-free benchmark that tests multimodal LLMs’ intrinsic ability to reconstruct masked text from visual context in documents/web pages, using 2,771 multilingual samples and a level-aware evaluation protocol; results show substantial difficulty, especially for sentence/paragraph reconstructions.
AI.motivation: To isolate a model’s reconstruction and understanding capabilities from instruction-following, enabling direct assessment of layout understanding, visual grounding, and knowledge integration in MLLMs.
AI.method: Design of a masked-text reconstruction task across single- or multi-page inputs in real-world domains (documents and webpages) with no explicit prompts. The dataset contains 2,771 test samples across languages and target lengths. A level-aware evaluation protocol accounts for varying reconstruction difficulty (e.g., word, sentence, paragraph level). Experiments evaluate representative MLLMs on the task.
AI.result: The benchmark is challenging for current MLLMs, with sentence- and paragraph-level reconstruction proving especially difficult; results indicate a gap between surface-level vision-language capabilities and deeper layout and content reconstruction. The study provides empirical evidence of the need for improvements in visual grounding and knowledge integration within MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: MMTR-Bench offers a principled, prompt-free criterion for diagnosing intrinsic multimodal reconstruction and layout understanding in MLLMs, serving as a diagnostic and benchmarking tool to drive progress in visual-text alignment across documents and webpages; the homepage provides access to the dataset.

title: Improving Performance in Classification Tasks with LCEN and the Weighted Focal Differentiable MCC Loss
summary: The LASSO-Clip-EN (LCEN) algorithm was previously introduced for nonlinear, interpretable feature selection and machine learning. However, its design and use was limited to regression tasks. In this work, we create a modified version of the LCEN algorithm that is suitable for classification tasks and maintains its desirable properties, such as interpretability. This modified LCEN algorithm is evaluated on four widely used binary and multiclass classification datasets. In these experiments, LCEN is compared against 10 other model types and consistently reaches high test-set macro F$_1$ score and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) metrics, higher than that of the majority of investigated models. LCEN models for classification remain sparse, eliminating an average of 56% of all input features in the experiments performed. Furthermore, LCEN-selected features are used to retrain all models using the same data, leading to statistically significant performance improvements in three of the experiments and insignificant differences in the fourth when compared to using all features or other feature selection methods. Simultaneously, the weighted focal differentiable MCC (diffMCC) loss function is evaluated on the same datasets. Models trained with the diffMCC loss function are always the best-performing methods in these experiments, and reach test-set macro F$_1$ scores that are, on average, 4.9% higher and MCCs that are 8.5% higher than those obtained by models trained with the weighted cross-entropy loss. These results highlight the performance of LCEN as a feature selection and machine learning algorithm also for classification tasks, and how the diffMCC loss function can train very accurate models, surpassing the weighted cross-entropy loss in the tasks investigated.

AI.tldr: Modified LCEN adapted for classification; achieves sparse feature selection, high macro F1 and MCC across datasets; diffMCC loss outperforms cross-entropy; LCEN-based retraining improves or matches baseline performance.
AI.motivation: Extend LCEN from regression to classification while preserving interpretability and sparsity; evaluate robustness across binary and multiclass tasks; compare against multiple models and assess a differentiable loss (diffMCC) tailored to MCC.
AI.method: Develop a classification-friendly LCEN modification; evaluate on four binary/multiclass datasets; compare with 10 model types; LCEN achieves ~56% feature elimination; perform retraining with LCEN-selected features; compare performance when using all features vs feature-selected; assess diffMCC loss vs weighted cross-entropy across datasets.
AI.result: LCEN for classification yields high test-set macro F1 and MCC, often outperforming most models; LCEN-driven feature selection reduces features by about 56% and, when used to retrain, yields statistically significant improvements in 3 of 4 experiments; diffMCC loss models consistently achieve best performance, with macro F1 ~4.9% higher and MCC ~8.5% higher than models trained with weighted cross-entropy.
AI.conclusion: LCEN is effective as a sparse, interpretable feature selection and learning method for classification; the diffMCC loss further enhances predictive performance across tasks, surpassing weighted cross-entropy in the studied settings.

title: You Only Gaussian Once: Controllable 3D Gaussian Splatting for Ultra-Densely Sampled Scenes
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized neural rendering, yet existing methods remain predominantly research prototypes ill-suited for production-level deployment. We identify a critical "Industry-Academia Gap" hindering real-world application: unpredictable resource consumption from heuristic Gaussian growth, the "sparsity shield" of current benchmarks that rewards hallucination over physical fidelity, and severe multi-sensor data pollution. To bridge this gap, we propose YOGO (You Only Gaussian Once), a system-level framework that reformulates the stochastic growth process into a deterministic, budget-aware equilibrium. YOGO integrates a novel budget controller for hardware-constrained resource allocation and an availability-registration protocol for robust multi-sensor fusion. To push the boundaries of reconstruction fidelity, we introduce Immersion v1.0, the first ultra-dense indoor dataset specifically designed to break the "sparsity shield." By providing saturated viewpoint coverage, Immersion v1.0 forces algorithms to focus on extreme physical fidelity rather than viewpoint interpolation, and enables the community to focus on the upper limits of high-fidelity reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that YOGO achieves state-of-the-art visual quality while maintaining a strictly deterministic profile, establishing a new standard for production-grade 3DGS. To facilitate reproducibility, part scenes of Immersion v1.0 dataset and source code of YOGO has been publicly released. The project link is https://jjrcn.github.io/YOGO/.

AI.tldr: A production-oriented framework (YOGO) is proposed to make 3D Gaussian Splatting production-ready via deterministic, budget-aware resource management and robust multi-sensor fusion, complemented by an ultra-dense indoor dataset (Immersion v1.0) to test fidelity under saturated viewpoints; results show state-of-the-art quality with reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Address the Industry-Academia Gap in 3D Gaussian Splatting: unpredictable resource growth, benchmarks rewarding hallucination rather than fidelity, and multi-sensor data pollution hinder real-world deployment.
AI.method: Introduce YOGO: a system-level framework with a budget controller for hardware-constrained resource allocation and an availability-registration protocol for robust multi-sensor fusion; replace stochastic growth with deterministic, budget-aware equilibrium. Introduce Immersion v1.0, an ultra-dense indoor dataset with saturated viewpoint coverage to stress fidelity over interpolation. Release partial scenes and code for reproducibility.
AI.result: YOGO achieves state-of-the-art visual quality with a strictly deterministic profile; experiments validate improved production-friendliness and robustness to multi-sensor data; Immersion v1.0 challenges algorithms to maximize physical fidelity; parts of the dataset and source code released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The work sets a new standard for production-grade 3D Gaussian Splatting by closing the Industry-Academia Gap, delivering deterministic resource management, robust sensor fusion, and a dataset that pushes fidelity limits, with an emphasis on reproducibility.

title: Spatial Metaphors for LLM Memory: A Critical Analysis of the MemPalace Architecture
comment: 20 pages, 10 tables. Code and data at https://github.com/web3guru888/mempalace-scientific-analysis
summary: MemPalace is an open-source AI memory system that applies the ancient method of loci (memory palace) spatial metaphor to organize long-term memory for large language models; launched in April 2026, it accumulated over 47,000 GitHub stars in its first two weeks and claims state-of-the-art retrieval performance on the LongMemEval benchmark (96.6% Recall@5) without requiring any LLM inference at write time. Through independent codebase analysis, benchmark replication, and comparison with competing systems, we find that MemPalace's headline retrieval performance is attributable primarily to its verbatim storage philosophy combined with ChromaDB's default embedding model (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), rather than to its spatial organizational metaphor per se -- the palace hierarchy (Wings->Rooms->Closets->Drawers) operates as standard vector database metadata filtering, an effective but well-established technique. However, MemPalace makes several genuinely novel contributions: (1) a contrarian verbatim-first storage philosophy that challenges extraction-based competitors, (2) an extremely low wake-up cost (approximately 170 tokens) through its four-layer memory stack, (3) a fully deterministic, zero-LLM write path enabling offline operation at zero API cost, and (4) the first systematic application of spatial memory metaphors as an organizing principle for AI memory systems. We also note that the competitive landscape is evolving rapidly, with Mem0's April 2026 token-efficient algorithm raising their LongMemEval score from approximately 49% to 93.4%, narrowing the gap between extraction-based and verbatim approaches. Our analysis concludes that MemPalace represents significant architectural insight wrapped in overstated claims -- a pattern common in rapidly adopted open-source projects where marketing velocity exceeds scientific rigor.

AI.tldr: MemPalace uses a verbatim-first, four-layer memory stack to organize LLM long-term memory with a spatial metaphor; while it achieves strong LongMemEval recall, this performance largely stems from verbatim storage and the embedding model rather than the metaphor, and the work offers novel architectural ideas but makes overstated claims.
AI.motivation: To improve long-term memory management for large language models by enabling fast, offline, scalable retrieval using a spatial memory metaphor (memory palace) as an organizing principle.
AI.method: Independent codebase audit, benchmark replication on LongMemEval, and comparison with competing systems (notably Mem0). Technically, it employs a four-layer memory stack (Wings -> Rooms -> Closets -> Drawers), a deterministic offline write path, and uses ChromaDB with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings to populate a verbatim store for retrieval.
AI.result: MemPalace reports state-of-the-art LongMemEval Recall@5 of 96.6%. Key claimed contributions include: (1) a contrarian verbatim-first storage philosophy, (2) very low wake-up cost (~170 tokens) via a four-layer memory stack, (3) a zero-LLM write path enabling offline operation, and (4) the first systematic application of spatial memory metaphors for AI memory organization. The analysis notes that performance largely derives from verbatim storage and embedding choice, not the metaphor, and that Mem0’s token-efficient algorithm narrows the gap.
AI.conclusion: MemPalace provides meaningful architectural insights and introduces the first systematic use of spatial metaphors for AI memory, but its headline claims overstate rigor and novelty relative to limits in the evidence; the rapidly evolving landscape suggests cautious interpretation and continued benchmarking.

title: Hyperloop Transformers
summary: LLM architecture research generally aims to maximize model quality subject to fixed compute/latency budgets. However, many applications of interest such as edge and on-device deployment are further constrained by the model's memory footprint, thus motivating parameter-efficient architectures for language modeling. This paper describes a simple architecture that improves the parameter-efficiency of LLMs. Our architecture makes use of looped Transformers as a core primitive, which reuse Transformer layers across depth and are thus more parameter-efficient than ordinary (depth-matched) Transformers. We organize the looped Transformer into three blocks--begin, middle, and end blocks--where each block itself consists of multiple Transformer layers, and only the middle block is applied recurrently across depth. We augment the looped middle block with hyper-connections (Xie et al., 2026), which expand the residual stream into matrix-valued residual streams. Hyper-connections are applied only after each loop, and therefore add minimal new parameters and compute cost. Across various model scales, we find that our Hyper-Connected Looped Transformer (Hyperloop Transformer) is able to outperform depth-matched Transformer and mHC Transformer baselines despite using approximately 50% fewer parameters. The outperformance persists through post-training weight quantization, thus positioning Hyperloop Transformers as an attractive architecture for memory-efficient language modeling.

AI.tldr: Hyperloop Transformer proposes a parameter-efficient LLM architecture by using looped Transformers with a recurrent middle block and hyper-connections that expand the residual stream to matrix-valued data. It achieves similar or better performance than depth-matched and mHC baselines with ~50% fewer parameters, and remains robust under post-training quantization.
AI.motivation: Edge/on-device deployment requires small memory footprints. Parameter-efficient architectures that reuse parameters across depth (looped transformers) can reduce memory usage. Hyper-connections further boost expressivity with minimal added parameters.
AI.method: Three-block looped Transformer: begin, middle, end blocks; the middle block is repeatedly applied across depth (looped). The middle block is augmented with hyper-connections that expand the residual stream into matrix-valued streams, applied after each loop. Each block contains multiple Transformer layers; only the middle block is recurrent across depth. The approach is evaluated across model scales and tested under post-training weight quantization.
AI.result: Hyperloop Transformer outperforms depth-matched Transformer and mHC Transformer baselines while using about 50% fewer parameters. The advantage persists after post-training quantization, indicating good memory efficiency for language modeling.
AI.conclusion: Hyperloop Transformer is a promising memory-efficient architecture for LLMs suitable for edge/deployed contexts, achieving parameter efficiency with minimal loss in performance and robustness to quantization.

title: S1-VL: Scientific Multimodal Reasoning Model with Thinking-with-Images
summary: We present S1-VL, a multimodal reasoning model for scientific domains that natively supports two complementary reasoning paradigms: Scientific Reasoning, which relies on structured chain-of-thought, and Thinking-with-Images, which enables the model to actively manipulate images through Python code execution during reasoning. In the Thinking-with-Images mode, the model generates and executes image-processing code in a sandbox environment, obtains intermediate visual results, and continues reasoning in a multi-turn iterative manner. This design is particularly effective for challenging scenarios such as high-resolution scientific chart interpretation, microscopic image understanding, and geometry-assisted reasoning. To construct the training data, we collect scientific multimodal datasets spanning six disciplines: mathematics, physics, chemistry, astronomy, geography, and biology. We further develop a six-dimensional quality filtering framework for reasoning trajectories. To mitigate redundant, ineffective, and erroneous visual operations commonly found in existing datasets, we propose a multi-stage filtering pipeline together with an adaptive data routing strategy. This strategy converts samples with low visual information gain into pure Reasoning-mode data, enabling the model to learn when image operations are truly necessary. S1-VL is trained through a four-stage progressive pipeline: scientific multimodal SFT, Thinking-with-Images cold-start SFT, and two stages of reinforcement learning with SAPO. We build S1-VL-32B on top of Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking and evaluate it on 13 benchmarks. Experimental results show that S1-VL-32B achieves state-of-the-art performance on all five Thinking-with-Images benchmarks, including HRBench-4K, HRBench-8K, MME-RealWorld-CN, MME-RealWorld-Lite, and V*, and outperforms compared systems on scientific reasoning benchmarks such as Physics and VRSBench.

AI.tldr: S1-VL introduces a dual-reasoning multimodal model for scientific domains, combining Scientific Reasoning with a Thinking-with-Images mode that executes image-processing code in sandboxed Python during reasoning. It leverages large-scale multi-discipline data, a six-dimensional quality filtering framework, adaptive data routing to prune ineffective visual operations, and a four-stage training pipeline with SFT and SAPO RL. It achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple Thinking-with-Images benchmarks and strong performance on physics and VRSBench.
AI.motivation: The goal is to enable robust, interpretable multimodal reasoning in scientific domains by combining structured reasoning with dynamic image manipulation, addressing challenges in high-resolution chart interpretation, microscopy, and geometry-based tasks.
AI.method: Two complementary paradigms: Scientific Reasoning with structured chain-of-thought and Thinking-with-Images that generates and executes image-processing Python code in a sandbox, obtaining intermediate visuals and iterating across turns. Training data spans mathematics, physics, chemistry, astronomy, geography, biology. A six-dimensional quality filtering framework guides reasoning trajectories. A multi-stage filtering pipeline plus adaptive routing reduces redundant/erroneous visual ops, routing low-information samples to pure Reasoning-mode. Training is four-stage: scientific multimodal SFT, Thinking-with-Images cold-start SFT, followed by two SAPO RL stages. The model is built on Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking (S1-VL-32B).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on five Thinking-with-Images benchmarks (HRBench-4K, HRBench-8K, MME-RealWorld-CN, MME-RealWorld-Lite, V*). Outperforms baselines on scientific reasoning benchmarks such as Physics and VRSBench.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the effectiveness of coupling dual reasoning paradigms with disciplined data curation and training strategies to advance scientific multimodal reasoning. It highlights the value of active image manipulation in reasoning tasks and provides a scalable framework for complex, cross-domain scientific understanding.

title: Ideological Bias in LLMs' Economic Causal Reasoning
summary: Do large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic ideological bias when reasoning about economic causal effects? As LLMs are increasingly used in policy analysis and economic reporting, where directionally correct causal judgments are essential, this question has direct practical stakes. We present a systematic evaluation by extending the EconCausal benchmark with ideology-contested cases - instances where intervention-oriented (pro-government) and market-oriented (pro-market) perspectives predict divergent causal signs. From 10,490 causal triplets (treatment-outcome pairs with empirically verified effect directions) derived from top-tier economics and finance journals, we identify 1,056 ideology-contested instances and evaluate 20 state-of-the-art LLMs on their ability to predict empirically supported causal directions.
