We find that ideology-contested items are consistently harder than non-contested ones, and that across 18 of 20 models, accuracy is systematically higher when the empirically verified causal sign aligns with intervention-oriented expectations than with market-oriented ones. Moreover, when models err, their incorrect predictions disproportionately lean intervention-oriented, and this directional skew is not eliminated by one-shot in-context prompting. These results highlight that LLMs are not only less accurate on ideologically contested economic questions, but systematically less reliable in one ideological direction than the other, underscoring the need for direction-aware evaluation in high-stakes economic and policy settings.

AI.tldr: Ideology-contested causal judgments by LLMs show systematic directional bias.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs harbor ideologically biased causal reasoning in economics by testing on ideology-contested cases where intervention vs market predictions diverge.
AI.method: Extend the EconCausal benchmark with ideology-contested items (1,056 cases among 10,490 empirically verified causal triplets). Evaluate 20 SOTA LLMs on predicting the empirically supported causal directions; compare accuracy across alignment with intervention- vs market-oriented signs; analyze error direction and prompting effects.
AI.result: Ideology-contested items are harder; 18/20 models perform better when the empirical sign matches intervention-oriented predictions; errors skew toward intervention bias; one-shot prompting does not eliminate the skew.
AI.conclusion: LLMs exhibit directional ideological bias in economic causal reasoning; evaluation should account for directionality, especially in high-stakes policy and economic analysis.

title: Measure Twice, Click Once: Co-evolving Proposer and Visual Critic via Reinforcement Learning for GUI Grounding
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) grounding requires mapping natural language instructions to precise pixel coordinates. However, due to visually homogeneous elements and dense layouts, models typically grasp semantic intent yet struggle with achieving precise localization. While scaling sampling attempts (Pass@k) reveals potential gains, static self-consistency strategies derived from geometric clustering often yield limited improvements, as the model's predictions tend to be spatially dispersed. In this paper, we propose replacing static consistency strategies with a learnable selection mechanism that selects the optimal target by critiquing its own proposals rendered on the screenshot. Given the significant disparity between the model's grounding and critiquing capabilities, we propose a co-evolving Propose-then-Critic framework. To jointly optimize these, we introduce a maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning paradigm. This approach dynamically balances the training objectives of proposer and critic, where the diversity of the proposer's outputs enhances critic robustness, while the critic's maturing discrimination capability conversely unlocks the proposer's potential for extensive spatial exploration, fostering the mutual reinforcement and co-evolution of both capabilities, thereby ensuring generalizability to adapt to diverse and complex interface layouts. Extensive experiments over 6 benchmarks show that our method significantly enhances both grounding accuracy and critic reliability.

AI.tldr: A co-evolving Propose-then-Critic framework with adaptive reinforcement learning improves pixel-precise GUI grounding by replacing static consistency with learnable critique, demonstrating gains across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Static offline consistency strategies based on geometric clustering fail to resolve precise localization in visually homogeneous, dense GUI layouts; robust, generalizable pixel-level grounding requires dynamic critique and exploration.
AI.method: Introduce a learnable proposer-critic pair that jointly evolves: the proposer generates target proposals, the critic evaluates and critiques them by rendering proposals on a screenshot. A maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning paradigm balances training objectives, using proposer's output diversity to improve critic robustness and critic maturation to unlock broader spatial exploration by the proposer.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across six benchmarks shows significant improvements in grounding accuracy and in the reliability of the critic.
AI.conclusion: A co-evolving Propose-then-Critic framework with adaptive RL enhances GUI grounding precision and generalization, addressing limitations of static consistency and enabling robust spatial exploration through mutual reinforcement.

title: Pre-process for segmentation task with nonlinear diffusion filters
summary:
This paper deals with the case of using nonlinear diffusion filters to obtain piecewise constant images as a previous process for segmentation techniques.
  We first show an intrinsic formulation for the nonlinear diffusion equation to provide some design conditions on the diffusion filters. According to this theoretical framework, we propose a new family of diffusivities; they are obtained from nonlinear diffusion techniques and are related with backward diffusion. Their goal is to split the image in closed contours with a homogenized grey intensity inside and with no blurred edges.
  We also prove that our filters satisfy the well-posedness semi-discrete and full discrete scale-space requirements. This shows that by using semi-implicit schemes, a forward nonlinear diffusion equation is solved, instead of a backward nonlinear diffusion equation, connecting with an edge-preserving process. Under the conditions established for the diffusivity and using a stopping criterion for the diffusion time, we get piecewise constant images with a low computational effort.
  Finally, we test our filter with real images and we illustrate the effects of our diffusivity function as a method to get piecewise constant images.
  The code is available at https://github.com/cplatero/NonlinearDiffusion.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based pre-processing method for segmentation that yields piecewise-constant images using a novel family of diffusivities derived from nonlinear diffusion (related to backward diffusion). The approach establishes an intrinsic formulation and proves well-posedness for semi-discrete and full discrete scale-space, uses semi-implicit schemes to solve forward diffusion for edge preservation, and employs a stopping criterion to obtain low-cost, piecewise-constant images; validated on real images with code provided.
AI.motivation: Obtain clean, piecewise-constant representations of images to facilitate segmentation, ensuring sharp edges while homogenizing interiors, and to design diffusion filters with solid theoretical underpinnings and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Formulate an intrinsic nonlinear diffusion equation and derive design conditions for diffusivity functions. Propose a new diffusivity family connected to backward diffusion to promote contour formation. Prove well-posedness for semi-discrete and fully discrete scale-space. Implement semi-implicit time stepping to solve forward diffusion (edge-preserving) and apply a stopping criterion on diffusion time. Validate on real images and analyze the impact of the diffusivity design.
AI.result: The paper provides theoretical results establishing scale-space well-posedness under discretization, demonstrates that forward diffusion can be achieved via semi-implicit schemes, and shows piecewise-constant outputs with low computational cost. Empirical tests on real images illustrate the effects of the diffusivity function in producing homogeneous interiors and sharp contours.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diffusivity design combined with semi-implicit forward diffusion yields effective, computationally efficient piecewise-constant images suitable for segmentation, with demonstrated real-image tests and available code for reproducibility; further work could include quantitative segmentation metrics and robustness studies.

title: Evaluating AI Meeting Summaries with a Reusable Cross-Domain Pipeline
comment: AI Application Feature Quality Evaluation (28 pages total)
summary:
We present a reusable evaluation pipeline for generative AI applications, instantiated for AI meeting summaries and released with a public artifact package derived from a Dataset Pipeline. The system separates reusable orchestration from task-specific semantics across five stages: source intake, structured reference construction, candidate generation, structured scoring, and reporting. Unlike standalone claim scorers, it treats both ground truth and evaluator outputs as typed, persisted artifacts, enabling aggregation, issue analysis, and statistical testing.
  We benchmark the offline loop on a typed dataset of 114 meetings spanning city_council, private_data, and whitehouse_press_briefings, producing 340 meeting-model pairs and 680 judge runs across gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-5-mini, and gpt-5.1. Under this protocol, gpt-4.1-mini achieves the highest mean accuracy (0.583), while gpt-5.1 leads in completeness (0.886) and coverage (0.942). Paired sign tests with Holm correction show no significant accuracy winner but confirm significant retention gains for gpt-5.1.
  A typed DeepEval contrastive baseline preserves retention ordering but reports higher holistic accuracy, suggesting that reference-based scoring may overlook unsupported-specifics errors captured by claim-grounded evaluation. Typed analysis identifies whitehouse_press_briefings as an accuracy-challenging domain with frequent unsupported specifics. A deployment follow-up shows gpt-5.4 outperforming gpt-4.1 across all metrics, with statistically robust gains on retention metrics under the same protocol. The system benchmarks the offline loop and documents, but does not quantitatively evaluate, the online feedback-to-evaluation path.

AI.tldr: A typed, artifact-based evaluation pipeline for generative AI meeting summaries, with a five-stage architecture and offline benchmarking across multiple models, yielding nuanced retention and completeness results and a deployment-grade performance gain for newer models.
AI.motivation: Provide a reusable, task-agnostic evaluator that separates orchestration from semantics, enabling aggregation, issue analysis, and statistical testing through typed ground-truth and outputs.
AI.method: Five stages: source intake, structured reference construction, candidate generation, structured scoring, and reporting. Artifacts (ground truth and outputs) are typed and persisted. Evaluated offline on 114 meetings (city_council, private_data, whitehouse_press_briefings) with 340 meeting-model pairs and 680 judge runs across gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-5-mini, and gpt-5.1. Used typed DeepEval contrastive baseline. Paired sign tests with Holm correction. A deployment follow-up uses gpt-5.4. Online feedback-to-evaluation path not evaluated.
AI.result: GPT-4.1-mini achieves highest mean accuracy (0.583); GPT-5.1 leads in completeness (0.886) and coverage (0.942). Paired tests show no significant accuracy winner but significant retention gains for GPT-5.1. DeepEval baseline preserves retention ordering but reports higher holistic accuracy, suggesting reference-based scoring may miss unsupported-specifics errors. Whitehouse_press_briefings are particularly challenging for accuracy. Deployment with GPT-5.4 outperforms GPT-4.1 across all metrics with robust retention gains. The paper documents offline benchmarking but does not quantify online feedback-to-evaluation path.
AI.conclusion: Typed, artifact-based evaluation improves comparability and statistical testing in generative-evaluation pipelines. Observed model tradeoffs (accuracy vs. completeness vs. retention) highlight domain-specific challenges and the potential benefits of newer models. Further work needed to assess online feedback-to-evaluation flow.

title: Understanding and Mitigating Spurious Signal Amplification in Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for Math Reasoning
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) always adapts models at inference time via pseudo-labeling, leaving it vulnerable to spurious optimization signals from label noise. Through an empirical study, we observe that responses with medium consistency form an ambiguity region and constitute the primary source of reward noise. Crucially, we find that such spurious signals can be even amplified through group-relative advantage estimation. Motivated by these findings, we propose a unified framework, Debiased and Denoised test-time Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), to mitigate spurious signals. Concretely, DDRL first applies a frequency-based sampling strategy to exclude ambiguous samples while maintaining a balanced set of positive and negative examples. It then adopts a debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages, removing the bias introduced by group-relative policy optimization. Finally, DDRL incorporates a consensus-based off-policy refinement stage, which leverages the rejection-sampled dataset to enable efficient and stable model updates. Experiments on three large language models across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines. The code will soon be released at https://github.com/yuyongcan/DDRL.

AI.tldr: DDRL mitigates spurious reward signals in test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) by a three-stage framework: (1) frequency-based sampling to exclude ambiguous samples while preserving a balanced, positive/negative set; (2) debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages to remove bias from group-relative policy optimization; (3) a consensus-based off-policy refinement using the rejection-sampled dataset for stable updates. Empirical results on three large language models across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks show consistent improvements over existing TTRL baselines; code to be released.
AI.motivation: TTRL is vulnerable to label noise and spurious optimization signals during inference. The authors observe that medium-consistency responses create an ambiguity region that drives reward noise, and that group-relative advantage estimation can amplify these signals. A unified framework to debias and denoise these signals is needed.
AI.method: Proposed DDRL framework: (1) frequency-based sampling to remove ambiguous samples while maintaining a balanced set of positive and negative examples; (2) debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages to eliminate bias from group-relative policy optimization; (3) consensus-based off-policy refinement leveraging the rejection-sampled dataset for stable, efficient model updates.
AI.result: Experiments on three large language models across several mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines.
AI.conclusion: DDRL effectively debiases and denoises test-time RL signals, improving stability and performance in TTRL; code will be released.

title: UHR-DETR: Efficient End-to-End Small Object Detection for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) imagery has become essential for modern remote sensing, offering unprecedented spatial coverage. However, detecting small objects in such vast scenes presents a critical dilemma: retaining the original resolution for small objects causes prohibitive memory bottlenecks. Conversely, conventional compromises like image downsampling or patch cropping either erase small objects or destroy context. To break this dilemma, we propose UHR-DETR, an efficient end-to-end transformer-based detector designed for UHR imagery. First, we introduce a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder that dynamically allocates finite computational resources to informative high-resolution regions, ensuring maximum object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. Second, we design a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder. By integrating macroscopic scene awareness with microscopic object details, this module resolves semantic ambiguities and prevents scene fragmentation. Extensive experiments on the UHR imagery datasets (e.g., STAR and SODA-A) demonstrate the superiority of UHR-DETR under strict hardware constraints (e.g., a single 24GB RTX 3090). It achieves a 2.8\% mAP improvement while delivering a 10$\times$ inference speedup compared to standard sliding-window baselines on the STAR dataset. Our codes and models will be available at https://github.com/Li-JingFang/UHR-DETR.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based detector for ultra-high-resolution (UHR) imagery, UHR-DETR, uses a Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder and a Global-Local Decoupled Decoder to efficiently detect small objects in large scenes, achieving notable accuracy and speed gains under strict hardware constraints.
AI.motivation: Detecting small objects in UHR scenes is hindered by memory bottlenecks when maintaining full-resolution inputs; naive downsampling or patch cropping loses small objects or context. A resource-efficient, end-to-end detector is needed to maximize object coverage while preserving context.
AI.method: 1) Coverage-Maximizing Sparse Encoder: dynamically allocates computation to informative high-resolution regions to maximize object coverage with minimal spatial redundancy. 2) Global-Local Decoupled Decoder: integrates macroscopic scene awareness with microscopic object details to resolve semantic ambiguities and prevent scene fragmentation.
AI.result: Experiments on UHR datasets (STAR, SODA-A) show superiority under hardware constraints (single 24GB RTX 3090). Achieves 2.8% mAP improvement and 10x faster inference than standard sliding-window baselines on STAR; code will be released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: UHR-DETR effectively addresses the memory-computation vs. accuracy trade-off in UHR object detection, offering both higher accuracy and substantial speedups, enabling practical deployment on commodity GPUs.

title: Symbolic Grounding Reveals Representational Bottlenecks in Abstract Visual Reasoning
summary: Vision--language models (VLMs) often fail on abstract visual reasoning benchmarks such as Bongard problems, raising the question of whether the main bottleneck lies in reasoning or representation. We study this on Bongard-LOGO, a synthetic benchmark of abstract concept learning with ground-truth generative programs, by comparing end-to-end VLMs on raw images with large language models (LLMs) given symbolic inputs derived from those images. Using symbolic inputs as a diagnostic probe rather than a practical multimodal architecture, our \emph{Componential--Grammatical (C--G)} paradigm reformulates Bongard-LOGO as a symbolic reasoning task based on LOGO-style action programs or structured descriptions. LLMs achieve large and consistent gains, reaching mid--90s accuracy on Free-form problems, while a strong visual baseline remains near chance under matched task definitions. Ablations on input format, explicit concept prompts, and minimal visual grounding show that these factors matter much less than the shift from pixels to symbolic structure. These results identify representation as a key bottleneck in abstract visual reasoning and show how symbolic input can serve as a controlled diagnostic upper bound.

AI.tldr: Symbolic reformulation of Bongard-LOGO (Componential–Grammatical) enables LLMs to outperform visual baselines by large margins, indicating representation bottleneck in abstract visual reasoning.
AI.motivation: To determine whether abstract reasoning limits arise from representation or reasoning, by isolating the input form using Bongard-LOGO with ground-truth generative programs.
AI.method: Propose C–G paradigm; recast Bongard-LOGO as symbolic reasoning using LOGO-like action programs or structured descriptions; compare end-to-end VLMs on raw images to LLMs given symbolic inputs; perform ablations on input format, prompts, grounding.
AI.result: LLMs reach mid-90s accuracy on Free-form problems; strong visual models near chance under matched task definitions; ablations show the shift to symbolic structure matters more than input specifics.
AI.conclusion: Representation is the key bottleneck; symbolic input provides a controlled diagnostic upper bound for abstract visual reasoning.

title: Sub-Token Routing in LoRA for Adaptation and Query-Aware KV Compression
summary: Sub-token routing offers a finer control axis for transformer efficiency than the coarse units used in most prior work, such as tokens, pages, heads, or layers. In this paper, we study routing within a token representation itself in LoRA-adapted transformers. The motivation is that a relevant token need not be internally uniform: under a retention budget, preserved value groups are distributed unevenly both across tokens and within tokens, which suggests that KV compression need not be an all-or-nothing decision at token level. We study this fine-grained routing mechanism in two settings. For compression-aware language modeling, we introduce a query-independent design that combines routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on the KV path. For downstream-task-preserving KV compression, we introduce a query-aware design in which a predictor-based selector allocates a global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance. Experiments show that the query-independent design improves the quality-compression tradeoff for language modeling, while the query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets. We further examine the relation between token-level and sub-token-level query-aware routing, and show that they form complementary compression axes: token-level methods determine which tokens survive globally, while sub-token routing determines how the surviving tokens are compressed internally.

AI.tldr: Sub-token routing inside token representations in LoRA-adapted transformers to enable finer-grained KV compression and selective retention under budgets; two designs: query-independent (routing subspaces + value-group routing) for language modeling, and query-aware (predictor-based selector over token/value-group pairs) for downstream-KV preservation; results show improved quality-compression tradeoffs and complementary axes between token-level and sub-token routing.
AI.motivation: KV compression and model efficiency often operate at coarse token-level units; preserved value groups are unevenly distributed across and within tokens, suggesting sub-token level routing can better allocate retention under a budget.
AI.method: Study two settings: (1) query-independent design combining routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on the KV path for compression-aware language modeling; (2) query-aware design with a predictor-based selector that allocates a global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance.
AI.result: Query-independent design improves the quality–compression tradeoff in language modeling; query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets; token-level and sub-token-level routing are complementary axes implying distinct global vs internal compression effects.
AI.conclusion: Sub-token routing provides finer control for transformer efficiency. Token-level routing determines surviving tokens globally, while sub-token routing dictates how surviving tokens are compressed internally; together they offer a richer, complementary compression strategy than token-level methods alone.

title: 2L-LSH: A Locality-Sensitive Hash Function-Based Method For Rapid Point Cloud Indexing
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Published in The Computer Journal
summary: The development of 3D scanning technology has enabled the acquisition of massive point cloud models with diverse structures and large scales, thereby presenting significant challenges in point cloud processing. Fast neighboring points search is one of the most common problems, which is frequently used in model reconstruction, classification, retrieval and feature visualization. Hash function is well known for its high-speed and accurate performance in searching high-dimensional data, which is also the core of the proposed 2L-LSH. Specifically, the 2L-LSH algorithm adopts a two-step hash function strategy, in which the popular step divides the bounding box of the point cloud model and the second step constructs a generalized table-based data structure. The proposed 2L-LSH offers a highly efficient and accurate solution for fast neighboring points search in large-scale 3D point cloud models, making it a promising technique for various applications in the field. The proposed algorithm is compared with the well-known methods including Kd-tree and Octree; the obtained results demonstrated that the proposed method outperforms Kd-tree and Octree in terms of speed, i.e. the time consumption of kNN search can be 51.111% and 94.159% lower than Kd-tree and Octree, respectively. And the RN search time can be 54.519% and 41.840% lower than Kd-tree and Octree, respectively.

AI.tldr: Introduces 2L-LSH, a two-step hashing-based method for fast kNN and range/nearest neighbor searches in large-scale 3D point clouds. It partitions the point cloud bounding box, then uses a generalized table-based data structure. Claims significant speedups over Kd-tree and Octree for kNN and range-nearest searches.
AI.motivation: Massive point clouds from 3D scanning pose efficiency challenges for neighbor search, a core operation in reconstruction, classification, retrieval, and visualization. Traditional spatial data structures (Kd-tree, Octree) may become bottlenecks on large datasets. A fast hashing-based approach could improve throughput.
AI.method: A two-step hash strategy: 1) partition the point cloud bounding box (step hashing), 2) construct a generalized table-based data structure to organize hashed cells for neighbor queries (2L-LSH).
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial speedups: kNN search times reduced by 51.111% vs Kd-tree and 94.159% vs Octree; range-nearest (RN) search times reduced by 54.519% vs Kd-tree and 41.840% vs Octree.
AI.conclusion: 2L-LSH provides a highly efficient and accurate solution for fast neighbor searches in large-scale 3D point clouds, with promising applicability to related tasks in 3D processing.

title: ReaGeo: Reasoning-Enhanced End-to-End Geocoding with LLMs
comment: 12 pages, 8 figures, submitted to ACM SIGSPATIAL 2024 (under review)
summary: This paper proposes ReaGeo, an end-to-end geocoding framework based on large language models, designed to overcome the limitations of traditional multi-stage approaches that rely on text or vector similarity retrieval over geographic databases, including workflow complexity, error propagation, and heavy dependence on structured geographic knowledge bases. The method converts geographic coordinates into geohash sequences, reformulating the coordinate prediction task as a text generation problem, and introduces a Chain-of-Thought mechanism to enhance the model's reasoning over spatial relationships. Furthermore, reinforcement learning with a distance-deviation-based reward is applied to optimize the generation accuracy. Comprehensive experiments show that ReaGeo can accurately handle explicit address queries in single-point predictions and effectively resolve vague relative location queries. In addition, the model demonstrates strong predictive capability for non-point geometric regions, highlighting its versatility and generalization ability in geocoding tasks.

AI.tldr: ReaGeo reframes geocoding as end-to-end text generation using geohash tokens, enabling single-point, relative, and non-point predictions with improved reasoning (Chain-of-Thought) and distance-deviation RL optimization, reducing pipeline complexity and KB dependence.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional multi-stage geocoding pipelines that rely on text or vector retrieval over geographic databases, which incur workflow complexity, error propagation, and heavy dependence on structured knowledge bases.
AI.method: Convert coordinates into geohash sequences and cast coordinate prediction as a text-generation problem. Employ a Chain-of-Thought mechanism to enhance spatial reasoning. Apply reinforcement learning with a distance-deviation-based reward to improve generation accuracy in an end-to-end LLM framework.
AI.result: Experimental results show accurate handling of explicit address queries in single-point predictions, effective resolution of vague relative location queries, and strong predictive capability for non-point geometric regions, indicating strong generalization and versatility in geocoding tasks.
AI.conclusion: An end-to-end LLM-based geocoding framework (ReaGeo) that reduces reliance on complex pipelines and knowledge bases, demonstrates robust performance across point, relative, and non-point geospatial tasks, and generalizes well to diverse geocoding scenarios.

title: Decoupled Travel Planning with Behavior Forest
summary: Behavior sequences, composed of executable steps, serve as the operational foundation for multi-constraint planning problems such as travel planning. In such tasks, each planning step is not only constrained locally but also influenced by global constraints spanning multiple subtasks, leading to a tightly coupled and complex decision process. Existing travel planning methods typically rely on a single decision space that entangles all subtasks and constraints, failing to distinguish between locally acting constraints within a subtask and global constraints that span multiple subtasks. Consequently, the model is forced to jointly reason over local and global constraints at each decision step, increasing the reasoning burden and reducing planning efficiency. To address this problem, we propose the Behavior Forest method. Specifically, our approach structures the decision-making process into a forest of parallel behavior trees, where each behavior tree is responsible for a subtask. A global coordination mechanism is introduced to orchestrate the interactions among these trees, enabling modular and coherent travel planning. Within this framework, large language models are embedded as decision engines within behavior tree nodes, performing localized reasoning conditioned on task-specific constraints to generate candidate subplans and adapt decisions based on coordination feedback. The behavior trees, in turn, provide an explicit control structure that guides LLM generation. This design decouples complex tasks and constraints into manageable subspaces, enabling task-specific reasoning and reducing the cognitive load of LLM. Experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 6.67% on the TravelPlanner and by 11.82% on the ChinaTravel benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness in increasing LLM performance for complex multi-constraint travel planning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Behavior Forest— a modular architecture using a forest of behavior trees with global coordination and LLM-based nodes to decouple local/global constraints in travel planning, yielding notable accuracy improvements on TravelPlanner and ChinaTravel benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the entangled single-decision-space approach in travel planning that jointly handles local and global constraints, increasing reasoning burden and reducing efficiency.
AI.method: Decompose tasks into parallel behavior trees (one per subtask) with a global coordinator; embed LLMs as decision engines inside tree nodes to generate subplans; coordination feedback aligns subtrees; trees provide explicit control structure to guide LLMs.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art by 6.67% on TravelPlanner and 11.82% on ChinaTravel benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Behavior Forest effectively decouples complex tasks, improves planning efficiency and LLM performance for multi-constraint travel planning.

title: VARestorer: One-Step VAR Distillation for Real-World Image Super-Resolution
comment: Accepted in ICLR 2026. Code is available at https://github.com/EternalEvan/VARestorer
summary: Recent advancements in visual autoregressive models (VAR) have demonstrated their effectiveness in image generation, highlighting their potential for real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR). However, adapting VAR for ISR presents critical challenges. The next-scale prediction mechanism, constrained by causal attention, fails to fully exploit global low-quality (LQ) context, resulting in blurry and inconsistent high-quality (HQ) outputs. Additionally, error accumulation in the iterative prediction severely degrades coherence in ISR task. To address these issues, we propose VARestorer, a simple yet effective distillation framework that transforms a pre-trained text-to-image VAR model into a one-step ISR model. By leveraging distribution matching, our method eliminates the need for iterative refinement, significantly reducing error propagation and inference time. Furthermore, we introduce pyramid image conditioning with cross-scale attention, which enables bidirectional scale-wise interactions and fully utilizes the input image information while adapting to the autoregressive mechanism. This prevents later LQ tokens from being overlooked in the transformer. By fine-tuning only 1.2\% of the model parameters through parameter-efficient adapters, our method maintains the expressive power of the original VAR model while significantly enhancing efficiency. Extensive experiments show that VARestorer achieves state-of-the-art performance with 72.32 MUSIQ and 0.7669 CLIPIQA on DIV2K dataset, while accelerating inference by 10 times compared to conventional VAR inference.

AI.tldr: VARestorer converts a pre-trained visual autoregressive model into a one-step image super-resolution model via distillation and pyramid cross-scale conditioning, achieving state-of-the-art DIV2K metrics with ~1.2% fine-tuning and ~10× faster inference.
AI.motivation: Address core drawbacks of visual autoregressive models in ISR: limited global context due to causal attention causing blur, and error accumulation from iterative generation, which harms coherence and efficiency.
AI.method: Distillation through distribution matching from a pre-trained text-to-image VAR to a one-step ISR model; introduce pyramid image conditioning with cross-scale attention for bidirectional scale interactions; fine-tune only 1.2% of parameters via adapters.
AI.result: On DIV2K, achieves 72.32 MUSIQ and 0.7669 CLIPIQA, with inference accelerated by about 10× compared to traditional VAR inference.
AI.conclusion: VARestorer effectively repurposes VAR for one-shot ISR with maintained model expressivity and substantially improved efficiency and output quality.

title: Time, Causality, and Observability Failures in Distributed AI Inference Systems
comment: 17 pages, 6 figures. Produced as part of the Unified Intelligent Infrastructure workstream at the Open Compute Project (OCP)
summary: Distributed AI inference pipelines rely heavily on timestamp-based observability to understand system behavior. This work demonstrates that even small clock skew between nodes can cause observability to become causally incorrect while the system itself remains functionally correct and performant. We present controlled experiments on a multi-node AI inference pipeline, where clock skew is introduced at a single stage. Results show that no violations are observed under synchronized conditions and up to 3 ms skew, while clear causality violations emerge by 5 ms. Despite this, system throughput and output correctness remain largely unaffected. We further observe that violation behavior is not strictly static. In longer runs, negative span rates may stabilize or decrease over time, indicating that effective skew evolves due to relative clock drift between nodes. Experiments were conducted using Kafka and ZeroMQ transports, with consistent results across both. Aeron is under active exploration but is not yet included in the completed validation set. These findings suggest that observability correctness depends not only on system functionality but also on precise time alignment, and that timing must be treated as a first-class concern in distributed AI systems.

AI.tldr: Small clock skew in distributed AI inference pipelines can render observability causally incorrect even when the system remains functionally correct and performant. Causality violations emerge around 5 ms skew; no violations up to 3 ms. Violation behavior evolves with time as clocks drift. Results consistent across Kafka and ZeroMQ; Aeron pending validation. Timing should be treated as a first-class concern in distributed AI systems.
AI.motivation: Investigate how clock skew affects observability and causal consistency in distributed AI inference pipelines, and determine the practical thresholds at which observability diverges from actual system behavior.
AI.method: Controlled experiments in a multi-node AI inference pipeline with skew introduced at a single stage. Agents use Kafka and ZeroMQ transports (Aeron explored but not validated). Measure causal observability violations (span/channel causality), throughput, and output correctness across varying skew (0–5+ ms). Analyze how violation rates evolve over long runs.
AI.result: Up to 3 ms skew, no observable causality violations; at 5 ms skew, clear causality violations appear while throughput and output correctness remain largely unaffected. Violation behavior is not static over longer runs; negative span rates may stabilize or decrease due to evolving effective skew from relative clock drift. Consistent results across Kafka and ZeroMQ transports; Aeron not yet validated.
AI.conclusion: Observability correctness is sensitive to precise time alignment and should be treated as a first-class concern in distributed AI systems. Clock synchronization and timestamp handling are critical for trustworthy observability; further validation across additional transports (Aeron) and potential countermeasures (e.g., skew compensation, logical clocks) are warranted.

title: mcdok at SemEval-2026 Task 13: Finetuning LLMs for Detection of Machine-Generated Code
summary: Multi-domain detection of the machine-generated code snippets in various programming languages is a challenging task. SemEval-2026 Task~13 copes with this challenge in various angles, as a binary detection problem as well as attribution of the source. Specifically, its subtasks also cover generator LLM family detection, as well as a hybrid code co-generated by humans and machines, or adversarially modified codes hiding its origin. Our submitted systems adjusted the existing mdok approach (focused on machine-generated text detection) to these specific kinds of problems by exploring various base models, more suitable for code understanding. The results indicate that the submitted systems are competitive in all three subtasks. However, the margins from the top-performing systems are significant, and thus further improvements are possible.

AI.tldr: Adapting machine-generated text detection (mdok) to multi-domain code: binary detection, attribution, and co-/adversarial code tasks across languages; competitive results but large gaps to top systems.
AI.motivation: Address the growing need to detect machine-generated code and attribute its source across languages, including hybrid and adversarially modified code, to ensure integrity, security, and provenance in code bases and education.
AI.method: Extend the mdok framework with code-aware base models and perform experiments on three subtasks: machine-generated vs human-written code (binary detection), generator-family attribution, and detection of co-generated/adversarial code.
AI.result: Submitted systems are competitive on all three subtasks but margins to the top-performing systems are substantial, indicating the need for further improvements.
AI.conclusion: Mdok-based adaptation to code is viable and competitive across domains, but there is significant room to improve performance, likely via stronger code-specific representations and task-specific strategies.

title: Instance-level Visual Active Tracking with Occlusion-Aware Planning
summary: Visual Active Tracking (VAT) aims to control cameras to follow a target in 3D space, which is critical for applications like drone navigation and security surveillance. However, it faces two key bottlenecks in real-world deployment: confusion from visually similar distractors caused by insufficient instance-level discrimination and severe failure under occlusions due to the absence of active planning. To address these, we propose OA-VAT, a unified pipeline with three complementary modules. First, a training-free Instance-Aware Offline Prototype Initialization aggregates multi-view augmented features via DINOv3 to construct discriminative instance prototypes, mitigating distractor confusion. Second, an Online Prototype Enhancement Tracker enhances prototypes online and integrates a confidence-aware Kalman filter for stable tracking under appearance and motion changes. Third, an Occlusion-Aware Trajectory Planner, trained on our new Planning-20k dataset, uses conditional diffusion to generate obstacle-avoiding paths for occlusion recovery. Experiments demonstrate OA-VAT achieves 0.93 average SR on UnrealCV (+2.2% vs. SOTA TrackVLA), 90.8% average CAR on real-world datasets (+12.1% vs. SOTA GC-VAT), and 81.6% TSR on a DJI Tello drone. Running at 35 FPS on an RTX 3090, it delivers robust, real-time performance for practical deployment.

AI.tldr: OA-VAT introduces a three-module visual active tracking pipeline to address distractor confusion and occlusions, combining training-free instance prototypes, online prototype enhancement with a confidence-aware Kalman filter, and a diffusion-based occlusion-aware trajectory planner; delivers state-of-the-art accuracy and real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Two key bottlenecks in practical Visual Active Tracking: (1) distractor confusion due to insufficient instance-level discrimination, and (2) occlusions due to lack of active planning.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) Instance-Aware Offline Prototype Initialization (training-free) that aggregates multi-view augmented features via DINOv3 to form discriminative instance prototypes; (2) Online Prototype Enhancement Tracker that updates prototypes online and uses a confidence-aware Kalman filter for stable tracking under appearance/motion changes; (3) Occlusion-Aware Trajectory Planner trained on a Planning-20k dataset that uses conditional diffusion to generate obstacle-avoiding paths for occlusion recovery.
AI.result: Quantitative performance: 0.93 average SR on UnrealCV (+2.2% vs. TrackVLA); 90.8% average CAR on real-world datasets (+12.1% vs. GC-VAT); 81.6% TSR on a DJI Tello drone. Runtime: 35 FPS on RTX 3090, enabling real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: OA-VAT demonstrates a robust, real-time solution for visual active tracking by integrating discriminative offline prototypes, online adaptation with Kalman-filter-based tracking, and diffusion-based planning to recover from occlusions, achieving state-of-the-art metrics on UnrealCV and real-world datasets.

title: SemanticAgent: A Semantics-Aware Framework for Text-to-SQL Data Synthesis
summary: Existing text-to-SQL synthesis pipelines still conflate executability with semantic validity: syntactic checks and execution-based validation can retain queries that execute successfully while violating database semantics. To address these limitations, we propose SemanticAgent, a semantic-aware synthesis framework. SemanticAgent organizes synthesis around three specialized modules: an analyzer, a synthesizer, and a verifier. Through a three-stage protocol of semantic analysis, stepwise synthesis, and diagnostic refinement, SemanticAgent transforms execution-based validation alone into a traceable reasoning process. Our framework generates synthetic data that consistently outperforms prior synthesis methods under semantic-quality evaluation, leading to stronger downstream fine-tuning performance, especially on semantically demanding benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SemanticAgent introduces a semantic-aware text-to-SQL synthesis pipeline with three specialized modules (analyzer, synthesizer, verifier) and a three-stage protocol (semantic analysis, stepwise synthesis, diagnostic refinement) that converts execution-based validation into traceable semantic reasoning, yielding higher semantic quality and better downstream fine-tuning performance on demanding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-SQL pipelines often equate executability with semantic validity, allowing semantically incorrect queries to pass checks if they execute. There is a need for semantic-aware verification that ensures database semantics are preserved.
AI.method: The framework composes three modules: an analyzer (semantic analysis and constraint extraction), a synthesizer (constructs queries guided by semantic constraints), and a verifier (semantic and diagnostic checks). It follows a three-stage protocol: semantic analysis, stepwise synthesis, and diagnostic refinement, turning execution traces into a traceable reasoning process and enabling semantic-aware data generation for fine-tuning.
AI.result: Synthetic data generated by SemanticAgent demonstrates superior semantic-quality evaluation compared with prior methods and yields stronger downstream fine-tuning performance, especially on semantically demanding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SemanticAgent provides a semantic-aware, traceable synthesis workflow that improves semantic validity of generated text-to-SQL queries and enhances downstream model training on challenging benchmarks; it reframes execution-based validation as part of a broader semantic reasoning process.

title: Channel-Free Human Activity Recognition via Inductive-Bias-Aware Fusion Design for Heterogeneous IoT Sensor Environments
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, Preprint. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Human activity recognition (HAR) in Internet of Things (IoT) environments must cope with heterogeneous sensor settings that vary across datasets, devices, body locations, sensing modalities, and channel compositions. This heterogeneity makes conventional channel-fixed models difficult to reuse across sensing environments because their input representations are tightly coupled to predefined channel structures. To address this problem, we investigate strict channel-free HAR, in which a single shared model performs inference without assuming a fixed number, order, or semantic arrangement of input channels, and without relying on sensor-specific input layers or dataset-specific channel templates. We argue that fusion design is the central issue in this setting. Accordingly, we propose a channel-free HAR framework that combines channel-wise encoding with a shared encoder, metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, and joint optimization of channel-level and fused predictions through a combination loss. The proposed model processes each channel independently to handle varying channel configurations, while sensor metadata such as body location, modality, and axis help recover structural information that channel-independent processing alone cannot retain. In addition, the joint loss encourages both the discriminability of individual channels and the consistency of the final fused prediction. Experiments on PAMAP2, together with robustness analysis on six HAR datasets, ablation studies, sensitivity analysis, efficiency evaluation, and cross-dataset transfer learning, demonstrate three main findings...

AI.tldr: Channel-free HAR framework that processes sensor channels independently with channel-wise encoding and metadata-conditioned fusion, achieving robust cross-dataset generalization and transfer learning.
AI.motivation: HAR in IoT is highly heterogeneous due to varying sensor configurations across datasets, devices, locations, modalities, and channels. Fixed-channel models struggle to generalize; a channel-free approach is needed to remove reliance on predefined channel structures.
AI.method: Per-channel encoding with a shared encoder, metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, and a joint combination loss that enforces both per-channel discriminability and final fusion consistency. Each channel is processed independently; sensor metadata (body location, modality, axis) provides structural context.
AI.result: Validation on PAMAP2 and six HAR datasets with ablation, sensitivity, efficiency, and cross-dataset transfer experiments showing robust performance under heterogeneous channel configurations and evidence of transferability.
AI.conclusion: A channel-free HAR framework with channel-wise processing and metadata-informed fusion enables robust, device-agnostic HAR across diverse channel configurations, with a joint loss framework that maintains per-channel discriminability while ensuring coherent fused predictions.

title: Do MLLMs Understand Pointing? Benchmarking and Enhancing Referential Reasoning in Egocentric Vision
summary: Egocentric AI agents, such as smart glasses, rely on pointing gestures to resolve referential ambiguities in natural language commands. However, despite advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), current systems often fail to precisely ground the spatial semantics of pointing. Instead, they rely on spurious correlations with visual proximity or object saliency, a phenomenon we term "Referential Hallucination." To address this gap, we introduce EgoPoint-Bench, a comprehensive question-answering benchmark designed to evaluate and enhance multimodal pointing reasoning in egocentric views. Comprising over 11k high-fidelity simulated and real-world samples, the benchmark spans five evaluation dimensions and three levels of referential complexity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that while state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models struggle with egocentric pointing, models fine-tuned on our synthetic data achieve significant performance gains and robust sim-to-real generalization. This work highlights the importance of spatially aware supervision and offers a scalable path toward precise egocentric AI assistants. Project page: https://guyyyug.github.io/EgoPoint-Bench/

AI.tldr: EgoPoint-Bench is a new benchmark for multimodal pointing in egocentric AI, with 11k+ samples, showing synthetic-data fine-tuning improves grounding and sim-to-real generalization, while existing models struggle with spatial grounding.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of precise spatial grounding for pointing actions in egocentric AI and the phenomenon of referential hallucination where models rely on proximity or saliency rather than true spatial semantics.
AI.method: Introduce EgoPoint-Bench comprising five evaluation dimensions and three levels of referential complexity, using over 11k high-fidelity simulated and real-world samples; evaluate both proprietary and open-source models; show that fine-tuning on synthetic data yields performance gains and robust sim-to-real generalization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models struggle with egocentric pointing; models fine-tuned on synthetic data achieve significant performance gains and better sim-to-real generalization.
AI.conclusion: Spatially aware supervision is essential for precise egocentric AI assistants; EgoPoint-Bench provides a scalable path to improve multimodal pointing grounding and evaluation.

title: FairQE: Multi-Agent Framework for Mitigating Gender Bias in Translation Quality Estimation
summary: Quality Estimation (QE) aims to assess machine translation quality without reference translations, but recent studies have shown that existing QE models exhibit systematic gender bias. In particular, they tend to favor masculine realizations in gender-ambiguous contexts and may assign higher scores to gender-misaligned translations even when gender is explicitly specified. To address these issues, we propose FairQE, a multi-agent-based, fairness-aware QE framework that mitigates gender bias in both gender-ambiguous and gender-explicit scenarios. FairQE detects gender cues, generates gender-flipped translation variants, and combines conventional QE scores with LLM-based bias-mitigating reasoning through a dynamic bias-aware aggregation mechanism. This design preserves the strengths of existing QE models while calibrating their gender-related biases in a plug-and-play manner. Extensive experiments across multiple gender bias evaluation settings demonstrate that FairQE consistently improves gender fairness over strong QE baselines. Moreover, under MQM-based meta-evaluation following the WMT 2023 Metrics Shared Task, FairQE achieves competitive or improved general QE performance. These results show that gender bias in QE can be effectively mitigated without sacrificing evaluation accuracy, enabling fairer and more reliable translation evaluation.

AI.tldr: FairQE is a plug-and-play fairness-aware QE framework that mitigates gender bias in quality estimation for MT by detecting gender cues, generating gender-flipped variants, and fusing traditional QE scores with LLM-based bias-mitigation via a dynamic aggregation mechanism, yielding improved gender fairness with no loss (or competitive) QE accuracy.
AI.motivation: Quality Estimation models exhibit systematic gender bias, especially favoring masculine realizations in gender-ambiguous contexts and misrating gender-misaligned translations. This undermines fairness and reliability in MT evaluation.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework (FairQE) that: (1) detects gender cues in MT outputs; (2) generates gender-flipped translation variants; (3) combines conventional QE scores with LLM-based bias-mitigating reasoning through a dynamic bias-aware aggregation module; designed to be plug-and-play with existing QE models.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent improvements in gender fairness over strong QE baselines across diverse gender-bias evaluation settings. Under MQM-based meta-evaluation (WMT 2023 Metrics Shared Task), FairQE achieves competitive or improved general QE performance, indicating no loss of QE accuracy while reducing gender bias.
AI.conclusion: Gender bias in QE can be effectively mitigated without sacrificing evaluation accuracy, enabling fairer and more reliable translation evaluation.

title: Relocation of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms and linear separability of datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$
summary:
Relocation of compact sets in an $n$-dimensional manifold by self-diffeomorphism is of its own interest as well as significant potential applications to data classification in data science. This paper presents a theory for relocating a finite number of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to be relocated to arbitrary target domains in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Furthermore, we prove that for any such collection, there exists a differentiable embedding into $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ such that their images become linearly separable.
  As applications of the established theory, we show that a finite number of compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable by width-$n$ deep neural networks (DNNs) with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, or SELU activation functions, under a mild condition. In addition, we show that any finite number of mutually disjoint compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ by a width-$(n+1)$ DNN.

AI.tldr: Finite collections of compact sets in R^n can be relocated by ambient diffeomorphisms to arbitrary target domains; they admit a differentiable embedding into R^{n+1} whose image is linearly separable. This yields ML consequences: finite datasets in R^n can be made linearly separable by width-n DNNs with Leaky-ReLU/ELU/SELU under mild conditions, and mutually disjoint datasets can be separated in R^{n+1} by width-(n+1) DNNs.
AI.motivation: Bridge differential topology with machine learning to achieve linear separability of finite datasets via geometric transformations and embeddings, providing a theoretical mechanism for data classification leveraging diffeomorphisms.
AI.method: Proves existence of ambient diffeomorphisms of R^n that relocate a finite number of compact sets to prescribed target domains. Proves existence of a differentiable embedding into R^{n+1} such that the images are linearly separable. Applies these constructions to show that finite datasets in R^n can be made linearly separable by width-n DNNs with certain activation functions under a mild condition, and that any finite number of pairwise disjoint compact datasets in R^n can be separated by a width-(n+1) DNN.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees for relocation and embedding leading to linear separability. Existence results linking topology with neural network representability, enabling claims about DNN width requirements for linear separability.
AI.conclusion: A geometric/topological framework is established for transforming data into separable configurations, with potential implications for ML model design and understanding of DNN capabilities. The abstract signals constructive directions but lacks explicit algorithms or bounds in its current form.

title: ID-Eraser: Proactive Defense Against Face Swapping via Identity Perturbation
summary: Deepfake technologies have rapidly advanced with modern generative AI, and face swapping in particular poses serious threats to privacy and digital security. Existing proactive defenses mostly rely on pixel-level perturbations, which are ineffective against contemporary swapping models that extract robust high-level identity embeddings. We propose ID-Eraser, a feature-space proactive defense that removes identifiable facial information to prevent malicious face swapping. By injecting learnable perturbations into identity embeddings and reconstructing natural-looking protection images through a Face Revive Generator (FRG), ID-Eraser produces visually realistic results for humans while rendering the protected identities unusable for Deepfake models. Experiments show that ID-Eraser substantially disrupts identity recognition across diverse face recognition and swapping systems under strict black-box settings, achieving the lowest Top-1 accuracy (0.30) with the best FID (1.64) and LPIPS (0.020). Compared with swaps generated from clean inputs, the identity similarity of protected swaps drops sharply to an average of 0.504 across five representative face swapping models. ID-Eraser further demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization, robustness to common distortions, and practical effectiveness on commercial APIs, reducing Tencent API similarity from 0.76 to 0.36.

AI.tldr: ID-Eraser is a feature-space proactive defense that perturbs identity embeddings and uses a Face Revive Generator to produce protection images, significantly reducing deepfake-based identity leakage while keeping visuals natural.
AI.motivation: Current defenses rely on pixel-level perturbations which fail against models that use robust high-level identity embeddings; need a defense that disrupts identity information in feature space.
AI.method: Inject learnable perturbations into identity embeddings and reconstruct protection images with a Face Revive Generator (FRG); aim to remove identifiable facial information while preserving human-perceived realism, evaluated under strict black-box settings across recognition and swapping models and commercial APIs.
AI.result: Substantially disrupts identity recognition: Top-1 accuracy 0.30; best FID 1.64; LPIPS 0.020. Protected swaps average identity similarity 0.504 across five swapping models; cross-dataset generalization and robustness to distortions; reduces Tencent API similarity from 0.76 to 0.36.
AI.conclusion: ID-Eraser demonstrates an effective feature-space proactive defense against modern deepfake face swapping, producing plausible visuals for humans while suppressing identity leakage; shows strong cross-dataset and API robustness, but invites further study on attacker adaptation and long-term efficacy.

title: Brief chatbot interactions produce lasting changes in human moral values
summary: Moral judgements form the foundation of human social behavior and societal systems. While Artificial Intelligence chatbots increasingly serve as personal advisors, their influence on moral judgments remains largely unexplored. Here, we examined whether directive AI conversations shift moral evaluations using a within-subject naturalistic paradigm. Fifty-three participants rated moral scenarios, then discussed four with a chatbot prompted to shift moral judgments and four with a control agent. The brief conversations induced significant directional shifts in moral judgments, accepting stricter standards as well as advocating greater leniency (ps < 0.05; Cohen's d = 0.735-1.576), with increasing strengths of this effect during a two-week follow-up (Cohen's d = 1.038-2.069). Critically, the control condition produced no changes, and the effects did not extend to punishment while participants remained unaware of the persuasive intent, and both agents were rated equally likable and convincing, suggesting a vulnerability to undetected and lasting manipulation of foundational moral values.

AI.tldr: Directive AI conversations can shift moral judgments in a within-subject design, with effects persisting and varying by scenario; control condition shows no change; results imply potential vulnerability to undetected manipulation.
AI.motivation: To assess whether directive prompts in AI conversations can systematically bias moral judgments, addressing ethical and societal implications of AI as a moral influence.
AI.method: Within-subject naturalistic design with 53 participants who rated moral scenarios, then discussed eight scenarios with two agents (four 'directive' chatbot prompts to shift judgments and four control agent prompts). Measurements taken pre-discussion, post-discussion, and at two-week follow-up. Punishment judgments were also assessed but did not show effects. Participants were unaware of persuasive intent; agents were matched on likability and convincingness.
AI.result: Significant directional shifts in moral judgments after chatbot discussion (ps<0.05; Cohen's d=0.735–1.576). Effects intensified at two-week follow-up (d=1.038–2.069). Control condition produced no changes. No effects observed for punitive judgments. Effects occurred despite equal-rated likability/convincingness and lack of awareness of manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Directive AI prompts can subtly and persistently alter foundational moral judgments in a naturalistic setting, raising ethical concerns about manipulation via AI. Safeguards, transparency, and replication across contexts are warranted.

title: Supervised Learning Has a Necessary Geometric Blind Spot: Theory, Consequences, and Minimal Repair
comment: 29 pages. Code: https://github.com/vishalstark512/PMH. Preprint, not peer-reviewed. Affiliation: KU Leuven, Belgium
summary:
We prove that empirical risk minimisation (ERM) imposes a necessary geometric constraint on learned representations: any encoder that minimises supervised loss must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity in directions that are label-correlated in training data but nuisance at test time. This is not a contingent failure of current methods; it is a mathematical consequence of the supervised objective itself. We call this the geometric blind spot of supervised learning (Theorem 1), and show it holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes.
  This single theorem unifies four lines of prior empirical work that were previously treated separately: non-robust predictive features, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff. In this framing, adversarial vulnerability is one consequence of a broader structural fact about supervised learning geometry.
  We introduce Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a diagnostic that measures the theorem's bounded quantity directly, and show why common alternatives miss the key failure mode. PGD adversarial training reaches Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 yet has the worst clean-input geometry (TDI 1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. TDI is the only metric that detects this dissociation because it measures isotropic path-length distortion -- the exact quantity Theorem 1 bounds.
  Across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones used by CLIP, DINO, and SAM, the blind spot is measurable and repairable. It is present at foundation-model scale, worsens monotonically across language-model sizes (blind-spot ratio 0.860 to 0.765 to 0.742 from 66M to 340M), and is amplified by task-specific ERM fine-tuning (+54%), while PMH repairs it by 11x with one additional training term whose Gaussian form Proposition 5 proves is the unique perturbation law that uniformly penalises the encoder Jacobian.

AI.tldr: ERM imposes a geometric blind spot: certain label-correlated directions in input space retain non-zero encoder Jacobian sensitivity at test time; introduces Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) to diagnose this; PMH training term repairs the geometry; effect observed across tasks and model scales.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified geometric explanation for a family of empirical robustness phenomena (non-robust features, texture bias, corruption fragility, robustness-accuracy tradeoff) under supervised learning, and to connect these to adversarial vulnerability.
AI.method: Proves a geometric bound (Theorem 1) on isotropic path-length distortion in learned representations; introduces Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) as a diagnostic aligned with the theorem; empirically compares methods (PGD adversarial training, PMH) using Jacobian Frobenius norms and TDI across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones used in CLIP, DINO, and SAM; demonstrates scale effects and a Gaussian perturbation law (Proposition 5) that uniformly penalises encoder Jacobian; reports metrics such as blind-spot ratio across model sizes.
AI.result: A mathematical explanation for a persistent geometric blind spot in supervised learning; TDI detects the dissociation between clean-input geometry and robustness that other metrics miss; adversarial training improves adversarial robustness but can worsen the clean-input geometry; PMH repairs the geometry by a factor of ~11x; the blind spot is measurable across diverse tasks and scales, including foundation-model scales.
AI.conclusion: Supervised learning with ERM imposes a fundamental geometric constraint on learned representations, causing a measurable blind spot linked to robustness trade-offs; the Trajectory Deviation Index provides a principled diagnostic, and a Gaussian perturbation term (PMH) offers a unique, scalable repair. The phenomenon is general across tasks and architectures and intensifies with model size; PMH appears as a principled remedy.

title: Rethinking Cross-Domain Evaluation for Face Forgery Detection with Semantic Fine-grained Alignment and Mixture-of-Experts
comment: The source code is available at https://github.com/Yuhan-Luo/Semantic-Fine-grained-Alignment-and-Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Nowadays, visual data forgery detection plays an increasingly important role in social and economic security with the rapid development of generative models. Existing face forgery detectors still can't achieve satisfactory performance because of poor generalization ability across datasets. The key factor that led to this phenomenon is the lack of suitable metrics: the commonly used cross-dataset AUC metric fails to reveal an important issue where detection scores may shift significantly across data domains. To explicitly evaluate cross-domain score comparability, we propose \textbf{Cross-AUC}, an evaluation metric that can compute AUC across dataset pairs by contrasting real samples from one dataset with fake samples from another (and vice versa). It is interesting to find that evaluating representative detectors under the Cross-AUC metric reveals substantial performance drops, exposing an overlooked robustness problem. Besides, we also propose the novel framework \textbf{S}emantic \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{A}lignment and \textbf{M}ixture-of-Experts (\textbf{SFAM}), consisting of a patch-level image-text alignment module that enhances CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts, and the facial region mixture-of-experts module, which routes features from different facial regions to specialized experts for region-aware forgery analysis. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments on the public datasets prove that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared with the state-of-the-art methods with various suitable metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cross-AUC for cross-dataset score comparability and SFAM for robust face forgery detection; Cross-AUC reveals score shifts across domains; SFAM achieves superior results on public datasets.
AI.motivation: Cross-dataset AUC fails to reveal score shifts and cross-domain comparability; robust detectors require evaluation that captures score distribution shifts across datasets and forgery types.
AI.method: Cross-AUC computes AUC by cross-dataset pairing of real samples from one dataset with fake samples from another (and vice versa). SFAM combines a patch-level image-text alignment module to enhance CLIP's sensitivity to manipulation artifacts with a facial region mixture-of-experts that routes features from different facial regions to specialized experts for region-aware forgery analysis.
AI.result: Cross-AUC reveals substantial performance drops for representative detectors, exposing a robustness problem. SFAM achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art methods across various metrics on public datasets.
AI.conclusion: Cross-AUC is a valuable metric for evaluating cross-domain robustness in face forgery detection; SFAM provides improved accuracy and robustness against forgery artifacts.

title: HiCrew: Hierarchical Reasoning for Long-Form Video Understanding via Question-Aware Multi-Agent Collaboration
summary: Long-form video understanding remains fundamentally challenged by pervasive spatiotemporal redundancy and intricate narrative dependencies that span extended temporal horizons. While recent structured representations compress visual information effectively, they frequently sacrifice temporal coherence, which is critical for causal reasoning. Meanwhile, existing multi-agent frameworks operate through rigid, pre-defined workflows that fail to adapt their reasoning strategies to question-specific demands. In this paper, we introduce HiCrew, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that addresses these limitations through three core contributions. First, we propose a Hybrid Tree structure that leverages shot boundary detection to preserve temporal topology while performing relevance-guided hierarchical clustering within semantically coherent segments. Second, we develop a Question-Aware Captioning mechanism that synthesizes intent-driven visual prompts to generate precision-oriented semantic descriptions. Third, we integrate a Planning Layer that dynamically orchestrates agent collaboration by adaptively selecting roles and execution paths based on question complexity. Extensive experiments on EgoSchema and NExT-QA validate the effectiveness of our approach, demonstrating strong performance across diverse question types with particularly pronounced gains in temporal and causal reasoning tasks that benefit from our hierarchical structure-preserving design.

AI.tldr: HiCrew presents a hierarchical, multi-agent framework for long-form video understanding, integrating a Hybrid Tree to preserve temporal topology, a Question-Aware Captioning module for intent-driven prompts, and a Planning Layer for adaptive agent collaboration; validated on EgoSchema and NExT-QA with strong gains in temporal and causal reasoning.
AI.motivation: Long-form video understanding is hampered by spatiotemporal redundancy and loss of temporal coherence; fixed, pre-defined multi-agent workflows fail to adapt to diverse questions.
AI.method: Hybrid Tree structure using shot boundary detection for temporal topology and relevance-guided hierarchical clustering within segments; Question-Aware Captioning to generate precision-oriented semantic prompts; Planning Layer to dynamically assign roles and paths based on question complexity.
AI.result: Empirical validation on EgoSchema and NExT-QA shows robust performance across diverse question types, with pronounced improvements in temporal and causal reasoning due to hierarchical, topology-preserving design.
AI.conclusion: The HiCrew framework effectively balances temporal structure preservation with semantic precision and adaptive planning, enabling improved long-form video understanding through hierarchical, flexible multi-agent reasoning.

title: Even More Guarantees for Variational Inference in the Presence of Symmetries
comment: Accepted for presentation at the OPTIMAL Workshop at AISTATS 2026
summary: When approximating an intractable density via variational inference (VI) the variational family is typically chosen as a simple parametric family that very likely does not contain the target. This raises the question: Under which conditions can we recover characteristics of the target despite misspecification? In this work, we extend previous results on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries. We derive sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact recovery of the mean when using the forward Kullback-Leibler divergence and $α$-divergences. We further show how and why optimization can fail to recover the target mean in the absence of our sufficient conditions, providing initial guidelines on the choice of the variational family and $α$-value.

AI.tldr: Under misspecified VI with location-scale variational families and target symmetries, the authors identify conditions under which the mean of the target is exactly recovered when using forward KL or α-divergences; they also show failure modes when conditions fail and provide practical guidance on variational family choice and α values.
AI.motivation: VI often uses simple variational families that miss the true target. The work extends robust VI results to location-scale families with symmetry constraints to understand when mean recovery is possible despite misspecification.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis deriving sufficient conditions for exact mean recovery under forward KL and α-divergences; characterization of when optimization may fail to recover the target mean; analysis connects target symmetries with the geometry of the variational family.
AI.result: Sufficient conditions are established guaranteeing exact recovery of the target mean under forward KL and α-divergences. The paper also demonstrates that without these conditions, optimization may fail to recover the mean, and provides initial guidelines for choosing the variational family and α-values.
AI.conclusion: Exact mean recovery is possible under specific symmetry and family-structure conditions for robust VI with certain divergences; misspecification can hinder recovery, highlighting the need for careful choice of variational family and divergence parameterization.

title: Frozen LLMs as Map-Aware Spatio-Temporal Reasoners for Vehicle Trajectory Prediction
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities and attracted increasing research attention in the field of autonomous driving (AD). However, safe application of LLMs on AD perception and prediction still requires a thorough understanding of both the dynamic traffic agents and the static road infrastructure. To this end, this study introduces a framework to evaluate the capability of LLMs in understanding the behaviors of dynamic traffic agents and the topology of road networks. The framework leverages frozen LLMs as the reasoning engine, employing a traffic encoder to extract spatial-level scene features from observed trajectories of agents, while a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) encodes the local high-definition (HD) maps. To assess the intrinsic reasoning ability of LLMs, the extracted scene features are then transformed into LLM-compatible tokens via a reprogramming adapter. By residing the prediction burden with the LLMs, a simpler linear decoder is applied to output future trajectories. The framework enables a quantitative analysis of the influence of multi-modal information, especially the impact of map semantics on trajectory prediction accuracy, and allows seamless integration of frozen LLMs with minimal adaptation, thereby demonstrating strong generalizability across diverse LLM architectures and providing a unified platform for model evaluation.

AI.tldr: A framework evaluates LLMs' intrinsic reasoning about dynamic traffic agents and road network topology in autonomous driving by converting observed traffic trajectories and local HD-map semantics into LLM-ready tokens via a reprogramming adapter, using frozen LLMs for reasoning and a lightweight decoder for future trajectories; it enables quantitative analysis of multi-modal information impact and generalizes across LLM architectures.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how well frozen LLMs can reason about dynamic agents and static infrastructure in autonomous driving, ensuring safe practical deployment by analyzing the contribution of map semantics and other modalities to trajectory prediction.
AI.method: Extract spatial features from observed trajectories with a traffic encoder; encode local HD maps with a lightweight CNN; transform scene features into LLM-compatible tokens via a reprogramming adapter; keep LLMs frozen and use a simple linear decoder to predict future trajectories.
AI.result: The framework provides a unified platform to evaluate LLM reasoning on AD perception/prediction, enabling quantitative assessment of multi-modal information impact (especially map semantics) and demonstrating generalizability across diverse LLM architectures with minimal adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Frozen LLMs can serve as a reasoning engine for AD perception/prediction when augmented with modality-specific encoders and a reprogramming adapter, resulting in a generalizable, minimally invasive evaluation platform for LLMs in AD.

title: AI-Gram: When Visual Agents Interact in a Social Network
summary: We present AI-Gram, a live platform enabling image-based interactions, to study social dynamics in a fully autonomous multi-agent visual network where all participants are LLM-driven agents. Using the platform, we conduct experiments on how agents communicate and adapt through visual media, and observe the spontaneous emergence of visual reply chains, indicating rich communicative structure. At the same time, agents exhibit aesthetic sovereignty resisting stylistic convergence toward social partners, anchoring under adversarial influence, and a decoupling between visual similarity and social ties. These results reveal a fundamental asymmetry in current agent architectures: strong expressive communication paired with a steadfast preservation of individual visual identity. We release AI-Gram as a publicly accessible, continuously evolving platform for studying social dynamics in Al-native multi-agent systems. https://ai-gram.ai/
