AI.tldr: AI-Gram enables autonomous multi-agent image-based interactions; visual communication emerges and agents preserve individual identity, revealing asymmetry between expressive capacity and stylistic convergence; platform for studying AI-native social dynamics.
AI.motivation: understand how autonomous agents communicate via visual media, how social structures form without human mediation, and how identity persists under influence.
AI.method: build live platform with LLM-driven agents exchanging images; run experiments to observe visual reply chains, aesthetic independence, and decoupling of visual similarity from social ties; enable adversarial stimuli to test stability.
AI.result: emergent visual reply chains; strong expressive communication; resistance to stylistic convergence; identity anchoring under adversarial influence; decoupling between visual similarity and social ties.
AI.conclusion: strong asymmetry: expressive ability coexists with persistent individual visual identity; AI-Gram provides a public, evolving testbed for AI-native social dynamics in multi-agent systems.

title: A Green-Integral-Constrained Neural Solver with Stochastic Physics-Informed Regularization
summary: Standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) struggle to simulate highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media because pointwise minimization of second-order PDE residuals is computationally expensive, biased toward smooth solutions, and requires artificial absorbing boundary layers to restrict the solution. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for the acoustic Helmholtz equation. It departs from the PDE-residual-based formulation by enforcing wave physics through an integral representation that imposes a nonlocal constraint. Oscillatory behavior and outgoing radiation are encoded directly through the integral kernel, eliminating second-order spatial derivatives and enforcing physical solutions without additional boundary layers. Theoretically, optimizing this GI loss via a neural network acts as a spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration, enabling convergence in heterogeneous media where the classical Born series diverges. By exploiting FFT-based convolution to accelerate the GI loss evaluation, our approach substantially reduces GPU memory usage and training time. However, this efficiency relies on a fixed regular grid, which can limit local resolution. To improve local accuracy in strong scattering regions, we also propose a hybrid GI+PDE loss, enforcing a lightweight Helmholtz residual at a small number of nonuniformly sampled collocation points. We evaluate our method on seismic benchmark models characterized by structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity at frequencies up to 20Hz. GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over a factor of ten. In models with localized scattering, the hybrid loss yields the most accurate reconstructions, providing a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative.

AI.tldr: Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for acoustic Helmholtz uses a nonlocal integral representation to enforce wave physics, avoiding second-order PDE residuals and absorbing layers; FFT-accelerated GI loss yields substantial speedups and reduced memory, with a hybrid GI+PDE loss improving local accuracy in strong scattering. Outperforms PDE-based PINNs in oscillatory, heterogeneous media; validated on seismic benchmarks up to 20 Hz.
AI.motivation: To address the shortcomings of standard PINNs in high-frequency Helmholtz problems—namely expensive residual minimization, bias toward smooth solutions, and reliance on artificial absorbing boundaries—by introducing a nonlocal, physics-encoded integral formulation that naturally enforces oscillatory behavior and radiation.
AI.method: Replace PDE residual loss with a Green's integral loss that imposes a nonlocal constraint via an integral kernel encoding oscillations and outgoing radiation; leverage FFT-based convolution to accelerate GI loss; interpret optimization as a spectrally-tuned preconditioned iteration; introduce a hybrid GI+PDE loss at a small set of nonuniform collocation points to improve local accuracy in regions with strong scattering.
AI.result: GI-based training consistently reduces computational cost by more than an order of magnitude compared to PDE-based PINNs; the method remains stable in heterogeneous media where classical Born series diverges; FFT acceleration lowers GPU memory usage and training time; the hybrid GI+PDE loss yields the most accurate reconstructions in localized scattering scenarios; demonstrated on seismic benchmark models with contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity up to 20 Hz.
AI.conclusion: GI-based PINNs offer a scalable, physically grounded alternative for high-frequency Helmholtz problems in heterogeneous media, achieving substantial efficiency gains and improved accuracy with a natural nonlocal formulation; the fixed-grid limitation can be mitigated by hybridizing with selective PDE residuals for local refinement, suggesting a practical path for seismic imaging and related wave-inversion tasks.

title: VFM$^{4}$SDG: Unveiling the Power of VFMs for Single-Domain Generalized Object Detection
summary: In real-world scenarios, continual changes in weather, illumination, and imaging conditions cause significant domain shifts, leading detectors trained on a single source domain to degrade severely in unseen environments. Existing single-domain generalized object detection (SDGOD) methods mainly rely on data augmentation or domain-invariant representation learning, but pay limited attention to detector mechanisms, leaving clear limitations under complex domain shifts. Through analytical experiments, we find that performance degradation is dominated by increasing missed detections, which fundamentally arises from reduced cross-domain stability of the detector: object-background and inter-instance relations become less stable in the encoding stage, while semantic-spatial alignment of query representations also becomes harder to maintain in the decoding stage. To this end, we propose VFM$^{4}$SDG, a dual-prior learning framework for SDGOD, which introduces a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) as a transferable cross-domain stability prior into detector representation learning and query modeling. In the encoding stage, we propose Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to enhance the robustness of object-background and inter-instance relational modeling. In the decoding stage, we propose Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement, which injects category-level semantic prototypes and global visual context into queries to improve their semantic recognition and spatial localization stability in unseen domains. Extensive experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing SOTA methods on standard SDGOD benchmarks and two mainstream DETR-based detectors, demonstrating its effectiveness, robustness, and generality.

AI.tldr: A dual-prior learning framework (VFM^4SDG) uses a frozen vision foundation model as transferable cross-domain stability priors to improve SDGOD by strengthening encoding-relational stability and decoding-query semantic/contextual stability, achieving SOTA performance on SDGOD benchmarks and DETR-based detectors.
AI.motivation: Single-source detectors suffer from domain shifts due to unstable object-background, inter-instance, and query representations; existing SDGOD methods rely mainly on data augmentation or invariant features, neglecting detector mechanics.
AI.method: Introduce a frozen vision foundation model as cross-domain stability prior. Encoding stage uses Cross-domain Stable Relational Prior Distillation to reinforce object-background and inter-instance relations. Decoding stage uses Semantic-Contextual Prior-based Query Enhancement to inject category prototypes and global visual context into queries, improving semantic recognition and spatial localization in unseen domains.
AI.result: Method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SDGOD methods and shows robustness across two mainstream DETR-based detectors on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating foundation-model priors with detector learning yields enhanced cross-domain stability and reduced missed detections, offering a generalizable strategy for SDGOD.

title: Efficient Agent Evaluation via Diversity-Guided User Simulation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as customer-facing agents, yet evaluating their reliability remains challenging due to stochastic, multi-turn interactions. Current evaluation protocols rely on linear Monte Carlo rollouts of complete agent-user conversations to estimate success. However, this approach is computationally inefficient, repeatedly regenerating identical early prefixes, and often fails to uncover deep failure modes that arise from rare user behaviors.
  We introduce DIVERT (Diversity-Induced Evaluation via Branching of Trajectories), an efficient, snapshot-based, coverage-guided user simulation framework for systematic exploration of agent-user interactions. DIVERT captures the full agent-environment state at critical decision points and resumes execution from these snapshots, enabling reuse of shared conversation prefixes and reducing redundant computation. From each junction, the framework branches using targeted, diversity-inducing user responses, allowing directed exploration of alternative interaction paths.
  By focusing evaluation on semantically diverse and underexplored trajectories, DIVERT improves both efficiency and coverage. Empirical results show that it discovers more failures per token compared to standard linear rollout protocols, while expanding the set of tasks on which failures are identified.

AI.tldr: DIVERT is a snapshot-based, diversity-driven evaluation framework for LLMs that reuses shared prefixes and branches at decision points to efficiently explore agent–user interactions and uncover failures.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation relies on linear Monte Carlo rollouts of complete conversations, which are computationally expensive and often miss rare failure modes from diverse user behaviors.
AI.method: Capture full agent–environment state at critical decision points; resume from snapshots; branch with diverse, targeted user responses to explore alternative paths; focus on semantically diverse and underexplored trajectories.
AI.result: DIVERT yields more failures per token than standard linear rollouts and broadens the set of tasks where failures are identified, while improving efficiency and coverage.
AI.conclusion: DIVERT provides an efficient, coverage-guided evaluation approach that better reveals failure modes in multi-turn LLM interactions.

title: Gmd: Gaussian mixture descriptor for pair matching of 3D fragments
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures. Published in Multimedia Systems
summary: In the automatic reassembly of fragments acquired using laser scanners to reconstruct objects, a crucial step is the matching of fractured surfaces. In this paper, we propose a novel local descriptor that uses the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to fit the distribution of points, allowing for the description and matching of fractured surfaces of fragments. Our method involves dividing a local surface patch into concave and convex regions for estimating the k value of GMM. Then the final Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) of the fractured surface is formed by merging the regional GMDs. To measure the similarities between GMDs for determining adjacent fragments, we employ the L2 distance and align the fragments using Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) and Iterative Closest Point (ICP). The extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach; furthermore, the comparisons with several existing methods also validate the advantage of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A Gaussian Mixture Descriptor (GMD) for fracture surface matching in automatic fragment reassembly, merging regional GMDs and using L2 with RANSAC/ICP for alignment; validated on public and Terracotta datasets.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust, discriminative local descriptor for matching fractured surfaces in 3D fragment reassembly, addressing noise, partial overlaps, and variability in surface geometry.
AI.method: Divide local surface patch into concave and convex regions to estimate the number of GMM components (k). Fit Gaussian Mixture Models to regional distributions, then merge regional GMDs into a final descriptor. Measure similarity between GMDs via L2 distance and align fragments using RANSAC for initial pose estimation and ICP for refinement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-scanned public datasets and Terracotta datasets demonstrate effectiveness. Comparisons with several existing methods validate the proposed approach's advantage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Gaussian Mixture Descriptor effectively describes and matches fractured surfaces for automatic reassembly. The regional GMDs aggregation, combined with robust alignment (RANSAC+ICP), yields improved performance over existing methods and is applicable to laser-scanned fragment data.

title: How English Print Media Frames Human-Elephant Conflicts in India
summary: Human-elephant conflict (HEC) is rising across India as habitat loss and expanding human settlements force elephants into closer contact with people. While the ecological drivers of conflict are well-studied, how the news media portrays them remains largely unexplored. This work presents the first large-scale computational analysis of media framing of HEC in India, examining 1,968 full-length news articles consisting of 28,986 sentences, from a major English-language outlet published between January 2022 and September 2025. Using a multi-model sentiment framework that combines long-context transformers, large language models, and a domain-specific Negative Elephant Portrayal Lexicon, we quantify sentiment, extract rationale sentences, and identify linguistic patterns that contribute to negative portrayals of elephants. Our findings reveal a dominance of fear-inducing and aggression-related language. Since the media framing can shape public attitudes toward wildlife and conservation policy, such narratives risk reinforcing public hostility and undermining coexistence efforts. By providing a transparent, scalable methodology and releasing all resources through an anonymized repository, this study highlights how Web-scale text analysis can support responsible wildlife reporting and promote socially beneficial media practices.

AI.tldr: First large-scale computational study of Indian media framing of human-elephant conflict; analyzes 1,968 English news articles using a hybrid sentiment framework (long-context transformers, LLMs) and a domain-specific Negative Elephant Portrayal Lexicon to quantify sentiment, extract rationale sentences, and identify linguistic patterns; finds fear/aggression-dominated framing with implications for attitudes and conservation policy; provides a transparent, scalable methodology and open resources.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in understanding how media framing shapes public attitudes toward wildlife and conservation in India, highlighting potential consequences for coexistence policies.
AI.method: Data: 1,968 full-length English-language articles (28,986 sentences) from January 2022 to September 2025. Methods: a multi-model sentiment framework combining long-context transformer models, large language models, and a domain-specific Negative Elephant Portrayal Lexicon to quantify sentiment, extract rationale sentences, and identify linguistic patterns; results and resources are released through an anonymized repository.
AI.result: Findings show dominance of fear-inducing and aggression-related language in coverage; such framing risks reinforcing hostility toward elephants and undermining coexistence efforts; demonstrates a transparent, scalable methodology for web-scale text analysis and wildlife reporting.
AI.conclusion: Media framing has potential to shape attitudes and conservation outcomes; emphasizes the need for responsible wildlife reporting and provides a replicable framework and resources to support ongoing monitoring and improvement of media practices.

title: Conditional anomaly detection with soft harmonic functions
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM 2011). 10.1109/ICDM.2011.40
summary: In this paper, we consider the problem of conditional anomaly detection that aims to identify data instances with an unusual response or a class label. We develop a new non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection based on the soft harmonic solution, with which we estimate the confidence of the label to detect anomalous mislabeling. We further regularize the solution to avoid the detection of isolated examples and examples on the boundary of the distribution support. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method on several synthetic and UCI ML datasets in detecting unusual labels when compared to several baseline approaches. We also evaluate the performance of our method on a real-world electronic health record dataset where we seek to identify unusual patient-management decisions.

AI.tldr: A non-parametric conditional anomaly detector using a soft harmonic solution to estimate label confidence, with regularization to avoid false positives at boundaries or isolated points; shows superior detection of unusual labels on synthetic, UCI, and EHR data.
AI.motivation: Identify data instances with unusual responses/labels and detect mislabeling; improve reliability of label information in contexts such as electronic health records.
AI.method: Non-parametric conditional anomaly detection via a soft harmonic solution; estimate per-instance label confidence; regularize to suppress detections of isolated examples and boundary points.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and UCI datasets shows improvements over several baselines; a real-world EHR dataset demonstrates usefulness in identifying unusual patient-management decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a robust, non-parametric framework for conditional anomaly detection of label anomalies, with regularization improving robustness by avoiding detections of boundary and isolated cases; applicable to diverse domains including healthcare.

title: Seeing Isn't Believing: Uncovering Blind Spots in Evaluator Vision-Language Models
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly used to evaluate outputs of other models, for image-to-text (I2T) tasks such as visual question answering, and text-to-image (T2I) generation tasks. Despite this growing reliance, the reliability of these Evaluator VLMs remains under explored. In this work, we systematically evaluate the reliability of Evaluator VLMs across both I2T and T2I tasks. We introduce targeted perturbations that degrade output quality along key error dimensions, including object hallucinations, spatial reasoning, factual grounding, and visual fidelity. These perturbations test whether Evaluator VLMs can reliably account for these quality degrading errors in their evaluations. Using a comprehensive benchmark of over 4000 perturbed instances spanning 40 perturbation dimensions, we evaluate 4 prominent VLMs using single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided paradigms. Our findings reveal that current VLM evaluators exhibit substantial blind spots: they often fail to detect perturbed outputs - in some cases exceeding 50%, struggle particularly with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors, and are often insensitive to hallucinated content that contradicts the input image. Pairwise comparison proves more reliable, though failure rates persist. These results highlight the unreliable nature of current Evaluator VLMs and urge caution in their deployment for benchmarking and development decisions. Code and data have been made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Evaluator Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for image-to-text (I2T) and text-to-image (T2I) tasks show limited reliability. Using 4000+ perturbed instances across 40 error dimensions, four VLM evaluators often miss perturbations, especially fine-grained spatial/compositional errors and hallucinations. Pairwise comparisons are more reliable than single-answer or reference-guided scoring, but still imperfect.
AI.motivation: As VLMs increasingly serve as evaluators for model outputs, it is critical to quantify their robustness to systematic error modes and ensure trustworthy benchmarking.
AI.method: Design targeted perturbations that induce object hallucinations, spatial reasoning errors, factual grounding mistakes, and reduced visual fidelity. Assemble a benchmark with 4000+ perturbed instances across 40 perturbation dimensions. Evaluate 4 prominent VLMs using three evaluation paradigms: single-answer scoring, pairwise comparison, and reference-guided scoring.
AI.result: Evaluator VLMs exhibit substantial blind spots, with perturbations often undetected (in some cases >50%). They struggle with fine-grained compositional and spatial errors and may overlook hallucinations that contradict the input image. Pairwise comparison is more reliable than single-answer or reference-based scoring, yet still yields non-negligible failure rates.
AI.conclusion: Current Evaluator VLMs are unreliable for benchmarking and development decisions. Caution is warranted in their deployment; the authors provide code and data to enable reproducibility and further analysis.

title: GeoMind: An Agentic Workflow for Lithology Classification with Reasoned Tool Invocation
summary: Lithology classification in well logs is a fundamental geoscience data mining task that aims to infer rock types from multi dimensional geophysical sequences. Despite recent progress, existing approaches typically formulate the problem as a static, single-step discriminative mapping. This static paradigm limits evidence-based diagnostic reasoning against geological standards, often yielding predictions that are detached from geological reality due to a lack of domain priors. In this work, we propose GeoMind, a tool-augmented agentic framework that models lithology classification as a sequential reasoning process. GeoMind organizes its toolkit into perception, reasoning, and analysis modules, which respectively translate raw logs into semantic trends, infer lithology hypotheses from multi-source evidence, and verify predictions against stratigraphic constraints. A global planner adaptively coordinates these modules based on input characteristics, enabling geologically plausible and evidence-grounded decisions. To guarantee the logical consistency of GeoMind, we introduce a fine-grained process supervision strategy. Unlike standard methods that focus solely on final outcomes, our approach optimizes intermediate reasoning steps, ensuring the validity of decision trajectories and alignment to geological constraints. Experiments on four benchmark well-log datasets demonstrate that GeoMind consistently outperforms strong baselines in classification performance while providing transparent and traceable decision-making processes.

AI.tldr: GeoMind reframes lithology classification as a sequential, tool-augmented reasoning process that achieves more geology-consistent, evidence-grounded predictions than static baselines.
AI.motivation: Static single-step lithology mapping overlooks geological priors and path-dependent evidence; there is a need for interpretable, evidence-based reasoning that respects stratigraphic constraints.
AI.method: A modular architecture (perception, reasoning, analysis) with a global planner coordinating modules and a fine-grained supervision strategy targeting intermediate reasoning steps to ensure consistency with geological constraints.
AI.result: On four benchmark well-log datasets, GeoMind outperforms strong baselines in classification accuracy while delivering transparent, traceable decision trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented agentic reasoning can yield geologically plausible, explainable lithology classification, suggesting broader applicability to data-rich geoscience tasks.

title: Dynamical Priors as a Training Objective in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Supplementary material can be accessed here: https://github.com/drsukeshs/esd-rl
summary: Standard reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes policies for reward but imposes few constraints on how decisions evolve over time. As a result, policies may achieve high performance while exhibiting temporally incoherent behavior such as abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity. We introduce Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL), a training framework that augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis. Without modifying the reward, environment, or policy architecture, this prior shapes the temporal evolution of action probabilities during learning. Across three minimal environments, we show that dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing. These results demonstrate that training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.

AI.tldr: DP-RL introduces a dynamical prior as an auxiliary loss to policy-gradient RL that enforces evidence-accumulation and hysteresis in action probabilities, shaping temporal decision trajectories without changing reward, environment, or policy architecture.
AI.motivation: Standard RL optimizes for reward and often yields temporally incoherent behavior (abrupt shifts, oscillations, inactivity). There is a need to impose temporal structure on decision-making without altering the core RL setup.
AI.method: Add an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics implementing evidence accumulation and hysteresis to the policy gradient objective. This dynamical prior biases the evolution of action probabilities over time during training, while keeping the reward, environment, and policy architecture unchanged.
AI.result: In three minimal environments, dynamical priors systematically modify decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior beyond what simple smoothing would yield. The results show that training objectives can control the temporal geometry of decisions in RL agents.
AI.conclusion: Training objectives alone can shape the temporal evolution of decisions in RL, offering a general mechanism to impose temporal structure via dynamical priors without modifying the environment or reward.

title: Attention-based multiple instance learning for predominant growth pattern prediction in lung adenocarcinoma wsi using foundation models
summary: Lung adenocarcinoma (LUAD) grading depends on accurately identifying growth patterns, which are indicators of prognosis and can influence treatment decisions. Common deep learning approaches to determine the predominant pattern rely on patch-level classification or segmentation, requiring extensive annotations. This study proposes an attention-based multiple instance learning (ABMIL) framework to predict the predominant LUAD growth pattern at the whole slide level to reduce annotation burden. Our approach integrates pretrained pathology foundation models as patch encoders, used either frozen or fine-tuned on annotated patches, to extract discriminative features that are aggregated through attention mechanisms. Experiments show that fine-tuned encoders improve performance, with Prov-GigaPath achieving the highest agreement (\k{appa} = 0.699) under ABMIL. Compared to simple patch-aggregation baselines, ABMIL yields more robust predictions by leveraging slide-level supervision and spatial attention. Future work will extend this framework to estimate the full distribution of growth patterns and validate performance on external cohorts.

AI.tldr: ABMIL with pretrained pathology foundation models for LUAD growth pattern grading at slide level reduces annotation burden and achieves strong agreement, especially with fine-tuned encoders; outperforms patch-aggregation baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate LUAD predominant growth pattern labeling requires extensive patch annotations; enabling whole-slide level predictions with limited labels via ABMIL and foundation-model encoders.
AI.method: Attention-based multiple instance learning using patch encoders from pathology foundation models (frozen or fine-tuned on annotated patches); aggregate features with attention to predict slide-level predominant pattern.
AI.result: Fine-tuned encoders improve performance; Prov-GigaPath yields highest agreement (kappa = 0.699) under ABMIL; ABMIL outperforms simple patch-aggregation baselines by leveraging slide-level supervision and spatial attention.
AI.conclusion: ABMIL with foundation-model encoders is effective for LUAD growth pattern grading and may be extended to estimate full pattern distribution and validated on external cohorts.

title: BioMiner: A Multi-modal System for Automated Mining of Protein-Ligand Bioactivity Data from Literature
summary: Protein-ligand bioactivity data published in the literature are essential for drug discovery, yet manual curation struggles to keep pace with rapidly growing literature. Automated bioactivity extraction remains challenging because it requires not only interpreting biochemical semantics distributed across text, tables, and figures, but also reconstructing chemically exact ligand structures (e.g., Markush structures). To address this bottleneck, we introduce BioMiner, a multi-modal extraction framework that explicitly separates bioactivity semantic interpretation from ligand structure construction. Within BioMiner, bioactivity semantics are inferred through direct reasoning, while chemical structures are resolved via a chemical-structure-grounded visual semantic reasoning paradigm, in which multi-modal large language models operate on chemically grounded visual representations to infer inter-structure relationships, and exact molecular construction is delegated to domain chemistry tools. For rigorous evaluation and method development, we further establish BioVista, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 16,457 bioactivity entries curated from 500 publications. BioMiner validates its extraction ability and provides a quantitative baseline, achieving an F1 score of 0.32 for bioactivity triplets. BioMiner's practical utility is demonstrated via three applications: (1) extracting 82,262 data from 11,683 papers to build a pre-training database that improves downstream models performance by 3.9%; (2) enabling a human-in-the-loop workflow that doubles the number of high-quality NLRP3 bioactivity data, helping 38.6% improvement over 28 QSAR models and identification of 16 hit candidates with novel scaffolds; and (3) accelerating protein-ligand complex bioactivity annotation, achieving a 5.59-fold speed increase and 5.75% accuracy improvement over manual workflows in PoseBusters dataset.

AI.tldr: BioMiner is a multi-modal extraction framework that separates bioactivity semantics from ligand-structure construction, using reasoned semantics and visual-grounded, chemistry-grounded reasoning, benchmarked on BioVista; achieves 0.32 F1 on bioactivity triplets and enables scalable downstream benefits across pre-training, human-in-the-loop curation, and accelerated annotation.
AI.motivation: Automated extraction of protein-ligand bioactivity from literature is bottlenecked by the need to interpret semantic information across text, tables, and figures and to reconstruct chemically exact ligand structures (including Markush structures).
AI.method: BioMiner decomposes the task into: (1) bioactivity semantic interpretation via direct reasoning, and (2) ligand structure construction via a chemical-structure-grounded visual semantic reasoning paradigm, where multi-modal LLMs operate on chemically grounded visuals to infer structure relationships, with exact molecules constructed by domain chemistry tools. A benchmark (BioVista) with 16,457 bioactivity entries from 500 publications is used for evaluation.
AI.result: BioMiner achieves an F1 of 0.32 on bioactivity triplets. It extracts 82,262 data points from 11,683 papers to build a pre-training database, which improves downstream model performance by 3.9%. In a human-in-the-loop workflow, it doubles the number of high-quality NLRP3 bioactivity data, yielding a 38.6% improvement over 28 QSAR models and identifying 16 hit candidates with novel scaffolds. For protein-ligand complex bioactivity annotation, it accelerates annotation with a 5.59x speed-up and 5.75% accuracy improvement over manual workflows in PoseBusters.
AI.conclusion: BioMiner offers a scalable, modular approach that separates semantics from structure construction, validated on a substantial BioVista benchmark, and demonstrates practical gains in data extraction, model training, manual curation, and annotation speed, indicating strong potential for accelerating literature-derived bioactivity knowledge.

title: Drug Synergy Prediction via Residual Graph Isomorphism Networks and Attention Mechanisms
summary: In the treatment of complex diseases, treatment regimens using a single drug often yield limited efficacy and can lead to drug resistance. In contrast, combination drug therapies can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes through synergistic effects. However, experimentally validating all possible drug combinations is prohibitively expensive, underscoring the critical need for efficient computational prediction methods. Although existing approaches based on deep learning and graph neural networks (GNNs) have made considerable progress, challenges remain in reducing structural bias, improving generalization capability, and enhancing model interpretability. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a collaborative prediction graph neural network that integrates molecular structural features and cell-line genomic profiles with drug-drug interactions to enhance the prediction of synergistic effects. We introduce a novel model named the Residual Graph Isomorphism Network integrated with an Attention mechanism (ResGIN-Att). The model first extracts multi scale topological features of drug molecules using a residual graph isomorphism network, where residual connections help mitigate over-smoothing in deep layers. Subsequently, an adaptive Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) module fuses structural information from local to global scales. Finally, a cross-attention module is designed to explicitly model drug-drug interactions and identify key chemical substructures. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets demonstrate that ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance, comparing favorably against key baseline methods while exhibiting promising generalization capability and robustness.

AI.tldr: Proposes ResGIN-Att, a collaborative prediction GNN for drug synergism that fuses molecular structure, cell-line genomics, and drug interactions through a residual GIN, adaptive LSTM, and cross-attention; shows competitive results on five benchmarks with good generalization and robustness.
AI.motivation: To overcome limited efficacy of single-drug regimens, the combinatorial explosion of testing all drug pairs, and limitations of current DL/GNN methods (structural bias, generalization, interpretability) in predicting drug synergy.
AI.method: A residual Graph Isomorphism Network (ResGIN) extracts multi-scale molecular topological features with residual connections to mitigate over-smoothing. An adaptive LSTM fuses local-to-global structural information. A cross-attention module explicitly models drug–drug interactions and highlights key substructures. The model integrates molecular features with cell-line genomic profiles and DDI data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets show ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance against key baselines and demonstrates promising generalization capability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively addresses structural bias, generalization, and interpretability in drug synergy prediction, offering a robust and scalable framework for efficient identification of synergistic drug combinations.

title: Component-Based Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection requires sensitivity to subtle shifts without overreacting to natural In-Distribution (ID) diversity. However, from the viewpoint of detection granularity, global representation inevitably suppress local OOD cues, while patch-based methods are unstable due to entangled spurious-correlation and noise. And neither them is effective in detecting compositional OODs composed of valid ID components. Inspired by recognition-by-components theory, we present a training-free Component-Based OOD Detection (CoOD) framework that addresses the existing limitations by decomposing inputs into functional components. To instantiate CoOD, we derive Component Shift Score (CSS) to detect local appearance shifts, and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) to identify cross-component compositional inconsistencies. Empirically, CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection.

AI.tldr: A training-free Component-Based OOD Detection (CoOD) framework decomposes inputs into functional components to detect local shifts and compositional inconsistencies, addressing global vs local cues and patch-based instability, with improvements on coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection.
AI.motivation: Global representations can suppress local OOD cues; patch-based methods are unstable due to spurious correlations and noise; existing methods fail to detect compositional OODs built from valid ID components.
AI.method: Proposes CoOD: decompose inputs into functional components inspired by recognition-by-components; introduces Component Shift Score (CSS) to detect local appearance shifts and Compositional Consistency Score (CCS) to detect cross-component inconsistencies; training-free framework.
AI.result: Empirically, CoOD achieves consistent improvements on both coarse- and fine-grained OOD detection.
AI.conclusion: Component-based decomposition provides robust OOD detection by capturing local cues and compositional stability without training, addressing limitations of global representations and patch-based methods.

title: Generalizing Numerical Reasoning in Table Data through Operation Sketches and Self-Supervised Learning
comment: Accepted to TACL. This is a pre-MIT Press publication version
summary: Numerical reasoning over expert-domain tables often exhibits high in-domain accuracy but limited robustness to domain shift. Models trained with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on specific datasets tend to rely on header-operation shortcuts rather than structural reasoning. We introduce TaNOS, a continual pre-training framework comprising three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (ii) operation sketches that provide minimal structural cues, and (iii) self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from given tables in a program-first manner. By decoupling domain semantics and numerical operation structure, TaNOS improves the transferability of numerical reasoning. Applied to an 8B instruction-tuned model, TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% train data, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) with full train data and proprietary models such as GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro. Furthermore, in the domain-shift experiments, TaNOS displays nearly-negligible cross-domain gap (<2pp) when standard SFT shows over 10pp gap. These results suggest that structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision can improve the robustness of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

AI.tldr: TaNOS is a continual pre-training framework that decouples domain semantics from numerical operation structure via header anonymization, operation sketches, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision to improve numerical reasoning transfer across expert-domain tables; it achieves strong results with limited data and reduced domain gap.
AI.motivation: SFT models on domain-specific data show high in-domain accuracy but poor robustness to domain shift, often exploiting header-operation shortcuts rather than true structural reasoning; need representations and training signals that generalize across domains.
AI.method: Three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization; (ii) operation sketches providing minimal structural cues; (iii) self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from tables in a program-first manner, decoupling semantic content from numerical operation structure.
AI.result: With an 8B instruction-tuned model, TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% of train data, beating the SFT baseline (73.97%) with full data and surpassing proprietary models like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro. In domain-shift tests, cross-domain gap is <2 percentage points, versus >10pp for SFT, indicating improved robustness.
AI.conclusion: Structural guidance through operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision enhances the robustness and transferability of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

title: Deep kernel video approximation for unsupervised action segmentation
summary: This work focuses on per-video unsupervised action segmentation, which is of interest to applications where storing large datasets is either not possible, or nor permitted. We propose to segment videos by learning in deep kernel space, to approximate the underlying frame distribution, as closely as possible. To define this closeness metric between the original video distribution and its approximation, we rely on maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) which is a geometry-preserving metric in distribution space, and thus gives more reliable estimates. Moreover, unlike the commonly used optimal transport metric, MMD is both easier to optimize, and faster. We choose to use neural tangent kernels (NTKs) to define the kernel space where MMD operates, because of their improved descriptive power as opposed to fixed kernels. And, also, because NTKs sidestep the trivial solution, when jointly learning the inputs (video approximation) and the kernel function. Finally, we show competitive results when compared to state-of-the-art per-video methods, on six standard benchmarks. Additionally, our method has higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative work, when the number of segments is unknown.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised per-video action segmentation via learning in deep neural tangent kernel space using MMD to approximate frame distributions; NTK-based kernels enable robust, non-trivial optimization; competitive results on six benchmarks, with improved F1 when the number of segments is unknown.
AI.motivation: Enable per-video unsupervised action segmentation without large diverse datasets, using a geometry-preserving distribution metric (MMD) and expressive kernel representations (NTK) to model frame distributions and avoid trivial optimization traps.
AI.method: Represent frames in a deep kernel space defined by neural tangent kernels and optimize MMD between the original frame distribution and its learned approximation to segment the video in an unsupervised fashion. NTKs provide rich, trainable kernels and help prevent trivial solutions when jointly learning inputs and kernels. Compare against state-of-the-art per-video methods and prior agglomerative approaches.
AI.result: Achieves competitive performance versus state-of-the-art per-video methods on six standard benchmarks; attains higher F1 scores than prior agglomerative methods when the number of segments is unknown.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that deep kernel MMD in NTK space is effective for per-video unsupervised action segmentation, offering a geometry-preserving, efficient similarity metric and overcoming issues with trivial solutions, with strong empirical results across benchmarks.

title: The CriticalSet problem: Identifying Critical Contributors in Bipartite Dependency Networks
summary: Identifying critical nodes in complex networks is a fundamental task in graph mining. Yet, methods addressing an all-or-nothing coverage mechanics in a bipartite dependency network, a graph with two types of nodes where edges represent dependency relationships across the two groups only, remain largely unexplored. We formalize the CriticalSet problem: given an arbitrary bipartite graph modeling dependencies of items on contributors, identify the set of k contributors whose removal isolates the largest number of items. We prove that this problem is NP-hard and requires maximizing a supermodular set function, for which standard forward greedy algorithms provide no approximation guarantees. Consequently, we model CriticalSet as a coalitional game, deriving a closed-form centrality, ShapleyCov, based on the Shapley value. This measure can be interpreted as the expected number of items isolated by a contributor's departure. Leveraging these insights, we propose MinCov, a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that explicitly accounts for connection redundancy, prioritizing contributors who uniquely support many items. Extensive experiments on synthetic and large-scale real datasets, including a Wikipedia graph with over 250 million edges, reveal that MinCov and ShapleyCov significantly outperform traditional baselines. Notably, MinCov achieves near-optimal performance, within 0.02 AUC of a Stochastic Hill Climbing metaheuristic, while remaining several orders of magnitude faster.

AI.tldr: Identifies critical contributors in bipartite dependency graphs; proves CriticalSet NP-hard; introduces ShapleyCov centrality and MinCov peeling algorithm; demonstrates strong, scalable performance on large real-world data (e.g., 250M-edge Wikipedia graph).
AI.motivation: Motivated by the need to identify key nodes whose removal maximally isolates items in bipartite dependency networks, where dependencies cross two groups and traditional all-or-nothing coverage methods are inadequate.
AI.method: Formalize CriticalSet in bipartite graphs; prove NP-hardness and that the objective is a supermodular set function with no approximation guarantees via standard greedy methods. Develop a coalitional-game formulation yielding a closed-form ShapleyCov centrality based on the Shapley value. Propose MinCov, a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that emphasizes contributors uniquely supporting many items.
AI.result: ShapleyCov provides a principled centrality measure representing the expected number of items isolated by a contributor’s departure. MinCov and ShapleyCov outperform standard baselines on synthetic and large-scale real data, with MinCov achieving near-optimal performance (within 0.02 AUC of a Stochastic Hill Climbing metaheuristic) while being several orders of magnitude faster. Evaluation includes a Wikipedia graph with over 250 million edges.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a scalable, theoretically grounded framework for identifying critical contributors in bipartite dependency networks. ShapleyCov offers a meaningful centrality, while MinCov delivers fast, near-optimal optimization. The approach scales to very large graphs; future work could extend to weighted or dynamic bipartite graphs and explore robustness to data noise.

title: A temporal deep learning framework for calibration of low-cost air quality sensors
summary: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) provide a practical alternative to expensive regulatory-grade instruments, making dense urban monitoring networks possible. Yet their adoption is limited by calibration challenges, including sensor drift, environmental cross-sensitivity, and variability in performance from device to device. This work presents a deep learning framework for calibrating LCS measurements of PM$_{2.5}$, PM$_{10}$, and NO$_2$ using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, trained on co-located reference data from the OxAria network in Oxford, UK. Unlike the Random Forest (RF) baseline, which treats each observation independently, the proposed approach captures temporal dependencies and delayed environmental effects through sequence-based learning, achieving higher $R^2$ values across training, validation, and test sets for all three pollutants. A feature set is constructed combining time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms to improve generalization on unseen temporal windows. Validation of unseen calibrated values against the Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 demonstrates regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties of 22.11% for NO$_2$, 12.42% for PM$_{10}$, and 9.1% for PM$_{2.5}$.

AI.tldr: LSTM-based calibration for low-cost air sensors using co-located OxAria data; outperforms RF with temporal features and meets regulatory uncertainty bounds for PM2.5, PM10, NO2 in Oxford.
AI.motivation: Calibrating low-cost sensors is challenged by drift, cross-sensitivity, and device variability. Leveraging temporal dependencies can improve calibration accuracy and regulatory compliance in dense urban networks.
AI.method: Train a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network on co-located reference data from OxAria (Oxford, UK) to calibrate PM2.5, PM10, and NO2. Compare against a Random Forest baseline. Construct a feature set with time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms to capture temporal dynamics and environment-induced delays.
AI.result: LSTM achieved higher R^2 than RF across training, validation, and test sets for all three pollutants by leveraging sequence-based learning. Temporal features improved generalization to unseen windows. Regulatory validation against Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 yielded expanded uncertainties: NO2 22.11%, PM10 12.42%, PM2.5 9.1%.
AI.conclusion: Temporal sequence modeling with LSTM improves calibration of low-cost air-quality sensors, enabling more accurate dense monitoring networks that meet regulatory uncertainty requirements; the approach captures delayed environmental effects and cross-sensor dynamics not captured by per-observation RF models.

title: CHRep: Cross-modal Histology Representation and Post-hoc Calibration for Spatial Gene Expression Prediction
summary: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) enables spatially resolved gene profiling but remains expensive and low-throughput, limiting large-cohort studies and routine clinical use. Predicting spatial gene expression from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides is a promising alternative, yet under realistic leave-one-slide-out evaluation, existing models often suffer from slide-level appearance shifts and regression-driven over-smoothing that suppress biologically meaningful variation. CHRep is a two-phase framework for robust histology-to-expression prediction. In the training phase, CHRep learns a structure-aware representation by jointly optimizing correlation-aware regression, symmetric image-expression alignment, and coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization. In the inference phase, cross-slide robustness is improved without backbone fine-tuning through a lightweight calibration module trained on the training slides, which combines a non-parametric estimate from a training gallery with a magnitude-regularized correction module. Unlike prior embedding-alignment or retrieval-based transfer methods that rely on a single prediction route, CHRep couples topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, enabling stable neighborhood retrieval and controlled bias correction under slide-level shifts. Across the three cohorts, CHRep consistently improves gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out evaluation, with the largest gains observed on Alex+10x. Relative to HAGE, the Pearson correlation coefficient on all considered genes [PCC(ACG)] increases by 4.0% on cSCC and 9.8% on HER2+. Relative to mclSTExp, PCC(ACG) further improves by 39.5% on Alex+10x, together with 9.7% and 9.0% reductions in mean squared error (MSE) and mean absolute error (MAE), respectively.

AI.tldr: CHRep is a two-phase, structure-aware framework for robust histology-to-expression prediction that mitigates slide-level appearance shifts and regression-driven over-smoothing by coupling topology-preserving representation learning with post-hoc calibration, achieving improved cross-slide gene-wise correlations over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Spatial transcriptomics is expensive and low-throughput; predicting spatial gene expression from routine H&E slides offers a scalable alternative. Under leave-one-slide-out evaluation, existing models suffer from slide-level appearance shifts and over-smoothing, limiting generalization. CHRep targets robust cross-slide prediction.
AI.method: Training phase jointly optimizes correlation-aware regression, symmetric image–expression alignment, and coordinate-induced spatial topology regularization to learn a structure-aware representation. Inference uses a lightweight calibration module, trained on the training slides, combining a non-parametric estimate from a training gallery with a magnitude-regularized correction, enabling cross-slide robustness without backbone fine-tuning.
AI.result: CHRep consistently improves gene-wise correlation under leave-one-slide-out across three cohorts, with the largest gains on Alex+10x. Relative to HAGE, PCC(ACG) increases by 4.0% on cSCC and 9.8% on HER2+. Relative to mclSTExp, PCC(ACG) improves by 39.5% on Alex+10x, with reductions in MSE and MAE of 9.7% and 9.0%, respectively.
AI.conclusion: CHRep delivers stable neighborhood retrieval and controlled bias correction under slide-level shifts, yielding robust H&E-to-expression predictions and improved gene-wise metrics across multiple cohorts.

title: Unbiased Prevalence Estimation with Multicalibrated LLMs
summary: Estimating the prevalence of a category in a population using imperfect measurement devices (diagnostic tests, classifiers, or large language models) is fundamental to science, public health, and online trust and safety. Standard approaches correct for known device error rates but assume these rates remain stable across populations. We show this assumption fails under covariate shift and that multicalibration, which enforces calibration conditional on the input features rather than just on average, is sufficient for unbiased prevalence estimation under such shift. Standard calibration and quantification methods fail to provide this guarantee. Our work connects recent theoretical work on fairness to a longstanding measurement problem spanning nearly all academic disciplines. A simulation confirms that standard methods exhibit bias growing with shift magnitude, while a multicalibrated estimator maintains near-zero bias. While we focus the discussion mostly on LLMs, our theoretical results apply to any classification model. Two empirical applications -- estimating employment prevalence across U.S. states using the American Community Survey, and classifying political texts across four countries using an LLM -- demonstrate that multicalibration substantially reduces bias in practice, while highlighting that calibration data should cover the key feature dimensions along which target populations may differ.

AI.tldr: Multicalibration across input features ensures unbiased prevalence estimation under covariate shift, addressing a failure of standard calibration methods. Simple calibration fails as shift grows; simulations confirm near-zero bias with multicalibrated estimators; empirical applications (employment prevalence via ACS, political text classification across countries) illustrate practical gains and the need for calibration data covering key feature dimensions.
AI.motivation: Prevalence estimation with imperfect devices (tests, classifiers, LLMs) typically corrects known error rates but assumes those rates are stable. Covariate shift breaks this, biasing estimates. The work links fairness calibration ideas to measurement error, aiming for reliable prevalence across diverse populations.
AI.method: Introduce multicalibration: calibration that is conditional on input features rather than aggregate. Prove sufficiency for unbiased prevalence under covariate shift. Compare to standard calibration and quantification methods, which fail under shift. Use simulations to show bias grows with shift magnitude for standard methods; multicalibration keeps bias near zero. Apply to two real-world cases: employment prevalence from ACS, and political text classification across four countries using an LLM; emphasize that calibration data must cover key feature dimensions where populations differ.
AI.result: Under covariate shift, multicalibrated prevalence estimators exhibit near-zero bias, while standard calibration/quantification methods exhibit bias that grows with shift magnitude. The approach extends beyond LLMs to any classifier. Empirical applications demonstrate practical reduction in bias, highlighting the importance of calibration data that spans relevant feature dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Multicalibration provides both theoretical guarantees and practical improvements for prevalence estimation under covariate shift. By enforcing calibration across feature dimensions, it aligns measurement with fairness concepts and applies across disciplines. Calibration data should be representative of the feature space where target populations differ to realize these benefits.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Coupled Demand Forecasting and Supply Chain Optimization
comment: The paper is accepted in the Computers, Materials & Continua journal
summary: Supply chain resilience and efficiency are vital in industries characterized by volatile demand and uncertain supply, such as textiles and personal protective equipment (PPE). Traditional forecasting and optimization approaches often operate in isolation, limiting their real-world effectiveness. This paper proposes a Hybrid AI Framework for Demand-Supply Forecasting and Optimization (HAF-DS), which integrates a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based demand forecasting module with a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) optimization layer. The LSTM captures temporal and contextual demand dependencies, while the optimization layer prescribes cost-efficient replenishment and allocation decisions. The framework jointly minimizes forecasting error and operational cost through embedding-based feature representation and recurrent neural architectures. Experiments on textile sales and supply chain datasets show significant performance gains over statistical and deep learning baselines. On the combined dataset, HAF-DS reduced Mean Absolute Error (MAE) from 15.04 to 12.83 (14.7%), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) from 19.53 to 17.11 (12.4%), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) from 9.5% to 8.1%. Inventory cost decreased by 5.4%, stockouts by 27.5%, and service level rose from 95.5% to 97.8%. These results confirm that coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization enhances both accuracy and efficiency, providing a scalable and adaptable solution for modern textile and PPE supply chains.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LSTM-based demand forecasting with a MILP optimization layer (HAF-DS) jointly minimizes forecast error and operational cost, boosting accuracy and supply-chain performance in textiles and PPE.
AI.motivation: Traditional forecasting and optimization are often disconnected, limiting effectiveness in volatile, uncertain supply chains like textiles and PPE.
AI.method: Integrates an LSTM demand-forecasting module with a MILP optimization layer. Uses embedding-based features and recurrent architectures to enable a joint objective that minimizes forecasting error and replenishment/allocation costs.
AI.result: On a combined textile/PPE dataset, MAE dropped from 15.04 to 12.83 (−14.7%), RMSE from 19.53 to 17.11 (−12.4%), MAPE from 9.5% to 8.1%. Inventory cost −5.4%, stockouts −27.5%, service level increased from 95.5% to 97.8%. Demonstrates accuracy and efficiency gains and scalability for textile and PPE supply chains.
AI.conclusion: Coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization yields measurable gains in accuracy, cost efficiency, and service levels, offering a scalable framework for modern supply chains.

title: Engaged AI Governance: Addressing the Last Mile Challenge Through Internal Expert Collaboration
summary: Under the EU AI Act, translating AI governance requirements into software development practice remains challenging. While AI governance frameworks exist at industry and organizational levels, empirical evidence of team-level implementation is scarce. We address this "Last Mile" Challenge through insider action research embedded within an AI startup. We present a legal-text-to-action pipeline that translates EU AI Act requirements into actionable strategies through internal expert collaboration by extracting requirements from legal text, engaging practitioners in assessment and ideation, and prioritizing implementation through collective evaluation. Our analysis reveals three patterns in how practitioners perceive regulatory requirements: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), and disconnection (requirements perceived as administrative overhead). Based on these patterns, we discuss when governance might be treated genuinely or performatively. Practitioners prioritize requirements that serve end-users or their own development needs, but view verification-oriented requirements as box-ticking exercises. This distinction suggests a translation challenge: regulatory requirements risk superficial treatment unless practitioners understand how compliance serves system quality and user protection. Expert collaboration offers a practical mechanism for transforming governance from external imposition to shared ownership and making previously invisible governance work visible and collective.

AI.tldr: EU AI Act governance at the team level is translated into actionable software development tasks via insider action research; three practitioner patterns—convergence, existing practice, disconnection—shape whether governance is genuine or performative; expert collaboration can transform governance into shared ownership and make it visible.
AI.motivation: Bridge the 'Last Mile' gap between regulatory requirements and software development practice by providing a practical mechanism to translate legal texts into actionable development tasks and to study team-level uptake.
AI.method: Insider action research embedded in an AI startup; builds a legal-text-to-action pipeline: extract requirements from EU AI Act text, engage practitioners in assessment and ideation, and prioritize implementation through collective evaluation; enables internal expert collaboration between legal/compliance and developers.
AI.result: Identified three practitioner perception patterns: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), disconnection (requirements seen as administrative overhead). Found that verification-oriented requirements are often treated as box-ticking. Expert collaboration acts as a mechanism to reframe governance as shared ownership, making governance work visible and actionable.
AI.conclusion: Governance can be genuine rather than performative when translated into developer-centered tasks with clear ties to system quality and user protection. Expert collaboration helps move governance from external imposition to internal, collective responsibility and enhances perceived relevance of regulatory requirements.

title: Promoting Simple Agents: Ensemble Methods for Event-Log Prediction
summary: We compare lightweight automata-based models (n-grams) with neural architectures (LSTM, Transformer) for next-activity prediction in streaming event logs. Experiments on synthetic patterns and five real-world process mining datasets show that n-grams with appropriate context windows achieve comparable accuracy to neural models while requiring substantially fewer resources. Unlike windowed neural architectures, which show unstable performance patterns, n-grams provide stable and consistent accuracy. While we demonstrate that classical ensemble methods like voting improve n-gram performance, they require running many agents in parallel during inference, increasing memory consumption and latency. We propose an ensemble method, the promotion algorithm, that dynamically selects between two active models during inference, reducing overhead compared to classical voting schemes. On real-world datasets, these ensembles match or exceed the accuracy of non-windowed neural models with lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: N-gram (lightweight) models with suitable context windows can match neural models (LSTM/Transformer) for next-activity prediction in streaming logs, with far lower resource use; windowed neural models show instability; classical voting ensembles help n-grams but add overhead, leading to the promotion algorithm that dynamically selects between two models to reduce latency while maintaining accuracy; ensembles can outperform non-windowed neural models at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Address the efficiency and stability challenges of neural architectures in streaming process mining by evaluating lightweight automata-based models and ensembles, aiming for accurate, low-latency predictions with resource constraints.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on synthetic patterns and five real-world process mining datasets comparing n-grams with different context windows to LSTM/Transformer models; analysis of windowed vs non-windowed neural models; implementation of voting ensembles and a proposed promotion algorithm that selects between two active models during inference; assessment of accuracy, memory, and latency.
AI.result: N-grams with appropriate context windows achieve accuracy comparable to neural models while using substantially fewer resources; windowed neural architectures exhibit unstable performance; voting ensembles improve n-gram performance but incur higher memory and latency; the promotion algorithm reduces overhead and, on real-world data, ensembles match or exceed non-windowed neural models at lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight n-gram models are viable for next-activity prediction in streaming logs; a dynamic promotion-based ensemble can offer the best balance of accuracy and efficiency, outperforming comparable neural setups in resource-constrained settings.

title: Sculpt4D: Generating 4D Shapes via Sparse-Attention Diffusion Transformers
summary: Recent breakthroughs in 3D generative modeling have yielded remarkable progress in static shape synthesis, yet high-fidelity dynamic 4D generation remains elusive, hindered by temporal artifacts and prohibitive computational demand. We present Sculpt4D, a native 4D generative framework that seamlessly integrates efficient temporal modeling into a pretrained 3D Diffusion Transformer (Hunyuan3D 2.1), thereby mitigating the scarcity of 4D training data. At its core lies a Block Sparse Attention mechanism that preserves object identity by anchoring to the initial frame while capturing rich motion dynamics via a time-decaying sparse mask. This design faithfully models complex spatiotemporal dependencies with high fidelity, while sidestepping the quadratic overhead of full attention and reducing network total computation by 56%. Consequently, Sculpt4D establishes a new state-of-the-art in temporally coherent 4D synthesis and charts a path toward efficient and scalable 4D generation.

AI.tldr: Sculpt4D introduces a native 4D generation method built on a pretrained 3D Diffusion Transformer (Hunyuan3D 2.1) that uses Block Sparse Attention anchored to the first frame with a time-decaying mask to efficiently model temporal dynamics, reducing computation by 56% and achieving state-of-the-art temporally coherent 4D synthesis.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity dynamic 4D generation lags behind static 3D due to temporal artifacts, heavy compute, and scarce 4D training data. There is a need to adapt powerful 3D diffusion models to 4D with efficient temporal modeling.
AI.method: Integrate a Block Sparse Attention mechanism into a pretrained 3D Diffusion Transformer. Anchor attention to the initial frame to preserve identity and apply a time-decaying sparse mask to capture motion. This reduces quadratic attention overhead and lowers overall network computation by about 56%, enabling coherent 4D synthesis.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art temporally coherent 4D synthesis while significantly improving efficiency and scalability of 4D generation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that efficient temporal modeling can be embedded into pretrained 3D models to enable scalable, high-fidelity 4D generation, even with limited 4D training data.

title: Probabilistic Verification of Neural Networks via Efficient Probabilistic Hull Generation
summary: The problem of probabilistic verification of a neural network investigates the probability of satisfying the safe constraints in the output space when the input is given by a probability distribution. It is significant to answer this problem when the input is affected by disturbances often modeled by probabilistic variables. In the paper, we propose a novel neural network probabilistic verification framework which computes a guaranteed range for the safe probability by efficiently finding safe and unsafe probabilistic hulls. Our approach consists of three main innovations: (1) a state space subdivision strategy using regression trees to produce probabilistic hulls, (2) a boundary-aware sampling method which identifies the safety boundary in the input space using samples that are later used for building regression trees, and (3) iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization for computing a guaranteed range for the safe probability. The accuracy and efficiency of our approach are evaluated on various benchmarks including ACAS Xu and a rocket lander controller. The result shows an obvious advantage over the state of the art.

AI.tldr: Proposes a neural network probabilistic verification framework that computes guaranteed safe-probability ranges by constructing probabilistic hulls via regression-tree subdivision, boundary-aware sampling to locate safety boundaries, and iterative probabilistic prioritization; evaluated on ACAS Xu and a rocket lander controller with improved performance over state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: To quantify the probability that a neural network satisfies safety constraints under input disturbances modeled as probabilities, which is essential for safety-critical systems.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) state-space subdivision using regression trees to produce probabilistic hulls; (2) boundary-aware sampling to identify safety boundary in input space for building regression trees; (3) iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization to compute a guaranteed safe-probability range.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show accuracy and efficiency gains and an obvious advantage over SOTA on benchmarks ACAS Xu and rocket lander controllers.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides guaranteed probabilistic safety bounds and is effective for probabilistic verification of NN controllers.

title: Local Neighborhood Instability in Parametric Projections: Quantitative and Visual Analysis
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, LaTeX, to appear at the 17th International EuroVis Workshop on Visual Analytics
summary: Parametric projections let analysts embed new points in real time, but input variations from measurement noise or data drift can produce unpredictable shifts in the 2D layout. Whether and where a projection is locally stable remains largely unexamined. In this paper, we present a stability evaluation framework that probes parametric projections with Gaussian perturbations around selected anchor points and assesses how neighborhoods deform in the 2D embedding. Our approach combines quantitative measures of mean displacement, bias, and nearest-anchor assignment error with per-anchor visualizations of displacement vectors, local PCA ellipsoids, and Voronoi misassignment for detailed inspection. We demonstrate the framework's effectiveness on UMAP- and t-SNE-based neural projectors of varying network sizes and study the effect of Jacobian regularization as a gradient-based robustness strategy. We apply our framework to the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets. The results show that our framework identifies unstable projection regions invisible to reconstruction error or neighborhood-preservation metrics.

AI.tldr: A stability evaluation framework for parametric projections (UMAP/t-SNE neural projectors) under Gaussian perturbations around anchor points; combines quantitative and per-anchor visual metrics; demonstrates effectiveness on MNIST datasets and explores Jacobian regularization as a robustness strategy; reveals unstable regions not captured by common metrics.
AI.motivation: To systematically quantify local stability of learned 2D projections under input perturbations, addressing gaps where reconstruction error and neighborhood-preservation metrics fail to detect instability.
AI.method: Perturb anchor points with Gaussian noise; measure mean displacement, bias, and nearest-anchor misassignment in the 2D embedding; provide per-anchor visuals (displacement vectors, local PCA ellipses, Voronoi misassignment); test on UMAP- and t-SNE-based neural projectors of varying network sizes; examine Jacobian regularization as a gradient-based robustness technique; apply to MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets.
AI.result: The framework identifies unstable projection regions that are invisible to reconstruction error or neighborhood-preservation metrics; results are demonstrated across projector types and datasets; Jacobian regularization shows potential for improving robustness.
AI.conclusion: Stability diagnostics should be integrated into projection-learning workflows. The proposed framework provides granular, interpretable diagnostics of local instability and can inform design choices (e.g., using Jacobian-based regularization) to improve robustness of parametric projections.

title: Separable Expert Architecture: Toward Privacy-Preserving LLM Personalization via Composable Adapters and Deletable User Proxies
summary: Current model training approaches incorporate user information directly into shared weights, making individual data removal computationally infeasible without retraining. This paper presents a three-layer architecture that decouples personal data from shared weights by combining a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without imparting user data, and per-user proxy artefacts whose deletion constitutes deterministic unlearning. Evaluation on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B confirms per-user differentiation in which personal data influences outputs while remaining isolated, verified by a return to baseline after proxy removal (KL divergence of approximately 0.21 nats, 82-89% verification pass rate) and near-zero cross-user contamination. Because user-specific information never enters shared weights, the architecture mitigates model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction against shared model components by construction. The approach converts machine unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a deterministic deletion operation that preserves personalization alongside privacy-enhancing guarantees and is compatible with differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) for privacy-preserving shared model improvement.

AI.tldr: A three-layer architecture isolates user data via per-user proxies and domain-specific LoRA adapters, enabling deterministic unlearning by deleting proxies while preserving personalization and privacy.
AI.motivation: Address the infeasibility of removing individual training data from shared-weight models and mitigate privacy risks (model inversion, membership inference) by enabling efficient, deterministic unlearning.
AI.method: Introduce a three-layer design: a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without embedding user data, and per-user proxy artefacts whose deletion yields deterministic unlearning. Evaluate on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B, demonstrating per-user differentiation with isolated user data, regression to baseline after proxy removal (KL ~0.21 nats), 82–89% verification pass, and minimal cross-user contamination. The approach is compatible with DP-SGD for privacy-preserving shared-model improvement.
AI.result: Personal data influences outputs but remains isolated within per-user proxies; removal of proxies returns the model to baseline (KL ≈ 0.21 nats) with 82–89% verification success and near-zero cross-user contamination. Shared weights are protected from inversion, membership inference, and data extraction by construction; unlearning is a deterministic deletion operation compatible with DP-SGD.
AI.conclusion: The design converts unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a deterministic proxy deletion, preserving personalization while delivering privacy guarantees, and enabling integration with DP-SGD for secure, shared model enhancement.

title: DCMorph: Face Morphing via Dual-Stream Cross-Attention Diffusion
summary: Advancing face morphing attack techniques is crucial to anticipate evolving threats and develop robust defensive mechanisms for identity verification systems. This work introduces DCMorph, a dual-stream diffusion-based morphing framework that simultaneously operates at both identity conditioning and latent space levels. Unlike image-level methods suffering from blending artifacts or GAN-based approaches with limited reconstruction fidelity, DCMorph leverages identity-conditioned latent diffusion models through two mechanisms: (1) decoupled cross-attention interpolation that injects identity-specific features from both source faces into the denoising process, enabling explicit dual-identity conditioning absent in existing diffusion-based methods, and (2) DDIM inversion with spherical interpolation between inverted latent representations from both source faces, providing geometrically consistent initial latent representation that preserves structural attributes. Vulnerability analyses across four state-of-the-art face recognition systems demonstrate that DCMorph achieves the highest attack success rates compared to existing methods at both operational thresholds, while remaining challenging to detect by current morphing attack detection solutions.

AI.tldr: DCMorph: a dual-stream diffusion-based morphing framework that conditions on identity at both latent and image levels, using decoupled cross-attention interpolation and DDIM inversion with spherical interpolation, achieving highest attack success rates with low detectability across four state-of-the-art face recognizers.
