AI.motivation: To anticipate evolving morphing threats to identity verification and address limitations of image-level or GAN-based morphs, which suffer from artifacts or limited reconstruction fidelity.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) decoupled cross-attention interpolation injecting identity features from both source faces into the denoising process, enabling explicit dual-identity conditioning; (2) DDIM inversion with spherical interpolation between inverted latent representations from both sources to preserve structural attributes in the latent space.
AI.result: Vulnerability analyses across four state-of-the-art face recognition systems show DCMorph achieving the highest attack success rates at operational thresholds compared to existing methods and remaining challenging for current morphing attack detectors.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based morphing with dual-identity conditioning yields stronger, more covert attacks than prior approaches, underscoring the need for robust defenses and further research into detection and mitigation of diffusion-driven morphing threats.

title: CoFEE: Reasoning Control for LLM-Based Feature Discovery
summary: Feature discovery from complex unstructured data is fundamentally a reasoning problem: it requires identifying abstractions that are predictive of a target outcome while avoiding leakage, proxies, and post-outcome signals. With the introduction of ever-improving Large Language Models (LLMs), our method provides a structured method for addressing this challenge. LLMs are well suited for this task by being able to process large amounts of information, but unconstrained feature generation can lead to weak features. In this work, we study reasoning control in LLMs by inducing cognitive behaviors for improving feature discovery. We introduce CoFEE (Cognitive Feature Engineering Engine), a reasoning control framework that enforces cognitive behaviors in how the LLM reasons during feature discovery. From a machine learning perspective, these cognitive behaviors act as structured inductive biases over the space of candidate features generated by the model. These behaviors have been exploited with success in ML models, and include backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, verification against observability and leakage criteria, and explicit backtracking of rejected reasoning paths. In a controlled comparison, we show that enforcing cognitive behaviors yields features with higher empirical predictability than those under unconstrained vanilla LLM prompts. CoFEE achieves an average Success Rate Score that is 15.2% higher than the vanilla approach, while generating 29% fewer features and reducing costs by 53.3%. Using held-out feature evaluation, we assess whether cognitively induced features generalize beyond the data used for discovery. Our results indicate that, in our evaluated setting, reasoning control is associated with improvements in quality and efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery.

AI.tldr: CoFEE: a cognitive feature engineering engine that constrains LLM reasoning to improve feature discovery; yields more predictive features with fewer candidates and lower cost; demonstrates generalization on held-out data.
AI.motivation: Feature discovery from unstructured data risks leakage, proxies, and post-outcome signals; LLMs can generate many features but unconstrained prompts are weak. There is a need for structured inductive biases to guide feature discovery.
AI.method: Introduce CoFEE that enforces cognitive behaviors during LLM reasoning: backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, verification against observability/leakage criteria, and explicit backtracking of rejected paths; treat these as inductive biases over candidate features; compare against vanilla prompts.
AI.result: CoFEE yields features with higher empirical predictability; +15.2% in Success Rate Score; 29% fewer features; 53.3% cost reduction; held-out evaluation suggests better generalization.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning control via cognitive behaviors improves quality and efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery and generalizes to unseen data; supports structured inductive biases in feature engineering.

title: Large-Scale Data Parallelization of Product Quantization and Inverted Indexing Using Dask
comment: To be published in the CSCE 2022 proceedings
summary: Large-scale Nearest Neighbor (NN) search, though widely utilized in the similarity search field, remains challenged by the computational limitations inherent in processing large scale data. In an effort to decrease the computational expense needed, Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search is often used in applications that do not require the exact similarity search, but instead can rely on an approximation. Product Quantization (PQ) is a memory-efficient ANN effective for clustering all sizes of datasets. Clustering large-scale, high dimensional data requires a heavy computational expense, in both memory-cost and execution time. This work focuses on a unique way to divide and conquer the large scale data in Python using PQ, Inverted Indexing and Dask, combining the results without compromising the accuracy and reducing computational requirements to the level required when using medium-scale data.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Python-based divide-and-conquer pipeline using Product Quantization, inverted indexing, and Dask to scale approximate NN search while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large-scale, high-dimensional NN search is computationally expensive (memory and time). Exact NN is often unnecessary; PQ offers memory efficiency for ANN. The work aims to reduce clustering/NN computation to medium-scale levels via divide-and-conquer in Python.
AI.method: A distributed pipeline combining Product Quantization encoding, inverted indexing to partition the search space, and Dask for parallel computation, with a strategy to merge partition results without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.result: Claims that the divide-and-conquer approach maintains accuracy while substantially reducing computational requirements, bringing large-scale data processing costs down toward medium-scale levels.
AI.conclusion: Presents a scalable PQ-based ANN framework using inverted indexing and Dask that enables large-scale clustering and similarity search with preserved accuracy and reduced compute.

title: DualSplat: Robust 3D Gaussian Splatting via Pseudo-Mask Bootstrapping from Reconstruction Failures
comment: 10 pages,6 figures, accepted to Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Conference 2026
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) achieves real-time photorealistic rendering, its performance degrades significantly when training images contain transient objects that violate multi-view consistency. Existing methods face a circular dependency: accurate transient detection requires a well-reconstructed static scene, while clean reconstruction itself depends on reliable transient masks. We address this challenge with DualSplat, a Failure-to-Prior framework that converts first-pass reconstruction failures into explicit priors for a second reconstruction stage. We observe that transients, which appear in only a subset of views, often manifest as incomplete fragments during conservative initial training. We exploit these failures to construct object-level pseudo-masks by combining photometric residuals, feature mismatches, and SAM2 instance boundaries. These pseudo-masks then guide a clean second-pass 3DGS optimization, while a lightweight MLP refines them online by gradually shifting from prior supervision to self-consistency. Experiments on RobustNeRF and NeRF On-the-go show that DualSplat outperforms existing baselines, demonstrating particularly clear advantages in transient-heavy scenes and transient regions.

AI.tldr: DualSplat converts first-pass reconstruction failures into priors for a second pass in 3D Gaussian Splatting to handle transient objects while preserving multi-view consistency.
AI.motivation: To break the circular dependency between accurate transient detection and reliable static-scene reconstruction in 3D Gaussian Splatting when training data contain transients.
AI.method: Construct object-level pseudo-masks from first-pass failures using photometric residuals, feature mismatches, and SAM2 instance boundaries; run a second-pass 3DGS optimization guided by these masks; employ a lightweight MLP to refine masks online, transitioning from prior supervision to self-consistency.
AI.result: DualSplat outperforms baselines on RobustNeRF and NeRF On-the-go, with pronounced benefits in scenes and regions with transient objects.
AI.conclusion: Failure-driven priors can stabilize and improve static reconstructions and real-time rendering in the presence of transients, enabling robust 3DGS performance.

title: To See the Unseen: on the Generalization Ability of Transformers in Symbolic Reasoning
summary: We investigate the ability of decoder-only transformer models to perform abstract symbolic reasoning; specifically solving propositional logic reasoning problems given in-context. Previous work demonstrated that models fail to generalize to problems involving variable names that were not observed during training, and it was shown that one reason behind this is the difficulty of copying (or generating) unseen tokens. We show both theoretically and empirically that a particular representational collapse also has a crucial role: the unembeddings (last-layer weights) of unseen tokens collapse to nearly the same vector during training. The collapse makes distinguishing multiple unseen variables difficult for the model (especially when the embedding and unembedding parameters are shared), and provides a mechanistic explanation for the effectiveness of existing heuristic interventions like "active forgetting", which periodically reset the token (un)embeddings. Based on these observations, we devise a combination of techniques, involving a small architecture change facilitating copying, data diversity, and freezing or resetting (un)embeddings, that achieves generalization to unseen tokens. We support our claims with extensive controlled experiments on propositional logic reasoning problems. Beyond synthetic experiments, we also observe evidence of (un)embedding collapse in the open-weight models in the Gemma 3 family, which includes 99 unused tokens reserved for downstream use. Empirically we find that the correlated embeddings of these tokens are a poor initialization for finetuning applications.

AI.tldr: Decoder-only transformers fail to generalize to unseen tokens in propositional logic tasks due to collapse of last-layer unembeddings; a combination of copying-friendly architecture, data diversity, and embedding freezing/resetting enables unseen-token generalization, supported by synthetic experiments and evidence from open-weight models (Gemma 3).
AI.motivation: Explain why models struggle with unseen variable names in in-context symbolic reasoning and identify a mechanistic factor (unembedding collapse) beyond copying difficulty; evaluate practical interventions to improve generalization.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of last-layer unembeddings; controlled empirical experiments on propositional logic reasoning tasks with in-context prompts; propose and test a small architectural change to ease copying; introduce data diversity and strategies to freeze or reset (un)embeddings; test on synthetic propositional logic tasks and inspect open-weight Gemma 3 models for unembedding behavior.
AI.result: Unembeddings of unseen tokens converge to nearly identical vectors during training, hindering differentiation of unseen variables, especially when embedding/unembedding are shared. This collapse explains limits of copying-based strategies and supports the utility of interventions like active forgetting. A combined approach (architecture tweaks for copying, diverse data, and embedding freezing/resetting) achieves generalization to unseen tokens. Evidence of unembedding collapse is observed in Gemma 3 open-weight models; correlated token embeddings are poor finetuning initializations.
AI.conclusion: Unembedding collapse is a critical bottleneck for unseen-token generalization in decoder-only LMs on symbolic tasks. Targeted interventions that manage token representations, alongside modest architecture changes and data strategies, can restore generalization to unseen tokens, with relevance to real-world open-weight models.

title: Dilated CNNs for Periodic Signal Processing: A Low-Complexity Approach
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures, the use of deep learning in IoT devices
summary: Denoising of periodic signals and accurate waveform estimation are core tasks across many signal processing domains, including speech, music, medical diagnostics, radio, and sonar. Although deep learning methods have recently shown performance improvements over classical approaches, they require substantial computational resources and are usually trained separately for each signal observation. This study proposes a computationally efficient method based on DCNN and Re-sampling, termed R-DCNN, designed for operation under strict power and resource constraints. The approach targets signals with varying fundamental frequencies and requires only a single observation for training. It generalizes to additional signals via a lightweight resampling step that aligns time scales in signals with different frequencies to re-use the same network weights. Despite its low computational complexity, R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods, such as autoregressive (AR)-based techniques, as well as conventional DCNNs trained individually for each observation. This combination of efficiency and performance makes the proposed method particularly well suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments without sacrificing denoising or estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: A light-weight DCNN-based denoising method with resampling (R-DCNN) for periodic signals that adapts to varying fundamental frequencies using a single observation for training, achieving competitive performance with lower computation compared to AR methods and per-observation DCNNs.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate denoising and waveform estimation of periodic signals under strict power and resource constraints, minimizing training data needs and avoiding per-signal retraining.
AI.method: Introduce R-DCNN: a deep convolutional neural network combined with a lightweight resampling step. The resampling aligns time scales across frequency variations so the same network weights can be reused for different signals. Training uses only a single observation.
AI.result: R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical autoregressive (AR) methods and conventional DCNNs trained per observation, while maintaining low computational complexity suitable for resource-constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: R-DCNN offers an efficient, generalizable solution for denoising and waveform estimation of periodic signals, enabling deployment in constrained settings without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Causal Disentanglement for Full-Reference Image Quality Assessment
summary: Existing deep network-based full-reference image quality assessment (FR-IQA) models typically work by performing pairwise comparisons of deep features from the reference and distorted images. In this paper, we approach this problem from a different perspective and propose a novel FR-IQA paradigm based on causal inference and decoupled representation learning. Unlike typical feature comparison-based FR-IQA models, our approach formulates degradation estimation as a causal disentanglement process guided by intervention on latent representations. We first decouple degradation and content representations by exploiting the content invariance between the reference and distorted images. Second, inspired by the human visual masking effect, we design a masking module to model the causal relationship between image content and degradation features, thereby extracting content-influenced degradation features from distorted images. Finally, quality scores are predicted from these degradation features using either supervised regression or label-free dimensionality reduction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves highly competitive performance on standard IQA benchmarks across fully supervised, few-label, and label-free settings. Furthermore, we evaluate the approach on diverse non-standard natural image domains with scarce data, including underwater, radiographic, medical, neutron, and screen-content images. Benefiting from its ability to perform scenario-specific training and prediction without labeled IQA data, our method exhibits superior cross-domain generalization compared to existing training-free FR-IQA models.

AI.tldr: A causal-inference-based FR-IQA method that decouples content and degradation, uses a masking module to reveal content-influenced degradation, and predicts quality from degradation features, achieving competitive IQA results and strong cross-domain generalization, including label-free settings.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of standard FR-IQA methods that rely on pairwise deep feature comparisons and to enhance cross-domain generalization and performance under limited or no labeled IQA data by leveraging causal disentanglement and content invariance.
AI.method: Decouple degradation and content representations by exploiting content invariance between reference and distorted images. Introduce a masking module to model the causal relationship between image content and degradation features, extracting content-influenced degradation features. Predict quality scores from these features using supervised regression or label-free dimensionality reduction (unsupervised/semisupervised). Interventions on latent representations implement a causal disentanglement process.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show highly competitive performance on standard IQA benchmarks across fully supervised, few-label, and label-free settings. The method also performs well on diverse non-standard domains with scarce data (underwater, radiographic, medical, neutron, screen-content) and exhibits superior cross-domain generalization compared to existing training-free FR-IQA models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed causal-disentanglement FR-IQA paradigm achieves strong accuracy and notably superior cross-domain generalization, with flexibility to operate under limited or no labeled IQA data through scenario-specific training and prediction.

title: GS-Quant: Granular Semantic and Generative Structural Quantization for Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown immense potential in Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC), yet bridging the modality gap between continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens remains a critical challenge. While recent quantization-based approaches attempt to align these modalities, they typically treat quantization as flat numerical compression, resulting in semantically entangled codes that fail to mirror the hierarchical nature of human reasoning. In this paper, we propose GS-Quant, a novel framework that generates semantically coherent and structurally stratified discrete codes for KG entities. Unlike prior methods, GS-Quant is grounded in the insight that entity representations should follow a linguistic coarse-to-fine logic. We introduce a Granular Semantic Enhancement module that injects hierarchical knowledge into the codebook, ensuring that earlier codes capture global semantic categories while later codes refine specific attributes. Furthermore, a Generative Structural Reconstruction module imposes causal dependencies on the code sequence, transforming independent discrete units into structured semantic descriptors. By expanding the LLM vocabulary with these learned codes, we enable the model to reason over graph structures isomorphically to natural language generation. Experimental results demonstrate that GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/mikumifa/GS-Quant.

AI.tldr: GS-Quant builds semantically coherent, hierarchically stratified discrete codes for KG entities to bridge continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens, via Granular Semantic Enhancement and Generative Structural Reconstruction, achieving strong KGC gains and opening the LLM vocabulary to structured graph reasoning.
AI.motivation: Addresses the modality gap between continuous KG embeddings and discrete LLM tokens; existing quantization compresses numerically but entangles semantics and ignores hierarchical, coarse-to-fine reasoning.
AI.method: Granular Semantic Enhancement injects hierarchical knowledge into the codebook, with earlier codes capturing global semantic categories and later codes refining attributes; Generative Structural Reconstruction imposes causal dependencies across the code sequence; LLM vocabulary is expanded with learned codes to enable isomorphic reasoning with natural language.
AI.result: Experimental results show GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines on KGC tasks; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: GS-Quant demonstrates the viability of semantically coherent, structured discrete representations for bridging graph modalities and enabling effective reasoning in LLMs; promotes a coarse-to-fine, hierarchy-aware quantization approach for KG entities.

title: Enabling and Inhibitory Pathways of University Students' Willingness to Disclose AI Use: A Cognition-Affect-Conation Perspective
summary: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised important questions regarding students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work. This study investigates the psychological mechanisms underlying university students' willingness to disclose AI use by applying the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. A sequential explanatory mixed-methods design was employed. In the quantitative phase, survey data were collected from 546 university students and analysed using structural equation modelling to examine the relationships among cognitive perceptions, affective responses, and disclosure intention. In the qualitative phase, semi-structured interviews with 22 students were conducted to further interpret the quantitative findings. The results indicate that psychological safety significantly increases students' willingness to disclose AI use and is positively shaped by perceived fairness, perceived teacher support, and perceived organisational support. Conversely, evaluation apprehension reduces disclosure intention and psychological safety, and is strengthened by perceived stigma, perceived uncertainty, and privacy concern. Qualitative findings further reveal that institutional clarity and supportive instructional practices encourage openness, whereas policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation often lead students to adopt cautious or strategic disclosure practices. Overall, the study highlights the dual role of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in shaping AI-use disclosure and underscores the importance of supportive institutional environments and clear guidance for promoting responsible AI transparency in higher education.

AI.tldr: Investigates factors shaping university students' willingness to disclose AI use in higher education using the CAC framework via sequential explanatory mixed methods; psychological safety promotes disclosure while evaluation apprehension inhibits it; institutional clarity and supportive practices encourage openness; policy ambiguity and stigma risk suppressing disclosure.
AI.motivation: Address concerns about transparency in AI-assisted student work and identify psychological mechanisms to inform policy and practice.
AI.method: Sequential explanatory mixed-methods: quantitative phase with 546 students analyzed via structural equation modeling; qualitative phase with 22 semi-structured interviews to interpret quantitative results.
AI.result: Psychological safety increases willingness to disclose AI use and is fostered by perceived fairness, teacher/organizational support; evaluation apprehension decreases disclosure and safety, amplified by stigma, uncertainty, and privacy concerns; institutional clarity and supportive teaching practices promote openness; policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation drive cautious disclosure strategies.
AI.conclusion: Dual roles of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms shape AI-use disclosure; clear guidance and supportive institutional environments are key to promoting responsible transparency in higher education.

title: Geometric Monomial (GEM): a family of rational 2N-differentiable activation functions
summary: The choice of activation function plays a crucial role in the optimization and performance of deep neural networks. While the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) remains the dominant choice due to its simplicity and effectiveness, its lack of smoothness may hinder gradient-based optimization in deep architectures. In this work we propose a family of $C^{2N}$-smooth activation functions whose gate follows a log-logistic CDF, achieving ReLU-like performance with purely rational arithmetic. We introduce three variants: GEM (the base family), E-GEM (an $ε$-parameterized generalization enabling arbitrary $L^p$-approximation of ReLU), and SE-GEM (a piecewise variant eliminating dead neurons with $C^{2N}$ junction smoothness). An $N$-ablation study establishes $N=1$ as optimal for standard-depth networks, reducing the GELU deficit on CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56 from 6.10% to 2.12%. The smoothness parameter $N$ further reveals a CNN-transformer tradeoff: $N=1$ is preferred for deep CNNs, while $N=2$ is preferred for transformers. On MNIST, E-GEM ties the best baseline (99.23%). On CIFAR-10 + ResNet-56, SE-GEM ($ε=10^{-4}$) surpasses GELU (92.51% vs 92.44%) -- the first GEM-family activation to outperform GELU. On CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56, E-GEM reduces the GELU deficit from 6.10% (GEM $N=2$) to just 0.62%. On GPT-2 (124M), GEM achieves the lowest perplexity (72.57 vs 73.76 for GELU), with GEM $N=1$ also beating GELU (73.32). On BERT-small, E-GEM ($ε=10$) achieves the best validation loss (6.656) across all activations. The $ε$-parameterization reveals a scale-dependent optimum: small $ε$ ($10^{-4}$--$10^{-6}$) for deep CNNs and larger transformers, with the special case of small transformers (BERT-small) benefiting from large $ε$ ($ε=10$) due to its limited depth and unconstrained gradients.

AI.tldr: Introduces GEM, a family of C^{2N}-smooth activations with a log-logistic gate; variants E-GEM and SE-GEM improve upon GELU across vision and language tasks, with N controlling smoothness and ε enabling L^p-ReLU approximations. N=1 is optimal for CNNs, N=2 for transformers; ε-parameterization yields architecture-dependent optima. Empirically, GEM-family achieves state-of-the-art or competitive results on CIFAR-10/100, MNIST, GPT-2, and BERT-small, often surpassing GELU.
AI.motivation: ReLU’s non-smoothness can hinder gradient-based optimization in deep networks; propose smooth, ReLU-like activation functions that retain efficient rational arithmetic and improve optimization and performance across architectures and tasks.
AI.method: Define GEM family of C^{2N}-smooth activation functions with a log-logistic CDF gate. Extend with E-GEM (ε-parameterized generalization enabling arbitrary L^p-approximation of ReLU) and SE-GEM (piecewise variant with C^{2N} junctions to prevent dead neurons). Conduct N-ablation across standard CNNs/transformers and comprehensive experiments across CIFAR/MNIST/GPT-2/BERT-small.
AI.result: N=1 best for standard-depth CNNs; deeper CNNs prefer N=1, transformers prefer N=2. CIFAR-100+ResNet-56: GELU deficit reduced from 6.10% (GEM N=2) to 0.62% (E-GEM). CIFAR-10+ResNet-56: SE-GEM (ε=1e-4) beats GELU (92.51% vs 92.44%). MNIST: E-GEM matches 99.23%. GPT-2 (124M): GEM achieves perplexity 72.57 (better than GELU 73.76); GEM N=1 also improves. BERT-small: E-GEM (ε=10) yields best validation loss 6.656 among activations.
AI.conclusion: GEM-family activations deliver smooth, ReLU-like performance with purely rational arithmetic, achieving improvements over GELU in several regimes. The ε-parameterization reveals depth-dependent optima, with small ε favored for deep CNNs and larger ε for transformers; SE-GEM mitigates dead neurons while preserving smoothness; these activations offer a tunable, architecture-aware alternative to GELU for modern models.

title: Sapiens2
summary: We present Sapiens2, a model family of high-resolution transformers for human-centric vision focused on generalization, versatility, and high-fidelity outputs. Our model sizes range from 0.4 to 5 billion parameters, with native 1K resolution and hierarchical variants that support 4K. Sapiens2 substantially improves over its predecessor in both pretraining and post-training. First, to learn features that capture low-level details (for dense prediction) and high-level semantics (for zero-shot or few-label settings), we combine masked image reconstruction with self-distilled contrastive objectives. Our evaluations show that this unified pretraining objective is better suited for a wider range of downstream tasks. Second, along the data axis, we pretrain on a curated dataset of 1 billion high-quality human images and improve the quality and quantity of task annotations. Third, architecturally, we incorporate advances from frontier models that enable longer training schedules with improved stability. Our 4K models adopt windowed attention to reason over longer spatial context and are pretrained with 2K output resolution. Sapiens2 sets a new state-of-the-art and improves over the first generation on pose (+4 mAP), body-part segmentation (+24.3 mIoU), normal estimation (45.6% lower angular error) and extends to new tasks such as pointmap and albedo estimation. Code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/sapiens2

AI.tldr: Sapiens2 is a high-resolution, human-centric vision transformer family (0.4–5B params) with native 1K and 4K capabilities, achieving state-of-the-art results across pose, body-part segmentation, and normal estimation via a unified pretraining objective and a large curated human image dataset.
AI.motivation: Improve generalization, versatility, and high-fidelity outputs in human-centric vision across dense prediction and semantic tasks, by combining robust pretraining, data scale, and architectural advances.
AI.method: Unified pretraining combining masked image reconstruction with self-distilled contrastive objectives; pretraining on ~1B high-quality human images; architectural improvements for longer training schedules; 4K models using windowed attention with 2K output resolution.
AI.result: New state-of-the-art performance: pose +4 mAP, body-part segmentation +24.3 mIoU, normal estimation 45.6% lower angular error; extends to new tasks such as pointmap and albedo estimation; 4K variants with 2K outputs.
AI.conclusion: Sapiens2 advances outperform its predecessor and demonstrates strong generalization and versatility in human-centric vision tasks; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Bridging the Training-Deployment Gap: Gated Encoding and Multi-Scale Refinement for Efficient Quantization-Aware Image Enhancement
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the Mobile AI (MAI) 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Image enhancement models for mobile devices often struggle to balance high output quality with the fast processing speeds required by mobile hardware. While recent deep learning models can enhance low-quality mobile photos into high-quality images, their performance is often degraded when converted to lower-precision formats for actual use on mobile phones. To address this training-deployment mismatch, we propose an efficient image enhancement model designed specifically for mobile deployment. Our approach uses a hierarchical network architecture with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement to preserve fine-grained visual features. Moreover, we incorporate Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate the effects of low-precision representation during the training process. This allows the network to adapt and prevents the typical drop in quality seen with standard post-training quantization (PTQ). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces high-fidelity visual output while maintaining the low computational overhead needed for practical use on standard mobile devices. The code will be available at https://github.com/GenAI4E/QATIE.git.

AI.tldr: Proposes a mobile-optimized image enhancement model that uses a hierarchical gated encoder with multiscale refinement and Quantization-Aware Training to bridge the gap between training-time accuracy and deployment-time quantization, achieving high quality with low compute on mobile devices; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between high-quality DL-based image enhancement and the need for fast, low-precision inference on mobile hardware; maintain fidelity under quantization.
AI.method: Hierarchical network architecture with gated encoder blocks; multiscale refinement to preserve fine details; Quantization-Aware Training to simulate low-precision during training and adapt the network; evaluation on mobile-friendly metrics.
AI.result: Shows high-fidelity visual output with low computational overhead on standard mobile devices; demonstration via experiments; code available.
AI.conclusion: QAT mitigates the performance drop from post-training quantization and yields a practical mobile-ready image enhancement solution.

title: Evaluating Post-hoc Explanations of the Transformer-based Genome Language Model DNABERT-2
comment: Accepted at the 4th World Conference on Explainable Artificial Intelligence, XAI-2026
summary: Explaining deep neural network predictions on genome sequences enables biological insight and hypothesis generation-often of greater interest than predictive performance alone. While explanations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been shown to capture relevant patterns in genome sequences, it is unclear whether this transfers to more expressive Transformer-based genome language models (gLMs). To answer this question, we adapt AttnLRP, an extension of layer-wise relevance propagation to the attention mechanism, and apply it to the state-of-the-art gLM DNABERT-2. Thereby, we propose strategies to transfer explanations from token and nucleotide level. We evaluate the adaption of AttnLRP on genomic datasets using multiple metrics. Further, we provide an extensive comparison between the explanations of DNABERT-2 and a baseline CNN. Our results demonstrate that AttnLRP yields reliable explanations corresponding to known biological patterns. Hence, like CNNs, gLMs can also help derive biological insights. This work contributes to the explainability of gLMs and addresses the comparability of relevance attributions across different architectures.

AI.tldr: Adapts AttnLRP to DNABERT-2 to explain gLM predictions on genome sequences; demonstrates explanations align with known biology and are comparable to CNN explanations.
AI.motivation: Explainability for transformer-based genome language models (gLMs) and the transferability of CNN-based explanation methods to gLMs; enable cross-architecture comparability of relevance attributions.
AI.method: Adapt AttnLRP (layer-wise relevance propagation for attention) to the DNABERT-2 model. Extend explanation transfer from token-level to nucleotide-level. Evaluate the adapted explanations on genomic datasets using multiple metrics and compare against a CNN baseline.
AI.result: AttnLRP yields reliable explanations that reflect known biological patterns; indicates that gLMs can provide biological insights similar to CNNs; demonstrates cross-architecture explainability.
AI.conclusion: Contributes to explainability of gLMs and to the comparability of relevance attributions across different architectures; supports use of gLMs for deriving biological insights beyond predictive performance.

title: WorldMark: A Unified Benchmark Suite for Interactive Video World Models
summary: Interactive video generation models such as Genie, YUME, HY-World, and Matrix-Game are advancing rapidly, yet every model is evaluated on its own benchmark with private scenes and trajectories, making fair cross-model comparison impossible. Existing public benchmarks offer useful metrics such as trajectory error, aesthetic scores, and VLM-based judgments, but none supplies the standardized test conditions -- identical scenes, identical action sequences, and a unified control interface -- needed to make those metrics comparable across models with heterogeneous inputs. We introduce WorldMark, the first benchmark that provides such a common playing field for interactive Image-to-Video world models. WorldMark contributes: (1) a unified action-mapping layer that translates a shared WASD-style action vocabulary into each model's native control format, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across six major models on identical scenes and trajectories; (2) a hierarchical test suite of 500 evaluation cases covering first- and third-person viewpoints, photorealistic and stylized scenes, and three difficulty tiers from Easy to Hard spanning 20-60s; and (3) a modular evaluation toolkit for Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency, designed so that researchers can reuse our standardized inputs while plugging in their own metrics as the field evolves. We will release all data, evaluation code, and model outputs to facilitate future research. Beyond offline metrics, we launch World Model Arena (warena.ai), an online platform where anyone can pit leading world models against each other in side-by-side battles and watch the live leaderboard.

AI.tldr: WorldMark is a standardized benchmark for interactive image-to-video world models, featuring a unified action-mapping layer, a 500-case hierarchical test suite, a modular evaluation toolkit, and an online World Model Arena for side-by-side benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Current interactive video models are evaluated on private, model-specific benchmarks, hindering fair cross-model comparisons. There is a lack of standardized, identical test conditions across models.
AI.method: Develop a unified WASD-style action vocabulary mapped to each model's control format; create a hierarchical 500-case test suite covering multiple viewpoints, scenes, and difficulty levels; design a modular evaluation toolkit for Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency; release data/code/models; launch an online arena (Warena) for live benchmarking.
AI.result: Introduces WorldMark with unified testing conditions and evaluation tools, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across six models on identical scenes/trajectories; provides data, code, and outputs to the community; complements an online World Model Arena for live leaderboards.
AI.conclusion: WorldMark provides a standardized, reusable platform to accelerate fair benchmarking and methodological development in interactive image-to-video world models, with both offline benchmarks and a live online competition platform.

title: Thinking with Reasoning Skills: Fewer Tokens, More Accuracy
comment: 10 pages, The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics -- Industry Track
summary: Reasoning LLMs often spend substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) when solving new problems. We propose to summarize and store reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration, and to retrieve these skills at inference time to guide future reasoning. Unlike the prevailing \emph{reasoning from scratch} paradigm, our approach first recalls relevant skills for each query, helping the model avoid redundant detours and focus on effective solution paths. We evaluate our method on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks, and find that it significantly reduces reasoning tokens while improving overall performance. The resulting lower per-request cost indicates strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes storing distilled, reusable reasoning skills to guide inference, reducing chain-of-thought tokens while improving performance on coding and math tasks.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost and latency of reasoning by reusing prior deliberations; shift from reasoning from scratch to retrieval-guided reasoning for efficiency and practicality.
AI.method: Distill extensive deliberation/trial-and-error into reusable skills, index them, and retrieve relevant skills per query to steer reasoning instead of starting from scratch; evaluate on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Significant reduction in reasoning tokens and improved overall performance; lower per-request cost, indicating strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-based reuse of distilled reasoning skills is an effective alternative to chain-of-thought, offering efficiency gains and competitive accuracy on complex reasoning tasks.

title: Towards Universal Tabular Embeddings: A Benchmark Across Data Tasks
summary: Tabular foundation models aim to learn universal representations of tabular data that transfer across tasks and domains, enabling applications such as table retrieval, semantic search and table-based prediction. Despite the growing number of such models, it remains unclear which approach works best in practice, as existing methods are often evaluated under task-specific settings that make direct comparison difficult. To address this, we introduce TEmBed, the Tabular Embedding Test Bed, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating tabular embeddings across four representation levels: cell, row, column, and table. Evaluating a diverse set of tabular representation learning models, we show that which model to use depends on the task and representation level. Our results offer practical guidance for selecting tabular embeddings in real-world applications and lay the groundwork for developing more general-purpose tabular representation models.

AI.tldr: TEmBed is a benchmark for evaluating tabular embeddings across cell, row, column, and table representations, showing that the best model depends on the task and representation level.
AI.motivation: There is no single, fair way to compare tabular foundation models across tasks; a unified benchmark is needed to understand capabilities and transferability.
AI.method: Introduce TEmBed and systematically evaluate a diverse set of tabular representation learning models across four representation levels (cell, row, column, table) and tasks such as retrieval and table-based prediction.
AI.result: Findings indicate that model suitability is task- and level-dependent, providing practical guidance for selecting tabular embeddings and establishing groundwork for more general-purpose tabular representations.
AI.conclusion: TEmBed offers a practical evaluation framework that guides model choice in real-world applications and supports future development of universal tabular representation models.

title: Efficient Logic Gate Networks for Video Copy Detection
summary: Video copy detection requires robust similarity estimation under diverse visual distortions while operating at very large scale. Although deep neural networks achieve strong performance, their computational cost and descriptor size limit practical deployment in high-throughput systems. In this work, we propose a video copy detection framework based on differentiable Logic Gate Networks (LGNs), which replace conventional floating-point feature extractors with compact, logic-based representations. Our approach combines aggressive frame miniaturization, binary preprocessing, and a trainable LGN embedding model that learns both logical operations and interconnections. After training, the model can be discretized into a purely Boolean circuit, enabling extremely fast and memory-efficient inference. We systematically evaluate different similarity strategies, binarization schemes, and LGN architectures across multiple dataset folds and difficulty levels. Experimental results demonstrate that LGN-based models achieve competitive or superior accuracy and ranking performance compared to prior models, while producing descriptors several orders of magnitude smaller and delivering inference speeds exceeding 11k samples per second. These findings indicate that logic-based models offer a promising alternative for scalable and resource-efficient video copy detection.

AI.tldr: LGN-based video copy detection uses differentiable logic gate networks to create compact, binary descriptors; discretization to Boolean circuits yields fast, memory-efficient inference with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Video copy detection requires robust similarity estimation under distortions at large scale, but deep networks provide accuracy with high computational cost and large descriptor sizes. A compact, scalable approach is needed for high-throughput systems.
AI.method: Train a differentiable LGN embedding model that learns logical operations and interconnections, coupled with aggressive frame miniaturization and binary preprocessing. After training, discretize the model into a purely Boolean circuit for extremely fast, memory-efficient inference.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation across multiple dataset folds and difficulty levels shows that LGN-based models achieve competitive or superior accuracy and ranking compared to prior models, while producing descriptors orders of magnitude smaller and achieving inference speeds above 11k samples per second.
AI.conclusion: Logic-based models offer a promising, resource-efficient alternative for scalable video copy detection.

title: Who Defines "Best"? Towards Interactive, User-Defined Evaluation of LLM Leaderboards
comment: Accepted to the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT 2026)
summary: LLM leaderboards are widely used to compare models and guide deployment decisions. However, leaderboard rankings are shaped by evaluation priorities set by benchmark designers, rather than by the diverse goals and constraints of actual users and organizations. A single aggregate score often obscures how models behave across different prompt types and compositions. In this work, we conduct an in-depth analysis of the dataset used in the LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) benchmark and investigate this evaluation challenge by designing an interactive visualization interface as a design probe. Our analysis reveals that the dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics, that model rankings vary across prompt slices, and that preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. Building on this analysis, we introduce a visualization interface that allows users to define their own evaluation priorities by selecting and weighting prompt slices and to explore how rankings change accordingly. A qualitative study suggests that this interactive approach improves transparency and supports more context-specific model evaluation, pointing toward alternative ways to design and use LLM leaderboards.

AI.tldr: Analyzes the LMArena dataset to reveal biases and how rankings shift across prompt slices, and presents an interactive visualization to let users weight prompt slices and customize evaluations, aiming for more transparent, context-aware leaderboards.
AI.motivation: Leaderboard evaluations are dominated by benchmark designers' priorities and single aggregate scores; these do not capture performance variation across different prompts or deployment constraints faced by real users.
AI.method: Dataset analysis of LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) to identify topic skew and slice-dependent rankings; examination of how preference-based judgments are used; development of an interactive visualization design probe; qualitative study of its usefulness for transparency and context-specific evaluation.
AI.result: Findings show heavy topic skew in the dataset; model rankings vary across prompt slices; preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. The interactive interface lets users select and weight prompt slices to redefine evaluation priorities, revealing how rankings change. Qualitative study indicates improved transparency and usefulness for context-specific evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Suggests alternative leaderboard designs based on user-defined, slice-weighted evaluations to achieve more transparent, deployment-relevant model assessment; indicates potential for broader adoption and refinement of interactive, multi-slice evaluation tools.

title: Fairness under uncertainty in sequential decisions
comment: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 2026
summary:
Fair machine learning (ML) methods help identify and mitigate the risk that algorithms encode or automate social injustices. Algorithmic approaches alone cannot resolve structural inequalities, but they can support socio-technical decision systems by surfacing discriminatory biases, clarifying trade-offs, and enabling governance. Although fairness is well studied in supervised learning, many real ML applications are online and sequential, with prior decisions informing future ones. Each decision is taken under uncertainty due to unobserved counterfactuals and finite samples, with dire consequences for under-represented groups, systematically under-observed due to historical exclusion and selective feedback. A bank cannot know whether a denied loan would have been repaid, and may have less data on marginalized populations.
  This paper introduces a taxonomy of uncertainty in sequential decision-making -- model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty -- providing shared vocabulary for assessing systems where uncertainty is unevenly distributed across groups. We formalize model and feedback uncertainty via counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning, and illustrate harms to decision makers (unrealized gains/losses) and subjects (compounding exclusion, reduced access) of policies that ignore the unobserved space. Algorithmic examples show it is possible to reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives (e.g. expected utility). Experiments on data simulated with varying bias show how unequal uncertainty and selective feedback produce disparities, and how uncertainty-aware exploration alters fairness metrics. The framework equips practitioners to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks. Where uncertainty drives unfairness rather than incidental noise, accounting for it is essential to fair and effective decision-making.

AI.tldr: Proposes a taxonomy and formal framework for uncertainty in sequential fair ML, combining counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning to analyze model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty; shows how ignoring unobserved outcomes harms disadvantaged groups and how uncertainty-aware approaches can reduce disparities while preserving objectives; offers diagnostic governance tools for fairness risks.
AI.motivation: Fairness research is strong in static supervised settings, but real ML systems act online and sequentially under uneven data across groups. Unobserved counterfactuals and selective feedback create unequal uncertainty, risking discriminatory outcomes. A unified framing is needed for diagnosis, auditing, and governance.
AI.method: Define a taxonomy of uncertainty (model, feedback, prediction). Formalize using counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning. Provide algorithmic examples and experiments with biased simulated data to illustrate disparities and the effect of uncertainty-aware exploration. Propose a framework to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks.
AI.result: Greater recognition that unequal uncertainty and selective feedback produce disparities in fairness metrics. Uncertainty-aware exploration can reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while maintaining institutional objectives (e.g., expected utility). The framework enables practitioners to diagnose and govern fairness risks and to adjust policies when uncertainty drives unfairness.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for uncertainty is essential for fair and effective sequential decision-making. The proposed taxonomy and framework support diagnosis, auditing, and governance of fairness risks in online systems, particularly where unobserved space drives disparity.

title: Discriminative-Generative Synergy for Occlusion Robust 3D Human Mesh Recovery
summary: 3D human mesh recovery from monocular RGB images aims to estimate anatomically plausible 3D human models for downstream applications, but remains challenging under partial or severe occlusions. Regression-based methods are efficient yet often produce implausible or inaccurate results in unconstrained scenarios, while diffusion-based methods provide strong generative priors for occluded regions but may weaken fidelity to rare poses due to over-reliance on generation. To address these limitations, we propose a brain-inspired synergistic framework that integrates the discriminative power of vision transformers with the generative capability of conditional diffusion models. Specifically, the ViT-based pathway extracts deterministic visual cues from visible regions, while the diffusion-based pathway synthesizes structurally coherent human body representations. To effectively bridge the two pathways, we design a diverse-consistent feature learning module to align discriminative features with generative priors, and a cross-attention multi-level fusion mechanism to enable bidirectional interaction across semantic levels. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on key metrics and shows strong robustness in complex real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: A brain-inspired dual-path framework integrates ViT-driven discriminative features with diffusion-based generative priors for robust 3D human mesh recovery under occlusion, using diversity-consistent feature alignment and cross-level fusion.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of regression-based methods (plausibility and accuracy in unconstrained scenes) and diffusion models (fidelity loss due to over-generation) in monocular 3D human reconstruction.
AI.method: A two-path pipeline: (1) ViT-based discriminative pathway extracts deterministic cues from visible regions; (2) diffusion-based pathway synthesizes structurally coherent body representations via conditional generation. Bridging is achieved with a diverse-consistent feature learning module that aligns features with generative priors and a cross-attention multi-level fusion mechanism enabling bidirectional interaction across semantic levels.
AI.result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show superior performance on key metrics and strong robustness in complex real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that integrating discriminative vision transformers with conditional diffusion priors, through feature alignment and cross-level fusion, yields improved accuracy and robustness for occluded 3D human mesh recovery.

title: Inferring High-Level Events from Timestamped Data: Complexity and Medical Applications
comment: This is the full version (with appendix) of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: In this paper, we develop a novel logic-based approach to detecting high-level temporally extended events from timestamped data and background knowledge. Our framework employs logical rules to capture existence and termination conditions for simple temporal events and to combine these into meta-events. In the medical domain, for example, disease episodes and therapies are inferred from timestamped clinical observations, such as diagnoses and drug administrations stored in patient records, and can be further combined into higher-level disease events. As some incorrect events might be inferred, we use constraints to identify incompatible combinations of events and propose a repair mechanism to select preferred consistent sets of events. While reasoning in the full framework is intractable, we identify relevant restrictions that ensure polynomial-time data complexity. Our prototype system implements core components of the approach using answer set programming. An evaluation on a lung cancer use case supports the interest of the approach, both in terms of computational feasibility and positive alignment of our results with medical expert opinions. While strongly motivated by the needs of the healthcare domain, our framework is purposely generic, enabling its reuse in other areas.

AI.tldr: A logic-based framework using ASP to detect temporally extended events from timestamped data with background knowledge; it defines existence and termination rules for simple temporal events, composes meta-events, and employs constraint-based repairs to handle incorrect inferences. Although reasoning is intractable in full generality, the authors identify restrictions yielding polynomial-time data complexity. A prototype in ASP and a lung cancer use case show feasibility and alignment with clinician opinions, with a generic, domain-agnostic design.
AI.motivation: Detect high-level temporally extended events in timestamped clinical data by leveraging background knowledge to infer disease episodes and therapies as meta-events. Address inconsistencies through constraints and repairs, balancing expressive power with computational tractability.
AI.method: Logic-based rules encode existence and termination of simple temporal events; rules combine them into meta-events. Constraints eliminate incompatible event combinations; a repair mechanism selects preferred consistent sets. The approach is implemented in answer set programming (ASP); tractability is pursued by identifying restrictions that yield polynomial-time data complexity.
AI.result: A prototype ASP-based system implementing core components; evaluation on a lung cancer use case demonstrates practical feasibility and results that align with medical expert judgments.
AI.conclusion: The framework is generic and reusable beyond healthcare, allowing structured temporal event detection in various domains while remaining mindful of computational limits.

title: Transferable Physics-Informed Representations via Closed-Form Head Adaptation
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have garnered significant interest for their potential in solving partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern a wide range of physical phenomena. By incorporating physical laws into the learning process, PINN models have demonstrated the ability to learn physical outcomes reasonably well. However, current PINN approaches struggle to predict or solve new PDEs effectively when there is a lack of training examples, indicating they do not generalize well to unseen problem instances. In this paper, we present a transferable learning approach for PINNs premised on a fast Pseudoinverse PINN framework (Pi-PINN). Pi-PINN learns a transferable physics-informed representation in a shared embedding space and enables rapid solving of both known and unknown PDE instances via closed-form head adaptation using a least-squares-optimal pseudoinverse under PDE constraints. We further investigate the synergies between data-driven multi-task learning loss and physics-informed loss, providing insights into the design of more performant PINNs. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Pi-PINN on various PDE problems, including Poisson's equation, Helmholtz equation, and Burgers' equation, achieving fast and accurate physics-informed solutions without requiring any data for unseen instances. Pi-PINN can produce predictions 100-1000 times faster than a typical PINN, while producing predictions with 10-100 times lower relative error than a typical data-driven model even with only two training samples. Overall, our findings highlight the potential of transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation to enhance the efficiency and generalization of PINNs across PDE families and scientific and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: Pi-PINN introduces a transferable physics-informed representation with a closed-form pseudoinverse head to rapidly adapt to unseen PDEs, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups and improved accuracy with minimal data, across Poisson, Helmholtz, and Burgers' equations.
AI.motivation: Address the poor generalization of standard PINNs to unseen PDE instances and data-scarce settings; enable rapid, data-free adaptation across PDE families via a shared physics-informed representation.
AI.method: Train a shared embedding encoding physics; perform closed-form head adaptation via a least-squares pseudoinverse under PDE constraints to solve new instances; study the interaction between data-driven multi-task loss and physics-informed loss.
AI.result: On Poisson, Helmholtz, and Burgers' equations, Pi-PINN achieves 100–1000x speedups and 10–100x lower relative error than typical data-driven models, even with only two training samples and without data for unseen instances.
AI.conclusion: Transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation can enhance speed and generalization of PINNs across PDE families, enabling fast, physics-consistent inference for diverse scientific and engineering applications.

title: Unlocking the Power of Critical Factors for 3D Visual Geometry Estimation
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. GitHub Page: https://github.com/aim-uofa/CARVE
summary: Feed-forward visual geometry estimation has recently made rapid progress. However, an important gap remains: multi-frame models usually produce better cross-frame consistency, yet they often underperform strong per-frame methods on single-frame accuracy. This observation motivates our systematic investigation into the critical factors driving model performance through rigorous ablation studies, which reveals several key insights: 1) Scaling up data diversity and quality unlocks further performance gains even in state-of-the-art visual geometry estimation methods; 2) Commonly adopted confidence-aware loss and gradient-based loss mechanisms may unintentionally hinder performance; 3) Joint supervision through both per-sequence and per-frame alignment improves results, while local region alignment surprisingly degrades performance. Furthermore, we introduce two enhancements to integrate the advantages of optimization-based methods and high-resolution inputs: a consistency loss function that enforces alignment between depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps, and an efficient architectural design that leverages high-resolution information. We integrate these designs into CARVE, a resolution-enhanced model for feed-forward visual geometry estimation. Experiments on point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation show that CARVE achieves strong and robust performance across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CARVE is a resolution-enhanced feed-forward model for visual geometry estimation that blends optimization-based consistency with high-resolution inputs, achieving strong, robust results across point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsics estimation; ablations underscore the roles of data diversity, loss design, and joint supervision.
AI.motivation: Multi-frame methods offer better cross-frame consistency but often lag behind strong per-frame methods in single-frame accuracy. A systematic ablation study is needed to identify factors that govern performance and to close the gap between per-frame accuracy and cross-frame consistency.
AI.method: Introduce a resolution-enhanced feed-forward architecture (CARVE) with a consistency loss that enforces alignment among depth maps, camera parameters, and point maps, and an efficient design to leverage high-resolution information. Perform comprehensive ablations on data diversity/quality, loss mechanisms, and supervision schemes, including per-sequence and per-frame supervision and local region alignment.
AI.result: CARVE achieves strong and robust performance across diverse benchmarks in point cloud reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose/intrinsic estimation, demonstrating improved cross-frame consistency without sacrificing single-frame accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Data diversity and supervision strategies critically influence performance; alignment constraints across representations can improve consistency; high-resolution, efficient architectures can leverage detailed cues. CARVE provides a practical pathway to merge optimization-based insights with feed-forward design for visual geometry estimation.

title: Learning to Communicate: Toward End-to-End Optimization of Multi-Agent Language Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems built on large language models have shown strong performance on complex reasoning tasks, yet most work focuses on agent roles and orchestration while treating inter-agent communication as a fixed interface. Latent communication through internal representations such as key-value caches offers a promising alternative to text-based protocols, but existing approaches do not jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning. Therefore we propose DiffMAS, a training framework that treats latent communication as a learnable component of multi-agent systems. DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling agents to jointly learn how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions. Experiments on mathematical reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense benchmarks show that DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods, achieving 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, and consistent gains across reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffMAS learns latent communication among LLM-based agents via parameter-efficient supervision over multi-agent latent trajectories, jointly optimizing how information is encoded and interpreted. It yields stronger reasoning accuracy and decoding stability than single-agent inference, text-only multi-agent systems, and prior latent-communication methods, e.g., 26.7% on AIME24 and 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent systems rely on fixed, text-based inter-agent interfaces. Latent communication through internal representations offers a more flexible, potentially more informative channel, but existing work does not jointly optimize communication with reasoning. There is a need for end-to-end learning of how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions.
AI.method: Introduce DiffMAS, a training framework that treats latent communication as a learnable component. It uses parameter-efficient supervised training on multi-agent latent trajectories to jointly optimize how information is encoded and interpreted across interactions.
AI.result: DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over baselines: single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods. Reported gains include 26.7% on AIME24 and 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, with consistent improvements across reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Learning latent communication within DiffMAS enhances multi-agent reasoning and decoding stability, outperforming existing approaches and demonstrating robust gains across diverse reasoning tasks.

title: PrismaDV: Automated Task-Aware Data Unit Test Generation
summary:
Data is a central resource for modern enterprises, and data validation is essential for ensuring the reliability of downstream applications. However, existing automated data unit testing frameworks are largely task-agnostic: they validate datasets without considering the semantics and requirements of the code that consumes the data.
  We present PrismaDV, a compound AI system that analyzes downstream task code together with dataset profiles to identify data access patterns, infer implicit data assumptions, and generate task-aware executable data unit tests. To further adapt the data unit tests over time to specific datasets and downstream tasks, we propose "Selective Informative Feedback for Task Adaptation" (SIFTA), a prompt-optimization framework that leverages the scarce outcomes from the execution of data unit tests and downstream tasks. We evaluate PrismaDV on two new benchmarks spanning 60 tasks across five datasets, where it consistently outperforms both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines in generating unit tests that reflect the end-to-end impact of data errors. Furthermore, we show that with SIFTA, we can automatically learn prompts for PrismaDV's modules that outperform prompts written by hand or generated from a generic prompt optimizer. We publicly release our benchmarks and prototype implementation.

AI.tldr: PrismaDV with SIFTA enables task-aware data unit tests by analyzing downstream code and data profiles, outperforming task-agnostic baselines; benchmarks released.
AI.motivation: Existing data validation frameworks are largely task-agnostic and ignore the semantic requirements of downstream code; there is a need for tests that reflect how data is used in real tasks to improve reliability.
AI.method: PrismaDV analyzes downstream task code alongside dataset profiles to identify data access patterns and implicit data assumptions, generating executable, task-aware data unit tests. SIFTA (Selective Informative Feedback for Task Adaptation) optimizes prompts using scarce feedback from test executions to adapt prompts over time.
AI.result: On two new benchmarks spanning 60 tasks across five datasets, PrismaDV outperforms both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines in generating unit tests reflecting end-to-end data error impacts. SIFTA automatically learns prompts for PrismaDV’s modules that surpass hand-written or generic-prompt prompts. Benchmarks and prototype released.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware data unit testing with adaptive prompting improves reliability by aligning tests with downstream data use; the approach outperforms baselines and provides reusable benchmarks and tools.

title: Building a Precise Video Language with Human-AI Oversight
summary: Video-language models (VLMs) learn to reason about the dynamic visual world through natural language. We introduce a suite of open datasets, benchmarks, and recipes for scalable oversight that enable precise video captioning. First, we define a structured specification for describing subjects, scenes, motion, spatial, and camera dynamics, grounded by hundreds of carefully defined visual primitives developed with professional video creators such as filmmakers. Next, to curate high-quality captions, we introduce CHAI (Critique-based Human-AI Oversight), a framework where trained experts critique and revise model-generated pre-captions into improved post-captions. This division of labor improves annotation accuracy and efficiency by offloading text generation to models, allowing humans to better focus on verification. Additionally, these critiques and preferences between pre- and post-captions provide rich supervision for improving open-source models (Qwen3-VL) on caption generation, reward modeling, and critique generation through SFT, DPO, and inference-time scaling. Our ablations show that critique quality in precision, recall, and constructiveness, ensured by our oversight framework, directly governs downstream performance. With modest expert supervision, the resulting model outperforms closed-source models such as Gemini-3.1-Pro. Finally, we apply our approach to re-caption large-scale professional videos (e.g., films, commercials, games) and fine-tune video generation models such as Wan to better follow detailed prompts of up to 400 words, achieving finer control over cinematography including camera motion, angle, lens, focus, point of view, and framing. Our results show that precise specification and human-AI oversight are key to professional-level video understanding and generation. Data and code are available on our project page: https://linzhiqiu.github.io/papers/chai/

AI.tldr: Introduces CHAI: a critique-based human-AI oversight framework and a structured visual primitive spec for scalable, precise video captioning; demonstrates that expert critique improves caption quality and model performance, surpassing some closed-source baselines, and extends to professional video re-captioning and controlled video generation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for precise, scalable video captioning and reliable evaluation by combining structured visual primitives with human-in-the-loop critique to improve open-source VLMs.
AI.method: Define a structured specification for subjects, scenes, motion, spatial and camera dynamics using visual primitives; implement CHAI where experts critique and revise pre-captions into post-captions; train/open-source models (Qwen3-VL) with supervision from pre-/post-caption critiques via SFT, DPO, and inference-time scaling; conduct ablations on critique quality; apply to professional videos and fine-tune Wan-based video generation for detailed prompts.
AI.result: Critique-based oversight improves annotation accuracy/efficiency; modest expert supervision enables open-source models to outperform certain closed-source models (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro); critique quality correlates with downstream performance; re-captioning professional videos and improving video generation yields finer cinematography control.
AI.conclusion: Structured representation plus human-AI oversight is crucial for professional-level video understanding and generation; the CHAI framework enhances captioning quality, model training signals, and controllable generation, with practical applicability to large-scale media.

title: An effective variant of the Hartigan $k$-means algorithm
summary: The k-means problem is perhaps the classical clustering problem and often synonymous with Lloyd's algorithm (1957). It has become clear that Hartigan's algorithm (1975) gives better results in almost all cases, Telgarsky-Vattani note a typical improvement of $5\%$ -- $10\%$. We point out that a very minor variation of Hartigan's method leads to another $2\%$ -- $5\%$ improvement; the improvement tends to become larger when either dimension or $k$ increase.

AI.tldr: Hartigan's k-means variant generally outperforms Lloyd's algorithm by 5-10% in typical cases; a minor modification of Hartigan's method yields an additional 2-5% improvement, with larger gains as dimension or number of clusters increases.
AI.motivation: Evaluate practical performance differences among classic k-means algorithms and quantify potential benefits from small algorithmic variations.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of Lloyd's algorithm, Hartigan's algorithm, and a slight variant of Hartigan's method across datasets with varying dimensionality and cluster count, reporting improvements in clustering objective.
AI.result: Hartigan's method improves over Lloyd's by about 5-10% on typical datasets; a minor variation of Hartigan's method adds an additional 2-5% improvement, with larger improvements observed as dimension or k increases.
AI.conclusion: Hartigan's algorithm is generally superior in practice; small, well-chosen variations can yield meaningful gains, particularly in high-dimensional or large-k scenarios; these findings motivate further empirical and theoretical study of k-means variants.

title: Ramen: Robust Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models with Active Sample Selection
summary: Pretrained vision-language models such as CLIP exhibit strong zero-shot generalization but remain sensitive to distribution shifts. Test-time adaptation adapts models during inference without access to source data or target labels, offering a practical way to handle such shifts. However, existing methods typically assume that test samples come from a single, consistent domain, while in practice, test data often include samples from mixed domains with distinct characteristics. Consequently, their performance degrades under mixed-domain settings. To address this, we present Ramen, a framework for robust test-time adaptation through active sample selection. For each incoming test sample, Ramen retrieves a customized batch of relevant samples from previously seen data based on two criteria: domain consistency, which ensures that adaptation focuses on data from similar domains, and prediction balance, which mitigates adaptation bias caused by skewed predictions. To improve efficiency, Ramen employs an embedding-gradient cache that stores the embeddings and sample-level gradients of past test images. The stored embeddings are used to retrieve relevant samples, and the corresponding gradients are aggregated for model updates, eliminating the need for any additional forward or backward passes. Our theoretical analysis provides insight into why the proposed adaptation mechanism is effective under mixed-domain shifts. Experiments on multiple image corruption and domain-shift benchmarks demonstrate that Ramen achieves strong and consistent performance, offering robust and efficient adaptation in complex mixed-domain scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/baowenxuan/Ramen .

AI.tldr: Ramen is a robust test-time adaptation framework for vision-language models under mixed-domain shifts. It selects domain-consistent samples for adaptation and uses an embedding-gradient cache to update the model efficiently without extra forward/backward passes, achieving strong performance on mixed-domain benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address distribution shifts in vision-language models, existing TTA assumes a single domain, but real-world data are mixed-domain; thus robust, efficient adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Active sample selection across past data using two criteria: domain consistency (domains similar to current input) and prediction balance (mitigates bias from skewed predictions). An embedding-gradient cache stores embeddings and per-sample gradients to enable retrieval and gradient aggregation for updates without new forward/backward passes.
AI.result: Empirical results on image corruption and domain-shift benchmarks show strong, consistent gains; the method is efficient due to no extra forward/backward passes; theoretical analysis provides intuition for effectiveness; code available.
AI.conclusion: Ramen enables robust test-time adaptation in mixed-domain settings by leveraging selective past-data retrieval and gradient aggregation, combining effectiveness with computational efficiency.

title: Alignment has a Fantasia Problem
summary: Modern AI assistants are trained to follow instructions, implicitly assuming that users can clearly articulate their goals and the kind of assistance they need. Decades of behavioral research, however, show that people often engage with AI systems before their goals are fully formed. When AI systems treat prompts as complete expressions of intent, they can appear to be useful or convenient, but not necessarily aligned with the users' needs.
