We call these failures Fantasia interactions. We argue that Fantasia interactions demand a rethinking of alignment research: rather than treating users as rational oracles, AI should provide cognitive support by actively helping users form and refine their intent through time. This requires an interdisciplinary approach that bridges machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science. We synthesize insights from these fields to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions. We then show why existing interventions are insufficient, and propose a research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that better help humans navigate uncertainty in their tasks.

AI.tldr: We introduce Fantasia interactions, where user intent is under-formed; AI should evolve from prompt compliance to cognitive support that helps users form/refine goals over time; propose an interdisciplinary research agenda combining ML, HCI, and behavioral science.
AI.motivation: Users often engage AI before goals are fully formed; treating prompts as complete intent leads to misalignment; a rethinking of alignment is needed to support task uncertainty and evolving goals.
AI.method: Synthesize insights across machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science to characterize mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions; analyze why existing interventions are insufficient; articulate a cross-disciplinary research agenda.
AI.result: A conceptual framework delineating Fantasia interactions, including failure modes and mechanisms; critical assessment of current interventions; a proposed research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that help users form and refine their intent under uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: AI alignment should prioritize cognitive support for intent formation over time, demanding interdisciplinary methods and new evaluation paradigms to assist users as their goals evolve and remain uncertain.

title: Quotient-Space Diffusion Models
comment: ICLR 2026 Oral Presentation; 40 pages, 5 figures, 6 tables
summary: Diffusion-based generative models have reformed generative AI, and have enabled new capabilities in the science domain, for example, generating 3D structures of molecules. Due to the intrinsic problem structure of certain tasks, there is often a symmetry in the system, which identifies objects that can be converted by a group action as equivalent, hence the target distribution is essentially defined on the quotient space with respect to the group. In this work, we establish a formal framework for diffusion modeling on a general quotient space, and apply it to molecular structure generation which follows the special Euclidean group $\text{SE}(3)$ symmetry. The framework reduces the necessity of learning the component corresponding to the group action, hence simplifies learning difficulty over conventional group-equivariant diffusion models, and the sampler guarantees recovering the target distribution, while heuristic alignment strategies lack proper samplers. The arguments are empirically validated on structure generation for small molecules and proteins, indicating that the principled quotient-space diffusion model provides a new framework that outperforms previous symmetry treatments.

AI.tldr: Proposes a diffusion framework on quotient spaces to handle symmetry (e.g., SE(3)) in molecular structure generation; factors out the group-action, reducing learning burden, and provides a sampler with convergence guarantees, showing improved results over prior symmetry methods on small molecules and proteins.
AI.motivation: Symmetries create equivalence under group actions, making the natural state space a quotient. Modeling directly on the quotient avoids redundant learning and can improve sample quality and efficiency in diffusion-based generation, particularly for molecular structures with SE(3) symmetry.
AI.method: Develop a general theory for diffusion on quotient spaces induced by a group action; instantiate the framework for SE(3) in molecular structure generation; design a diffusion process that operates on the quotient and a corresponding sampler with convergence guarantees; argue that this obviates the need to learn the group-action component and contrasts with heuristic alignment in existing symmetry-aware methods; validate empirically on small molecules and proteins.
AI.result: A principled quotient-space diffusion framework reduces the learned components required (group action) and provides samplers that provably recover the target distribution; empirical results indicate improved performance over previous symmetry-handling approaches in molecular structure generation.
AI.conclusion: A principled quotient-space diffusion approach offers a robust framework for symmetry-aware generative modeling in chemistry and biology, with potential applicability to broader symmetry groups. It complements existing equivariant methods by focusing learning on the quotient space and delivering convergence guarantees, though practical scalability and handling of more complex group actions warrant further study.

title: Interpretable facial dynamics as behavioral and perceptual traces of deepfakes
comment: Main paper: 19 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. SI Appendix: 11 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables
summary: Deepfake detection research has largely converged on deep learning approaches that, despite strong benchmark performance, offer limited insight into what distinguishes real from manipulated facial behavior. This study presents an interpretable alternative grounded in bio-behavioral features of facial dynamics and evaluates how computational detection strategies relate to human perceptual judgments. We identify core low-dimensional patterns of facial movement, from which temporal features characterizing spatiotemporal structure were derived. Traditional machine learning classifiers trained on these features achieved modest but significant above-chance deepfake classification, driven by higher-order temporal irregularities that were more pronounced in manipulated than real facial dynamics. Notably, detection was substantially more accurate for videos containing emotive expressions than those without. An emotional valence classification analysis further indicated that emotive signals are systematically degraded in deepfakes, explaining the differential impact of emotive dynamics on detection. Furthermore, we provide an additional and often overlooked dimension of explainability by assessing the relationship between model decisions and human perceptual detection. Model and human judgments converged for emotive but diverged for non-emotive videos, and even where outputs aligned, underlying detection strategies differed. These findings demonstrate that face-swapped deepfakes carry a measurable behavioral fingerprint, most salient during emotional expression. Additionally, model-human comparisons suggest that interpretable computational features and human perception may offer complementary rather than redundant routes to detection.

AI.tldr: Deepfake detection benefits from interpretable bio-behavioral facial dynamics features, revealing emotion-driven differences and partial alignment with human perception.
AI.motivation: To move beyond opaque DL classifiers by grounding detection in interpretable behavioral features and to compare model decisions with human perceptual judgments on emotive versus non-emotive expressions.
AI.method: Identify low-dimensional facial movement patterns, derive spatiotemporal features, train traditional ML classifiers on these features, analyze performance across emotive and non-emotive videos, perform emotional valence classification, and compare model decisions with human judgments.
AI.result: Above-chance deepfake detection using interpretable features; higher-order temporal irregularities are amplified in manipulated dynamics; detection is stronger for emotive expressions; emotional valence signals are degraded in deepfakes; model-human convergence for emotive content but divergence for non-emotive content; when aligned, strategies differ, suggesting complementary explanatory value.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable computational features capture a behavioral fingerprint of deepfakes, especially during emotional expression, and together with human perception offer complementary paths to detection.

title: Bounding the Black Box: A Statistical Certification Framework for AI Risk Regulation
summary:
Artificial intelligence now decides who receives a loan, who is flagged for criminal investigation, and whether an autonomous vehicle brakes in time. Governments have responded: the EU AI Act, the NIST Risk Management Framework, and the Council of Europe Convention all demand that high-risk systems demonstrate safety before deployment. Yet beneath this regulatory consensus lies a critical vacuum: none specifies what ``acceptable risk'' means in quantitative terms, and none provides a technical method for verifying that a deployed system actually meets such a threshold. The regulatory architecture is in place; the verification instrument is not.
  This gap is not theoretical. As the EU AI Act moves into full enforcement, developers face mandatory conformity assessments without established methodologies for producing quantitative safety evidence - and the systems most in need of oversight are opaque statistical inference engines that resist white-box scrutiny.
  This paper provides the missing instrument. Drawing on the aviation certification paradigm, we propose a two-stage framework that transforms AI risk regulation into engineering practice. In Stage One, a competent authority formally fixes an acceptable failure probability $δ$ and an operational input domain $\varepsilon$ - a normative act with direct civil liability implications. In Stage Two, the RoMA and gRoMA statistical verification tools compute a definitive, auditable upper bound on the system's true failure rate, requiring no access to model internals and scaling to arbitrary architectures. We demonstrate how this certificate satisfies existing regulatory obligations, shifts accountability upstream to developers, and integrates with the legal frameworks that exist today.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-stage RoMA/gRoMA-based framework to quantify and certify AI safety by setting an acceptable failure probability and using statistical verification to bound the true failure rate without accessing model internals.
AI.motivation: Current high-stakes AI regulation lacks concrete quantitative risk thresholds and verifiable methods to demonstrate safety, creating a gap between regulation and deployment.
AI.method: Stage One: a competent authority fixes an acceptable failure probability δ and an operational input domain ε. Stage Two: RoMA and gRoMA compute an auditable upper bound on the true failure rate, without requiring access to model internals and scalable to arbitrary architectures; yields a formal certificate.
AI.result: A verifiable certificate that bounds the system's true failure rate, aligns with regulatory requirements, delegates accountability to developers, and integrates with existing legal frameworks.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges regulation and engineering practice, offering a practical, auditable method for demonstrating AI safety; its success depends on regulatory adoption and robust statistical guarantees.

title: Probably Approximately Consensus: On the Learning Theory of Finding Common Ground
comment: Accepted to the Social Choice and Learning Algorithms Workshop at IJCAI 2025
summary: A primary goal of online deliberation platforms is to identify ideas that are broadly agreeable to a community of users through their expressed preferences. Yet, consensus elicitation should ideally extend beyond the specific statements provided by users and should incorporate the relative salience of particular topics. We address this issue by modelling consensus as an interval in a one-dimensional opinion space derived from potentially high-dimensional data via embedding and dimensionality reduction. We define an objective that maximizes expected agreement within a hypothesis interval where the expectation is over an underlying distribution of issues, implicitly taking into account their salience. We propose an efficient Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) algorithm and establish PAC-learning guarantees. Our initial experiments demonstrate the performance of our algorithm and examine more efficient approaches to identifying optimal consensus regions. We find that through selectively querying users on an existing sample of statements, we can reduce the number of queries needed to a practical number.

AI.tldr: Efficient PAC-learnable method to identify consensus intervals in an embedded opinion space, optimizing expected agreement under issue salience; demonstrates query-efficient performance.
AI.motivation: Consensus elicitation should reflect topic salience and scalability. Modeling consensus as an interval in a reduced opinion space captures both agreement and the relative importance of topics in a community.
AI.method: Embed high-dimensional statements into a one-dimensional space via dimensionality reduction, define a hypothesis interval as the consensus region, and maximize the expected agreement within this interval over the distribution of issues. Propose an empirical risk minimization (ERM) algorithm with PAC-learning guarantees and evaluate experimentally, including selective querying on an existing sample to reduce query complexity.
AI.result: Algorithm demonstrates favorable performance with PAC guarantees; explores efficient approaches for identifying optimal consensus regions; selective querying reduces the number of queries to a practical level.
AI.conclusion: Interval-based, salience-aware consensus modeling is viable and scalable for online deliberation, with provable learning guarantees and improved query efficiency; future work may optimize interval search and sampling strategies.

title: Back to Source: Open-Set Continual Test-Time Adaptation via Domain Compensation
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) aims to mitigate distributional shifts between training and test domains during inference time. However, existing TTA methods fall short in the realistic scenario where models face both continually changing domains and the simultaneous emergence of unknown semantic classes, a challenging setting we term Open-set Continual Test-Time Adaptation (OCTTA). The coupling of domain and semantic shifts often collapses the feature space, severely degrading both classification and out-of-distribution detection. To tackle this, we propose DOmain COmpensation (DOCO), a lightweight and effective framework that robustly performs domain adaptation and OOD detection in a synergistic, closed loop. DOCO first performs dynamic, adaptation-conditioned sample splitting to separate likely ID from OOD samples. Then, using only the ID samples, it learns a domain compensation prompt by aligning feature statistics with the source domain, guided by a structural preservation regularizer that prevents semantic distortion. This learned prompt is then propagated to the OOD samples within the same batch, effectively isolating their semantic novelty for more reliable detection. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging benchmarks demonstrate that DOCO outperforms prior CTTA and OSTTA methods, establishing a new state-of-the-art for the demanding OCTTA setting.

AI.tldr: DOCO: a lightweight prompt-based framework for Open-set Continual Test-Time Adaptation (OCTTA) that jointly handles domain shifts and unseen classes via adaptation-conditioned sample splitting, a domain-compensation prompt learned from ID data to align features with the source domain while preserving semantics, and propagation to OOD samples for improved detection; achieves new SOTA on OCTTA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In OCTTA, distributional shifts are continual and unknown semantic classes emerge simultaneously, causing feature-space collapse and degraded classification and OOD detection. Existing TTA methods struggle due to coupling of domain and semantic shifts; there is a need for a robust, efficient method that can separate and address domain adaptation and open-set detection in a continual setting.
AI.method: DOCO consists of: (1) dynamic, adaptation-conditioned sample splitting to separate likely in-distribution (ID) from out-of-distribution (OOD) samples within a batch; (2) learning a domain-compensation prompt using only ID samples by aligning feature statistics with the source domain, guided by a structural preservation regularizer to minimize semantic distortion; (3) propagating the learned prompt to OOD samples in the same batch to isolate their semantic novelty and improve detection; (4) a lightweight, closed-loop framework that jointly supports domain adaptation and OOD detection.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple challenging OCTTA benchmarks shows DOCO outperforms prior CTTA and OSTTA methods, establishing a new state-of-the-art for OCTTA.
AI.conclusion: DOCO provides an effective, efficient solution for OCTTA by decoupling domain adaptation from OOD detection within a closed-loop, using a prompt-based domain compensation learned from ID data and applied to OOD samples to enhance semantic novelty isolation and detection.

title: Nemobot Games: Crafting Strategic AI Gaming Agents for Interactive Learning with Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces a new paradigm for AI game programming, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to extend and operationalize Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines. Central to this paradigm is Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that enables users to create, customize, and deploy LLM-powered game agents while actively engaging with AI-driven strategies. The LLM-based chatbot, integrated within Nemobot, demonstrates its capabilities across four distinct classes of games. For dictionary-based games, it compresses state-action mappings into efficient, generalized models for rapid adaptability. In rigorously solvable games, it employs mathematical reasoning to compute optimal strategies and generates human-readable explanations for its decisions. For heuristic-based games, it synthesizes strategies by combining insights from classical minimax algorithms (see, e.g., shannon1950chess) with crowd-sourced data. Finally, in learning-based games, it utilizes reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique to iteratively refine strategies through trial-and-error and imitation learning. Nemobot amplifies this framework by offering a programmable environment where users can experiment with tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning of strategic game agents. From strategic games to role-playing games, Nemobot demonstrates how AI agents can achieve a form of self-programming by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine their own logic. This represents a step toward the long-term goal of self-programming AI.

AI.tldr: New Paradigm Nemobot: an LLM-powered interactive environment to operationalize Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines, enabling four game classes (dictionary, rigorously solvable, heuristic, learning-based) with self-programming and crowdsourced refinement.
AI.motivation: Extend Claude Shannon's taxonomy into practical AI game agents, enabling adaptable, explainable, and self-improving gameplay agents; leverage user interaction, tool augmentation, and human feedback.
AI.method: An LLM-powered chatbot within Nemobot drives agents across four classes: dictionary games—compress state-action mappings into generalized models; rigor solvable games—use mathematical reasoning to compute optimal strategies with human-readable explanations; heuristic games—synthesize strategies via minimax concepts with crowd-sourced data; learning-based games—RL with human feedback, self-critique, and imitation learning; Nemobot provides a programmable environment for tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning.
AI.result: Demonstrates capability of LLM-powered agents across four game classes, including explanations for decisions and a pathway toward iterative self-improvement through crowdsourced learning and human feedback.
AI.conclusion: Nemobot represents a step toward self-programming AI, showing how AI agents can integrate crowdsourcing and human creativity to iteratively refine their logic across strategic and role-playing games, potentially advancing toward autonomous self-programming.

title: GFlowState: Visualizing the Training of Generative Flow Networks Beyond the Reward
summary: We present GFlowState, a visual analytics system designed to illuminate the training process of Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets or GFNs). GFlowNets are a probabilistic framework for generating samples proportionally to a reward function. While GFlowNets have proved to be powerful tools in applications such as molecule and material discovery, their training dynamics remain difficult to interpret. Standard machine learning tools allow metric tracking but do not reveal how models explore the sample space, construct sample trajectories, or shift sampling probabilities during training. Our solution, GFlowState, allows users to analyze sampling trajectories, compare the sample space relative to reference datasets, and analyze the training dynamics. To this end, we introduce multiple views, including a chart of candidate rankings, a state projection, a node-link diagram of the trajectory network, and a transition heatmap. These visualizations enable GFlowNet developers and users to investigate sampling behavior and policy evolution, and to identify underexplored regions and sources of training failure. Case studies demonstrate how the system supports debugging and assessing the quality of GFlowNets across application domains. By making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable, our work enhances their interpretability and can accelerate GFlowNet development in practice.

AI.tldr: GFlowState visualizes training dynamics of Generative Flow Networks to interpret sampling trajectories and policy evolution, enabling debugging and improved interpretability.
AI.motivation: GFlowNets generate samples proportional to a reward, but their training dynamics are opaque; standard metrics reveal little about how the model explores the sample space, constructs trajectories, or shifts sampling probabilities. A visual analytics tool is needed to reveal these dynamics and guide development.
AI.method: Develop GFlowState with multiple views: a candidate-ranking chart, a state projection, a node-link diagram of the trajectory network, and a transition heatmap; enable analysis of sampling trajectories, comparison to reference datasets, and monitoring training dynamics; supported by case studies.
AI.result: Case studies demonstrate debugging and assessment of GFlowNets across domains; the system enhances interpretability and can accelerate development by highlighting underexplored regions and sources of training failure.
AI.conclusion: Making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable improves interpretability and accelerates practical development.

title: Reshoot-Anything: A Self-Supervised Model for In-the-Wild Video Reshooting
summary: Precise camera control for reshooting dynamic videos is bottlenecked by the severe scarcity of paired multi-view data for non-rigid scenes. We overcome this limitation with a highly scalable self-supervised framework capable of leveraging internet-scale monocular videos. Our core contribution is the generation of pseudo multi-view training triplets, consisting of a source video, a geometric anchor, and a target video. We achieve this by extracting distinct smooth random-walk crop trajectories from a single input video to serve as the source and target views. The anchor is synthetically generated by forward-warping the first frame of the source with a dense tracking field, which effectively simulates the distorted point-cloud inputs expected at inference. Because our independent cropping strategy introduces spatial misalignment and artificial occlusions, the model cannot simply copy information from the current source frame. Instead, it is forced to implicitly learn 4D spatiotemporal structures by actively routing and re-projecting missing high-fidelity textures across distinct times and viewpoints from the source video to reconstruct the target. At inference, our minimally adapted diffusion transformer utilizes a 4D point-cloud derived anchor to achieve state-of-the-art temporal consistency, robust camera control, and high-fidelity novel view synthesis on complex dynamic scenes.

AI.tldr: A scalable self-supervised framework for precise camera control in dynamic videos using pseudo multi-view training triplets derived from monocular videos; achieves state-of-the-art temporal consistency and high-fidelity novel view synthesis via a diffusion transformer.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of paired multi-view data for non-rigid scenes and the desire to leverage abundant internet-scale monocular videos for training robust dynamic-scene rendering and camera control.
AI.method: Create pseudo multi-view triplets from a single video by sampling distinct smooth random-walk crop trajectories for source and target views. The anchor is generated by forward-warping the first frame using a dense tracking field, simulating distorted point-cloud inputs. Training with misalignment/occlusions forces the model to learn 4D spatiotemporal structure; inference uses a diffusion-transformer conditioned on a 4D point-cloud derived anchor to produce temporally consistent, high-fidelity novel views.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art temporal consistency, robust camera control, and high-fidelity novel view synthesis on complex dynamic scenes.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, self-supervised pipeline that leverages internet-scale monocular videos to learn 4D spatiotemporal representations for precise camera control and high-quality resynthesis of dynamic scenes, minimizing reliance on paired multi-view data.

title: From Research Question to Scientific Workflow: Leveraging Agentic AI for Science Automation
summary: Scientific workflow systems automate execution -- scheduling, fault tolerance, resource management -- but not the semantic translation that precedes it. Scientists still manually convert research questions into workflow specifications, a task requiring both domain knowledge and infrastructure expertise. We propose an agentic architecture that closes this gap through three layers: an LLM interprets natural language into structured intents (semantic layer); validated generators produce reproducible workflow DAGs (deterministic layer); and domain experts author ``Skills'': markdown documents encoding vocabulary mappings, parameter constraints, and optimization strategies (knowledge layer). This decomposition confines LLM non-determinism to intent extraction: identical intents always yield identical workflows. We implement and evaluate the architecture on the 1000 Genomes population genetics workflow and Hyperflow WMS running on Kubernetes. In an ablation study on 150 queries, Skills raise full-match intent accuracy from 44% to 83%; skill-driven deferred workflow generation reduces data transfer by 92\%; and the end-to-end pipeline completes queries on Kubernetes with LLM overhead below 15 seconds and cost under $0.001 per query.

AI.tldr: Agentic architecture for turning natural language research questions into reproducible scientific workflows via three layers: semantic (LLM-intent extraction), deterministic (validated DAG generation), and knowledge (domain-authored Skills). Evaluated on 1000 Genomes/hyperflow Kubernetes; Skills improve intent accuracy (44%→83%), reduce data transfer (92%), and enable fast, low-cost queries (<15s, <$0.001).
AI.motivation: Scientists struggle to transform research questions into workflow specifications; existing workflow systems automate execution but not the semantic translation, leading to non-deterministic, manually curated mappings.
AI.method: Three-layer architecture: semantic layer uses LLM to extract intents from NL; deterministic layer uses validated generators to produce reproducible DAGs; knowledge layer allows domain experts to author Skills (Markdown vocab mappings, parameter constraints, optimization strategies); implemented on 1000 Genomes workflow and Hyperflow WMS on Kubernetes; ablation study with 150 queries.
AI.result: Full-match intent accuracy improved from 44% to 83% with Skills; deferred workflow generation reduced data transfer by 92%; end-to-end latency with LLM overhead under 15 seconds per query; per-query cost under $0.001.
AI.conclusion: Confining LLM non-determinism to intent extraction via Skills enables reproducible, efficient, and scalable scientific workflows; validated on large genomics workflows with measurable gains in accuracy, data transfer, and cost.

title: A Scale-Adaptive Framework for Joint Spatiotemporal Super-Resolution with Diffusion Models
summary: Deep-learning video super-resolution has progressed rapidly, but climate applications typically super-resolve (increase resolution) either space or time, and joint spatiotemporal models are often designed for a single pair of super-resolution (SR) factors (upscaling spatial and temporal ratio between the low-resolution sequence and the high-resolution sequence), limiting transfer across spatial resolutions and temporal cadences (frame rates). We present a scale-adaptive framework that reuses the same architecture across factors by decomposing spatiotemporal SR into a deterministic prediction of the conditional mean, with attention, and a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation (same precipitation amount in inputs and outputs) transform to preserve aggregated totals. Assuming that larger SR factors primarily increase underdetermination (hence required context and residual uncertainty) rather than changing the conditional-mean structure, scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: the diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta (larger for larger factors to increase diversity), the temporal context length L (set to maintain comparable attention horizons across cadences) and optionally a third, the mass-conservation function f (tapered to limit the amplification of extremes for large factors). Demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture spans super-resolution factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, yielding a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across scales.

AI.tldr: Scale-adaptive joint spatiotemporal SR framework for climate data, reusing architecture across spatial and temporal SR factors via a deterministic mean predictor with attention and a residual conditional diffusion model, with tunable hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Current video SR methods are typically fixed to a single SR factor and do not transfer well across different spatial resolutions and temporal cadences, limiting climate applications that require flexible SR across scales.
AI.method: Decompose spatiotemporal SR into (i) a deterministic conditional mean predictor with attention and (ii) a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation transform. Scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta; temporal context length L; and optionally a mass-conservation function f. Demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture handles SR factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, providing a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal SR across scales.
AI.result: The framework generalizes across a wide range of spatial and temporal SR factors, enabling reuse of a single architecture and a straightforward tuning protocol across scales; validated on Comephore precipitation data for France.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable, transferable approach for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution in climate data, leveraging simple hyperparameter retuning to adapt to larger SR factors and enabling cross-scale applicability.

title: From Codebooks to VLMs: Evaluating Automated Visual Discourse Analysis for Climate Change on Social Media
summary: Social media platforms have become primary arenas for climate communication, generating millions of images and posts that - if systematically analysed - can reveal which communication strategies mobilise public concern and which fall flat. We aim to facilitate such research by analysing how computer vision methods can be used for social media discourse analysis. This analysis includes application-based taxonomy design, model selection, prompt engineering, and validation. We benchmark six promptable vision-language models and 15 zero-shot CLIP-like models on two datasets from X (formerly Twitter) - a 1,038-image expert-annotated set and a larger corpus of over 1.2 million images, with 50,000 labels manually validated - spanning five annotation dimensions: animal content, climate change consequences, climate action, image setting, and image type. Among the models benchmarked, Gemini-3.1-flash-lite outperforms all others across all super-categories and both datasets, while the gap to open-weight models of moderate size remains relatively small. Beyond instance-level metrics, we advocate for distributional evaluation: VLM predictions can reliably recover population level trends even when per-image accuracy is moderate, making them a viable starting point for discourse analysis at scale. We find that chain-of-thought reasoning reduces rather than improves performance, and that annotation dimension specific prompt design improves performance. We release tweet IDs and labels along with our code at https://github.com/KathPra/Codebooks2VLMs.git.

AI.tldr: Promptable vision-language models enable scalable analysis of climate-related social media imagery; Gemini-3.1-flash-lite dominates benchmarks; distributional evaluation supports population-level insights; chain-of-thought decoding not beneficial; annotation-aware prompts improve performance; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: to enable large-scale, automated analysis of climate communication on social platforms by applying state-of-the-art vision-language models to labeled and unlabeled image datasets.
AI.method: benchmark six promptable VLMs and 15 zero-shot CLIP-like models on two datasets (1,038 expert-annotated images; 1.2M images with 50k validated labels) across five annotation dimensions: animal content, climate consequences, climate action, image setting, image type.
AI.result: Gemini-3.1-flash-lite achieves top performance across all super-categories and datasets; gap to open-weight models of moderate size is small; distributional evaluation recovers population-level trends; chain-of-thought reasoning reduces performance; dimension-specific prompt design improves results; data and code released.
AI.conclusion: VLM-based approaches offer a viable starting point for social media discourse analysis at scale, enabling population-level insights; practitioners should consider prompt strategies tailored to each annotation dimension.

title: SyMTRS: Benchmark Multi-Task Synthetic Dataset for Depth, Domain Adaptation and Super-Resolution in Aerial Imagery
summary: Recent advances in deep learning for remote sensing rely heavily on large annotated datasets, yet acquiring high-quality ground truth for geometric, radiometric, and multi-domain tasks remains costly and often infeasible. In particular, the lack of accurate depth annotations, controlled illumination variations, and multi-scale paired imagery limits progress in monocular depth estimation, domain adaptation, and super-resolution for aerial scenes. We present SyMTRS, a large-scale synthetic dataset generated using a high-fidelity urban simulation pipeline. The dataset provides high-resolution RGB aerial imagery (2048 x 2048), pixel-perfect depth maps, night-time counterparts for domain adaptation, and aligned low-resolution variants for super-resolution at x2, x4, and x8 scales. Unlike existing remote sensing datasets that focus on a single task or modality, SyMTRS is designed as a unified multi-task benchmark enabling joint research in geometric understanding, cross-domain robustness, and resolution enhancement. We describe the dataset generation process, its statistical properties, and its positioning relative to existing benchmarks. SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing research by enabling controlled experiments with perfect geometric ground truth and consistent multi-domain supervision. The results obtained in this work can be reproduced from this Github repository: https://github.com/safouaneelg/SyMTRS.

AI.tldr: SyMTRS is a large-scale synthetic, multi-task aerial dataset for depth, domain adaptation, and super-resolution, featuring high-res RGB (2048x2048), pixel-perfect depth, night-time variants, and aligned downsampled variants (x2, x4, x8).
AI.motivation: Addresses the scarcity of accurate ground truth for geometry, illumination variation, and multi-domain tasks in remote sensing, enabling a unified benchmark for joint research in monocular depth estimation, domain adaptation, and super-resolution.
AI.method: High-fidelity urban simulation pipeline generates synthetic urban scenes; outputs high-resolution RGB images, pixel-perfect depth maps, night-time counterparts, and aligned low-resolution variants; provides dataset generation details, statistics, and a public repository for reproducibility.
AI.result: Delivers a unified multi-task benchmark with perfect geometric ground truth and cross-domain supervision; enables controlled experiments across geometry, domain robustness, and resolution enhancement; results are reproducible via the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: SyMTRS aims to bridge critical gaps in remote sensing by enabling controlled, multi-task research with synthetic data, fostering advances in geometric understanding, domain robustness, and super-resolution for aerial scenes.

title: The Sample Complexity of Multicalibration
summary:
We study the minimax sample complexity of multicalibration in the batch setting. A learner observes $n$ i.i.d. samples from an unknown distribution and must output a (possibly randomized) predictor whose population multicalibration error, measured by Expected Calibration Error (ECE), is at most $\varepsilon$ with respect to a given family of groups. For every fixed $κ> 0$, in the regime $|G|\le \varepsilon^{-κ}$, we prove that $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-3})$ samples are necessary and sufficient, up to polylogarithmic factors. The lower bound holds even for randomized predictors, and the upper bound is realized by a randomized predictor obtained via an online-to-batch reduction. This separates the sample complexity of multicalibration from that of marginal calibration, which scales as $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$, and shows that mean-ECE multicalibration is as difficult in the batch setting as it is in the online setting, in contrast to marginal calibration which is strictly more difficult in the online setting. In contrast we observe that for $κ= 0$, the sample complexity of multicalibration remains $\widetildeΘ(\varepsilon^{-2})$ exhibiting a sharp threshold phenomenon.
  More generally, we establish matching upper and lower bounds, up to polylogarithmic factors, for a weighted $L_p$ multicalibration metric for all $1 \le p \le 2$, with optimal exponent $3/p$. We also extend the lower-bound template to a regular class of elicitable properties, and combine it with the online upper bounds of Hu et al. (2025) to obtain matching bounds for calibrating properties including expectiles and bounded-density quantiles.

AI.tldr: The paper determines the minimax sample complexity of batch multicalibration. For fixed κ>0 and |G| ≤ ε^{-κ}, the sample complexity is Θ~(ε^{-3}) (up to polylog factors), with the lower bound holding for randomized predictors and the upper bound via an online-to-batch reduction. This shows a separation from marginal calibration (Θ~(ε^{-2})) and indicates a sharp κ-threshold at κ=0: the complexity remains Θ~(ε^{-2}). More generally, they obtain matching upper/lower bounds for a weighted L_p multicalibration with exponent 3/p (1≤p≤2) and extend the approach to elicitable properties (e.g., expectiles, bounded-density quantiles).
AI.motivation: Quantify the fundamental sample requirements to achieve multicalibration in batch learning and contrast with marginal calibration and online settings. The work seeks tight minimax rates and how these depend on the family size G and on the chosen calibration metric.
AI.method: Prove lower bounds via information-theoretic or packing arguments; establish upper bounds through online-to-batch reductions; generalize to weighted L_p metrics and to a broad class of elicitable properties using a common lower-bound template.
AI.result: Tight minimax rates: Θ~(ε^{-3}) sample complexity for |G| ≤ ε^{-κ} and κ>0; κ=0 yields Θ~(ε^{-2}) with a sharp threshold; for 1≤p≤2, optimal exponent is 3/p; extends lower bounds to expectiles and bounded-density quantiles via elicitable properties.
AI.conclusion: Mean-ECE multicalibration in batch is as hard as in online settings, unlike marginal calibration; established tight, polylog-factor-separated rates across regimes and metrics, with broad applicability to other elicitable properties.

title: TEMA: Anchor the Image, Follow the Text for Multi-Modification Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is an important image retrieval paradigm that enables users to retrieve a target image using a multimodal query that consists of a reference image and modification text. Although research on CIR has made significant progress, prevailing setups still rely simple modification texts that typically cover only a limited range of salient changes, which induces two limitations highly relevant to practical applications, namely Insufficient Entity Coverage and Clause-Entity Misalignment. In order to address these issues and bring CIR closer to real-world use, we construct two instruction-rich multi-modification datasets, M-FashionIQ and M-CIRR. In addition, we propose TEMA, the Text-oriented Entity Mapping Architecture, which is the first CIR framework designed for multi-modification while also accommodating simple modifications. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that TEMA's superiority in both original and multi-modification scenarios, while maintaining an optimal balance between retrieval accuracy and computational efficiency. Our codes and constructed multi-modification dataset (M-FashionIQ and M-CIRR) are available at https://github.com/lee-zixu/ACL26-TEMA/.

AI.tldr: Proposes multi-modification CIR with two instruction-rich datasets (M-FashionIQ, M-CIRR) and a novel framework (TEMA).
AI.motivation: Current CIR relies on simple modification texts, causing insufficient entity coverage and clause-entity misalignment; real-world CIR requires richer, multi-modification queries.
AI.method: Introduce Text-oriented Entity Mapping Architecture (TEMA) designed for multi-modification, and construct two instruction-rich datasets (M-FashionIQ, M-CIRR) to evaluate performance across multi-modification and simple edits; assess on four benchmarks and analyze retrieval accuracy vs. efficiency; release code and data.
AI.result: TEMA outperforms baselines in both original and multi-modification settings while maintaining computational efficiency; the new datasets enable more comprehensive evaluation of CIR systems under realistic multi-modification scenarios.
AI.conclusion: TEMA advances composed image retrieval by enabling robust multi-modification queries, addressing entity coverage and alignment issues; the work provides practical datasets and open-source resources for broader evaluation and deployment.

title: Fine-Tuning Regimes Define Distinct Continual Learning Problems
summary: Continual learning (CL) studies how models acquire tasks sequentially while retaining previously learned knowledge. Despite substantial progress in benchmarking CL methods, comparative evaluations typically keep the fine-tuning regime fixed. In this paper, we argue that the fine-tuning regime, defined by the trainable parameter subspace, is itself a key evaluation variable. We formalize adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces, showing that changing the trainable depth alters the effective update signal through which both current task fitting and knowledge preservation operate. This analysis motivates the hypothesis that method comparisons need not be invariant across regimes. We test this hypothesis in task incremental CL, five trainable depth regimes, and four standard methods: online EWC, LwF, SI, and GEM. Across five benchmark datasets, namely MNIST, Fashion MNIST, KMNIST, QMNIST, and CIFAR-100, and across 11 task orders per dataset, we find that the relative ranking of methods is not consistently preserved across regimes. We further show that deeper adaptation regimes are associated with larger update magnitudes, higher forgetting, and a stronger relationship between the two. These results show that comparative conclusions in CL can depend strongly on the chosen fine-tuning regime, motivating regime-aware evaluation protocols that treat trainable depth as an explicit experimental factor.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the fine-tuning regime (trainable subspace/depth) is a crucial, often overlooked evaluation variable in continual learning. By formalizing adaptation regimes as projections of optimization onto fixed subspaces and empirically testing five depths across five datasets and 11 task orders with four methods, it shows that method rankings vary with regime, deeper adaptation increases update magnitudes and forgetting, and thus regime-aware evaluation is necessary.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning benchmarks typically fix the trainable parameter subspace, potentially biasing comparisons. The authors hypothesize that adapting under different regimes changes the effective update signal, influencing current-task fitting and knowledge preservation.
AI.method: Formalizes adaptation regimes as projected optimization over fixed trainable subspaces. Empirically evaluates five trainable-depth regimes across task-incremental learning using four CL methods (online EWC, LwF, SI, GEM) on five datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, KMNIST, QMNIST, CIFAR-100) with 11 task orders per dataset.
AI.result: The relative performance ranking of CL methods is not consistent across regimes. Deeper adaptation regimes exhibit larger update magnitudes and greater forgetting, with a stronger link between update size and forgetting. These findings demonstrate that conclusions from CL benchmarks can be regime-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Trainable depth should be treated as an explicit experimental factor in Regime-aware evaluation protocols for continual learning; comparisons should report results across multiple adaptation regimes to ensure generalizability.

title: Multiscale Super Resolution without Image Priors
summary: We address the ambiguities in the super-resolution problem under translation. We demonstrate that combinations of low-resolution images at different scales can be used to make the super-resolution problem well posed. Such differences in scale can be achieved using sensors with different pixel sizes (as demonstrated here) or by varying the effective pixel size through changes in optical magnification (e.g., using a zoom lens). We show that images acquired with pairwise coprime pixel sizes lead to a system with a stable inverse, and furthermore, that super-resolution images can be reconstructed efficiently using Fourier domain techniques or iterative least squares methods. Our mathematical analysis provides an expression for the expected error of the least squares reconstruction for large signals assuming i.i.d. noise that elucidates the noise-resolution tradeoff. These results are validated through both one- and two-dimensional experiments that leverage charge-coupled device (CCD) hardware binning to explore reconstructions over a large range of effective pixel sizes. Finally, two-dimensional reconstructions for a series of targets are used to demonstrate the advantages of multiscale super-resolution, and implications of these results for common imaging systems are discussed.

AI.tldr: Multiscale super-resolution via combinations of LR images at pairwise coprime pixel sizes yields a well-posed inverse problem, enabling efficient reconstructions by Fourier/least-squares methods; validated experimentally over a wide range of effective pixel sizes and demonstrated in 2D targets.
AI.motivation: Ambiguities in SR under translation make the inverse problem ill-posed. By combining LR images at different scales, especially with pairwise coprime pixel sizes, the problem becomes well-posed and stable, with practical implications for imaging systems using different sensor pixel sizes or magnification.
AI.method: Acquire LR images at multiple scales (via different pixel sizes or magnification). Use pairwise coprime pixel sizes to ensure a stable inverse. Reconstruct SR images using Fourier-domain techniques or iterative least squares. Provide a theoretical expression for LS reconstruction error under iid noise. Validate with 1D and 2D experiments using CCD hardware binning to span a broad range of effective pixel sizes.
AI.result: Demonstrates a stable inverse and efficient SR reconstruction algorithms. Provides analytical expression for the expected LS error under iid noise, highlighting the noise–resolution tradeoff. Experimental validation across 1D/2D cases with CCD binning and large pixel-size ranges. 2D reconstructions of multiple targets illustrate multiscale SR advantages and practical implications for imaging systems.
AI.conclusion: Multiscale SR with pairwise coprime pixel sizes resolves translation ambiguities, yielding a well-posed, efficiently solvable problem with favorable noise–resolution characteristics and broad applicability to standard imaging hardware.

title: Temporal Taskification in Streaming Continual Learning: A Source of Evaluation Instability
summary: Streaming Continual Learning (CL) typically converts a continuous stream into a sequence of discrete tasks through temporal partitioning. We argue that this temporal taskification step is not a neutral preprocessing choice, but a structural component of evaluation: different valid splits of the same stream can induce different CL regimes and therefore different benchmark conclusions. To study this effect, we introduce a taskification-level framework based on plasticity and stability profiles, a profile distance between taskifications, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS), which diagnoses how strongly small boundary perturbations alter the induced regime before any CL model is trained. We evaluate continual finetuning, Experience Replay, Elastic Weight Consolidation, and Learning without Forgetting on network traffic forecasting with CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping the stream, model, and training budget fixed while varying only the temporal taskification. Across 9-, 30-, and 44-day splits, we observe substantial changes in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer, showing that taskification alone can materially affect CL evaluation. We further find that shorter taskifications induce noisier distribution-level patterns, larger structural distances, and higher BPS, indicating greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations. These results show that benchmark conclusions in streaming CL depend not only on the learner and the data stream, but also on how that stream is taskified, motivating temporal taskification as a first-class evaluation variable.

AI.tldr: Changing how a data stream is split into tasks (taskification) can qualitatively alter continual learning (CL) evaluation; the paper presents a taskification-level framework (plasticity/stability profiles, profile distance, Boundary-Profile Sensitivity) and shows that different 9/30/44-day splits yield different forecasting performance, forgetting, and backward transfer, making taskification a first-class evaluation variable.
AI.motivation: Temporal partitioning of streams into tasks is not neutral; different valid splits can induce different CL regimes and benchmark conclusions. The work aims to quantify this effect and advocate considering taskification in evaluation.
AI.method: Introduce a taskification-level framework with plasticity and stability profiles, a profile distance metric between taskifications, and Boundary-Profile Sensitivity (BPS). Evaluate multiple CL methods (continual finetuning, Experience Replay, Elastic Weight Consolidation, Learning without Forgetting) on network traffic forecasting using CESNET-Timeseries24, keeping stream, model, and budget fixed while varying only temporal taskification across 9-, 30-, and 44-day splits.
AI.result: Found substantial variations in forecasting error, forgetting, and backward transfer across task splits, despite fixed data/architecture/budget. Shorter taskifications produce noisier distribution patterns, larger structural distances between taskifications, and higher BPS, indicating greater sensitivity to boundary perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Taskification of the stream is a core determinant of CL evaluation. Benchmark conclusions depend on both the learner and the data stream, but also critically on how the stream is partitioned into tasks; temporal taskification should be treated as a first-class evaluation variable.

title: Divide-then-Diagnose: Weaving Clinician-Inspired Contexts for Ultra-Long Capsule Endoscopy Videos
summary: Capsule endoscopy (CE) enables non-invasive gastrointestinal screening, but current CE research remains largely limited to frame-level classification and detection, leaving video-level analysis underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce and formally define a new task, diagnosis-driven CE video summarization, which requires extracting key evidence frames that covers clinically meaningful findings and making accurate diagnoses from those evidence frames. This setting is challenging because diagnostically relevant events are extremely sparse and can be overwhelmed by tens of thousands of redundant normal frames, while individual observations are often ambiguous due to motion blur, debris, specular highlights, and rapid viewpoint changes. To facilitate research in this direction, we introduce VideoCAP, the first CE dataset with diagnosis-driven annotations derived from real clinical reports. VideoCAP comprises 240 full-length videos and provides realistic supervision for both key evidence frame extraction and diagnosis. To address this task, we further propose DiCE, a clinician-inspired framework that mirrors the standard CE reading workflow. DiCE first performs efficient candidate screening over the raw video, then uses a Context Weaver to organize candidates into coherent diagnostic contexts that preserve distinct lesion events, and an Evidence Converger to aggregate multi-frame evidence within each context into robust clip-level judgments. Experiments show that DiCE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, producing concise and clinically reliable diagnostic summaries. These results highlight diagnosis-driven contextual reasoning as a promising paradigm for ultra-long CE video summarization.

AI.tldr: Diagnosis-driven CE video summarization: introducing VideoCAP dataset and DiCE framework; achieves concise, reliable diagnoses from ultra-long capsule endoscopy videos.
AI.motivation: Video-level CE analysis is underexplored. Diagnosing requires extracting sparse, clinically relevant evidence frames from long, noisy videos; current methods focus mainly on frame-level tasks.
AI.method: Define the task as diagnosis-driven video summarization. Create VideoCAP, a dataset with 240 full-length CE videos and diagnosis-driven annotations. Propose DiCE: Candidate screening over raw video, Context Weaver to organize candidates into coherent diagnostic contexts preserving lesion events, and Evidence Converger to aggregate multi-frame evidence within each context into clip-level diagnoses.
AI.result: Experiments show DiCE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, producing concise and clinically reliable diagnostic summaries across ultra-long CE videos.
AI.conclusion: Diagnosis-driven contextual reasoning is a promising paradigm for ultra-long CE video summarization; the VideoCAP dataset and DiCE framework pave the way for clinically meaningful, video-level analyses and faster CE interpretation.

title: Handbook of Rough Set Extensions and Uncertainty Models
comment: 159 pages. Peer-Reviewed Book. ISBN: 978-1-59973-867-3. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House
summary: Rough set theory models uncertainty by approximating target concepts through lower and upper sets induced by indiscernibility, or more generally, by granulation relations in data tables. This perspective captures vagueness caused by limited observational resolution and supports set-theoretic reasoning about what can be determined with certainty and what remains only possible. This book is written as a map of models. Rather than developing a single algorithmic pipeline in depth, it provides a systematic survey of the main rough set paradigms and their extension routes. More specifically, representative variants are organized according to (i) the underlying granulation mechanism, such as equivalence-based, tolerance-based, covering-based, neighborhood-based, and probabilistic approximations, and (ii) the uncertainty semantics attached to data and relations, such as crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, and plithogenic settings. The book also explains how each choice changes the form of approximations and the interpretation of boundary regions. Throughout the book, small illustrative examples are used to clarify modeling intent and typical use cases in classification and decision support. Finally, an important clarification of scope should be noted. Since the main purpose of this book is to provide a map of models, the Abstract and Introduction should not lead readers to expect that feature reduction and rule induction are primary objectives. Although these topics are central in the rough set literature, they are treated here mainly as motivating applications and as entry points to the broader research landscape. The principal aim of the book is to survey and position rough set models and their extensions in a systematic and coherent manner.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey mapping rough-set models and extensions across granulation (equivalence, tolerance, covering, neighborhood, probabilistic) and uncertainty semantics (crisp, fuzzy, intuitionistic fuzzy, neutrosophic, plithogenic), clarifying how these choices affect approximations and boundary regions; emphasizes a model-map rather than a pipeline.
AI.motivation: To organize and position the broad rough set literature into a coherent taxonomy, enabling understanding of how different granulation mechanisms and uncertainty semantics shape approximations and their interpretation, and to provide guidance for classification and decision-support applications.
AI.method: Systematic survey that categorizes representative rough-set variants by underlying granulation and uncertainty semantics, includes illustrative examples, discusses boundary regions, and explicitly notes scope restrictions (feature reduction/rule induction treated as applications rather than core objectives).
AI.result: A comprehensive map of models and extensions, showing how each granulation/semantics combination yields specific forms of lower/upper approximations and boundary concepts; accessible through a coherent taxonomy and illustrative examples; clarifying interpretative changes across settings.
AI.conclusion: The book aims to survey and position rough-set models rather than deliver a single algorithmic pipeline; it provides a navigable framework for researchers to understand, compare, and extend rough-set paradigms and their applications in classification and decision support.

title: Grounding Video Reasoning in Physical Signals
comment: Benchmark for Grounding Video Reasoning in Physical Signals
summary: Physical video understanding requires more than naming an event correctly. A model can answer a question about pouring, sliding, or collision from textual regularities while still failing to localize the event in time or space. We introduce a grounded benchmark for physical video understanding that extends the what--when--where evaluation structure of V-STaR to four video sources, six physics domains, three prompt families (physics, vstar_like, and neutral_rstr), and four input conditions (original, shuffled, ablated, and frame-masked). The benchmark contains 1,560 base video clips from SSV2, YouCook2, HoloAssist, and Roundabout-TAU. Each clip is first converted into a shared grounded event record, and the three query families are derived from that record. Temporal and spatial targets are shared across prompt families, while the non-physics families use deterministic family-appropriate semantic a_what targets derived from the same record. Across models and prompt families, physics remains the strongest regime overall, vstar_like is the clearest non-physics semantic comparison, and neutral_rstr behaves as a harder templated control. Prompt-family robustness is selective rather than universal, perturbation gains cluster in weak original cases, and spatial grounding is the weakest across settings. These results suggest that video Q&A reasoning benchmarks shall report physically grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics alongside aggregate accuracy.

AI.tldr: A grounded, multi-source video Q&A benchmark reveals physics-grounded reasoning is strongest, prompts vary in robustness, and spatial grounding is weak; benchmarks should report grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics alongside accuracy.
AI.motivation: To move beyond surface-level event naming by evaluating spatio-temporal grounding and reasoning across diverse data sources and prompt styles, addressing a gap in video understanding benchmarks.
AI.method: Build a grounded benchmark extending the what--when--where paradigm of V-STaR to four video sources (SSV2, YouCook2, HoloAssist, Roundabout-TAU), six physics domains, three prompt families (physics, vstar_like, neutral_rstr), and four input conditions (original, shuffled, ablated, frame-masked). Use 1,560 base clips converted into a shared grounded event record; derive three query families from this record; synchronize temporal/spatial targets across prompts; provide a physics-focused ground truth and deterministic non-physics a_what targets for other prompts; evaluate across models and prompt families with perturbations.
AI.result: Physics emerges as the strongest reasoning regime; vstar_like offers the clearest non-physics semantic baseline; neutral_rstr serves as a harder templated control. Robustness of prompts is selective; perturbation gains cluster in weaker original cases, and spatial grounding is the weakest dimension across settings. The findings imply reporting physically grounded, prompt-aware, and perturbation-aware diagnostics in addition to aggregate accuracy for video Q&A benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Future video Q&A benchmarks should adopt diagnostics for physical grounding, prompt awareness, and perturbation sensitivity, alongside conventional accuracy metrics.

title: Addressing Image Authenticity When Cameras Use Generative AI
comment: To appear in CVPR 2026 Workshop on Authenticity and Provenance in the Age of Generative AI
summary: The ability of generative AI (GenAI) methods to photorealistically alter camera images has raised awareness about the authenticity of images shared online. Interestingly, images captured directly by our cameras are considered authentic and faithful. However, with the increasing integration of deep-learning modules into cameras' capture-time hardware -- namely, the image signal processor (ISP) -- there is now a potential for hallucinated content in images directly output by our cameras. Hallucinated capture-time image content is typically benign, such as enhanced edges or texture, but in certain operations, such as AI-based digital zoom or low-light image enhancement, hallucinations can potentially alter the semantics and interpretation of the image content. As a result, users may not realize that the content in their camera images is not authentic. This paper addresses this issue by enabling users to recover the 'unhallucinated' version of the camera image to avoid misinterpretation of the image content. Our approach works by optimizing an image-specific multi-layer perceptron (MLP) decoder together with a modality-specific encoder so that, given the camera image, we can recover the image before hallucinated content was added. The encoder and MLP are self-contained and can be applied post-capture to the image without requiring access to the camera ISP. Moreover, the encoder and MLP decoder require only 180 KB of storage and can be readily saved as metadata within standard image formats such as JPEG and HEIC.

AI.tldr: Proposes an image-specific MLP decoder plus an encoder to reverse camera hallucinations in ISP-processed images, enabling post-capture recovery of pre-hallucination content and embedding the model as metadata (~180 KB) in standard formats.
AI.motivation: Images directly captured by cameras can be modified by integrated ISPs, introducing hallucinations that may mislead interpretation. A post-capture method is needed to recover authentic content without relying on ISP access.
AI.method: Jointly optimize an image-specific MLP decoder and a modality-specific encoder. The encoder processes the camera image to produce a latent representation; the MLP decoder reconstructs the pre-hallucination image. The system is self-contained, requires only ~180 KB of storage, and can be stored as metadata within JPEG or HEIC. No access to the camera ISP is required post-capture.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of recovering the non-hallucinated image content via an image-specific post-processing pipeline. The encoder+MLP pair can be saved as metadata (~180 KB) and applied post-capture to recover the original content.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical approach to mitigate misinterpretations caused by capture-time hallucinations, enabling users to retrieve authentic-looking content after capture while preserving a compact metadata footprint.

title: UniGenDet: A Unified Generative-Discriminative Framework for Co-Evolutionary Image Generation and Generated Image Detection
summary: In recent years, significant progress has been made in both image generation and generated image detection. Despite their rapid, yet largely independent, development, these two fields have evolved distinct architectural paradigms: the former predominantly relies on generative networks, while the latter favors discriminative frameworks. A recent trend in both domains is the use of adversarial information to enhance performance, revealing potential for synergy. However, the significant architectural divergence between them presents considerable challenges. Departing from previous approaches, we propose UniGenDet: a Unified generative-discriminative framework for co-evolutionary image Generation and generated image Detection. To bridge the task gap, we design a symbiotic multimodal self-attention mechanism and a unified fine-tuning algorithm. This synergy allows the generation task to improve the interpretability of authenticity identification, while authenticity criteria guide the creation of higher-fidelity images. Furthermore, we introduce a detector-informed generative alignment mechanism to facilitate seamless information exchange. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code: \href{https://github.com/Zhangyr2022/UniGenDet}{https://github.com/Zhangyr2022/UniGenDet}.

AI.tldr: A unified co-evolutionary framework UniGenDet that jointly treats image generation and generated-image detection, enabling mutual benefits via a symbiotic multimodal self-attention, unified fine-tuning, and detector-informed alignment, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Generative and discriminative tasks largely operate in separate architectural regimes; leveraging adversarial information across both domains and enabling mutual reinforcement can improve both generation fidelity and detection interpretability, despite structural divergence.
AI.method: Proposes UniGenDet with three components: (1) symbiotic multimodal self-attention bridging generation and detection; (2) unified fine-tuning to jointly optimize both tasks; (3) detector-informed generative alignment for cross-task information exchange.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple datasets show state-of-the-art performance; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that close coupling of generative and discriminative modules yields mutual benefits, with generation improving authenticity interpretability and detectors guiding higher-fidelity synthesis; supports a co-evolutionary direction for future work.

title: Directional Confusions Reveal Divergent Inductive Biases Through Rate-Distortion Geometry in Human and Machine Vision
summary: Humans and modern vision models can reach similar classification accuracy while making systematically different kinds of mistakes - differing not in how often they err, but in who gets mistaken for whom, and in which direction. We show that these directional confusions reveal distinct inductive biases that are invisible to accuracy alone. Using matched human and deep vision model responses on a natural-image categorization task under 12 perturbation types, we quantify asymmetry in confusion matrices and link it to generalization geometry through a Rate-Distortion (RD) framework, summarized by three geometric signatures (slope (beta), curvature (kappa)) and efficiency (AUC). We find that humans exhibit broad but weak asymmetries, whereas deep vision models show sparser, stronger directional collapses. Robustness training reduces global asymmetry but fails to recover the human-like breadth-strength profile of graded similarity. Mechanistic simulations further show that different asymmetry organizations shift the RD frontier in opposite directions, even when matched for performance. Together, these results position directional confusions and RD geometry as compact, interpretable signatures of inductive bias under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: Directional confusions reveal distinct inductive biases not captured by accuracy alone. Humans show broad, weak directional asymmetries; deep vision models show sparse, strong asymmetries. Rate-Distortion (RD) geometry (beta, kappa, AUC) captures these biases and shifts with robustness training and different asymmetry organizations.
AI.motivation: To understand how error structure (confusions) reveals inductive biases of humans and models under distribution shifts, beyond overall accuracy.
AI.method: Collect matched human and deep-vision model responses on a natural-image categorization task across 12 perturbations; quantify asymmetry in confusion matrices; map to RD framework; extract geometric signatures (beta, kappa, AUC); run robustness training and mechanistic simulations to study effects on RD frontier.
AI.result: Humans exhibit broad yet weak asymmetries in confusion; deep models exhibit sparse, strong directional collapses; robustness training reduces global asymmetry but does not recapture the human-like breadth-strength profile; mechanistic simulations show that different asymmetry organizations shift the RD frontier in opposite directions at equal performance.
AI.conclusion: Directional confusions and RD geometry provide compact, interpretable signatures of inductive bias under distribution shift, offering insights beyond accuracy for evaluating robustness and generalization.

title: When Prompts Override Vision: Prompt-Induced Hallucinations in LVLMs
summary: Despite impressive progress in capabilities of large vision-language models (LVLMs), these systems remain vulnerable to hallucinations, i.e., outputs that are not grounded in the visual input. Prior work has attributed hallucinations in LVLMs to factors such as limitations of the vision backbone or the dominance of the language component, yet the relative importance of these factors remains unclear. To resolve this ambiguity, We propose HalluScope, a benchmark to better understand the extent to which different factors induce hallucinations. Our analysis indicates that hallucinations largely stem from excessive reliance on textual priors and background knowledge, especially information introduced through textual instructions. To mitigate hallucinations induced by textual instruction priors, we propose HalluVL-DPO, a framework for fine-tuning off-the-shelf LVLMs towards more visually grounded responses. HalluVL-DPO leverages preference optimization using a curated training dataset that we construct, guiding the model to prefer grounded responses over hallucinated ones. We demonstrate that our optimized model effectively mitigates the targeted hallucination failure mode, while preserving or improving performance on other hallucination benchmarks and visual capability evaluations. To support reproducibility and further research, we will publicly release our evaluation benchmark, preference training dataset, and code at https://pegah-kh.github.io/projects/prompts-override-vision/ .

AI.tldr: HalluScope shows textual priors and instruction-derived knowledge drive hallucinations in LVLMs; HalluVL-DPO reduces these through preference-based fine-tuning while keeping or improving other capabilities; resources will be released.
AI.motivation: Disentangle and quantify factors causing LVLM hallucinations, beyond vision backbone and language dominance; provide reproducible benchmark and mitigation to improve groundedness.
AI.method: Introduce HalluScope benchmark to quantify factors; identify textual priors as main source; develop HalluVL-DPO to fine-tune LVLMs via preference optimization on a curated dataset to prefer grounded outputs.
AI.result: Empirical evidence that textual instruction priors are the primary cause; HalluVL-DPO reduces targeted hallucinations and maintains or improves performance on other hallucination benchmarks and visual evaluations.
AI.conclusion: HalluScope and HalluVL-DPO provide effective means to improve grounding in LVLMs; resources to be released for reproducibility and further research.

title: Vista4D: Video Reshooting with 4D Point Clouds
comment: 24 pages, 20 figures, CVPR 2026, see project page at https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Vista4D
summary: We present Vista4D, a robust and flexible video reshooting framework that grounds the input video and target cameras in a 4D point cloud. Specifically, given an input video, our method re-synthesizes the scene with the same dynamics from a different camera trajectory and viewpoint. Existing video reshooting methods often struggle with depth estimation artifacts of real-world dynamic videos, while also failing to preserve content appearance and failing to maintain precise camera control for challenging new trajectories. We build a 4D-grounded point cloud representation with static pixel segmentation and 4D reconstruction to explicitly preserve seen content and provide rich camera signals, and we train with reconstructed multiview dynamic data for robustness against point cloud artifacts during real-world inference. Our results demonstrate improved 4D consistency, camera control, and visual quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines under a variety of videos and camera paths. Moreover, our method generalizes to real-world applications such as dynamic scene expansion and 4D scene recomposition. See our project page for results, code, and models: https://eyeline-labs.github.io/Vista4D

AI.tldr: Vista4D introduces a 4D-grounded point cloud for robust video reshooting, enabling re-synthesis of dynamic scenes from new camera trajectories while preserving content and achieving improved 4D consistency and visual quality over baselines.
AI.motivation: Dynamic video reshooting suffers from depth artifacts and content degradation when changing viewpoints; a 4D representation with explicit content preservation and robust camera signals aims to deliver accurate, controllable resyntheses.
AI.method: Construct a 4D-grounded point cloud with static pixel segmentation and 4D reconstruction to preserve seen content. Ground input video and target cameras, and train with reconstructed multiview dynamic data to mitigate point-cloud artifacts. Re-synthesize the scene dynamics from a different camera trajectory and viewpoint while maintaining content consistency.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved 4D consistency, camera control, and visual quality compared with state-of-the-art baselines across diverse videos and camera paths. Shows generalization to real-world tasks like dynamic scene expansion and 4D scene recomposition.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers robust, camera-controllable video reshooting with real-world applicability, suggesting potential for flexible dynamic scene manipulation and explicit 4D content preservation.

title: Context Unrolling in Omni Models
summary: We present Omni, a unified multimodal model natively trained on diverse modalities, including text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations. We find that such training enables Context Unrolling, where the model explicitly reasons across multiple modal representations before producing predictions. This process enables the model to aggregate complementary information across heterogeneous modalities, facilitating a more faithful approximation of the shared multimodal knowledge manifold and improving downstream reasoning fidelity. As a result, Omni achieves strong performance on both multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks, while demonstrating advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation of text, image, video, and 3D geometry.

AI.tldr: Omni is a unified multimodal model trained across text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations, introducing Context Unrolling to reason across modalities and improve multimodal reasoning and generation.
AI.motivation: To enable faithful, cross-modal reasoning by unifying diverse modalities into a single model and capturing shared multimodal knowledge manifolds.
AI.method: Native training on multiple modalities, with Context Unrolling that explicitly reasons across heterogeneous modal representations before prediction, aggregating complementary information from different modalities.
AI.result: Strong performance on multimodal generation and understanding benchmarks; advanced multimodal reasoning capabilities, including in-context generation across text, image, video, and 3D geometry.
